blocks:
Ayahs

ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَجَعَلَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ وَٱلنُّورَ ۖ ثُمَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ (١)
All praise belongs to Allah who created the heavens and the earth and made the darkness and the light. Yet the faithless equate [others] with their Lord. (1)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن طِينٍ ثُمَّ قَضَىٰٓ أَجَلًا ۖ وَأَجَلٌ مُّسَمًّى عِندَهُۥ ۖ ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ تَمْتَرُونَ (٢)
It is He who created you from clay, then ordained the term [of your life]—the specified term is with Him—and yet you are in doubt. (2)

Surah Al-An'am: The Surah of Struggle Against All Forms of Polytheism and Idolatry

It is said that this Surah is the sixty-ninth Surah revealed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in Mecca. However, there is discussion regarding a few of its verses, as some believe these specific verses were revealed in Medina. Nevertheless, from the narrations that have reached us through the Ahl al-Bayt (علیهم السلام), it is understood that one of the distinctions of this Surah is that all its verses were revealed at once; therefore, the entire Surah is considered "Meccan."

The fundamental objective of this Surah, like other Meccan Surahs, is the invitation to the three principles of "Monotheism" (Tawhid), "Prophethood," and the "Resurrection" (Ma'ad). However, it revolves more than anything else around the issue of the Oneness of God and the struggle against polytheism (shirk) and idolatry, such that in a significant portion of the verses of this Surah, the address is directed toward "polytheists and idolaters." Accordingly, the thread of discussion occasionally shifts to the actions, behaviors, and innovations (bid'ah) of the polytheists.

In any case, contemplation and reflection upon the verses of this Surah—which are infused with vivid and clear arguments—revive the spirit of Monotheism and God-worship within man and demolish the foundations of polytheism. Perhaps it was due to this spiritual cohesion and the priority of the issue of Monotheism over other matters that all its verses were revealed at once.

It is also for this reason that in the narrations regarding the virtue of this Surah, we repeatedly read that seventy thousand angels escorted Surah Al-An'am during its revelation, and whoever recites it (and in its light, their soul and spirit are quenched by the fountainhead of Monotheism), all those angels seek forgiveness for him! Careful attention to the verses of this Surah can uproot the spirit of hypocrisy and division among Muslims, making ears hearing, eyes seeing, and hearts wise.

Yet, it is strange that some content themselves only with reciting its words and organize long and elaborate sessions for the "Khatm of Al-An'am" and the resolution of their personal and private problems with special ceremonies. Certainly, if the content of the Surah were reflected upon in these sessions, not only personal problems but also the general problems of Muslims would be solved. Alas, many people view the Quran as a series of "incantations" (awrad) possessing mysterious and unknown properties and think of nothing but reciting its words, whereas the Quran is entirely a lesson and a school, a program and an awakening, a mission and an awareness.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ جَعَلَ الظُّلُماتِ وَ النُّورَ ثُمَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ (1) هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ طِينٍ ثُمَّ قَضى‌ أَجَلاً وَ أَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى عِنْدَهُ ثُمَّ أَنْتُمْ تَمْتَرُونَ (2)

Commentary

This Surah begins with the praise and glorification of the Lord.

First, through the creation of the macrocosm (the heavens and the earth) and their systems, and then through the creation of the "microcosm, namely man," It directs people's attention to the principle of Monotheism, stating first: "Praise be to Allah, Who created the heavens and the earth" (الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ).

"The God Who is the source of light and darkness and, contrary to the belief of the dualists, is the Creator of all things" (وَ جَعَلَ الظُّلُماتِ وَ النُّورَ).

"But the polytheists and disbelievers, instead of learning the lesson of Monotheism from this unified system, set up equals and likenesses for their Lord" (ثُمَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ) [1]. It is noteworthy that the belief of the polytheists is mentioned with the word "thumma" (then), which in the Arabic language denotes "sequence with a gap." This indicates that in the beginning, Monotheism was an innate principle and a general, universal belief of mankind, and polytheism emerged later as a deviation from this innate principle.

As for why the word "khalaqa" (created) is used regarding the creation of the earth and heavens, while the word "ja'ala" (made/appointed) is used for light and darkness, commentators have offered various explanations. What seems closest to the truth is that "khalaqa" refers to the original existence of a thing, while "ja'ala" refers to the properties, effects, and qualities that follow existence. Since light and darkness have a secondary or derivative aspect, they are expressed with "ja'ala."

[1]: Ya'dilun is from the root 'adl (on the scale of hifz), meaning equal and of the same weight, and here it means attributing a partner or likeness.

It is interesting to note that in a narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), regarding the interpretation of this verse, it is mentioned that he said: "This verse actually responds to three groups of deviants: first, the materialists who imagined the world to be eternal and denied creation; second, the dualists who considered light and darkness to be two independent sources; and third, a refutation of the Arab polytheists who attributed partners and likenesses to God." [1]

Is Darkness Among the Created Things?

From the above verse, it is understood that just as "light" is a creation of Allah, "darkness" is also His creation. However, it is well-known among philosophers and natural scientists that "darkness" is nothing but the absence of light, and we know that the term "created" cannot be applied to non-existence. Therefore, how does the verse under discussion count darkness among the creations of Allah?! In response to this objection, it can be said:

Firstly, darkness does not always mean absolute darkness; rather, darkness is often used to mean very little and weak light in contrast to abundant and strong light. For example, we all say "a dark night," even though it is certain that in the night there is no absolute darkness; rather, the darkness of the night is always mixed with the faint light of stars or other light sources. Therefore, the meaning of the verse becomes that Allah appointed for you the brightness of the day and the darkness of the night—one being strong light and the other very weak light—and it is obvious that darkness in this sense is among the creations of God.

Secondly, while it is true that absolute darkness is a non-existent matter, a non-existent matter, when it occurs under specific conditions, certainly stems from an existential matter. That is, one who brings about absolute darkness under specific conditions for certain purposes must necessarily use existential means. For example, if we want to darken a room at a specific moment to develop a photograph, we must block the light so that darkness occurs at that specific moment; such darkness is created (created by consequence). In technical terms, although absolute non-existence is not created, "specific non-existence" has a share of existence and is created.

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 701.

Light is a Symbol of Unity and Darkness is a Symbol of Dispersion

Another point to be noted here is that in the verses of the Quran, "light" (nur) is brought in the singular form, while "darkness" (zulumat) is in the plural.

This expression might be a subtle allusion to the fact that darkness (whether sensory or spiritual) is always the source of dispersions, separations, and estrangements, whereas light is the symbol of unity and gathering.

We have often seen with our own eyes that on a summer night, if we light a lamp in the middle of a courtyard or a desert, in a short time, all kinds of insects gather around it, forming a community of life in diverse forms. But when we turn it off, each goes in a different direction and they disperse. It is the same in spiritual and social matters: the light of knowledge, the Quran, and faith is the cause of unity, while the darkness of ignorance, disbelief, and hypocrisy causes dispersion.

We said that this Surah, to strengthen the foundations of God-worship and Monotheism in hearts, first directs man toward the macrocosm, and in the next verse, it directs attention to the microcosm, namely man. In this regard, it points to the most amazing issue—his creation from dust and clay—saying: "He is the One Who created you from clay" (هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ طِينٍ).

It is true that our creation was from our fathers and mothers, not directly from dust, but since the creation of the first human was from dust and clay, it is correct that we also be addressed in this manner.

Then, referring to the developmental stages of human life, it says: "Then He decreed a term [for you to live]" so that during this period, man may grow and evolve on the earth (ثُمَّ قَضى‌ أَجَلًا).

Ajal originally means a "fixed period," and qada' ajal means determining the period or bringing the period to an end. However, it often happens that ajal is also used for the final deadline; for example, they say the ajal of a debt has arrived, meaning the final time for payment has come. The reason the arrival of death is also called ajal is that the last moment of a person's life is at that time.

Then, to complete this discussion, it says: "And a specified term is with Him" (وَ أَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى عِنْدَهُ). Afterward, it says: You polytheists harbor doubt and hesitation regarding the Creator Who created man from this worthless origin—clay—and passed him through these astonishing and wonder-inducing stages; you place worthless beings like idols in His rank, or you doubt the power of the Lord regarding the Resurrection and the reviving of the dead (ثُمَّ أَنْتُمْ تَمْتَرُونَ).

What is the "Specified Term" (Ajal Musamma)?

There is no doubt that the terms "specified term" (ajal musamma) and "a term" (ajalan) in the verse have two different meanings. The view of some who have taken both to mean the same thing is not at all consistent with the repetition of the word ajal, especially with the addition of the qualifier "specified" (musamma) the second time.

Therefore, commentators have discussed the difference between these two extensively. However, from what is understood by the context of other Quranic verses and the narrations that have reached us through the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), the difference between the two is that ajal alone means the lifespan, time, and duration that is not certain (provisional), while ajal musamma means the certain and fixed lifespan and duration. In other words, ajal musamma is natural death, and ajal is premature death.

To explain: many beings, in terms of their natural and inherent structure, have the potential and capability to survive for a long time. However, during this period, obstacles may arise that prevent them from reaching their maximum natural lifespan. For example, an oil lamp, considering its oil reservoir, might have the potential to stay lit for twenty hours. However, a strong wind, rain, or lack of care might cause its life to be shortened.

In this case, if the lamp encounters no obstacles and burns until its last drop of oil and then goes out, it has reached its "certain term" (ajal hatmi). If obstacles cause the lamp to go out before that, we call its lifespan the "uncertain term" (ajal ghayr hatmi).

The same applies to a human being. If all conditions for his survival are met and obstacles are removed, his structure and potential require him to live for a long time—though this period ultimately has an end and a limit. However, due to poor nutrition, falling into various addictions, committing suicide, or committing sins, he may die much earlier than that period. Death in the first case is called the "certain term" and in the second case, the "uncertain term."

In other words, the certain term is when we look at the sum of "complete causes," and the uncertain term is when we only consider the "potentialities."

By considering these two types of ajal, many matters become clear, including the narrations we read stating that "maintaining family ties (silat al-rahm) increases life" or "severing family ties shortens life" (the meaning of life and ajal in these cases is the uncertain term).

Or in the verse where we read: "When their term comes, they cannot put it back by a single hour, nor can they put it forward" [1]. Here, the meaning of ajal is that very certain death.

Therefore, the aforementioned verse only relates to the case where a person has reached his final lifespan and does not include premature deaths at all.

In any case, it must be noted that both terms are determined by God—one absolutely and the other as conditional or suspended. It is just as if we say: "This lamp will go out after twenty hours without any condition," and we also say, "If a storm blows, it will go out after two hours." Regarding humans, nations, and peoples, it is the same: we say God has willed that a certain person or nation will definitely perish after a certain amount of life, and we also say if they adopt injustice, oppression, hypocrisy, division, negligence, and laziness, they will perish in one-third of that time. Both terms are from God—one absolute and the other conditional.

It is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding the above verse that he said: "They are two terms: a decreed term (ajal mahtum) and a suspended term (ajal mawquf)." In other hadiths that have been transmitted on this subject, it is explicitly stated that the uncertain (conditional) term is subject to being advanced or delayed, while the certain term is not subject to change (Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 504).

[1]: Surah Al-A'raf, Verse 34.

Ayah

وَهُوَ ٱللَّهُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَفِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ يَعْلَمُ سِرَّكُمْ وَجَهْرَكُمْ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تَكْسِبُونَ (٣)
He is Allah in the heavens and on the earth: He knows your secret and your overt [matters], and He knows what you earn. (3)

Commentary

In this verse, to complete the previous discussion regarding Monotheism and the Oneness of God, and to respond to those who believe in a separate god for every category of beings—claiming there is a god of rain, a god of war, a god of peace, a god of the heavens, and the like—it states:

"He is the God whose Divinity governs all the heavens and the earth" (وَ هُوَ اللَّهُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ فِي الْأَرْضِ) [^1]. This means that since He is the Creator of all things, He is also the Disposer and Administrator of all. This is because even the polytheists of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) considered "Allah" to be the Creator, yet they attributed governance and influence to idols.

The verse responds to them: He who is the Creator, the governance and influence everywhere are also in His hands.

There is also another possibility in the interpretation of this verse: that Allah is present everywhere, in the heavens and on the earth, and no place is devoid of Him; not in the sense that He is a physical body or occupies a space, but rather that He encompasses all places.

It is evident that He who governs everywhere, has the management of all things in His hand, and is present everywhere, knows all secrets and hidden things. Therefore, in the next sentence, it says: "Such a Lord knows your hidden and your manifest, and He knows what you earn" (يَعْلَمُ سِرَّكُمْ وَ جَهْرَكُمْ وَ يَعْلَمُ ما تَكْسِبُونَ).

It might be said that "hidden" (sirr) and "manifest" (jahr) in the verse also include human actions and their intentions; thus, there would be no need to mention "ما تَكْسِبُونَ" (what you earn/do).

However, it must be noted that "earning" (kasb) refers to the results of actions and the spiritual states resulting from good and bad deeds. That is, He is aware of both your actions and intentions, as well as the effects these actions leave upon your soul. In any case, the mention of this phrase is for emphasis regarding human actions.

[^1]: Regarding the grammatical structure of this sentence, there is discussion among commentators, but the apparent meaning is that "هو" (He) is the subject (mubtada), "اللَّه" is the predicate (khabar), and "فى السماوات ..." is a prepositional phrase (jar wa majrur) relating to a verb understood from the word "Allah." The meaning of the sentence is effectively: "He is the Unique One in the heavens in His Divinity."

Ayahs

وَمَا تَأْتِيهِم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ مِّنْ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضِينَ (٤)
There did not come to them any sign from among the signs of their Lord, but that they used to disregard it. (4)
فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِٱلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِيهِمْ أَنۢبَـٰٓؤُا۟ مَا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٥)
They have certainly denied the truth when it came to them, but soon there will come to them the news of what they have been deriding. (5)

Commentary

We have stated that in Surah Al-An'am, the discourse is primarily addressed to the polytheists, and the Quran resorts to various means for their awakening and awareness; this verse and the many verses that follow it pursue this very subject.

In this verse, it points to the spirit of stubbornness, indifference, and arrogance of the polytheists in the face of the truth and the signs of Allah, stating: "They are so stubborn and indifferent that whenever a sign from the signs of their Lord comes to them, they immediately turn away from it." (وَ ما تَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ آيَةٍ مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا كانُوا عَنْها مُعْرِضِينَ) [^1] This means that the most basic condition for guidance and finding the path—which is research and investigation—does not exist within them. Not only is there no passion or love for finding the truth in their being, such that they would seek the truth like thirsty people running after water, but even if a spring of crystal-clear water were to gush at their doorstep, they would turn their faces away and not look at it at all; even if these signs are from their Lord (ربهم) and are revealed for the purpose of their own upbringing and perfection!

This spirit was not exclusive to the era of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) and the Arab polytheists; even now, we see many who, in a sixty-year lifespan, do not even grant themselves the trouble of one hour of research and investigation regarding Allah and religion. Far from it, if a book or writing in this field falls into their hands, they do not look at it, and if someone speaks to them about it, they do not listen. These are the stubborn and ignorant ones who may sometimes appear in the guise of scholars!

Then, it points to the result of this action of theirs and says: "The result was that they denied the truth when it came to them," whereas if they had contemplated the signs and tokens of their Lord, they would have clearly seen, recognized, and believed the truth (فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوا بِالْحَقِّ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ).

"And they will soon receive the consequence of this denial, and the news of what they used to mock will reach them" (فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِيهِمْ أَنْباءُ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ). In the two aforementioned verses, reference is actually made to three stages of disbelief that intensify stage by stage: first, the stage of aversion and turning away; then, the stage of denial (takdhib); and subsequently, the stage of mockery and ridiculing the truths and signs of Allah.

This matter shows that a person does not stop at one stage on the path of disbelief; rather, the further he proceeds, the more his intensity of denial, enmity, and hostility toward the truth and alienation from Allah increases.

The purpose of the threat mentioned at the end of the verse is that in the near or distant future, the ominous consequences of faithlessness will overtake them in this world and the Hereafter; the subsequent verses also bear witness to this interpretation.

[^1]: It should be noted that "آية" (sign) is an indefinite noun (nakirah) appearing in a context of negation; therefore, it denotes generality. This means they do not submit to any sign or token whatsoever and are not willing to conduct any study.

Ayah

أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن قَرْنٍ مَّكَّنَّـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَا لَمْ نُمَكِّن لَّكُمْ وَأَرْسَلْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ عَلَيْهِم مِّدْرَارًا وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرَ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهِمْ فَأَهْلَكْنَـٰهُم بِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَأَنشَأْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ قَرْنًا ءَاخَرِينَ (٦)
Have they not regarded how many a generation We have destroyed before them whom We had granted power in the land in respects that We did not grant you, and We sent abundant rains for them from the sky and made streams run for them? Then We destroyed them for their sins, and brought forth another generation after them. (6)

Commentary

The Fate of the Transgressors

From this verse onward, the Quran presents a step-by-step educational program to awaken the idolaters and polytheists—proportionate to the various motives behind polytheism and idol worship. First, it sets out to crush the factor of "pride," which is one of the major causes of rebellion, defiance, and deviation. By recalling the state of past nations and their painful end, it warns those whose eyes have been veiled by pride, saying: "Have they not seen how many generations We destroyed before them, whom We had established in the land with a position We have not given to you?" (أَ لَمْ يَرَوْا كَمْ أَهْلَكْنا مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنْ قَرْنٍ مَكَّنَّاهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ ما لَمْ نُمَكِّنْ لَكُمْ).

Among these blessings was that "We sent the skies pouring abundant rain upon them" (وَ أَرْسَلْنَا السَّماءَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِدْراراً) [1] "and We made rivers flow beneath them [their settlements and within their reach]" (وَ جَعَلْنَا الْأَنْهارَ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهِمْ).

However, when they chose the path of rebellion, none of these facilities could protect them from Divine punishment, "and We destroyed them for their sins" (فَأَهْلَكْناهُمْ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ).

[1]: Midrar is originally derived from the root drr, meaning milk, and was subsequently applied to liquids like rain that pour down. Midrar is an intensive form (mubalaghah), and the phrase "We sent the sky" is actually intended to express even greater emphasis.

"And We raised after them other generations" (وَ أَنْشَأْنا مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ قَرْناً آخَرِينَ).

Should not the study of the state of those who came before serve as an example for them?! Should they not wake up from the sleep of negligence and become sober from the intoxication of pride? Is the God who acted thus toward the predecessors not capable of implementing the same program regarding these people?! Here, several points should be noted:

1- Although "Qarn" (century/generation) is usually used to mean a long period of time (one hundred years, seventy years, or thirty years), sometimes—as linguists have specified—it refers to a people and a population living at the same time. (Fundamentally, qarn is derived from the root iqtiran, meaning "closeness" or "conjunction," and because the people of a single era and contiguous times are close to one another, both they and their time are called a qarn).

2- In the verses of the Quran, it is repeatedly mentioned that abundant material possibilities cause pride and negligence in people of weak character, for by possessing these, they imagine themselves independent of the Lord. They are heedless of the fact that if Divine help and aid do not reach them moment by moment and second by second, they will be destroyed and extinguished, just as We read: "Indeed, man transgresses because he sees himself self-sufficient" [1].

3- This warning is not exclusive to idolaters; today, the Quran also warns the wealthy mechanized world—which has become intoxicated by the wine of pride due to the availability of life's amenities—not to forget the state of the predecessors: how they lost everything due to the factor of sin. You too, with the ignition of the spark of another world war, might lose everything and return to the era before your industrial civilization. Be mindful that the cause of their misery was nothing but sin, injustice, oppression, tyranny, and lack of faith; this same factor has become apparent in your society as well.

Truly, studying the history of the lives of the Pharaohs of Egypt, the Kings of Sheba, the Sovereigns of Chaldea and Assyria, and the Caesars of Rome—with those legendary lives and boundless luxury—and then studying the painful consequences that folded the scroll of their lives due to disbelief and tyranny, is a great and clear lesson for everyone and for all of us.

[1]: Surah Al-Alaq, verses 6 and 7.

Ayah

وَلَوْ نَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ كِتَـٰبًا فِى قِرْطَاسٍ فَلَمَسُوهُ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ لَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٧)
Had We sent down to you a Book on paper so they could touch it with their [own] hands, [still] the faithless would have said, ‘This is nothing but plain magic.’ (7)

Commentary

The Ultimate Degree of Obstinacy

Another factor in their deviation is arrogance and obstinacy, which is alluded to in this verse. Arrogant individuals are usually obstinate people, as arrogance does not allow them to submit to the truth. This causes them to become stubborn, so to speak, and to deny every clear proof and obvious logic in some way, even if it reaches the extent of denying self-evident truths! We have frequently witnessed this phenomenon among arrogant and selfish individuals.

Here, the Quran refers to the demand of a group of idolaters (it is said these individuals were Nadr ibn al-Harith, Abdullah ibn Abi Umayyah, and Nawfal ibn Khuwaylid, who told the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "We will only believe if You bring down a letter from Allah to us accompanied by four angels!!"). It says: "And even if We had sent down to you a written scripture on a page [of paper or the like], and they had touched it with their hands, those who disbelieve would say, 'This is nothing but obvious magic!'" (وَ لَوْ نَزَّلْنا عَلَيْكَ كِتاباً فِي قِرْطاسٍ فَلَمَسُوهُ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ لَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُبِينٌ).

This means the circle of their obstinacy has expanded to such an extent that they deny the clearest of sensory perceptions—that which is perceived through "observation" and "touch"—and refuse to submit to it under the pretext of magic. This is while in their daily lives, they settle for even one-tenth of such signs to prove realities and consider them certain and definitive! This is due to nothing but the selfishness, arrogance, and severe obstinacy that has cast a shadow over their souls.

Furthermore, it should be noted that "Qirtas" (قرطاس) refers to anything upon which one writes, whether it be paper, skin, or tablets. If today "Qirtas" is used only for paper, it is because paper is the most common material used for writing.

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ ۖ وَلَوْ أَنزَلْنَا مَلَكًا لَّقُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ ثُمَّ لَا يُنظَرُونَ (٨)
They say, ‘Why has not an angel been sent down to him?’ Were We to send down an angel, the matter would surely be decided, and then they would not be granted any respite. (8)
وَلَوْ جَعَلْنَـٰهُ مَلَكًا لَّجَعَلْنَـٰهُ رَجُلًا وَلَلَبَسْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّا يَلْبِسُونَ (٩)
Had We made him an angel, We would have surely made him a man, and We would have surely confounded them just as they confound [the truth now]. (9)
وَلَقَدِ ٱسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَحَاقَ بِٱلَّذِينَ سَخِرُوا۟ مِنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (١٠)
Apostles were certainly derided before you. Then those who ridiculed them were besieged by what they used to deride. (10)

Commentary

Excuse-making

Another factor of disbelief and denial is excuse-making, although excuse-making itself is a product of other factors such as arrogance and selfishness; however, it gradually turns into a negative mindset and becomes a factor in itself for refusing to submit to the Truth.

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 5; p. 160

Among the excuses the polytheists raised against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—which have been mentioned in several verses of the Quran and also appear in the verse under discussion—is that they would say: Why has the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله undertaken this great mission alone? "Why does a being from a non-human species, from the genus of angels, not accompany him in this mission?" Can a human being who is of our own kind carry the burden of messengership alone? (وَ قالُوا لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ).

This is while, despite the clear proofs and manifest signs of his prophethood, there is no room for such excuses. Furthermore, an angel possesses neither a power higher than a human nor a greater readiness or talent for messengership than him; rather, the human is far more prepared than an angel.

The Quran responds to them with two sentences, each containing an argument:

First: "If an angel were to be sent down, and then they did not believe, the lives of all of them would be ended" (وَ لَوْ أَنْزَلْنا مَلَكاً لَقُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ ثُمَّ لا يُنْظَرُونَ).

But why would the deniers face death and destruction with the coming of an angel and his accompaniment of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله? The reason is the same as what was mentioned a few verses prior: if prophethood takes on a witnessed and sensory aspect—meaning that with the coming of the angel, the Unseen turns into the witnessed and they see everything with their own eyes—it becomes the final stage of the completion of proof (itmam al-hujjah). Since no proof higher than this can be imagined, if someone were to oppose it under such circumstances, their punishment and retribution would be certain. However, Allah, out of His grace and mercy toward His servants, so that they may have an opportunity for reconsideration, does not do this except in specific cases where He knows the party has full readiness and the capacity to accept, or in cases where the party deserves destruction—meaning they have committed acts that warrant Divine punishment. At such a time, their request is granted, and when they do not accept, the command for their destruction is issued.

The second response is that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, by virtue of his position of leadership and being responsible for the education of the people and providing them with a practical role model, must be of the same species as the people, sharing their color and attributes, and possessing all human instincts and qualities. This is because an angel, in addition to being invisible to humans, cannot serve as a practical role model for them, as he is neither aware of their needs and pains nor familiar with the state of their instincts and desires. For this reason, his leadership over a being who differs from him in every respect would be completely inadequate.

Therefore, in the second response, the Quran says: "If We had made him an angel and acted upon their suggestion, it would still have been necessary to create all human attributes within him, and to place him in the form and character of a man" (وَ لَوْ جَعَلْناهُ مَلَكاً لَجَعَلْناهُ رَجُلًا) [^1]. From what we have said, it becomes clear that the meaning of the phrase "لَجَعَلْناهُ رَجُلًا" (We would have made him a man) is not merely that We would give him a human shape, as some commentators have imagined; rather, it means that We would endow him with human attributes in both appearance and inner nature.

Then it concludes that, in such a case, they would repeat the same previous objections against Us: why have You given the mission of leadership to a human and obscured the face of reality from us? (وَ لَلَبَسْنا عَلَيْهِمْ ما يَلْبِسُونَ).

Labs (rhyming with dars) means concealment and causing confusion, while libs (rhyming with qufl) means wearing clothes. (The past tense of the first is labasa like daraba, and the past tense of the second is labisa like hasiba). It is clear that in the verse, the first meaning is intended. That is, if We had sent an angel, he would have to be in human form and character, and at that point, in their view, We would have led the people into confusion and error, and they would repeat the same previous attributions against Us, just as they themselves lead ignorant and uninformed individuals into confusion and error and obscure the face of reality from them. Therefore, the attribution of "labs" and concealment to Allah is from the perspective of their viewpoint.

In the end, Allah consoles His Prophet and says: Do not be concerned by their opposition, obstinacy, and stubbornness, for "A group of messengers before you were also mocked and ridiculed, but finally, that which they used to mock encompassed them, and Divine punishment descended upon them" (وَ لَقَدِ اسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ فَحاقَ بِالَّذِينَ سَخِرُوا مِنْهُمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ).

In reality, this verse is both a source of consolation for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله so that not the slightest wavering occurs in his will on his path, and a threat to the stubborn opponents to reflect upon the grim and painful consequences of their actions. [^1]

[^1]: The pronoun in "ja'alnahu" can refer to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, or it may refer to the one sent with the Prophet to reinforce his prophethood. In the second case, their suggestion would have been acted upon, and in the first case, it would be something even higher than their suggestion.

Ayah

قُلْ سِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (١١)
Say, ‘Travel over the land, and then observe how was the fate of the deniers.’ (11)

Commentary

Here, the Quran approaches the awakening of these stubborn and egoistic individuals from another angle, instructing the Prophet to command them to travel and journey throughout the earth and see with their own eyes the consequences of those who denied the truths, so that perhaps they might awaken: (قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ ثُمَّ انْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ‌) "Say, 'Travel through the land; then observe how was the end of the deniers.'"

There is no doubt that observing the remains of predecessors and nations who tread the path of annihilation and destruction due to rejecting the truths has a far greater impact than merely studying their history in books; for these remains make the truth perceptible and tangible.

Perhaps it is for this reason that the phrase unthuru (look/observe) has been used rather than tafakkaru (reflect/think).

Furthermore, the use of the word thumma (then), which is usually employed for a conjunction involving a lapse of time, may be to draw attention to the fact that they should not rush in this journey and judgment. Rather, once they have observed the remains of the predecessors, they should reflect with patience and precision, then draw a conclusion and see with their own eyes the ultimate end of their affairs. Regarding traveling through the earth and its extraordinary effect on awakening thoughts, we have discussed this in detail under verse 137 of Surah Al-Imran (Volume 3, page 102).

Ayahs

قُل لِّمَن مَّا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ قُل لِّلَّهِ ۚ كَتَبَ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ ٱلرَّحْمَةَ ۚ لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ ۚ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٢)
Say, ‘To whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth?’ Say, ‘To Allah. He has made mercy incumbent upon Himself. He will surely gather you on the Day of Resurrection, in which there is no doubt. Those who have ruined their souls will not have faith.’ (12)
۞ وَلَهُۥ مَا سَكَنَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (١٣)
To Him belongs whatever abides in the night and the day, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (13)

Commentary

In this verse, the discussion with the polytheists continues. In the preceding verses, the focus was on the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the worship of the One God; however, in this verse, the focus shifts to the issue of the Resurrection (Ma'ad). By referencing the principle of Monotheism, the matter of the Resurrection is pursued through a fascinating method. The structure of the verse is in the form of a question and answer, where the speaker of both the question and the answer is the same, which is a beautiful literary style.

The argument for the Resurrection here consists of two premises:

1- First, He says: "Say: To whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth?" (قُلْ لِمَنْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ). Immediately following that, He says: Answer them yourself from the tongue of their own fitrah (innate nature) and soul: "To Allah" (قُلْ لِلَّهِ).

According to this premise, the entire universe is the property of Allah, and its governance is in His hands.

2- "The Lord of the worlds is the source of all mercies; He is the One who has taken mercy upon Himself as an obligation, and He bestows countless blessings upon everyone" (كَتَبَ عَلى‌ نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ).

Is it possible for such a God to allow the thread of human life to be completely severed by death, and for perfection and life not to continue? Does this align with the principle of His being "All-Bounteous" (Fayyad) and His "All-Encompassing Mercy"? Is it possible for Him to show such unkindness toward His servants—of whom He is the Owner and Governor—that after a period, they tread the path of annihilation and turn into nothingness?

Certainly not. Rather, His All-Encompassing Mercy necessitates that He advances creatures, especially humans, along the path of perfection. Just as in the light of His mercy He transforms a small, insignificant seed into a massive, fruitful tree or a beautiful branch of flowers, and just as in the light of His grace He transforms an insignificant drop of fluid into a complete human being, this same mercy necessitates that He brings man—who possesses the potential for survival and eternal life—into a new garment of life in a vaster world after death, and that His hand of mercy supports him in this eternal journey of perfection.

Therefore, following these two premises, He says: "He will surely gather you all on the Day of Resurrection, a day about which there is no doubt" (لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ).

It is noteworthy that the verse begins with a question—a so-called rhetorical interrogation (istifham taqriri) intended to obtain an admission from the opposing party. Since this matter was certain from the perspective of innate nature (fitrah) and the polytheists themselves admitted that the ownership of the universe does not belong to idols but to Allah, He immediately provides the answer to the question Himself.

This is considered a beautiful method in presenting various issues.

Another point is that the Resurrection has been argued for in other places through various means—the law of justice, the law of evolution/perfection, and the wisdom of the Lord—but the argument through "Mercy" is a fresh reasoning discussed in the above verse.

At the end of the verse, He refers to the fate and end of the stubborn polytheists, saying:

"Those who have lost their own souls [the capital of their existence] in the marketplace of life's trade do not believe in these truths" (الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ).

What an amazing expression! Sometimes a person loses wealth, status, or another of his assets; in these cases, although he has suffered a loss, he has lost things that were not part of his essence—meaning they were external to his being. However, the greatest loss, which can be called the "true loss," occurs when a person loses the very essence of his being and gambles away his own existence.

The enemies of truth and stubborn individuals lose the capital of their lives, their intellect, their reason, their innate nature, and all their spiritual and physical blessings—which they should have employed in the path of truth to reach their worthy perfection—entirely. Neither the capital remains, nor the capitalist!

This expression appears in several verses of the Holy Quran and is a soul-shaking description that clarifies the painful end of the deniers of truth and the defiled sinners.

Question

It might be said that eternal life is an instance of "mercy" only for the believers, but for others, it will be nothing but "hardship and misery."

Answer

There is no doubt that Allah's work is to provide the grounds for mercy. He created man, gave him intellect, sent prophets for his leadership and guidance, placed various blessings at his disposal, and opened a path toward eternal life for everyone. These are, without exception, mercy.

Now, if in the process of bringing these mercies to fruition, man himself deviates from his path and turns all the grounds of mercy into torture and hardship for himself, this does not in any way detract from their being mercy. All blame is directed at the human who has transformed the grounds of mercy into punishment.

The next verse is, in fact, a complement to the previous one. While the previous verse referred to Allah's ownership of all creatures through their placement in the horizon of "space" (hence saying Allah owns what is in the heavens and the earth), this verse refers to His ownership through their placement in the horizon and expanse of "time." Thus, it says: "And to Him belongs whatever dwells in the night and the day" (وَ لَهُ ما سَكَنَ فِي اللَّيْلِ وَ النَّهارِ).

In reality, the material world is not devoid of these two—"time" and "space"—and all creatures that exist within the vessel of time and space (meaning the entire material world) belong to Him. It should not be imagined that night and day are exclusive to the solar system; rather, all creatures of the earth and heaven have night and day. Some live in perpetual day without night, and some in night without day. For example, on the sun, it is always day because there is light and no darkness exists, while some extinguished and lightless celestial bodies in the heavens that are not adjacent to stars live in the darkness of eternal night. The above verse encompasses all of these.

Additionally, it should be noted that the meaning of sakan (dwelling/resting) is to reside in the sense of staying and being situated in something, whether that creature is in a state of motion or rest. For example, we say: "We reside (sakin) in such-and-such a city," meaning we are established and staying there, regardless of whether we are moving in the city streets or are at rest.

There is also the possibility in the verse that "rest" (sukun) here is only the opposite of motion. Since these two are relative matters, mentioning one makes us independent of the other. Therefore, the meaning of the verse becomes: "Whatever is in a state of rest or motion in the day and night and the horizon of time, all belongs to Allah."

In this case, the verse can refer to one of the arguments for Monotheism, because "motion" and "rest" are two accidental states that are certainly "originated" (hadith) and cannot be eternal (qadim). This is because motion consists of something being in two different places at two different times, and rest is something being in one specific place at two different times. Thus, within the essence of motion and rest, a reference to a preceding state is hidden, and we know that something preceded by another state cannot be eternal.

From this statement, we conclude that: "Bodies are not devoid of motion and rest."

And: "That which is not devoid of motion and rest cannot be eternal."

Therefore, all bodies are originated (hadith), and because they are originated, they are in need of a Creator (reflect carefully).

However, since Allah is not a body, He has neither motion nor rest, neither time nor space; for this reason, He is Eternal and Everlasting. At the end of the verse, after mentioning Monotheism, He refers to two prominent attributes of Allah, saying: "And He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing" (وَ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ).

This indicates that the vastness of the universe and the creatures situated in the horizons of time and space never prevent Allah from being aware of their secrets. Rather, He hears their words and even perceives the movement of a weak ant in the heart of a dark night upon a black, dark stone in the depths of a silent and remote valley; He is aware of its needs and the needs of others, and is informed of the actions and deeds of all.

Ayahs

قُلْ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَتَّخِذُ وَلِيًّا فَاطِرِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ يُطْعِمُ وَلَا يُطْعَمُ ۗ قُلْ إِنِّىٓ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ أَوَّلَ مَنْ أَسْلَمَ ۖ وَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٤)
Say, ‘Shall I take for guardian [anyone] other than Allah, the originator of the heavens and the earth, who feeds and is not fed?’ Say, ‘I have been commanded to be the first of those who submit [to Allah], and [told,] “Never be one of the polytheists.” ’ (14)
قُلْ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّى عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٥)
Say, ‘Indeed, should I disobey my Lord, I fear the punishment of a tremendous day.’ (15)
مَّن يُصْرَفْ عَنْهُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمَهُۥ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٦)
Whoever is spared of it on that day, He has certainly been merciful to him, and that is manifest success. (16)

Commentary

There is No Refuge Other than Allah

Some have narrated a "Reason for Revelation" (Shan al-Nuzul) for the above verses: "A group from the people of Mecca came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and said: 'O Muhammad! You have abandoned the religion of your people, and we know that nothing caused this except poverty! We are ready to divide our wealth with you and make you completely rich so that you may let go of our gods and return to our original religion.' The above verses were revealed and responded to them." [^1] Of course, as mentioned previously, according to the narrated traditions, the verses of this Surah were revealed all at once in Mecca; therefore, each cannot have a specific individual reason for revelation. However, in the times prior to the revelation of this Surah, there were dialogues and debates between the Prophet and the polytheists, and a portion of the verses of this Surah oversees those dialogues. Thus, there is no obstacle to such words having been exchanged between the Prophet and the polytheists, with Allah referring to those words in these verses and providing the answer.

In any case, the objective in these verses is also to prove Monotheism (Tawhid) and to combat polytheism and idol-worship. Although the polytheists considered the creation of the world to be exclusive to the Essence of Allah, they had chosen idols as their support and refuge, and sometimes relied on one of the idols for each of their needs. They believed in multiple gods: the god of rain, the god of light, the god of darkness, the god of war and peace, and the god of sustenance and provision; this is the same belief in the "Lords of Species" (Arbab al-Anwa') that also existed in ancient Greece.

[^1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi and Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan under the verse.

To destroy this false notion, the Quran commands the Prophet as follows:

"Say to them: Shall I take as a guardian and refuge anyone other than Allah? While He is the Creator of the heavens and the earth, and the Provider of all beings without Himself having any need for provision" (قُلْ أَ غَيْرَ اللَّهِ أَتَّخِذُ وَلِيًّا فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ هُوَ يُطْعِمُ وَ لا يُطْعَمُ).

Therefore, when He is the Creator of everything, and has brought the entire universe into existence without relying on any other power, and the sustenance of everyone is in His hand, what reason is there for a human to take someone other than Him as a guardian, protector, and support? Fundamentally, everyone else is a creature and is in need of Him at every moment of their existence; how can they fulfill the needs of another?

Interestingly, in the above verse, when speaking of the creation of the heaven and the earth, it introduces Allah as "Fatir." "Fatir" is derived from the root "Futur," meaning "to split." It is narrated from Ibn Abbas that he said: "I only understood the meaning of 'Fatir al-Samawati wal-Ard' when two Arabs were disputing over a water well; one of them, to prove his ownership, said: 'Ana fatartuha' (I split it and initiated it)!" But today, with the help of modern sciences, we can understand the meaning of "Fatir" even better than Ibn Abbas, for this is a fascinating expression that harmonizes with the most precise modern scientific theories regarding the origin of the universe. According to the research of scientists, the macro-universe (the cosmos) and the micro-universe (the solar system) were all initially a single mass that split apart due to successive explosions, and galaxies, systems, and spheres came into being. In verse 30 of Surah Al-Anbiya, this matter is stated with even greater clarity where it says: "أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ كانَتا رَتْقاً فَفَتَقْناهُما" (Do the disbelievers not know that the heavens and the earth were joined together and We separated them?).

Another point that should not be neglected is that among the attributes of Allah here, emphasis is placed only on feeding the servants and providing them with sustenance. This expression is perhaps because most dependencies in the material life of man are due to this very material need; it is this so-called "eating a morsel of bread" that compels individuals to humble themselves before the masters of gold and power, and sometimes they bow before them to the extent of worship. The Quran, in the above phrase, says: Your sustenance is in His hand, not in the hands of such individuals nor in the hands of "idols." The masters of gold and power are themselves needy and in need of being fed; it is only Allah who feeds and has no need to be fed.

In other verses of the Quran, we also see emphasis placed on the issue of Allah's ownership, His role as the Provider, the sending of rain, and the nurturing of plants, so as to completely remove the thought of dependency on creatures from the minds of human beings.

Then, to respond to the proposal of those who invited him to join the religion of polytheism, He says: In addition to the fact that reason commands me to rely only on the One who is the Creator of the heavens and the earth, divine revelation has also commanded me to be the first Muslim and by no means to be among the polytheists (قُلْ إِنِّي أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ أَوَّلَ مَنْ أَسْلَمَ وَ لا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ). [^1] There is no doubt that before the Prophet of Islam, other prophets and their righteous nations were also Muslims—

[^1]: The phrase "إِنِّي أُمِرْتُ..." is of the nature of indirect address, and the phrase "وَ لا تَكُونَنَّ..." is a direct address. Perhaps this difference is because being free and distant from polytheism is degrees more important than being the first Muslim; therefore, the issue of distancing from polytheism is expressed as a direct address and with the "Nun of heavy emphasis" (Nun al-Tawkid al-Thaqilah).

—and were surrendered to the will of Allah. Therefore, when He says: "I am commanded to be the first Muslim," it means the first Muslim of this nation.

And this, in reality, points to an important educational matter as well: that every leader must be ahead of all individuals in implementing the commands of his own school of thought. He must be the first believer in his own religion, the first practitioner, the most diligent individual, and the most self-sacrificing person for his school of thought.

And in the next verse, to place more emphasis on this divine command revealed to the Prophet through revelation, He says:

I too, for my part, feel a sense of responsibility and am by no means an exception to divine laws; "I too, if I deviate from the command of my Lord and follow the path of compromise with the polytheists and commit sin and disobedience toward Him, am fearful and afraid of the punishment of that Great Day—the Day of Resurrection" (قُلْ إِنِّي أَخافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّي عَذابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ). [^1] From this verse, it is also well understood that the sense of responsibility of the prophets is greater than the sense of responsibility of others.

And in the final verse, to prove that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) also cannot do anything without relying on the grace and mercy of Allah, and that everything is in His hand—and even the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself has fixed his eyes of hope upon the infinite mercy of the Lord and seeks his salvation and victory from Him—He says: "Whoever is spared from the punishment of the Lord on that Great Day has indeed been encompassed by Allah's mercy, and that is a manifest success and victory" (مَنْ يُصْرَفْ عَنْهُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمَهُ وَ ذلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْمُبِينُ).

These verses express the highest degree of Monotheism (Tawhid) and explicitly respond even to those who believed in an independent status for the prophets alongside Allah—such as the Christians who considered Christ to be the savior and deliverer—stating that even the prophets are in need of His mercy!

[^1]: Interestingly, the arrangement of the sentence structure would normally require the word "akhāfu" (I fear) to be mentioned after the phrase "in 'asaytu Rabbī" (if I disobey my Lord), as it serves as the result of the condition. However, the Prophet's fear and sense of responsibility before the command of the Lord caused the word "akhāfu" to be brought forward for the sake of emphasis!

Ayahs

وَإِن يَمْسَسْكَ ٱللَّهُ بِضُرٍّ فَلَا كَاشِفَ لَهُۥٓ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ وَإِن يَمْسَسْكَ بِخَيْرٍ فَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٧)
Should Allah visit you with some distress there is no one to remove it except Him; and should He bring you some good, then He has power over all things. (17)
وَهُوَ ٱلْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ (١٨)
And He is the All-dominant over His servants, and He is the All-wise, the All-aware. (18)

Commentary

The Overpowering Power of the Lord

We have stated that the primary objective of this Surah is to uproot the factors of polytheism and idol worship; this same reality is pursued in the two aforementioned verses.

First, it asks: "Why do you turn to other than Allah? And why do you seek refuge in fabricated deities to solve your problems, repel harm, or gain benefit, while 'if Allah touches you with harm, none can remove it but He; and if He touches you with good, it is from the radiance of His power, for He is Able to do all things'" (وَ إِنْ يَمْسَسْكَ اللَّهُ بِضُرٍّ فَلا كاشِفَ لَهُ إِلَّا هُوَ وَ إِنْ يَمْسَسْكَ بِخَيْرٍ فَهُوَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ) [^1]. In reality, turning to other than Allah occurs because people consider them either the source of blessings or the removers of calamities and difficulties—just as submission to the point of worship before the possessors of power and wealth stems from one of these two motives. The verse states that the will of Allah governs everything; if He withdraws a blessing from someone or bestows a blessing upon someone, there is no source of power in the world that can alter it. So why do they bow their heads in submission before other than Allah?

The expression "يمسسك" (touches you) regarding "good" and "evil," derived from the root "مس" (touch), indicates that even the smallest good or evil is impossible without His will and power.

It is also necessary to mention that this verse, with complete clarity, rejects the belief of the "Dualists" (Zoroastrians/Manichaeans) who believed in two origins for good and evil, attributing both to Allah. However, we have explained in its proper place that "absolute evil" does not exist in the universe. Therefore, when evil is attributed to Allah, it refers to matters that outwardly appear as a deprivation of blessing but are, in reality and in their own context, good—whether for a wake-up call, education and training, removing arrogance, rebellion, and selfishness, or for other divine wisdoms.

In the next verse, to complete this discussion, it says: "And He is the Overpowering (Al-Qahir) over His servants" (وَ هُوَ الْقاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبادِهِ‌).

[^1]: "Harm" (Durr) consists of the deficiencies that afflict a human being, whether they are physical, such as the loss of a limb or various diseases, or spiritual, such as ignorance, folly, and insanity, or other aspects like the loss of wealth, status, and children.

"Qahr" (Overpowering) and "Ghalabah" (Victory/Dominance), although conveying a similar meaning, differ in their linguistic roots. "Qahr" and "Qahiriyyah" refer to that type of dominance and victory where the opponent cannot show any resistance whatsoever. However, the word "Ghalabah" does not necessarily contain this concept; one might become victorious after some resistance is overcome. In other words, a "Qahir" (Overpowering) person is one who has such dominance and superiority over the opponent that he leaves no room for resistance, much like a vessel of water poured onto a small flame that extinguishes it instantly.

Some commentators believe that "Qahiriyyah" is usually used where the opponent is a rational being, while "Ghalabah" is more general and includes victories over non-rational beings as well. [^1] Therefore, if the previous verse pointed to the universality of Allah's power against fabricated deities and possessors of power, it does not mean He is forced to grapple with other powers for a time to bring them to their knees; rather, His power is an overpowering power (Qahirah), and the expression "above His servants" (فوق عباده) is to complete this very meaning.

With this being the case, how is it possible for a conscious human being to leave Him and follow beings and individuals who possess no power of their own, and whose even meager power is from Allah?

However, so that the misconception does not arise that Allah, like some possessors of power, might make the slightest misuse of His unlimited power, at the end of the verse He says: "And yet He is the All-Wise, and all His actions are based on calculation; and He is the All-Aware, and makes not the slightest mistake or error in the exercise of power" (وَ هُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْخَبِيرُ). We read regarding the state of "Pharaoh" that when threatening the Children of Israel with the killing of their children, he said: "And indeed, we are overpowering (Qahirun) over them" (وَ إِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قاهِرُونَ‌) [^1]. He took this overpowering power of his—which was in reality nothing more than a meager power—as a justification for oppression, tyranny, and disregard for the rights of others. But Allah, the All-Wise and All-Aware, with that overpowering power, is far too exalted to permit the slightest injustice or wrongdoing toward the lowliest of His servants.

It also goes without saying that the meaning of the word "above His servants" (فوق عباده) refers to superiority in rank and status, not in physical location, for it is clear that Allah has no physical place.

It is strange that some flawed minds have taken the expression of the above verse as evidence for Allah having a body, while there is no doubt that the expression "above" (fawq) is used to express the spiritual superiority of Allah in terms of power over His servants. Even in the case of Pharaoh, although he was a human being and possessed a body, this same word was used for superiority in status, not superiority in physical location (reflect on this).

[^1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 7, p. 34. [^1]: Al-A'raf, 127.

Ayahs

قُلْ أَىُّ شَىْءٍ أَكْبَرُ شَهَـٰدَةً ۖ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ ۖ شَهِيدٌۢ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ ۚ وَأُوحِىَ إِلَىَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانُ لِأُنذِرَكُم بِهِۦ وَمَنۢ بَلَغَ ۚ أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَشْهَدُونَ أَنَّ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ ءَالِهَةً أُخْرَىٰ ۚ قُل لَّآ أَشْهَدُ ۚ قُلْ إِنَّمَا هُوَ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ وَإِنَّنِى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ (١٩)
Say, ‘What thing is greatest as witness?’ Say, ‘Allah! [He is] witness between me and you, and this Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn thereby you and whomever it may reach.’ ‘Do you indeed bear witness that there are other gods besides Allah?’ Say, ‘I do not bear witness [to any such thing].’ Say, ‘Indeed He is the One God, and I indeed disown what you associate [with Him].’ (19)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَعْرِفُونَهُۥ كَمَا يَعْرِفُونَ أَبْنَآءَهُمُ ۘ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٢٠)
Those whom We have given the Book recognize him just as they recognize their sons. Those who have ruined their souls will not have faith. (20)

Commentary

The Greatest Witness

As a group of commentators have narrated, a number of the polytheists of Mecca came to the Prophet and said: "What kind of a prophet are You, for we see no one agreeing with You?

We even inquired about You from the Jews and Christians, and they too gave no testimony or witness to Your truthfulness based on the contents of the Torah and the Gospel. At least show us someone who can bear witness to Your mission." The above verses are directed at this incident.

The Prophet is commanded to say to these stubborn opponents—who had closed their eyes, ignored all these signs of the truthfulness of His call, and were still demanding a witness: "In your opinion, whose testimony is the greatest?" (قُلْ أَيُّ شَيْ‌ءٍ أَكْبَرُ شَهادَةً).

Is it not that the greatest testimony is the testimony of the Lord? "Say: Allah is Witness between me and you" (قُلِ اللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ بَيْنِي وَ بَيْنَكُمْ‌).

"And the best proof of this is that He has revealed this Quran to me" (وَ أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ‌).

A Quran that could not possibly be the product of human thought, especially in that era and time, and in that environment and place; a Quran that contains various types of miraculous evidence: its words are miraculous and its meanings even more miraculous. Is not this one great witness sufficient proof of Allah's testimony to the truthfulness of my call?

Furthermore, it is understood from this phrase that the Quran is the greatest miracle and the highest witness to the truth of the Prophet's claim.

Then, addressing the purpose of the Quran's revelation, He says: "This Quran has been revealed to me so that I may warn you and all those whom my words reach throughout human history, across the span of time, and in all parts of the world, against disobeying Allah's command, and to draw attention to the painful consequences of this opposition" (لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَ مَنْ بَلَغَ‌).

If we see that only "warning" (inzar) and instilling fear are mentioned here—whereas it is usually accompanied by glad tidings—it is because the discourse was directed at stubborn individuals who insisted on opposition.

Additionally, by mentioning the phrase "and whomsoever it reaches" (وَ مَنْ بَلَغَ), He declares the global mission of the Quran and its universal call. In fact, no expression more concise and comprehensive than this can be imagined to convey this purpose, and reflecting on its breadth can remove any ambiguity regarding the Quranic call not being restricted to the Arab race or a specific time or region. A group of scholars have also derived the issue of the Finality of the Prophet (Khatamiyyat) from expressions such as the one above, because according to this phrasing, the Prophet was sent to all those whom His words reach, and this includes everyone who steps into this world until the end of time.

From the hadiths narrated through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, it is understood that the "reaching" of the Quran does not only mean that the exact Arabic text reaches other nations; even the arrival of its translations in other languages is included in the meaning of the verse.

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, when asked about the above verse, he said: "بكل لسان" [1] Meaning: "In every language."

Furthermore, one of the established rules of the Principles of Jurisprudence (Usul al-Fiqh), which is the rule of "The Ugliness of Punishment without Clarification" (Qubh al-Iqab bila Bayan), is also derived from the above verse.

To explain: it is proven in Usul al-Fiqh that as long as a ruling does not reach someone, they bear no responsibility toward it (unless they were negligent in learning the ruling). The above verse also says that those whom my words reach are responsible for it, and thus, those to whom it has not been conveyed through no fault of their own bear no responsibility.

[1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 707, under the verse.

In Tafsir "al-Manar," it is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that a group of captives were brought to him. He asked: "Have they been invited to Islam?" They said: "No." He ordered them to be released, then recited the above verse and said: "Let them return to their place, for the truth of Islam has not reached them and they have not been invited to it." [1] It is also understood from this verse that applying the word "thing" (shay')—equivalent to the word "chiz" in Persian—to Allah is permissible, but He is a "thing" unlike other things that are created and limited; rather, He is the Creator and Unlimited.

Following this, He commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to ask them: "Do you truly bear witness that there are other gods with Allah?" (أَ إِنَّكُمْ لَتَشْهَدُونَ أَنَّ مَعَ اللَّهِ آلِهَةً أُخْرى‌). Then He says: "Tell them explicitly: I never bear such witness. Say: He is the One God, and I am indeed innocent of what you associate with Him" (قُلْ لا أَشْهَدُ، قُلْ إِنَّما هُوَ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ وَ إِنَّنِي بَرِي‌ءٌ مِمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ‌).

In fact, mentioning these few sentences at the end of the verse is for an important psychological point: it is possible that the polytheists might imagine that their discussions had perhaps created some hesitation in the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله spirit, and they might leave the gathering with hope, telling their friends that perhaps Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله might reconsider His call after this. These sentences, which are full of clarity and decisiveness, turn this hope entirely into despair and show them that the matter is far beyond what they imagine, and not the slightest hesitation will appear in His call. Experience has shown that mentioning such decisive words at the end of a discussion has a profound effect on reaching the final result!

[1]: Al-Manar, Vol. 7, p. 341.

In the next verse, He explicitly responds to those who claimed that the People of the Book give no testimony regarding the Prophet of Islam, saying: "Those to whom We gave the Scripture recognize him as they recognize their own sons!" (الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ يَعْرِفُونَهُ كَما يَعْرِفُونَ أَبْناءَهُمُ‌).

That is, they are not only aware of the origin of His appearance and call, but they also know His details, characteristics, and precise signs. Therefore, if a group of the people of Mecca said they had consulted the People of the Book and they had no information about the Prophet, they were either truly lying and had not investigated, or the People of the Book were concealing the truths and not relaying them, just as other verses of the Quran point to their concealment. (Further explanation of this subject was provided in Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 366, under verse 146 of Surah Al-Baqarah).

At the end of the verse, as a final conclusion, He declares: "Only those will not believe in this Prophet (despite all these clear signs) who have lost everything in the market of life's trade and have gambled away the capital of their own existence" (الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

Ayahs

وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٢١)
Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or denies His signs? Indeed the wrongdoers will not be felicitous. (21)
وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوٓا۟ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآؤُكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ (٢٢)
On the day when We gather them all together, We shall say to those who ascribed partners [to Allah] ‘Where are your partners that you used to claim?’ (22)
ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُن فِتْنَتُهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ وَٱللَّهِ رَبِّنَا مَا كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ (٢٣)
Then their only excuse will be to say, ‘By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists.’ (23)
ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَذَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۚ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٢٤)
Look, how they forswear themselves, and what they used to fabricate has forsaken them! (24)

Commentary

The Greatest Injustice

Following the program of comprehensively dismantling "shirk" (polytheism) and idol worship, the first verse above explicitly asks in the form of a rhetorical question: "Who is more unjust than the polytheists who have fabricated a lie against Allah by assigning partners to Him, or have rejected His signs?"

(وَ مَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِآياتِهِ‌).

In reality, the first phrase refers to the denial of Monotheism (Tawhid), and the second phrase refers to the denial of Prophethood (Nubuwwah). Truly, no injustice can be greater than a human being placing a worthless inanimate object or a weak human as an equal to the Infinite Being who governs the entire universe. This act is considered an injustice from three perspectives: injustice toward His Holy Essence by attributing a partner to Him; injustice toward oneself by lowering one's dignity and existential value to the level of worshipping a piece of stone or wood; and injustice toward society, which, due to polytheism, becomes afflicted with division, fragmentation, and distance from the spirit of unity and oneness.

Certainly, no oppressor—especially such oppressors whose injustice is all-encompassing—will ever see prosperity or salvation (إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ‌).

Of course, the word "shirk" is not explicitly mentioned in the above verse, but considering the preceding and following verses, which all revolve around the issue of polytheism, it becomes clear that the word "fabrication" (افتراء) in this verse refers to the slander of attributing partners to the Essence of God.

It is noteworthy that in 15 instances in the Holy Quran, certain individuals are introduced as the most unjust and oppressive of people, all beginning with the interrogative phrase "And who is more unjust..." (وَ مَنْ أَظْلَمُ) or "Then who is more unjust..." (فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ). Although many of these verses speak about polytheism, idol worship, and the denial of divine signs—meaning they regard the principle of Monotheism—some of them also concern other matters, such as: "And who is more unjust than he who forbids the mosques of Allah from having His name mentioned in them?" (Al-Baqarah: 114).

And in another instance, we read: "And who is more unjust than he who conceals a testimony he has from Allah?" (Al-Baqarah: 140). Here, a question arises: How is it possible for each of these groups to be the "most unjust" of people, when "most unjust" (superlative) should logically apply to only one group?

Answer

It can be said that all these matters actually stem from a single root, which is the issue of polytheism, disbelief, and stubbornness. Forbidding people from the remembrance of God in mosques and striving to destroy them is a sign of disbelief and polytheism. Similarly, concealing testimony—which apparently refers to concealing testimony regarding truths that lead to people wandering in the wilderness of disbelief—is one of the various faces of polytheism and the denial of the One God.

The next verse discusses the fate of the polytheists on the Day of Resurrection, to make it clear that by relying on weak creatures such as idols, they secured neither peace for themselves in this world nor in the next. It says: "The day We shall gather them all together, We shall say to those who associated partners [with Allah]: 'Where are your partners whom you used to claim?'" And why do they not hasten to your aid? Why is there no trace of their display of power seen in this terrifying arena? (وَ يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا أَيْنَ شُرَكاؤُكُمُ الَّذِينَ كُنْتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ‌).

Was it not the plan that they would help you in your difficulties? And did you not take refuge in them with this hope? Then why is not the slightest trace of them seen?

They will fall into a strange state of bewilderment, astonishment, and terror, and they will have no answer to this question except to swear an oath: "By Allah, our Lord, we were never polytheists," thinking that even there they can deny the truths (ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُنْ فِتْنَتُهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا وَ اللَّهِ رَبِّنا ما كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ‌).

There is a discussion among commentators regarding the meaning of "fitnah" (فتنة) in the above verse. Some have interpreted it as an excuse or apology, some as an answer, and some as polytheism itself [1]. There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that "fitnah" and "iftinan" refer to being infatuated with something; meaning the result of their infatuation with polytheism and idol worship, which cast a veil over their thought and intellect, is that on the Day of Judgment—when the veils are removed—they realize their great error, disavow their actions, and deny them entirely.

The root of "fitnah" in the language, as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, is to cast gold into the fire and subject it to the pressure of heat so that its inner reality is revealed and it becomes known whether it is pure or impure. This meaning can be accepted as another interpretation for the above verse, because when they are submerged in the severe pressure and terrors of the Day of Resurrection, they wake up, become aware of their mistake, and deny their past actions to save themselves.

In the next verse, so that people may take a lesson from the disgraceful fate of these individuals, it says: "Look carefully and see where their situation leads, that they completely disavow their method and path and deny it, and they even lie against themselves" (انْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَذَبُوا عَلى‌ أَنْفُسِهِمْ‌).

[1]: If it means apology or answer, the verse does not require an implied word; but if it means polytheism, the word "result" must be implied, meaning "the result of their polytheism was that they swore they were not polytheists."

And all the supports they had chosen for themselves and whom they imagined to be partners of Allah will all be lost to them, and their hands will reach nothing (وَ ضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ‌).

Key Points

  1. The meaning of "Look" (انظر) is certainly to look with the eye of the intellect, not the eye of the senses, because the scenes of the Resurrection cannot be observed in this world.

  2. The statement that they lied against themselves either means that they deceived themselves in this world and deviated from the path of truth, or that in the other world, when they swear they were not polytheists, they are in fact lying against themselves because they were certainly polytheists.

  3. A question remains here: It is understood from the above verse that the polytheists deny their history of polytheism on the Day of Judgment, whereas the state of the Day of Judgment and the sensory observation of truths is such that no one would allow themselves to speak contrary to the truth—just as in broad daylight, we do not see any liar standing before the sun saying the weather is dark. Furthermore, it is understood from some other verses that on the Day of Judgment, they explicitly confess to their polytheism and do not conceal any truth: "And they will not conceal [any] statement from Allah" (An-Nisa: 42).

Two answers can be given to this question:

First, there are stages on the Day of Judgment. In the initial stages, the polytheists imagine they can escape the painful divine punishments by lying; therefore, according to their old habit, they resort to lying. But in the later stages, when they realize there is no opening for escape through this path, they confess to their deeds.

In reality, it is as if on the Day of Resurrection, the veils are gradually removed from before the human eye. In the beginning, when the polytheists have not yet carefully examined their records, they resort to lying. But in the later stages, when the veils rise further and they see everything present, they have no choice but to confess. This is just like criminals who, at the start of an interrogation, deny everything—even knowing their friends—but when the living documents and evidence of the crime are presented to them, they see the matter is so clear that there is no room for denial; thus, they confess and tell everything.

This answer has been narrated in a Hadith from Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام [1]. Another answer is that the above verse concerns those who did not actually see themselves as polytheists, such as the Christians who believe in a trinity yet consider themselves monotheists, or those who claimed monotheism but their actions smelled of polytheism because they trampled the commands of the Prophets, relied on other than God, and denied the guardianship (Wilayah) of the friends of Allah, yet considered themselves monotheists. These people will swear on the Day of Judgment that they were monotheists, but they will soon be made to understand that inwardly they were among the polytheists. This answer has also been narrated in several traditions from Ali عليه السلام and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام [2]. Both answers are acceptable.

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 708.

[2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 708.

Ayahs

وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَجَعَلْنَا عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً أَن يَفْقَهُوهُ وَفِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَقْرًا ۚ وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ كُلَّ ءَايَةٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهَا ۚ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءُوكَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَكَ يَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٢٥)
There are some of them who prick up their ears at you, but We have cast veils on their hearts lest they should understand it, and a deafness into their ears; and though they should see every sign, they will not believe in it. When they come to you, to dispute with you, the faithless say, ‘These are nothing but myths of the ancients.’ (25)
وَهُمْ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ وَيَنْـَٔوْنَ عَنْهُ ۖ وَإِن يُهْلِكُونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٢٦)
They dissuade [others] from [following] him, and [themselves] avoid him; yet they destroy no one except themselves, but they are not aware. (26)

Commentary

The Impenetrable Ones

This verse refers to the psychological state of some polytheists who show not the slightest flexibility upon hearing the truths—rather, they rise in enmity against them and use the labels of slander to keep themselves and others away from the truth. Regarding them, it says: "And among them are those who listen to you, but We have placed coverings over their hearts lest they understand it, and in their ears a heaviness [deafness]!" (وَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ وَ جَعَلْنا عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً أَنْ يَفْقَهُوهُ وَ فِي آذانِهِمْ وَقْراً) [^1]. In reality, blind ignorant prejudices, immersion in material interests, and following whims and desires have so dominated their intellect and perception that it is as if they are under a shroud or veil; they neither hear any truth nor have a correct understanding of matters.
