Perhaps we have repeatedly stated that when such matters are attributed to Allah, it is actually a reference to the law of "causality" and the property of "action." That is, persistence in deviance and insistence on stubbornness and pessimism result in shaping the human soul and spirit after their own likeness, making it like a warped and crooked mirror that shows the image of everything as warped and crooked. Experience has proven this truth: that evildoers and sinners initially feel discomfort with their actions, but gradually they become accustomed to them, and a day may come when they consider their ugly deeds as necessary and obligatory. In other words, this is one of the punishments for persistence and insistence on sin and opposition to the truth that overtakes stubborn sinners.

Therefore, it says their condition has reached a point where "even if they see every sign, they will not believe in it" (وَ إِنْ يَرَوْا كُلَّ آيَةٍ لا يُؤْمِنُوا بِها).

Even more than this, when they come to you, instead of turning their hearts' ears toward your words and at least thinking about them as a seeker—considering the possibility of finding a truth—they appear before you with a negative spirit and mind, and "they have no goal but dispute and aggression and fault-finding" (حَتَّى إِذا جاؤُكَ يُجادِلُونَكَ).

[^1]: "Akinna" is the plural of "Kinan," meaning a veil or anything that conceals, and "Waqr" means heaviness of the ear.

Upon hearing your words, which emanate from the spring of revelation and flow from your truth-speaking tongue, they resort to the blow of slander, saying: "These are nothing but the fables and fabricated stories of the ancients!" (يَقُولُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ هذا إِلَّا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ).

In the next verse, it says they are not content even with this; in addition to being lost themselves, they constantly strive to prevent truth-seeking individuals from traversing this path through various forms of poisoning the atmosphere. Thus, "they forbid [others] from approaching the Prophet" (وَ هُمْ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ).

"And they themselves keep away from him" (وَ يَنْأَوْنَ عَنْهُ) [^1], unaware that whoever contends with the truth strikes at his own roots. Ultimately, according to the fixed law of creation, the face of truth will emerge from behind the clouds and will triumph through its inherent attraction, while falsehood will perish like worthless foam upon the water. Therefore, "their efforts and activities will lead to their own defeat, and they destroy none but themselves, but they do not perceive this reality" (وَ إِنْ يُهْلِكُونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَ ما يَشْعُرُونَ).

A Great Slander Against Abu Talib, the Believer of Quraysh

From what has been said in the commentary of the above verse, it is clearly evident that this verse follows the discussions related to the stubborn polytheists and the fierce enemies of the Prophet. According to the rules of grammar, the pronoun "them" (hum) refers to those who were the subject of the verse—namely, the prejudiced disbelievers who spared no effort in harassing the Prophet and creating obstacles in the path of his mission.

However, with great regret, it is observed that some Sunni commentators, contrary to all grammatical rules, have detached the second verse from the preceding one and attributed it to "Abu Talib," the father of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام! They interpret the verse as: "There are a group who defend the Prophet of Islam but at the same time keep their distance from him!" (وَ هُمْ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ وَ يَنْأَوْنَ عَنْهُ).

They also cite other verses of the Quran as evidence for their claim, such as verse 114 of Surah At-Tawbah and verse 56 of Surah Al-Qasas, which will be addressed in their proper places.

However, all Shia scholars and some prominent Sunni figures—such as Ibn Abi al-Hadid (the commentator of Nahj al-Balagha), al-Qastallani in Irshad al-Sari, and Zayni Dahlan in his marginalia on Sirat al-Halabiyya—consider Abu Talib to be among the believers of Islam. In authentic Islamic sources, we find numerous evidences for this subject, the study of which leaves one in deep amazement as to why "Abu Talib" has been subjected to such unkindness and accusation by a certain group. How is it possible for someone who defended the Prophet of Islam with his entire being, and repeatedly placed himself and his son as a shield before the Prophet in times of danger, to be the target of such an accusation?! It is here that insightful researchers have surmised that the wave of opposition against Abu Talib is a political wave originating from the opposition of the "Accursed Tree of the Umayyads" (Shajarah al-Mal'unah) toward the status of Ali عليه السلام.

For it is not only Abu Talib who has been thus attacked because of his closeness to Ali عليه السلام; rather, we see that anyone in Islamic history who had a close connection to the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in any way, has not remained safe from these cowardly attacks. In reality, Abu Talib had no sin other than being the father of Ali ibn Abi Talib, the great leader of Islam! Here, we provide a summary of various evidences that clearly testify to the faith of Abu Talib in list form, leaving further details to the books written on this subject:

1- Before the mission of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, Abu Talib knew well that his nephew would reach the station of prophethood. Historians have written that during the journey Abu Talib took with the Quraysh caravan to Syria, he took his twelve-year-old nephew, Muhammad, with him. On this journey, in addition to the various miracles he witnessed from him, as soon as the caravan encountered a monk named "Bahira"—who had spent many years in worship in a monastery and was knowledgeable about the scriptures of the Old and New Testaments, and whom merchant caravans would visit on their route—Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, who was no more than twelve years old at the time, caught the monk's attention.

After staring for a while with deep and meaningful looks, Bahira asked, "To which of you does this child belong?" The group pointed to Abu Talib, who stated, "He is my nephew."

Bahira said, "This child has a brilliant future; he is the same Prophet whose mission and prophethood the heavenly books have foretold, and I have read all his characteristics in the books!" [^1] Before this encounter and others, Abu Talib had realized the prophethood of the Noble Prophet and his spiritual status through other indications as well.

According to the report of the Sunni scholar al-Shahrastani (author of Al-Milal wa al-Nihal) and others, in one of the years, the sky of Mecca withheld its blessings from its people, and a severe drought afflicted the people. Abu Talib ordered that his nephew Muhammad, who was then a nursing infant, be brought. After the child was given to him wrapped in swaddling clothes, he stood before the Kaaba and, with specific supplication, tossed the infant upward three times, saying each time: "O Lord! By the right of this child, send down upon us a blessed rain." It was not long before a mass of clouds appeared from the horizon and covered the sky of Mecca; rain poured down so heavily that it was feared the Masjid al-Haram would be destroyed.

Shahrastani then writes that this very incident, which indicates Abu Talib's awareness of his nephew's mission and prophethood from early childhood, proves his faith in the Prophet. Abu Talib later composed the following verses on this occasion: And a fair-faced one, for whose sake the clouds are asked for rain; the refuge of orphans and the protector of widows. The perishing ones from the clan of Hashim seek refuge in him, and through him, they are in bounty and excellence. A scale of justice that does not deviate by a grain, and a weigher of truth whose weighing is not feared for error.

The incident of the Quraysh turning to Abu Talib during the drought and Abu Talib swearing to Allah by his [the Prophet's] right has been narrated by many great historians besides al-Shahrastani. Allamah Amini in Al-Ghadir has cited it from the books Sharh al-Bukhari, Al-Mawahib al-Ladunniyyah, Al-Khasa'is al-Kubra, Sharh Bahjat al-Mahafil, Sirat al-Halabiyya, Sirat al-Nabawiyya, and Talabat al-Talib. [^1]

[^1]: Summarized from Sirat Ibn Hisham, Vol. 1, p. 191; Sirat al-Halabiyya, Vol. 1, p. 131, and other books. [^1]: Al-Ghadir, Vol. 7, p. 346.

2- Furthermore, in famous Islamic books, poems by Abu Talib are available to us, collected in a volume called Diwan Abu Talib. We mention some of them below: "By Allah, they shall never reach you with their collective force, until I am laid to rest in the earth." "So proclaim your matter, for there is no shame upon you; rejoice in that and let your eyes be comforted!" "You invited me and I knew you were my sincere advisor; you indeed invited and you were then trustworthy." "And I have certainly known that the religion of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله is among the best of the religions of mankind!" [^1] He also said: "Did you not know that we found Muhammad to be a Messenger like Moses, written of in the first of the Books?" "And that there is love for him among the servants [of Allah]; and there is no injustice in one whom Allah has singled out for love." [^2]

[^1] and [^2]: Khizanat al-Adab, Tarikh Ibn Kathir, Sharh Ibn Abi al-Hadid, Fath al-Bari, Bulugh al-Arab, Tarikh Abi al-Fida, Sirat al-Nabawiyya, etc., as cited in Al-Ghadir, Vol. 8.

Ibn Abi al-Hadid, after quoting a large portion of Abu Talib's poems (which Ibn Shahr Ashub in Mutashabih al-Quran estimates at three thousand verses), says: "From studying the collection of these poems, no doubt remains for us that Abu Talib believed in the school of his nephew."

3- Hadiths have also been narrated from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that clarify his testimony to the faith of his self-sacrificing uncle, Abu Talib. Among them, according to the author of Abu Talib, the Believer of Quraysh: When Abu Talib passed away, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, after his funeral procession and while mourning the loss of his uncle, said: "O my father! O Abu Talib! How grieved I am for you! How can I be consoled for you, O you who raised me when I was small, and answered me when I was grown, and I was to you like the eye in its socket and the soul in the body." [^1] He also constantly stated: "The Quraysh could never inflict anything I disliked upon me until Abu Talib died." [^2]

4- On the other hand, it is certain that years before the death of Abu Talib, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was commanded not to have any friendly relations with polytheists. Despite this, all this expression of affection and love for Abu Talib shows that the Prophet considered him a believer in the school of monotheism; otherwise, how would it be possible for him to forbid others from befriending polytheists while he himself loved Abu Talib to the point of devotion?!

5- In the hadiths reached through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, there are also numerous documents regarding the faith and sincerity of Abu Talib, the narration of which would be lengthy here. These hadiths are mixed with logical and rational arguments, such as the narration from the fourth Imam عليه السلام. After stating in response to a question that Abu Talib was a believer, he said: "Indeed, there are people here who claim he was a disbeliever." Then he said: "How very strange! Do they cast aspersions on Abu Talib or on the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, while Allah forbade him from letting a believing woman remain with a disbeliever in more than one verse of the Quran? And no one doubts that Fatima bint Asad (may Allah be pleased with her) was among the foremost believing women, and she remained under [the marriage of] Abu Talib until Abu Talib (may Allah be pleased with him) died." [^1]

6- Beyond all this, if we doubt everything, no one can doubt the fact that Abu Talib was among the primary supporters of Islam and the Prophet. His support for the Prophet and Islam was to such an extent that it can never be explained by mere kinship ties or tribal prejudices. A living example is the story of the Shi'b Abi Talib (the Valley of Abu Talib). All historians have written that when the Quraysh placed the Prophet and the Muslims under a severe economic, social, and political siege and cut off their relations with them, Abu Talib, as the sole supporter and defender of the Prophet, abandoned all his affairs for three years and took the Banu Hashim to a valley located between the mountains of Mecca, known as the Shi'b Abi Talib, and settled there. He reached such a level of sacrifice that, in addition to building special towers to prevent attacks from the Quraysh, every night he would wake the Prophet from his sleeping place and provide another spot for him to rest, and he would have his beloved son Ali عليه السلام sleep in his place. When Ali عليه السلام said, "O father, I will eventually be killed in this situation," he replied, "My dear, do not lose patience; every living being is headed toward death. I have sacrificed you for the son of Abdullah." Interestingly, Ali عليه السلام said in response to his father, "O father, my words were not because I fear being killed in the way of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, but because I wanted you to know how obedient I am to you and ready to aid Ahmad." [^1]

We believe that anyone who sets aside prejudice and impartially studies the golden lines of history regarding Abu Talib will join Ibn Abi al-Hadid, the commentator of Nahj al-Balagha, in saying: If it were not for Abu Talib and his son, the religion would never have stood as a person and risen. The former sheltered and protected in Mecca, and the latter in Yathrib plunged into the depths of death! [^2]

[^1]: Shaykh al-Abtah, as cited in Abu Talib, the Believer of Quraysh. [^2]: Al-Tabari, as cited in Abu Talib, the Believer of Quraysh. [^1]: Kitab al-Hujjah; Darajat al-Rafia, as cited in Al-Ghadir, Vol. 8. [^1]: Al-Ghadir, Vol. 8. [^2]: Al-Ghadir, Vol. 8.

Ayahs

وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذْ وُقِفُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ فَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰلَيْتَنَا نُرَدُّ وَلَا نُكَذِّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّنَا وَنَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٢٧)
Were you to see when they are brought to a halt by the Fire, whereupon they will say, ‘If only we were sent back [into the world]! Then we will not deny the signs of our Lord, and we will be among the faithful!’ (27)
بَلْ بَدَا لَهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يُخْفُونَ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَلَوْ رُدُّوا۟ لَعَادُوا۟ لِمَا نُهُوا۟ عَنْهُ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (٢٨)
Indeed, what they used to hide before has now become evident to them. But were they to be sent back they would revert to what they were forbidden, and they are indeed liars. (28)

Commentary

A Fleeting and Ineffective Awakening!

In the previous two verses, a portion of the stubborn actions of the polytheists was mentioned, and in these two verses, a scene of the consequences of their actions is depicted so that they may know what a grim fate awaits them and awaken—or at least so that their situation may serve as a lesson for others! First, It says: "If you could but see when they are made to stand before the Fire of Hell, you would testify to the painful end they have met" (وَ لَوْ تَرى‌ إِذْ وُقِفُوا عَلَى النَّارِ ... [1]).

[1]: Therefore, "لو" (if) is used as a conditional particle, and its result (apodosis) has been omitted due to its clarity.

In that state, they become so overwhelmed that "they cry out: 'Oh, would that we were sent back [to the world] to escape this grim fate and compensate for our past evil deeds; then we would not deny the signs of our Lord and we would be among the believers!'" (فَقالُوا يا لَيْتَنا نُرَدُّ وَ لا نُكَذِّبَ بِآياتِ رَبِّنا وَ نَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌) [1].

In the next verse, It adds that this is nothing more than a false wish; rather, it is because in that world, "all that they used to conceal of their beliefs, intentions, and evil deeds has now become manifest to them," and they have momentarily awakened (بَلْ بَدا لَهُمْ ما كانُوا يُخْفُونَ مِنْ قَبْلُ‌).

However, this awakening is not a lasting or stable one; it has arisen only due to specific circumstances and conditions. Therefore, "even if—hypothetically—they were sent back to this world, they would surely return to the very things they were forbidden from" (وَ لَوْ رُدُّوا لَعادُوا لِما نُهُوا عَنْهُ‌).

Consequently, "they are not sincere in their wish and claim, and they are indeed liars" (و انهم لكاذبون).

[1]: An important point to note in this verse is that according to the famous recitation we have, "نرد" is read with raf’ (nominative), while "لا نكذب" and "نكون" are read with nasb (accusative), even though they appear to be conjoined to one another and should be identical. The best explanation is that "نرد" is part of the wish (tamanni), and "لا نكذب" is actually its response. The "و" (and) here functions like "فاء" (so/then), and we know that the response to a wish, when it follows "فاء", takes the nasb case. Commentators such as Fakhr al-Razi, the late Tabarsi, and Abu al-Futuh al-Razi have mentioned other possibilities in this regard. However, what was stated above is the clearest. Thus, the verse is similar to verse 58 of Surah Az-Zumar: "لَوْ أَنَّ لِي كَرَّةً فَأَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌" (If only I had another chance, then I could be among the doers of good).

Key Points

1- First, from the literal meaning of the phrase "بَدا لَهُمْ‌" (it became manifest to them), it is inferred that they were concealing a series of truths not only from the people but even from themselves, which are revealed to them in the arena of Resurrection. It is not surprising for a human to hide a truth even from himself and place a lid over his conscience and innate nature (fitrah) in order to achieve a false sense of peace.

The issue of deceiving the conscience and hiding truths from oneself is a notable subject that has been carefully examined in discussions regarding the activity of the conscience. For example, we see many pleasure-seekers who have become aware of the severe harm of their lustful actions, yet to continue their deeds with a relaxed mind, they try to somehow suppress this awareness within themselves.

However, many commentators, without noting the expression "لهم" (to them), have interpreted the verse in a way that applies to actions they were hiding from the people. (Reflect on this).

2- It might be said that a wish is not something that can be characterized by truth or falsehood, as it is technically a "compositional" (insha’i) statement, and truth or falsehood do not apply to insha’. However, this is not correct, because many compositional statements carry an underlying "informative" (khabari) concept where truth and falsehood can enter. For example, someone might say:

"I wish Allah would give me great wealth so I could help you." This is indeed a wish, but its underlying meaning is: "If Allah gives me such wealth, I will help you." This is an informative concept that could be a lie. Therefore, the listener, who is aware of the speaker's stinginess and narrow-mindedness, says: "You are lying; even if He gave it to you, you would never do such a thing." (This theme is seen in many compositional sentences).

3- The fact that we read in the verse that if they were to return to the world, they would repeat the same actions, is because many people, when they see the results of their deeds with their own eyes—that is, when they reach the stage of "witnessing" (shuhud)—become temporarily upset and regretful, wishing they could somehow compensate for their actions. However, these regrets, which pertain to that state of witnessing the result of the action, are unstable regrets that occur to everyone when faced with tangible punishment. But once the tangible observation is removed, this effect also vanishes, and the previous state is repeated.

This is like the idolaters who, when caught in severe storms at sea and standing on the brink of death and destruction, would forget everything except Allah. But as soon as the storm subsided and they reached the safety of the shore, everything returned to how it was before! [1]

4- It should be noted that the above states are specific to a group of idolaters mentioned in the preceding verses, not all of them. Therefore, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was commanded to admonish others and to awaken and guide them.

[1]: Surah Yunus, Verse 22.

Ayahs

وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا ٱلدُّنْيَا وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَبْعُوثِينَ (٢٩)
They say, ‘There is nothing but our life of this world, and we shall not be resurrected.’ (29)
وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذْ وُقِفُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ ۚ قَالَ أَلَيْسَ هَـٰذَا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّنَا ۚ قَالَ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (٣٠)
Were you to see when they are brought to a halt before their Lord. He will say, ‘Is this not a fact?’ They will say, ‘Yes, by our Lord!’ He will say, ‘So taste the punishment because of what you used to deny.’ (30)
قَدْ خَسِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِلِقَآءِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً قَالُوا۟ يَـٰحَسْرَتَنَا عَلَىٰ مَا فَرَّطْنَا فِيهَا وَهُمْ يَحْمِلُونَ أَوْزَارَهُمْ عَلَىٰ ظُهُورِهِمْ ۚ أَلَا سَآءَ مَا يَزِرُونَ (٣١)
They are certainly losers who deny the encounter with Allah. When the Hour overtakes them suddenly, they will say, ‘Alas for us, for what we neglected in it!’ And they will bear their burdens on their backs. Look! Evil is what they bear! (31)
وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ ۖ وَلَلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ ۗ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٣٢)
The life of the world is nothing but play and diversion, and the abode of the Hereafter is surely better for those who are Godwary. Do you not exercise your reason? (32)

Commentary

Regarding the commentary on the first verse, two possibilities exist. One is that it is a continuation of the speech of the stubborn and harsh polytheists who, upon witnessing the scenes of the Resurrection, wish to return to this world once more to make amends. However, the Quran states that if they were to return, not only would they not think of making amends and would continue their previous ways, but they would fundamentally deny the Resurrection and Judgment Day as well, saying with utmost amazement:

"Life is nothing but this worldly life of ours, and we shall never be raised again!" (وَ قالُوا إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا حَياتُنَا الدُّنْيا وَ ما نَحْنُ بِمَبْعُوثِينَ‌) [1] The other possibility is that the verse recounts a separate discussion regarding a group of polytheists who completely denied the Hereafter; for among the Arab polytheists, there was a group who had no belief in the Resurrection, while others believed in some form of it.

In the following verse, the Quran points to their fate on the Day of Resurrection, saying: "If you could but see when they are made to stand before their Lord and it is said to them: 'Is this not the truth?'" (وَ لَوْ تَرى‌ إِذْ وُقِفُوا عَلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ قالَ أَ لَيْسَ هذا بِالْحَقِ‌).

"They will say in response: 'Yes, by our Lord, it is the truth!'" (قالُوا بَلى‌ وَ رَبِّنا).

[^1]: According to this possibility, "وَ قالُوا" (and they said) is conjoined to "عادوا" (they returned), and this possibility was chosen by the author of Tafsir al-Manar.

Once again, "it will be said to them: 'Then taste the punishment for what you used to deny and disbelieve!'" (قالَ فَذُوقُوا الْعَذابَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ‌).

Certainly, the meaning of "standing before the Lord" is not that Allah occupies a physical place; rather, it means standing before the scenes of His punishment—as some commentators have stated—or it is a metaphor for being present in the Divine Court, just as a person says during prayer, "I am standing before Allah."

In the next verse, referring to the loss and detriment of those who deny the Hereafter and Resurrection, He says: "Those who denied the meeting with Allah have certainly suffered loss" (قَدْ خَسِرَ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِلِقاءِ اللَّهِ‌).

The meaning of "meeting the Lord," as previously mentioned, is either a spiritual meeting and intuitive faith (inner witnessing) or encountering the scenes of the Resurrection and His rewards and punishments.

Then it states that this denial will not continue forever, but will last until "suddenly the Hour (Resurrection) comes upon them, and they find themselves before these terrifying scenes and see the results of their deeds with their own eyes. At this moment, their cry will rise: 'Alas for us! How we neglected this day!'" (حَتَّى إِذا جاءَتْهُمُ السَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً قالُوا يا حَسْرَتَنا عَلى‌ ما فَرَّطْنا فِيها).

The meaning of "the Hour" (al-Sa'ah) is the Day of Judgment, and "suddenly" (baghtatan) means it occurs in an instantaneous and leaping manner such that no one but Allah knows its timing. The reason for choosing this name (al-Sa'ah) for the Day of Judgment is either because the accounting of people's deeds is performed in that hour with speed, or it refers to the suddenness of its occurrence, where in a lightning-fast hour, people are transferred from the world of Barzakh to the realm of Resurrection.

Hasrat means regret over something, but when an Arab is deeply affected, he addresses "Regret" itself and says: "O our regret!" (Ya Hasratana): as if the intensity of the regret is such that it has manifested as a being standing before him! Then the Quran says: "And they shall bear their burdens on their backs" (وَ هُمْ يَحْمِلُونَ أَوْزارَهُمْ عَلى‌ ظُهُورِهِمْ‌).

"Awzar" is the plural of "wizr," meaning a heavy load, and here it refers to sins. This verse can be one of the proofs for the "embodiment of deeds" (tajassum al-a'mal), as it says they carry their sins on their backs.

It can also be a metaphor for the weight of responsibility, as responsibilities are always likened to a heavy burden.

And at the end of the verse, He says: "Behold, evil is that which they bear!" (أَلا ساءَ ما يَزِرُونَ‌). In the above verse, mention is made of the loss and detriment of the deniers of the Hereafter; the reason for this is clear, because faith in the Hereafter, in addition to preparing man for an eternal blissful life and inviting him to acquire scientific and practical perfections, has a profound effect on controlling man against pollution and sins. In the discussions related to the Hereafter, we will, God willing, explain its constructive effect from an individual and social perspective.

Then, to describe the status of the worldly life in comparison to the life of the Hereafter, He says: "The life of this world is nothing but play and amusement" (وَ مَا الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَعِبٌ وَ لَهْوٌ).

Therefore, those who are only attached to the world and seek nothing but it are, in reality, capricious children who have spent a lifetime in play and amusement and have remained oblivious to everything else!

The comparison of worldly life to play and amusement is because plays and amusements are usually hollow and baseless activities that are far from the essence of true life. Neither those who win in the game have truly succeeded, nor those who lose have truly failed, because after the game ends, everything returns to its place! It is often seen that children sit together and start a game; one becomes the "Emir," another the "Minister," one the "Thief," and another the "Caravan." But an hour does not pass before there is no trace of the Emir, nor the Minister, nor the Thief, nor the Caravan. Or in plays performed for entertainment, scenes of war, love, or enmity are manifested, but after an hour, there is no trace of any of them.

The world is like a play whose actors are the people of this world, and sometimes this childish game occupies even our wise and sensible ones; but how quickly the end of this amusement and performance is announced.

"La'ib" (play) is originally derived from "lu'ab" (saliva), meaning the spit that flows from the lips. The reason play is called "la'ib" is that it is like the flowing of saliva from the mouth, which is done without a purpose.

Then, He compares it with the life of the other abode, saying: "But the home of the Hereafter is better for those who are righteous. Do you not then reason?" (وَ لَلدَّارُ الْآخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ أَ فَلا تَعْقِلُونَ‌).

For it is an eternal and imperishable life in a much vaster world and at a much higher level; in a realm that deals with truth, not metaphor, and with reality, not imagination; in a world where its blessings are not mixed with pain and suffering, and it is entirely pure blessing without pain or hardship.

Since understanding these realities—given the deceptive manifestations of the world—is not possible for those who do not reflect, the address at the end of the verse is directed toward such individuals.

In a tradition from Hisham ibn al-Hakam, it is narrated from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام that he said: "O Hisham, Allah has exhorted the people of intellect and made them interested in the Hereafter, saying: 'The life of this world is nothing but play and amusement, and the home of the Hereafter is better for those who are righteous. Will you not then use your intellect?'" [1] It perhaps needs no reminder that the goal of these verses is to combat dependency and attachment to the manifestations of the material world and forgetting its ultimate destination; otherwise, those who have used the world as a means for happiness are, in truth, seekers of the Hereafter, not the world.

Ayahs

قَدْ نَعْلَمُ إِنَّهُۥ لَيَحْزُنُكَ ٱلَّذِى يَقُولُونَ ۖ فَإِنَّهُمْ لَا يُكَذِّبُونَكَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ (٣٣)
We certainly know that what they say grieves you. Yet it is not you that they deny, but it is Allah’s signs that the wrongdoers impugn. (33)
وَلَقَدْ كُذِّبَتْ رُسُلٌ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَصَبَرُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا كُذِّبُوا۟ وَأُوذُوا۟ حَتَّىٰٓ أَتَىٰهُمْ نَصْرُنَا ۚ وَلَا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِمَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَكَ مِن نَّبَإِى۟ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٣٤)
Apostles were certainly denied before you, yet they patiently bore being denied and tormented until Our help came to them. Nothing can change the words of Allah, and there have certainly come to you some of the accounts of the apostles. (34)

Commentary

There Have Always Been Challenges in the Path of Reformers

There is no doubt that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), in his logical dialogues and intellectual struggles with the stubborn and persistent polytheists, would sometimes become grieved and sorrowful—at times due to the intensity of their obstinacy and the lack of impact his words had on their souls, and at other times due to the unjust accusations they leveled against him. In the Holy Quran, Allah repeatedly comforted His Prophet in such instances so that he might pursue his mission with greater heart and steadfastness. Among these instances is the first verse mentioned above, which states: "We know indeed that what they say grieves you" (قَدْ نَعْلَمُ إِنَّهُ لَيَحْزُنُكَ الَّذِي يَقُولُونَ).

But know that they do not deny you; rather, they are in truth denying Our signs. Therefore, their opponent is actually Us, not you (فَإِنَّهُمْ لا يُكَذِّبُونَكَ وَ لكِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ بِآياتِ اللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ).

A similar expression is found in common human discourse, where a "superior" might say to his representative when the latter is upset: "Do not be grieved; their opposition is actually toward me, and if a problem is created, it is for me, not for you." In this way, he provides comfort to his heart.

Commentators have suggested other possibilities in the interpretation of the above verse, but the apparent meaning of the verse is what we have stated above.

There is also another noteworthy possibility: the verse means that "your opponents actually believe in your truthfulness and sincerity and have no doubt about the authenticity of your call, even if the fear of their interests being endangered prevents them from submitting to the truth, or if prejudice and stubbornness do not allow them to accept it."

Islamic history also indicates that even the staunch opponents of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) inwardly believed in the truthfulness of his call. For instance, it is narrated that one day Abu Jahl met the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and shook hands with him. Someone objected to him, saying, "Why do you shake hands with this man?" He replied, "By Allah, I know that he is a Prophet, but we were once followers of 'Abd Manaf!" (meaning that accepting his call would mean becoming followers of their tribe). It is also narrated that one night Abu Jahl, Abu Sufyan, and Akhnas ibn Shariq—who were among the most stubborn leaders of the polytheists—each went secretly, without anyone noticing (and even without the three of them knowing about each other), to listen to the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) words. They hid in a corner and listened to the recitation of the Quranic verses until morning. When dawn broke, they dispersed, but they met each other on the way back. Each explained his excuse to the others, and then they pledged not to repeat this act, for if the youth of the Quraysh were to find out, it would cause them to lean toward Muhammad.

The next night, each thinking that his companions would not come that night, came near the Prophet's house or the gathering place of the Muslims to listen to the verses of the Quran. However, in the morning, their secret was again revealed to one another, and they began to reproach each other, once again making a covenant that this would be the last time.

But as it happened, this occurred again on the third night. In the morning, Akhnas ibn Shariq took his staff and went to Abu Sufyan, saying to him: "Tell me frankly, what is your opinion about the words you heard from Muhammad?" He replied, "By Allah, I heard things that I understood well and whose meaning and content I comprehend, but I also heard verses whose meaning and purpose I did not understand."

Akhnas said, "By Allah, I feel the same way." Then he stood up and went to Abu Jahl and asked him the same question: "What is your opinion about the words you heard from Muhammad?" He replied, "What do you want me to hear? The truth is that we and the sons of 'Abd Manaf are in competition for leadership. They fed the people, so we fed them so as not to fall behind; they provided mounts, so we provided mounts; they gave other gifts, and so did we. Thus, we were moving neck and neck. Now they say, 'We have a Prophet to whom heavenly revelation descends.' How can we compete with them in this matter?! By Allah, we will never believe in him and we will never testify to his truth (والله لا نؤمن به ابدا و لا نصدقه)." Akhnas then stood up and left his assembly.

In another narration, we read that one day Akhnas ibn Shariq encountered Abu Jahl when no one else was there. He said to him, "Tell me the truth, is Muhammad truthful or a liar? There is no one from the Quraysh here except you and me to hear our words."

Abu Jahl said, "Woe to you! By Allah, in my opinion, he is truthful and has never told a lie. But if the family of Muhammad were to seize all the positions—the banner of Hajj, the providing of water to pilgrims, the custodianship of the Kaaba, and the station of Prophethood—what would remain for the rest of the Quraysh?!" [^1] From these narrations and similar ones, it is understood that many of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) staunch enemies inwardly confessed to the truth of his words, but tribal rivalries and the like did not allow them, or they lacked the courage, to officially believe.

Of course, we also know that this kind of internal belief, as long as it is not mixed with the spirit of submission, will not have the slightest effect and does not place a person among the ranks of true believers.

[^1]: The above narrations are adapted from Tafsir al-Manar and Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan under the commentary of this verse.

In the next verse, to complete this consolation, Allah refers to the situation of previous prophets, saying: This matter is not unique to you; "Messengers before you were also denied" (وَ لَقَدْ كُذِّبَتْ رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ). "But they were patient with being denied and persecuted until Our help came to them, and they were ultimately victorious" (فَصَبَرُوا عَلى‌ ما كُذِّبُوا وَ أُوذُوا حَتَّى أَتاهُمْ نَصْرُنا).

"And this is a divine law (Sunnah) that nothing can change" (وَ لا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِماتِ اللَّهِ).

Therefore, do not lose the spirit of patience and steadfastness in the face of the denials, persecutions, and attacks of stubborn and persistent enemies. Know that according to this same law, divine assistance and the boundless grace of the Lord will come to you, and you will eventually triumph over all of them. "And the news that has reached you of the previous messengers—how they stood firm against opposition and hardships and were victorious—is a clear proof for you" (وَ لَقَدْ جاءَكَ مِنْ نَبَإِ الْمُرْسَلِينَ).

In fact, the above verse points to a general principle: that the righteous leaders of society, who rose to guide the masses by presenting a school of thought and constructive plans against the degraded ideas, superstitions, and wrong traditions of society, were always met with the fierce opposition of a group of profiteers and bullies whose interests were endangered by the flourishing of the new school.

They spared no effort to advance their sinister goals and used every weapon: the weapon of denial, slander, social boycott, harassment and torture, murder, plunder, and any other means. However, the truth, with its inherent attraction, pull, and depth, will eventually—according to a divine law—do its work, and these thorns in the path will be cleared away. But there is no doubt that the essential condition for this victory is patience, endurance, and steadfastness.

It is noteworthy that in the above verse, the divine laws (Sunan) are referred to as "the words of Allah" (كلمات الله). This is because "Kalm" and "Kalam" originally mean an effect perceived by the eye or ear ("Kalm" meaning objective effects, and "Kalam" meaning effects perceived by the ear). Later, the meaning expanded, and in addition to words, "Kalimah" is also applied to meanings, and even to "belief," "school of thought," and "method and tradition."

Ayahs

وَإِن كَانَ كَبُرَ عَلَيْكَ إِعْرَاضُهُمْ فَإِنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتَ أَن تَبْتَغِىَ نَفَقًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَوْ سُلَّمًا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَتَأْتِيَهُم بِـَٔايَةٍ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَمَعَهُمْ عَلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ ۚ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (٣٥)
And should their aversion be hard on you, find, if you can, a tunnel into the ground, or a ladder into sky, that you may bring them a sign. Had Allah wished, He would have brought them together on guidance. So do not be one of the ignorant. (35)
۞ إِنَّمَا يَسْتَجِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْمَعُونَ ۘ وَٱلْمَوْتَىٰ يَبْعَثُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُونَ (٣٦)
Only those who listen will respond [to you]. As for the dead, Allah will resurrect them, then they will be brought back to Him. (36)

Commentary

The Living Dead

These two verses are a continuation of the comfort and consolation offered to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) that appeared in the preceding verses.

Since the mind and soul of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) were deeply distressed and troubled by the misguidance and stubbornness of the polytheists, and he desired to use every possible means to draw them into the ranks of the believers, Allah says: "If their aversion and turning away is hard and heavy upon you, then if you are able to seek a tunnel into the earth and bore through it, or set up a ladder to the sky and search the horizons of the heavens to bring them another sign or miracle, then do so" (but know that they are so stubborn that they still will not believe) (وَ إِنْ كانَ كَبُرَ عَلَيْكَ إِعْراضُهُمْ فَإِنِ اسْتَطَعْتَ أَنْ تَبْتَغِيَ نَفَقاً فِي الْأَرْضِ أَوْ سُلَّماً فِي السَّماءِ فَتَأْتِيَهُمْ بِآيَةٍ) [1]. The word "Nafaq" (نفق) originally means a "hole" or "underground passage," and if a hypocrite is called a "Munafiq," it is appropriately because, in addition to his outward path, he has a hidden path for himself. "Sullam" (سلم) means a ladder.

With this sentence, Allah makes His Prophet understand that there is no deficiency in your teachings, your call, or your efforts; rather, the deficiency is on their part. They have resolved not to accept the truth. Therefore, no amount of effort will be effective; do not be distressed.

However, so that no one imagines that Allah is unable to compel them to submit, He immediately says: "If Allah willed, He could have gathered them all upon guidance—meaning He could have forced them to submit to your call and to confess the truth and believe" (وَ لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ لَجَمَعَهُمْ عَلَى الْهُدى‌).

But it is clear that such a forced faith is futile. The creation of man is for perfection based on choice and free will. It is only in the presence of free will that the value of the "believer" is distinguished from the "disbeliever," the "virtuous" from the "wicked," the "trustworthy" from the "treacherous," and the "truthful" from the "liars." Otherwise, in compulsory faith and piety, there would be no difference between good and evil, and these concepts would entirely lose their value in the context of compulsion.

Then He says: "We said these things so that you may not be among the ignorant" (فَلا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْجاهِلِينَ‌). This means:

Do not be restless, do not lose your patience and steadfastness, and do not distress yourself excessively over their disbelief and polytheism, and know that the path you are traversing is the correct one.

There is no doubt that the Prophet was aware of these truths, but Allah recounts them as a reminder and consolation for His Prophet. It is exactly like when we say to someone who has lost a child:

"Do not grieve; the world is a place of transience, everyone will depart from this world, and furthermore, you are still young and will have other children; therefore, do not be so restless."

Certainly, the transience of the world or the youth of the person is not something hidden from them; it is said only as a reminder.

Despite the fact that the above verse is one of the proofs for the negation of fatalism (Jabr), some commentators like Fakhr al-Razi have taken it as evidence for the "school of fatalism"! He relies on the phrase "And if He willed..." (و لو شاء ...) and says: From this verse, it is known that Allah does not want the disbelievers to believe! He is oblivious to the fact that "will and volition" in the above verse refers to a compulsory will; that is, Allah does not want people to believe by force and compulsion, but rather He wants them to believe by their own will and desire. Therefore, the verse is a clear witness against the belief of the fatalists.

In the next verse, to complete this subject and provide further comfort to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), He says: "Only those who have a listening ear will respond to your call and accept it" (إِنَّما يَسْتَجِيبُ الَّذِينَ يَسْمَعُونَ‌). "But as for those who are practically in the ranks of the dead, they will not believe until Allah raises them on the Day of Resurrection and they are returned to Him" (وَ الْمَوْتى‌ يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللَّهُ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُونَ‌) [2]. It is on that day that they will believe upon witnessing the scenes of the Resurrection, but their faith will be of no use, for everyone believes upon seeing that great scene—a type of forced faith.

It perhaps needs no explanation that the meaning of "the dead" (الموتى) in the above verse is not the physically dead, but the spiritually dead. For we have two types of life and death: material life and death, and spiritual life and death. Similarly, hearing and seeing are of two kinds: material and spiritual. For this reason, it often happens that regarding those who have eyes and ears, or are alive and healthy but do not perceive truths, we say:

They are blind and deaf, or they are dead altogether, because they do not show the reaction that a hearing and seeing human, or a living human, should show toward truths. In the Holy Quran, such expressions are frequently seen and possess a specific sweetness and attraction. In fact, the Quran does not give much importance to material and biological life—the signs of which are merely "eating, sleeping, and breathing"—but always emphasizes spiritual and human life, which is intertwined with duty, responsibility, feeling, pain, awakening, and awareness.

It is also necessary to mention this point: spiritual blindness, deafness, and death originate from themselves. It is they who, due to continuous sin and persistence and stubbornness in it, reach this stage. For just as if a person closes his eyes for a long time, he will gradually lose his sight and vision and perhaps one day become completely blind, those who close the eye of their soul to truths will gradually lose their power of spiritual vision!

[^1]: In reality, the phrase "If you are able..." is a conditional sentence whose result (Jaza) is omitted, and its estimation is: "If you are able... then do so, but they will not believe."

[^2]: From the perspective of syntax, "The dead" (الموتى) is the subject (Mubtada) and "Allah raises them" (يبعثهم اللَّه) is the predicate (Khabar). The fact that it says He raises the dead implies that no change will occur in their state except that they will be raised in the Resurrection and see the truths.

Ayah

وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ ۚ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَادِرٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يُنَزِّلَ ءَايَةً وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٧)
They say, ‘Why has not a sign been sent down to him from his Lord?’ Say, ‘Allah is indeed able to send down a sign,’ but most of them do not know. (37)

Commentary

In this verse, one of the pretexts of the polytheists is addressed. As mentioned in some narrations, a group of the leaders of the Quraysh, when they found themselves unable to challenge or oppose the Quran, said to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "These [verses] are of no use; if you are truthful, bring us miracles like the staff of Moses and the she-camel of Salih." Regarding this, the Quran says: "And they said: 'Why has a sign (miracle) not been sent down to him from his Lord?'" (وَ قالُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ‌).

It is clear that they did not make this proposal out of a search for truth, for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had already brought sufficient miracles for them. Even if he had nothing in his hand but the Quran with its sublime content—the same Quran that in several verses officially challenged them to a confrontation (the so-called tahaddi) and before which they remained helpless—it would have been enough to prove his prophethood. However, these fickle-minded pretext-seekers wanted, on one hand, to belittle the Quran and, on the other, to refuse the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله invitation. Therefore, they repeatedly requested new miracles. Certainly, if the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had submitted to their demands, they would have denied them all by saying, "This is manifest magic," as is understood from other verses of the Quran.

Thus, the Quran says in response to them: Tell them, "Allah is certainly able to send down a sign (the miracle you propose) to His Prophet" (قُلْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ قادِرٌ عَلى‌ أَنْ يُنَزِّلَ آيَةً).

However, there is one problem with this that most of you are unaware of: if such requests, made out of stubbornness, were granted and you then failed to believe, you would all be seized by divine punishment and destroyed. This is because it would be the ultimate disrespect toward the Holy Presence of the Lord, His Messenger, and His signs and miracles. Therefore, at the end of the verse, He says: "But most of them do not know" (وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

Objection

As inferred from the commentary Majma' al-Bayan, for centuries, some opponents of Islam have used this verse as a pretext, arguing that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله performed no miracles. They claim that when he was asked for a miracle, he contented himself only by saying that Allah has the power to do such a thing and that most of you do not know. Incidentally, some recent writers have followed this ancient myth and revived this objection in their writings.

Answer

Firstly, those who raise such an objection have seemingly not properly examined the preceding and succeeding verses, which speak of stubborn individuals who were in no way ready to submit to the truth. If the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not submit to their demands, it was for this very reason. Otherwise, where in the Quran do we find that truth-seeking individuals requested a miracle from the Prophet and he rejected their request? In verse 111 of this same Surah Al-An'am, we read regarding such people: "And even if We had sent down to them the angels and the dead had spoken to them and We had gathered together every [created] thing before them, they would not have believed."

Secondly, as understood from Islamic narrations, this proposal came from a group of Quraysh leaders who raised it to belittle and ignore the Quran. It is certain that the Prophet would not submit to proposals originating from such motives.

Thirdly, those who raise this objection have seemingly overlooked other verses of the Quran, where the Quran itself is explicitly introduced as an eternal miracle and has repeatedly challenged opponents to a confrontation, revealing their weakness and inability. They have also forgotten the first verse of Surah Al-Isra, which explicitly states that Allah took His Prophet from Al-Masjid al-Haram to Al-Masjid al-Aqsa in a single night.

Fourthly, it is unbelievable that the Quran would be full of the miracles and supernatural acts of the prophets, and the Prophet of Islam would claim, "I am the Seal of the Prophets and superior to them all, and my religion is the highest religion," yet fail to show the slightest miracle in response to the request of truth-seekers. In such a case, would not a significant point of ambiguity arise in his call in the eyes of impartial and truth-seeking individuals? If he had no miracles, he should have refrained from speaking about the miracles of other prophets altogether to justify his own program and close the paths of objection against himself. The fact that he speaks so openly and frequently about the miracles of others, recounting the supernatural acts of Moses son of Imran, Jesus son of Mary, Abraham, Salih, and Noah علیهم السلام, is a clear proof that he was completely confident regarding his own miracles.

Therefore, in Islamic histories, authentic narrations, and Nahj al-Balagha, various supernatural acts have been narrated from the Prophet, the sum of which reaches the level of tawatur (certainty through multiple chains of transmission).

Ayah

وَمَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا طَـٰٓئِرٍ يَطِيرُ بِجَنَاحَيْهِ إِلَّآ أُمَمٌ أَمْثَالُكُم ۚ مَّا فَرَّطْنَا فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يُحْشَرُونَ (٣٨)
There is no animal on land, nor a bird that flies with its wings, but they are communities like yourselves. We have not omitted anything from the Book. Then they will be mustered toward their Lord. (38)

Commentary

Since this verse entails extensive sections, we must first mention the vocabulary of the verse and then its general commentary before proceeding to other sections:

"Dabbah" (دابة) is derived from the root "dabb" (دبب), meaning "to walk slowly and take short steps." It is commonly applied to all living creatures on earth. The reason a slanderer or tale-bearer is called "daybub" (ديبوب)—and it is mentioned in the hadith: "No daybub shall enter Paradise"—is from this same perspective, as he moves slowly between two people to make them cynical toward one another.

"Ta’ir" (طائر) refers to any kind of bird. However, since this word is sometimes applied to spiritual matters that possess progress and leaps, the phrase:

yatiru bi-janahayhi (يطير بجناحيه), meaning "flies with its two wings," has been added to the verse under discussion so that the focus remains solely on "birds."

"Umam" (امم) is the plural of "Ummah," and Ummah refers to a group that shares a commonality, such as a single religion, a single language, or shared characteristics and actions.

"Yuhsharun" (يحشرون) is from the root "hashr" (حشر), meaning "gathering." However, in the Quran, it is usually used for the gathering on the Day of Resurrection, especially when accompanied by the phrase "to their Lord" (إِلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ‌).

Following the previous verses that discussed the polytheists and alerted them to the fate they await in the Hereafter, the above verse speaks of the "Hashr" (Gathering) and the universal resurrection of all living beings and all species of animals. It first states:

"There is no creature on the earth, nor a bird that flies with its two wings, but they are communities like you" (وَ ما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ لا طائِرٍ يَطِيرُ بِجَناحَيْهِ إِلَّا أُمَمٌ أَمْثالُكُمْ‌).

In this way, every species of animal and bird constitutes a community for itself, just like humans. But in what aspect does this "likeness" and similarity lie? There is discussion among commentators. Some consider their similarity to humans to be in the wondrous secrets of their creation, as both carry signs of the greatness of the Creator and Maker.

Others see it in terms of the various needs of life or the means by which they satisfy their diverse requirements.

Meanwhile, another group believes that the intended meaning is their similarity to humans in terms of perception, understanding, and consciousness. That is, they also possess knowledge, consciousness, and perception within their own world; they know Allah and glorify and sanctify Him according to their capacity, even if their thought is at a lower level than human thought and understanding. The end of this verse—as will be explained—strengthens this latter view.

Then, in the next phrase, it says: "We have neglected nothing in the Book" (ما فَرَّطْنا فِي الْكِتابِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

It is possible that "the Book" refers to the Quran, in which everything (meaning all matters related to the education, guidance, and perfection of man) exists. Sometimes it is stated in general terms, such as the call to every kind of science and knowledge, and sometimes it delves into details, such as many Islamic rulings and moral issues.

Another possibility is that "the Book" refers to the "Universe," for the world of creation is like a great book in which everything is recorded and nothing has been neglected.

There is no obstacle to both interpretations being intended by the verse, for neither has anything regarding educational matters been neglected in the Quran, nor does any deficiency or lack exist in the world of creation.

At the end of the verse, it says: "Then unto their Lord they shall be gathered" (ثُمَّ إِلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ يُحْشَرُونَ‌).

It appears that the pronoun "them" (هم) in this phrase refers to the various types and categories of creatures and birds. Thus, the Quran has also posited a resurrection for them. Most commentators have accepted this point—that all types of living beings have a resurrection, reward, and punishment. Only a few have denied this subject and interpreted this verse and other verses differently. For example, they said that "gathering unto Allah" refers to the return to the end of life and death [1]. However, as we pointed out, the apparent meaning of this expression in the Holy Quran is indeed the resurrection and being raised on the Day of Judgment.

In this regard, the verse warns the polytheists: How is it possible that the God who created all categories of animals, provided for their needs, monitors all their actions, and established a resurrection for them all, would not establish a gathering and resurrection for you? And as some polytheists claimed, there would be nothing but the life of this world and its living and dying.

[1]: This possibility was narrated by the author of Al-Manar from Ibn Abbas.

Several matters must be carefully considered here

1- Does Resurrection Exist for Animals as Well?

There is no doubt that the first condition for accounting and retribution is the matter of intellect and consciousness, followed by obligation and responsibility. Proponents of this view say there is evidence showing that animals also possess perception and understanding to their own extent, including:

The lives of many animals are intertwined with an interesting and wondrous order that illustrates their high level of understanding and consciousness. Who has not heard of ants and honeybees, their extraordinary civilization, and the amazing system of the nest and the hive, and has not praised their admirable perception and consciousness? Although some prefer to consider all of this a type of instinctive inspiration, there is no evidence that their actions are performed unconsciously (instinct without intellect).

What prevents these actions from arising from intellect and perception, as their outward appearances suggest? It often happens that animals, without prior experience, take initiative in the face of unforeseen events. For example, a sheep that has never seen a wolf in its life, upon seeing it for the first time, well recognizes the danger of this enemy and resorts to any means it can to defend itself and escape the danger.

The affection that many animals gradually develop for their owners is another witness to this subject. Many fierce and dangerous dogs behave toward their owners and even their small children like a kind servant.

Many stories of the loyalty of animals and how they repay human services are common in books and among people, and not all of them can be considered legends.

It is certain that these cannot easily be attributed to instinct, for instinct is usually the source of uniform and continuous actions. However, actions performed as a reaction under specific conditions that were not predictable are more similar to understanding and consciousness than to instinct.

Even today, many animals are trained for significant purposes. Police dogs are trained to catch criminals, pigeons to deliver letters, some animals to buy goods from shops, and hunting animals to hunt; they perform their heavy duties with amazing precision. (Today, schools have even been officially opened for some animals!)

Beyond all this, in several verses of the Quran, matters are seen that are considered significant evidence for the understanding and consciousness of some animals. The story of the ants fleeing from Solomon's (عليه السلام) army and the story of the Hoopoe coming to the region of "Sheba and Yemen" and bringing exciting news to Solomon (عليه السلام) are witnesses to this claim.

In Islamic narrations, several hadiths are also seen regarding the resurrection of animals, including: It is narrated from Abu Dharr (رضي الله عنه) who said: We were with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) when two goats butted each other in front of us. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "Do you know why they butted each other?" Those present said: "No." The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "But Allah knows why, and He will soon judge between them." [1]

[1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan and Nur al-Thaqalayn under the above verse.

In a narration from Sunni sources, it is narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) that in the commentary of this verse he said: "Indeed, Allah will gather these communities on the Day of Resurrection and will take retaliation (qisas) for some from others, until He takes retaliation for the hornless animal from the horned one [that butted it without cause]." [1] In verse 5 of Surah At-Takwir, we also read: "And when the wild beasts are gathered" (وَ إِذَا الْوُحُوشُ حُشِرَتْ‌). If we take the meaning of this verse to be the gathering in the Resurrection (not the gathering at the end of this world), it will be another textual evidence for the above discussion.

2- If They Have a Resurrection, Do They Also Have Obligations?

An important question arises here, and until it is resolved, the commentary of the above verse will not be clear: Can we accept that animals have obligations, given that one of the established conditions for obligation is intellect, and for this reason, a child or an insane person is outside the circle of obligation? Do animals possess such intellect to be subject to obligation? And can it be believed that an animal has more perception than an immature child or even the insane? If we accept that they do not have such intellect and perception, how is it possible for obligation to be directed toward them?

In response to this question, it must be said that obligation has stages, and each stage requires a corresponding level of perception and intellect. The numerous obligations that exist in Islamic laws for a human being are such that their performance is not possible without having a high level of intellect and understanding, and we can never accept such obligations for animals because the condition for it is not present in them. However, a simpler and lower stage of obligation can be imagined for which a brief understanding and consciousness are sufficient. We cannot entirely deny such understanding, consciousness, and such obligations regarding animals.

Even regarding children and the insane who understand some issues, it is difficult to deny all obligations. For example, if we consider 14-year-old adolescents who have not reached the age of maturity but have fully read and understood the matters—if they intentionally commit murder while knowing all the harms of this act, can it be said that no sin has been committed by them?! The penal laws of the world also punish non-adult individuals for certain crimes, even if their punishments are certainly lighter.

Therefore, maturity and perfect intellect are conditions for obligation in the high and complete stage. In lower stages—that is, in the case of certain sins whose vileness and ugliness are fully perceivable even for lower individuals—maturity and perfect intellect cannot be considered a condition.

Given the difference in the levels of obligation and the difference in the levels of intellect, the above problem regarding animals is also resolved.

3- Is This Verse Evidence for Reincarnation?

It is strange that some proponents of the superstitious belief in "reincarnation" have used this verse to argue for their path, saying that the verse states animals are communities like you, while we know they are not inherently like us. Therefore, we must say it is possible that the souls of humans, after separating from the body, are placed in the bodies of animals so that they may see the punishment for some of their evil deeds!

However, besides the fact that the belief in reincarnation is contrary to the law of evolution and contrary to the logic of reason, and its necessity is the denial of the Resurrection (as we have explained in detail in its place), the above verse in no way indicates this path. For as we said, animal societies are, in some respects, like human societies, and this similarity is of a functional nature, not potential. For they also have a share of perception and consciousness, a share of responsibility, and a share of resurrection, and in these respects, they bear a similarity to humans.

Let it not be misunderstood: the obligation and responsibility of various types of living beings in a specific stage does not mean that they have a leader and guide for themselves or have a Sharia and religion, as has been narrated from some Sufis. Rather, their leader in such cases is only their inner perception and consciousness; that is, they perceive certain matters and are responsible for them to the extent of their consciousness.

Ayah

وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا صُمٌّ وَبُكْمٌ فِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ ۗ مَن يَشَإِ ٱللَّهُ يُضْلِلْهُ وَمَن يَشَأْ يَجْعَلْهُ عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٣٩)
Those who deny Our signs are deaf and dumb, in a manifold darkness. Allah leads astray whomever He wishes, and whomever He wishes He puts him on a straight path. (39)

Commentary

The Deaf and the Dumb!

Once again, the Quran addresses the stubborn deniers and says: "Those who reject Our signs are deaf and dumb, in the darkness" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا صُمٌّ وَ بُكْمٌ فِي الظُّلُماتِ‌).

They possess neither hearing ears to listen to the truths, nor a truth-speaking tongue to convey a reality to others if they were to perceive it. And because the darkness of selfishness, egoism, stubbornness, and ignorance has surrounded them, they cannot see the face of the truths. In this way, they are deprived of these three great blessings (hearing, seeing, and speaking) which connect a human being with the external world.

Some commentators believe that the "deaf" refers to the blind followers who unquestioningly follow leaders of misguidance and have closed their ears so as not to hear the voice of divine leaders. The "dumb" refers to those very leaders of misguidance who perceive the truths well but have placed the seal of silence upon their lips to preserve their status and material interests. Both groups are trapped in the darkness of ignorance and egoism [^1]. Following this, He says: "Whomever Allah wills, He leaves to stray, and whomever He wills, He places him on a straight path" (مَنْ يَشَإِ اللَّهُ يُضْلِلْهُ وَ مَنْ يَشَأْ يَجْعَلْهُ عَلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ‌).

We have previously stated that attributing guidance and misguidance to the will and volition of Allah is a subject that other verses of the Quran interpret well. In one place we read: يُضِلُّ اللَّهُ الظَّالِمِينَ‌ "Allah leaves the wrongdoers to stray," and in another place: وَ ما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ‌ "He misleads thereby only the defiant disobedient," and elsewhere: وَ الَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا "And those who strive for Us—We will surely guide them to Our ways." From these and other verses of the Quran, it is clearly understood that the guidance and misguidance attributed to Allah's will in these instances are, in reality, the rewards and punishments He grants to His servants in response to their good and evil deeds. To put it more clearly, sometimes exceptionally ugly acts are committed by a person, as a result of which a terrifying darkness encompasses his soul; his truth-perceiving eyes are taken from him, his ears no longer hear the voice of truth,

[^1]: Al-Mizan, Volume 7, Page 84.

and his tongue is restrained from speaking the truth.

Conversely, sometimes so many good deeds are performed by him that a world of light and radiance is sprinkled upon his soul; his vision and perception become broader, his thought more luminous, and his tongue more eloquent in speaking the truth. This is the meaning of guidance and misguidance that is attributed to the will of Allah.

Ayahs

قُلْ أَرَءَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتَىٰكُمْ عَذَابُ ٱللَّهِ أَوْ أَتَتْكُمُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ تَدْعُونَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٤٠)
Say, ‘Tell me, should Allah’s punishment overtake you, or should the Hour overtake you, will you supplicate anyone other than Allah, should you be truthful? (40)
بَلْ إِيَّاهُ تَدْعُونَ فَيَكْشِفُ مَا تَدْعُونَ إِلَيْهِ إِن شَآءَ وَتَنسَوْنَ مَا تُشْرِكُونَ (٤١)
No, Him you will supplicate, and He will remove that for which you supplicated Him, if He wishes, and you will forget what you ascribe [to Him] as [His] partners.’ (41)

Commentary

Innate Monotheism (Tawhid al-Fitri)

Once again, the address turns toward the polytheists, presenting another argument for Monotheism and the worship of the One God. This is done by reminding them of the extraordinarily difficult and painful moments of life, appealing to their consciences: in such moments, when they consign everything else to oblivion, do they consider any refuge for themselves other than "Allah"? "O Prophet, say to them: If the painful punishment of Allah were to come upon you, or if the Resurrection were to be established with all its terror, excitement, and horrific events, tell me truly, would you call upon any other than Allah to remove your hardships?"

(قُلْ أَرَأَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتَاكُمْ عَذَابُ اللَّهِ أَوْ أَتَتْكُمُ السَّاعَةُ أَغَيْرَ اللَّهِ تَدْعُونَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صَادِقِينَ) [^1] The essence of this verse’s meaning is understandable not only for polytheists but for everyone during the onset of hardships and severe incidents. In normal circumstances and during minor events, a person might resort to others besides God; however, when an event becomes extraordinarily intense, man forgets everything. Yet, in that very state, in the depths of his heart, he feels a kind of hope for salvation that springs from a mysterious and unidentified source of power. This is the very attention to Allah and the reality of Monotheism.

Even the polytheists and idolaters do not mention their idols in such moments

[^1]: As Arabic grammarians have specified, the "ka" in "ara'aytaka" and "kum" in "ara'aytakum" is neither a noun nor a pronoun, but rather a "particle of address" (harf al-khitab) used for emphasis. In such cases, the verb always remains in the singular form, and its singular, dual, or plural nature is manifested through changes in this particle of address. Therefore, in "ara'aytakum," although the addressees are plural, the verb "ra'ayta" is brought in the singular, and its plurality is identified by "kum," the particle of address. Some believe that this phrase as a whole is equivalent in meaning to "Tell me" (akhbirni/akhbiruni), but the truth is that the phrase fully retains its interrogative meaning, and "tell me" is a necessary implication of its meaning, not the meaning itself (reflect on this).

and they consign them all to oblivion.

In the next verse, He says: "Rather, Him alone you will call, and He will remove that [distress] for which you call upon Him, if He wills, and you will forget what you used to associate [with Him]" (بَلْ إِيَّاهُ تَدْعُونَ فَيَكْشِفُ مَا تَدْعُونَ إِلَيْهِ إِنْ شَاءَ وَتَنْسَوْنَ مَا تُشْرِكُونَ).

Several points should be noted here

1- The reasoning seen in the two verses above is the argument of "Innate Monotheism" (Tawhid al-Fitri), which can be utilized in two discussions: one regarding the proof of the existence of Allah, and the other regarding the proof of His Oneness. Consequently, in Islamic narrations as well as in the discourses of scholars, this has been used as an argument against both atheists and polytheists.

2- It is noteworthy that in the above argument, the establishment of the "Hour" (the Day of Resurrection) is mentioned, while it might be said that they did not believe in such a day at all. Therefore, how is it possible to argue against them in this manner?

However, it must be noted that: Firstly, not all of them denied the Resurrection; rather, a group of them believed in some form of afterlife. Secondly, it is possible that "the Hour" refers to the hour of death or the hour of terrifying events that place a person on the brink of death. Thirdly, this expression might be an allusion to horrific events, as Quranic verses repeatedly state that the beginning of the Resurrection is accompanied by a series of extraordinarily terrifying events: earthquakes, storms, lightning bolts, and the like occur at that time.

3- While we know that the Day of Resurrection and the events preceding it are certain matters and are by no means changeable, why does the verse above say: "If Allah wills, He will remove it"? Is the intention merely to express the power of the Lord? Or was another meaning intended?

In response to this question, it must be said: The intention is not that Allah would eliminate the essence of the establishment of the Hour and the Day of Resurrection through supplication. Rather, the intention is that the polytheists—and even the non-polytheists—when they find themselves on the threshold of the Resurrection, will be terrified by its events, hardships, and the severe punishments ahead of them, and they will ask Allah to make this situation easy for them and deliver them from the dangers. In reality, the supplication is for their own salvation from painful events, not a supplication for the negation of the Resurrection itself.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمَمٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْبَأْسَآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَضَرَّعُونَ (٤٢)
We have certainly sent [apostles] to nations before you, then We seized them with stress and distress so that they might entreat [Us]. (42)
فَلَوْلَآ إِذْ جَآءَهُم بَأْسُنَا تَضَرَّعُوا۟ وَلَـٰكِن قَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٤٣)
Why did they not entreat when Our punishment overtook them! But their hearts had hardened, and Satan had made what they had been doing seem decorous to them. (43)
فَلَمَّا نَسُوا۟ مَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ أَبْوَٰبَ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فَرِحُوا۟ بِمَآ أُوتُوٓا۟ أَخَذْنَـٰهُم بَغْتَةً فَإِذَا هُم مُّبْلِسُونَ (٤٤)
So when they forgot what they had been admonished of, We opened for them the gates of all [good] things. When they became proud of what they were given, We seized them suddenly, whereat, behold, they were despondent. (44)
فَقُطِعَ دَابِرُ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ۚ وَٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٤٥)
Thus the wrongdoing lot were rooted out, and all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. (45)

Commentary

The Fate of the Unteachable

In these verses, the dialogue with the astray and the polytheists continues, and the Quran pursues the matter through another path to awaken them. That is, it takes them by the hand and leads them through past centuries and eras, explaining the conditions of the astray, oppressive, and polytheistic nations—how all means of education and awakening were employed for them, yet a group of them paid no heed to any of these. Consequently, such an ill fate overtook them that they became a lesson for future generations.

First, It says: "We sent messengers to nations before you, and when they paid no heed, We afflicted them with hardships and severe events—with poverty, drought, famine, disease, pain, suffering, ba’sa’, and darra’ [^1]—so that they might become mindful and return to Allah" (وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا إِلَىٰ أُمَمٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَأَخَذْنَاهُم بِالْبَأْسَاءِ وَالضَّرَّاءِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَضَرَّعُونَ).

In the next verse, It says: "Why did they not take heed and

[^1]: Ba’sa’ originally means severity and suffering, and is also used to mean war, as well as famine, drought, poverty, and the like. However, darra’ means spiritual or mental distress, such as grief, sorrow, and ignorance, or the distress arising from illness and the loss of status, property, and wealth. Perhaps the difference between the two is that ba’sa’ has more of an external aspect while darra’ has a spiritual aspect—meaning spiritual distress is darra’, and thus ba’sa’ would be one of the factors creating darra’ (reflect on this).

learn a lesson from these painful and awakening factors, and why did they not wake up and return to Allah?" (فَلَوْلَا إِذْ جَاءَهُم بَأْسُنَا تَضَرَّعُوا).

In reality, the cause of their failure to awaken was two things: First, "due to the abundance of sin and persistence in polytheism, their hearts had become dark and hardened, and their spirits inflexible" (وَلَٰكِن قَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ).

Second, "Satan (exploiting their whimsical desires) had made their deeds seem fair to them; thus, they perceived every ugly act they performed as beautiful and every wrong deed as correct" (وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ مَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

In the following verse, It adds that when the pressures and reprimands proved ineffective, We approached them through the path of grace and affection: "And when they forgot the first lessons, We began the second lesson for them and opened the gates of all kinds of blessings to them, so that perhaps they might wake up, pay attention to the Creator and Bestower of those blessings, and find the right path" (فَلَمَّا نَسُوا مَا ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ أَبْوَابَ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ).

However, all these blessings actually had a dual nature: they were both an expression of affection for awakening and a prelude to a painful punishment if they did not wake up. For we know that when a person is immersed in luxury and blessings, and suddenly all those blessings are taken away, it will be severely painful for him, unlike if they were taken away gradually, which would not affect him as much.

Therefore, It says: "We gave them so many blessings until they became completely jubilant, yet they did not wake up; so We seized them suddenly and punished them, and all doors of hope were closed to

them" (حَتَّىٰ إِذَا فَرِحُوا بِمَا أُوتُوا أَخَذْنَاهُم بَغْتَةً فَإِذَا هُم مُّبْلِسُونَ) [^1].

"And thus, the roots of the oppressive people were cut off, and no other generation of them arose" (فَقُطِعَ دَابِرُ الْقَوْمِ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا). Dabir originally means the tail or the end of something.

And since Allah did not fall short in any way in employing the means of education for them, He says at the end of the verse: "Praise and thanks belong to Allah, who is the Lord and Sustainer of all the worlds" (وَالْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ).

Key Points

1- Sometimes it is imagined that there is a contradiction between these verses and the preceding ones, because the previous verses explicitly stated that the polytheists turn to Allah during the onslaught of hardships and forget others besides Him, while these verses say that they do not wake up even during the onslaught of hardships.

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 5; p. 237

Attention to one point resolves this apparent contradiction: temporary and fleeting awakenings during the occurrence of hardships are not considered true awakening, because the person soon returns to their original state.

In the previous verses, since the purpose was to explain innate monotheism (tawhid al-fitri), those very fleeting awakenings and turnings—and forgetting others besides Allah even if only for the moment of the incident—were sufficient to prove it. However, in these verses, the discussion is about being guided and returning from the wrong path to the right one;

[^1]: Mublisun is originally from the root iblas, meaning the grief and sorrow that overtakes a person due to the severity of unpleasant events. The name Iblis is also derived from this. The above expression indicates the intensity of the grief and sorrow that envelops the sinners.

certainly, a fleeting and temporary awakening has no effect on that.

Sometimes it is imagined that the difference between the two is that the previous verses relate to the polytheists contemporary with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, while the verses under discussion relate to past nations; therefore, they do not contradict each other. [^1] But it seems very unlikely that the stubborn polytheists contemporary with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله were better than the previous astray ones; therefore, the solution is what we mentioned above.

2- In the above verses, we read that when the occurrence of hardships had no educational effect, Allah opens the doors of blessings to such sinners. Is this for the sake of encouragement after punishment, or is it a prelude to the painfulness of the punishment? That is, are these blessings what is called istidraj (gradual entrapment), which gradually immerses the rebellious servant in luxury, blessings, joy, and a kind of heedlessness, then suddenly takes everything away from them?
