There are some indications in the verse that strengthen the second possibility, but there is no obstacle to both being intended: first, encouragement for awakening, and if it is not effective, it becomes a prelude to the withdrawal of blessings and a painful punishment. In a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated:

"When you see Allah giving a servant what he loves of this world despite his sins, it is only istidraj (gradual entrapment)." Then the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله recited: "So when they forgot..." (فَلَمَّا نَسُوا...)

(Majma' al-Bayan and Nur al-Thaqalayn, under the verse).

In a hadith from Ali عليه السلام, it is narrated that he said:

"O son of Adam! When you see your Lord, glorified be He, bestowing His blessings upon you continuously while you are disobeying Him, then beware of Him." (Nahj al-Balagha, Saying 25).

"O son of Adam! When you see Allah bestowing blessings upon you successively

[^1]: Fakhr al-Razi has pointed out this difference in his commentary, Vol. 12, p. 224.

while you are sinning, fear His punishment, for this is a prelude to punishment."

In the book Talkhis al-Aqwal, it is narrated from Imam Hasan al-Askari عليه السلام that Qanbar, the servant of the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام, was brought before Hajjaj. Hajjaj asked, "What did you used to do for Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام?" He said, "I used to provide the water for his ablution!" He asked, "When he finished his ablution, what would he say?" He said, "He would recite this verse: 'So when they forgot that which they had been reminded of, We opened for them the gates of all things...'" and he recited until the end of the verse. Hajjaj said, "I think he was applying this verse to us." Qanbar said courageously, "Yes!" (Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 18).

3- In these verses, we read that the purpose of many painful events is to create a state of mindfulness and awakening, and this is one of the philosophies of "afflictions and calamities" which we have discussed in the topic of monotheism [^1]. However, it is noteworthy that, firstly, He mentions this subject with the word la'alla (perhaps), because the existence of calamities alone is not sufficient for awakening; rather, it is a groundwork for hearts that have readiness (we have previously stated that la'alla in the word of Allah is usually used in cases where other conditions are also involved).

Secondly, the word tadarru' is used here, which originally means the flow of milk into the udder and its submission and yielding before the milker; then it came to mean submission mixed with humility and lowliness! That is, We created these painful events so that they might descend from the mount of pride, rebellion, and selfishness, and submit to the Truth.

4- Interestingly, at the end of the verse, Allah says the phrase "Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds" (الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ), and this is a sign that cutting off the roots of oppression and corruption and the annihilation of a generation

[^1]: Refer to the book The Creator of the World and the book In Search of God.

that could continue this work is so important that it is a place for gratitude and praise.

In a hadith from Fudayl ibn Iyad from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said:

"Whoever loves the survival of the oppressors has loved that Allah be disobeyed. Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted be He, praised Himself for the destruction of the oppressors, saying: 'So the roots of the people who did wrong were cut off. And praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds.'"

"Whoever loves the survival of the oppressors, its meaning is that he loves that Allah be disobeyed (the subject of oppression is so important that) Allah, Blessed and Exalted be He, praised and lauded Himself for annihilating the oppressors and said: 'The tail of the oppressive people was cut off, and praise is specific to Allah, the Lord of the worlds.'"

Ayahs

قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ سَمْعَكُمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرَكُمْ وَخَتَمَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِكُم مَّنْ إِلَـٰهٌ غَيْرُ ٱللَّهِ يَأْتِيكُم بِهِ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ثُمَّ هُمْ يَصْدِفُونَ (٤٦)
Say, ‘Tell me, should Allah take away your hearing and your sight and set a seal on your hearts, which god other than Allah can bring it [back] to you?’ Look, how We paraphrase the signs variously; nevertheless they turn away. (46)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتَىٰكُمْ عَذَابُ ٱللَّهِ بَغْتَةً أَوْ جَهْرَةً هَلْ يُهْلَكُ إِلَّا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٤٧)
Say, ‘Tell me, should Allah’s punishment overtake you suddenly or visibly, will anyone be destroyed except the wrongdoing lot?’ (47)
وَمَا نُرْسِلُ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ ۖ فَمَنْ ءَامَنَ وَأَصْلَحَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٤٨)
We do not send the apostles except as bearers of good news and warners. As for those who are faithful and righteous, they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (48)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَمَسُّهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ (٤٩)
But as for those who deny Our signs, the punishment shall befall them because of the transgressions they used to commit. (49)

Commentary

Recognize the Bestower of Blessings

The discourse continues to address the polytheists.

In these verses, an argument is presented in another form to awaken them, relying on the instinct of "averting harm." First, it says: "If Allah were to take away His precious blessings from you, such as your hearing and your sight, and seal your hearts so that you could no longer distinguish between good and evil or truth and falsehood, who is the god besides Allah who could restore these blessings to you?!" (قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَخَذَ اللَّهُ سَمْعَكُمْ وَأَبْصَارَكُمْ وَخَتَمَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِكُمْ مَنْ إِلَٰهٌ غَيْرُ اللَّهِ يَأْتِيكُمْ بِهِ).

In reality, even the polytheists accepted that the Creator and Provider is Allah, and they worshipped idols as intercessors before Him. The Quran says: instead of worshipping worthless idols that possess nothing, why do you not go directly to the door of Allah—the God who is the source of all goodness and blessings?

In addition to the belief that all idolaters held regarding God, their intellect is also called to judgment here: how can idols, which themselves possess neither sight, nor hearing, nor intellect, nor consciousness, bestow such blessings upon others?! Then it says: "See how We explain the signs and evidences in various ways for them, yet they still turn away from the truth" (انْظُرْ كَيْفَ نُصَرِّفُ الْآيَاتِ ثُمَّ هُمْ يَصْدِفُونَ).

Regarding the meaning of "sealing" (ختم) and also the reason why "hearing" (سمع) usually appears in the singular form in Quranic verses while "sight" (ابصار) appears in the plural, we discussed this in the first volume of this Tafsir on page 50.

"Nusarrifu" (نصرف) is derived from the root "tasrif" (تصريف), meaning "change" or "diversification," and here it refers to presenting various arguments in different forms. "Yasdifun" (يصدفون) is from the root "sadaf" (صدف), which means a side or direction; since a person turns toward another side or direction when turning away and avoiding something, this word is used to mean "turning away." However, as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat:

This root is used to mean intense avoidance and turning away.

In the following verse, after mentioning these three great Divine blessings (sight, hearing, and understanding) which are the source of all blessings in this world and the Hereafter, it refers to the possibility of the total withdrawal of all blessings, saying: "Say to them: If the punishment of Allah should come upon you suddenly and without warning, or openly and with prior notice, would any be destroyed except the wrongdoing people?" (قُلْ أَرَأَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتَاكُمْ عَذَابُ اللَّهِ بَغْتَةً أَوْ جَهْرَةً هَلْ يُهْلَكُ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الظَّالِمُونَ) [1]. "Baghtatan" (بغتة) means "suddenly" and "jahratan" (جهرة) means "openly." Ordinarily, "secretly" should be mentioned as the opposite of "openly," not "suddenly." However, because the preliminaries of sudden events are usually hidden and secret—for if they were not hidden, they would not be sudden—the concept of secrecy is embedded within the meaning of the word "baghtatan." The point is that the only one capable of various punishments and taking away blessings is Allah, and idols have no role in this matter.

[1]: Regarding the meaning of "ara'aytakum" (أرأيتكم) and its grammatical analysis, we discussed it under verse 40 of this same Surah and stated there is no reason to take it to mean "inform me"; rather, its concept is "did you know?" or "have you considered?"

Therefore, there is no reason to seek refuge in them. However, since Allah is All-Wise and All-Merciful, He only punishes the wrongdoers.

Furthermore, it is understood from this expression that "wrongdoing" (ظلم) has a broad meaning that includes all types of polytheism and sins; in fact, in Quranic verses, polytheism is introduced as a "great injustice" (ظلم عظيم), as Luqman said to his son: لَا تُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ إِنَّ الشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ—"O my son, do not associate partners with Allah; indeed, polytheism is a great injustice" (Surah Luqman, Verse 13).

In the next verse, it refers to the status of the Divine Prophets, saying: "Not only are the lifeless idols incapable of doing anything, but even the great Prophets and Divine leaders have no task other than delivering the message, giving glad tidings and warnings, and providing encouragement and threats. Every blessing that exists is by the command of Allah and from His side, and they too ask Him for whatever they desire" (وَمَا نُرْسِلُ الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ). Another possibility for the connection of this verse with the preceding ones is that since the previous verses spoke of encouragements and threats, this verse states that this is the very purpose for which the Prophets were sent; their work was also glad tidings and warning.

Then it says: "The path to salvation is restricted to two things: those who believe and reform themselves (and perform righteous deeds) shall have no fear of Divine punishments, nor shall they grieve over their past actions" (فَمَنْ آمَنَ وَأَصْلَحَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ).

"And in contrast, those who deny Our signs will be afflicted by Divine punishment because of this defiance and disobedience" (وَالَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا يَمَسُّهُمُ الْعَذَابُ بِمَا كَانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ).

It is noteworthy that regarding the punishment of those who deny Allah's signs, the expression "yamassuhum al-adhab" (يَمَسُّهُمُ الْعَذَابُ) is used, meaning "the punishment touches them." It is as if the punishment seeks them out everywhere and then seizes them in the worst possible way. It is also necessary to mention that "fisq" (فسق) has a broad meaning, encompassing every kind of disobedience and departure from the path of servitude to Allah, including disbelief; this is the meaning intended in the above verse. Therefore, there remains no room for the debates that Fakhr al-Razi and other commentators have engaged in here regarding "fisq," considering it to include [only minor] sins and then rising to defend that position.

Ayah

قُل لَّآ أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِندِى خَزَآئِنُ ٱللَّهِ وَلَآ أَعْلَمُ ٱلْغَيْبَ وَلَآ أَقُولُ لَكُمْ إِنِّى مَلَكٌ ۖ إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ ۚ قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ ۚ أَفَلَا تَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٥٠)
Say, ‘I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah, nor do I know the Unseen, nor do I say to you that I am an angel. I follow only what is revealed to me.’ Say, ‘Are the blind one and the seer equal? So do you not reflect?’ (50)

Commentary

Knowledge of the Unseen

The above verse continues the response to various objections raised by the disbelievers and polytheists, addressing three parts of their criticisms in short sentences:

First, they used to propose strange and extraordinary miracles to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, each according to their own whim. They were not even satisfied with witnessing the miracles requested by others. Sometimes they demanded houses made of gold, sometimes the descent of angels, and at other times the transformation of the dry and scorching land of Makkah into a lush garden full of water and fruit, along with other requests. This will be detailed in the commentary of Surah Al-Isra, under verse 90.

It seems that with these strange and extraordinary demands, they expected a kind of status of divinity and ownership of the earth and heavens for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

Therefore, in response to these individuals, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is commanded to say: "I do not claim to you that the treasures of Allah are with me" (قُلْ لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللَّهِ).

"Khaza'in" (treasures) is the plural of "khazinah," meaning the source and center of anything collected for preservation and kept out of reach of others. Considering the verse: "And there is not a thing but with Us are its treasures, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (وَ إِنْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَ ما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ) (Surah Al-Hijr, verse 21), it becomes clear that "the treasures of Allah" encompass the source of everything. In reality, this source stems from His infinite Essence, which is the fountainhead of all perfections and powers.

Then, in response to those who expected the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to inform them of all the secrets of the past and future—even telling them what events would occur in their future lives so they could act to avert harm or gain benefit—he says:

"Nor do I claim that I know the Unseen" (وَ لا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ).

As we have stated previously, the One who is aware of everything is He who is present and observant in every place and at every time, and that is only the Pure Essence of Allah. However, anyone other than Him, whose existence is limited to a specific time and place, naturally cannot be aware of everything.

Yet, there is no obstacle to Allah placing a portion of the knowledge of the Unseen—which He deems appropriate and necessary for the completion of the leadership of Divine leaders—at their disposal. Of course, this is not called "essential knowledge of the Unseen" (ilm al-ghayb bi-dhat), but rather "accidental knowledge of the Unseen" (ilm al-ghayb bi-l- عرض), or in other words, learning and instruction from the Knower of the Unseen.

Numerous verses of the Quran testify that Allah has granted such knowledge not only to Prophets and Divine leaders but sometimes even to others. For instance, in Surah Al-Jinn, verses 26 and 27, we read: "[He is] Knower of the Unseen, and He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except whom He has approved of messengers" (عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى‌ غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى‌ مِنْ رَسُولٍ).

Fundamentally, the completion of the station of leadership—especially a global and universal leadership—requires awareness of many matters that are hidden from other people. If Allah did not place this knowledge of the Unseen at the disposal of His messengers and His Awliya (saints), their station of leadership would not be complete.

(Reflect deeply).

Beyond this, sometimes a living creature needs to know a corner of the Unseen for its continued survival, and Allah provides it. For example, we have heard that some insects predict winter weather conditions during the summer; that is, Allah places this specific "unseen" at their disposal because their lives might otherwise be subject to destruction without this knowledge. A further explanation of this topic will be provided, God willing, in the commentary of verse 188 of Surah Al-A'raf.

In the third sentence, responding to the criticism of those who expected the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself to be an angel, or to have an angel accompanying him, and that no human traits such as eating food or walking in the streets and markets should be seen in him, he says: "Nor do I say to you that I am an angel" (وَ لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ).

Rather, "I only follow what is revealed to me" (إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى‌ إِلَيَ).

From this sentence, it is clearly understood that whatever the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله possessed and whatever he did originated from heavenly revelation. He did not act upon his own ijtihad (independent reasoning) as some have imagined, nor upon analogy (qiyas) or anything else; rather, his program in religious matters was solely following revelation.

At the end of the verse, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is commanded: "Say, 'Are the blind and the seeing equal?' Are those whose eyes, thoughts, and intellects are closed equal to those who see and perceive realities well? 'Will you not then reflect?'" (قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمى‌ وَ الْبَصِيرُ أَ فَلا تَتَفَكَّرُونَ).

The mention of this sentence after the three preceding sentences may be because in the previous statements, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "I neither possess the treasures of Allah, nor am I a knower of the Unseen, nor am I an angel; I am only a follower of revelation." However, this does not mean that I am the same as you stubborn idolaters; rather, I am a seeing human, while you are like the blind, and these two are not equal! Another possibility for the connection of this sentence to the previous ones is that the proofs of Monotheism (Tawhid) as well as the truthfulness of the Prophet are manifest, but they require a seeing eye to behold them. If you do not accept them, it is not because the subject is ambiguous or complex, but because you are not seeing. Are the seeing and the blind equal?!

Ayah

وَأَنذِرْ بِهِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخَافُونَ أَن يُحْشَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ ۙ لَيْسَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ وَلِىٌّ وَلَا شَفِيعٌ لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ (٥١)
And warn by its means those who fear being mustered toward their Lord, besides whom they shall have neither any guardian nor any intercessor, so that they may be Godwary. (51)

Commentary

At the end of the previous verse, it was stated that the blind and the seeing are not equal. Following that, in this verse, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is commanded: "Warn and awaken by means of the Quran those who fear the Day of Resurrection." This means those whose inner eyes are open to the extent that they consider it possible there will be an accounting and a reckoning, and in light of this possibility and the fear of responsibility, they have found the readiness to accept the truth (وَ أَنْذِرْ بِهِ الَّذِينَ يَخافُونَ أَنْ يُحْشَرُوا إِلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ‌).

Perhaps we have said repeatedly that the mere existence of a capable leader and comprehensive educational programs is not sufficient for the guidance of individuals; rather, a type of readiness within the individuals themselves is also necessary. Just as sunlight alone is not enough to distinguish the path from the pit but also requires a seeing eye, and just as a potent seed will not bear fruit as long as there is no ready and fertile soil.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that the pronoun "به" (by it) refers back to the Quran, even though the Quran was not explicitly mentioned in the preceding verses, it is clear from the context.

Furthermore, the meaning of "يخافون" (they fear) is that very possibility of harm and loss that arises in the mind of every rational person when confronted with the call of the Prophets and divine leaders—that perhaps their words are the truth, and opposing them would result in loss and ruin. Therefore, it is better to study their call and examine their evidence.

This is one of the primary conditions for guidance, and it is the same principle that scholars of theology (Aqidah) have established as the necessity of "averting potential harm" (daf' al-darar al-muhtamal) as a reason for the obligation to investigate the claim of one who professes prophethood and the necessity of researching the recognition of God.

Then it says that such awakened individuals fear that day when there is no protector or intercessor besides Allah (لَيْسَ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ وَلِيٌّ وَ لا شَفِيعٌ‌).

Yes, warn such individuals and invite them toward the Truth, for there is hope of piety and righteousness for them (لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ‌).

Of course, the negation of "intercession" (shafa'ah) and "guardianship" (wilayah) for other than Allah in this verse in no way contradicts the intercession and guardianship of the men of God. As we have previously pointed out, the intent is the negation of intercession and guardianship in essence (per se). That is, these two positions belong inherently to Allah, and if anyone other than Him possesses the status of guardianship or intercession, it is by His permission, leave, and command. As the Quran explicitly states: "مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يَشْفَعُ عِنْدَهُ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ‌" — "Who is there that can intercede with Him except by His permission?" (Al-Baqarah: 255).

For further explanation in this regard and regarding the discussion of intercession in general, please refer to Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 160, and Volume 2, pages 189 and 196.

Ayahs

وَلَا تَطْرُدِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَدَوٰةِ وَٱلْعَشِىِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُۥ ۖ مَا عَلَيْكَ مِنْ حِسَابِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ وَمَا مِنْ حِسَابِكَ عَلَيْهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَتَطْرُدَهُمْ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥٢)
Do not drive away those who supplicate their Lord morning and evening desiring His face. Neither are you accountable for them in any way, nor are they accountable for you in any way, so that you may drive them away and thus become one of the wrongdoers. (52)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ فَتَنَّا بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لِّيَقُولُوٓا۟ أَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنۢ بَيْنِنَآ ۗ أَلَيْسَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَعْلَمَ بِٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (٥٣)
Thus do We test them by means of one another so that they should say, ‘Are these the ones whom Allah has favoured from among us?!’ Does not Allah know best the grateful?! (53)

Commentary

Circumstances of Revelation

Several narrations have been cited regarding the circumstances of the revelation of the above verses, which bear great similarity to one another. Among them, it is narrated in the tafsir Al-Durr Al-Manthur that a group from the Quraysh passed by a gathering of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله while Suhayb, Ammar, Bilal, Khabbab, and other similar low-income and working-class Muslims were in the service of the Prophet.

They were astonished by observing this scene (and since they defined personality through wealth, property, and status, they could not comprehend the greatness of the spiritual station of these great men, nor their constructive role in creating the great Islamic and human society of the future). They said: "O Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله! Have you contented yourself with these individuals from among the people? Are these the ones whom Allah has chosen from among us?! Should we be followers of these people? Dismiss them from your presence as soon as possible; perhaps then we will draw near to you and follow you."

The above verses were revealed and vehemently rejected this proposal.

Some commentators of the Ahl al-Sunnah, such as the author of Al-Manar, have narrated a similar hadith and then added: "Umar ibn al-Khattab was present there and suggested to the Prophet: 'What is the harm in accepting their proposal? Let us see what they will do.'" The above verses rejected his suggestion as well.

Let there be no misunderstanding: mentioning the circumstances of revelation for some verses of this Surah does not contradict the fact that the entire Surah was revealed at once. As we have previously pointed out, it is possible that various events occurred at different intervals before the revelation of the Surah, and this Surah was addressing those events.

It seems necessary to mention here that in some narrations, it is stated that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not accept their proposal, they asked him to create an alternation between the nobles of the Quraysh and his poor companions—meaning he should designate one day for the former and one day for the latter, so they would not sit together in one gathering. The Prophet accepted this proposal, perhaps as a means for them to embrace faith. They said this matter must be written as a contract. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله commissioned Ali عليه السلام to write the contract, at which point the above verse was revealed and forbade this action.

However, this narration—in addition to being inconsistent with the spirit of Islamic teachings, which have never shown flexibility toward such discrimination but rather always speak of the unity of the Islamic community—is also inconsistent with the preceding verse which stated: "إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى‌ إِلَيَّ" ("I only follow what is revealed to me"). How can it be believed that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would submit to such a proposal without waiting for Divine revelation?

Furthermore, the phrase "وَ لا تَطْرُدِ" (and do not dismiss) which we read at the beginning of the verse under discussion, indicates that they had proposed the absolute dismissal of that group of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله companions, not a proposal of alternation. Alternation is very different from dismissal, and this indicates that the circumstances of revelation are indeed what we mentioned at the beginning.

Combating Classist Thought

In this verse, another one of the polytheists' excuses is mentioned: they expected the Prophet to grant privileges to the wealthy relative to the poor class. They believed that sitting alongside the poor companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was a great flaw and disgrace for them, unaware that Islam came to end such hollow and baseless privileges. Therefore, they insisted on this proposal that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله should drive this group away. However, the Quran explicitly and by citing living reasons negates their proposal. First, it says: "And do not dismiss those who call upon their Lord morning and evening, seeking [nothing but] His Countenance" (وَ لا تَطْرُدِ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَداةِ وَ الْعَشِيِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُ) [1]. It is noteworthy that here, without mentioning the names or titles of these individuals,

[1]: Wajh literally means "face" and is sometimes used to mean "Essence" (Dhat). In the above verse, the second meaning is intended. For more details on this subject, please refer to Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 263.

the text contents itself with mentioning this attribute: that they are in remembrance of Allah morning and evening—or in other words, always. This worship, supplication, and attention to the Lord is not for any other reason like hypocrisy, but solely for His Pure Essence; they want and seek Him only for Himself, and no privilege equals this privilege.

It is understood from various verses of the Quran that the wealthy and arrogant polytheists did not make such a proposal to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله for the first or last time; rather, they repeatedly raised this objection: why has the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله gathered a group of the destitute around himself? And they insisted he should dismiss them.

In reality, based on an ancient false tradition, they considered the merit of individuals to be their wealth. They believed that social classes created based on wealth must always be preserved, and any religion or call that sought to disrupt class-based life and ignore these privileges was, in their view, rejected and unacceptable.

Regarding the state of the Prophet Noah عليه السلام, we also read that the nobles of his time said to him: (وَ ما نَراكَ اتَّبَعَكَ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ هُمْ أَراذِلُنا بادِيَ الرَّأْيِ): "And we do not see any follow you except those who are the lowest of us [and] immature in opinion" (Hud, Verse 27), and they considered this as evidence for the invalidity of his mission.

One of the signs of the greatness of Islam, the Quran, and generally the greatness of the school of the Prophets is that they resisted such proposals with the utmost tenacity. In societies where class distinction was considered an eternal matter, they set about crushing this illusory privilege so it would be known that pure-hearted, faithful, and intelligent individuals like Salman, Abu Dharr, Suhayb, Khabbab, and Bilal suffer not the slightest deficiency for lacking property and wealth, and that the brainless, blind-hearted, selfish, and arrogant wealthy cannot enjoy social and spiritual privileges because of their wealth.

In the next sentence, He says: There is no reason for you to drive away such faithful people, because neither is their account upon you, nor is your account upon them (ما عَلَيْكَ مِنْ حِسابِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ وَ ما مِنْ حِسابِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

Nevertheless, if you drive them away, you will be among the wrongdoers (فَتَطْرُدَهُمْ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ).

As for what is meant by "account" (hisab) here, there is discussion among commentators. Some have suggested that "account" refers to the account of their sustenance; meaning if their hands are empty of property and wealth, they place no burden upon your shoulders, for the account of their sustenance is with Allah, just as you do not place the burden of your life upon their shoulders and the account of your sustenance is not with them.

However, considering the explanation we will provide, this possibility seems unlikely.

Rather, it is apparent that "account" refers to the account of deeds—as many commentators have stated. But how does Allah say "the account of their deeds is not upon you" when they had committed no evil deed that would necessitate such a sentence? This is because the polytheists accused the poor companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله of being far from God due to their lack of wealth, arguing that if their deeds were accepted in the presence of God, why had He not provided them with a prosperous life? Furthermore, they accused them that their faith was perhaps for the sake of securing a livelihood and obtaining bread and water.

The Quran responds that even assuming they were such, their account is with Allah. As long as they have expressed faith and joined the ranks of Muslims, they must not be driven away at any price. In this way, it blocks the excuses of the Quraysh nobles.

The evidence for this interpretation is what appears in the story of the Prophet Noah, which is similar to the story of the Quraysh nobles, where the people of Noah said to him: أَ نُؤْمِنُ لَكَ وَ اتَّبَعَكَ الْأَرْذَلُونَ: "Shall we believe in you while the lowest people follow you?" Noah replied: وَ ما عِلْمِي بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ إِنْ حِسابُهُمْ إِلَّا عَلى‌ رَبِّي لَوْ تَشْعُرُونَ وَ ما أَنَا بِطارِدِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ: "And what knowledge have I of what they used to do? Their account is only upon my Lord, if you but perceived. And I am not one to drive away the believers." [1] The conclusion is that the Prophet's duty is to accept any individual who expresses faith without any difference or discrimination, from whatever stratum or class they may be, let alone pure-hearted and faithful individuals who seek nothing but Allah and whose only "sin" is that their hands are empty of wealth and they are not polluted by the wretched life of the nobles.

A Great Privilege of Islam

We know that in contemporary Christianity, the scope of the authority of religious leaders has expanded in a ridiculous manner to the point where they claim for themselves the right to forgive sins, and for this reason, they can dismiss and excommunicate someone for the smallest thing, or accept them.

The Quran, in the above verse and other verses, explicitly reminds us that not only religious scholars but even the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself does not have the right to dismiss someone who expresses faith and has not committed an act that results in leaving Islam. The forgiveness of sins and the accounting of the servants is solely in the hands of Allah, and no one but Him has the right to interfere in such a matter.

However, let there be no mistake: the subject of discussion in the verse is "religious dismissal," not "legal dismissal." This means that if, for example, a school was endowed for specific students, and someone initially met the criteria but later lacked them, their expulsion from that school is not prohibited. Likewise, if the trustee of a school had authorities for the sake of observing the school's interests, there is no harm in using those legitimate authorities to maintain order and its position (therefore, the points seen in Tafsir Al-Manar under this verse contrary to this matter arise from confusing religious dismissal with legal dismissal).

In the next verse, He warns the faithless wealthy that these events are trials for them, and if they emerge failed from the furnace of these trials, they must endure the painful consequences. He says: "And thus We have tried some of them by others" (وَ كَذلِكَ فَتَنَّا بَعْضَهُمْ بِبَعْضٍ). "Fitnah" here means trial. [1] What trial could be heavier than this: that the nobles and wealthy, who for years have been accustomed to completely separating their account from the lower classes—neither sharing in their joy nor in their grief and suffering, and even having their graves distant from one another—should suddenly break all these customs and traditions, remove these massive chains from their hands and feet, and join a religion whose pioneers are considered to be from the so-called poor and lower class?

Then He adds: The state of these wealthy people reaches a point where they look at the true believers with contempt and say: "Are these the ones whom Allah has favored from among us, and bestowed upon them the blessing of faith and Islam?" Are these people worthy of

[1]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 13 and 17, under verses 191 and 193 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

such things?! (لِيَقُولُوا أَ هؤُلاءِ مَنَّ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ بَيْنِنا) [1] And then He answers them that these faithful individuals are people who have fulfilled the gratitude for the blessing of action and discernment and applied it, and likewise have fulfilled the gratitude for the blessing of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله call and accepted it. What blessing is greater than that and what gratitude is higher than that? And because of this, Allah has made faith firm in their hearts. "Is not Allah most knowing of those who are grateful?" (أَ لَيْسَ اللَّهُ بِأَعْلَمَ بِالشَّاكِرِينَ).

Ayahs

وَإِذَا جَآءَكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَقُلْ سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ كَتَبَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ ٱلرَّحْمَةَ ۖ أَنَّهُۥ مَنْ عَمِلَ مِنكُمْ سُوٓءًۢا بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ ثُمَّ تَابَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ وَأَصْلَحَ فَأَنَّهُۥ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٥٤)
When those who have faith in Our signs come to you, say, ‘Peace to you! Your Lord has made mercy incumbent upon Himself: whoever of you commits an evil [deed] out of ignorance and then repents after that and reforms, then He is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (54)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ وَلِتَسْتَبِينَ سَبِيلُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٥٥)
Thus do We elaborate the signs, so that the way of the guilty may be exposed. (55)

Commentary

Some believe that the first verse was revealed concerning those whom the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was commanded in previous verses to refrain from rejecting and not to separate them from himself. Others believe it was regarding a group of sinners who came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and stated, "We have committed many sins," and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله remained silent until the above verse was revealed.

However, whatever its occasion of revelation may be, there is no doubt that the concept of the verse is a general and broad meaning that encompasses everyone. This is because it first instructs the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as a general law not only to refrain from rejecting all believing individuals—even if they are sinners—but to accept them into his presence, saying:

"When those who believe in Our signs come to you, say: 'Peace be upon you' (وَ إِذا جاءَكَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِآياتِنا فَقُلْ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ)."

This greeting (Salam) may be from Allah's side through the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, or directly from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself; in either case, it is evidence of acceptance, welcoming, understanding, and friendship with them.

In the second sentence, He adds: "Your Lord has prescribed mercy upon Himself" (كَتَبَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلى‌ نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ).

"Kataba" (He wrote), which comes from the root "Kitabat" meaning writing, is in many instances an allusion to obligation, necessity, and commitment, because one of the effects of writing is the finalization and permanence of something.

In the third sentence, which is in fact an explanation and interpretation of Divine mercy, He says in an affectionate expression: "Whoever among you does evil out of ignorance, then repents thereafter and amends, then He is Oft-Forgiving, Most Merciful" (أَنَّهُ مَنْ عَمِلَ مِنْكُمْ سُوءاً بِجَهالَةٍ ثُمَّ تابَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ وَ أَصْلَحَ فَأَنَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ). As we have previously stated [^1], the meaning of "ignorance" (jahalah) in such cases is the dominance and rebellion of lust, where a person—not out of enmity and hostility toward the truth, but due to the dominance of whims and desires—becomes such that they lose the light of reason and the control over lust. Such a person, despite knowing the sin and the prohibition, is referred to as "ignorant" because their knowledge has been overshadowed by whims and desires. Certainly, such a person is responsible for their sin, but since it was not out of enmity and hostility, they strive to amend and compensate.

In reality, the above verse instructs the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله not only to refrain from driving away any believing individual from any class, race, or condition, but to open his arms equally to all, even if they are individuals tainted by many sins, to accept and reform them.

[^1]: As mentioned under verse 164 of Surah Al-Imran, "Mann" (favor) originally means bestowing a blessing; for further explanation, refer to Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 158.

[^1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 3, page 313.

And in the next verse, to emphasize the matter, He says: "Thus do We explain the signs and instructions clearly and distinctly, so that the way of the seekers of truth and the obedient may become manifest, as well as the way of the stubborn sinners and enemies of truth" (وَ كَذلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ وَ لِتَسْتَبِينَ سَبِيلُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ) [^1]. It is clear that the meaning of "criminal" (mujrim) in the above verse does not refer to every sinner, because in this verse, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is commissioned to accept sinners if they come to him, even if they have committed wrong acts out of ignorance. Therefore, the meaning of "criminal" refers to those stubborn and persistent sinners who do not submit to the truth by any means.

[^1]: In reality, the phrase "and so that the way... may become manifest" is conjoined to a deleted phrase understood by context, meaning: "so that the way of the obedient believers may become manifest and the way of the criminals may become manifest."

That is to say, after this general and universal invitation toward the truth—even the invitation of sinners who are regretful of their actions—the way and manner of the stubborn and inflexible criminals will be completely recognized.

Ayahs

قُلْ إِنِّى نُهِيتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قُل لَّآ أَتَّبِعُ أَهْوَآءَكُمْ ۙ قَدْ ضَلَلْتُ إِذًا وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ (٥٦)
Say, ‘I have been forbidden to worship those whom you invoke besides Allah.’ Say, ‘I do not follow your desires, for then I will have gone astray, and I will not be among the [rightly] guided.’ (56)
قُلْ إِنِّى عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى وَكَذَّبْتُم بِهِۦ ۚ مَا عِندِى مَا تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنِ ٱلْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ ۖ يَقُصُّ ٱلْحَقَّ ۖ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْفَـٰصِلِينَ (٥٧)
Say, ‘Indeed I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord and you have denied it. What you seek to hasten is not up to me. Judgement belongs only to Allah; He expounds the truth and He is the best of judges.’ (57)
قُل لَّوْ أَنَّ عِندِى مَا تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِۦ لَقُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥٨)
Say, ‘If what you seek to hasten were with me, the matter would surely have been decided between you and me, and Allah knows best the wrongdoers.’ (58)

Commentary

Unreasonable Persistence

In these verses, the address continues toward the stubborn polytheists and idolaters—just as most verses of this Surah follow this same discussion. The tone of these verses suggests that they had invited the Prophet to incline toward their creed. The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) is commanded to explicitly tell them: "I have been forbidden from worshipping those whom you invoke besides Allah" (قُلْ إِنِّي نُهِيتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ) [^1]. The phrase "I have been forbidden" (نُهِيتُ), which is brought in the passive past tense, indicates that the prohibition of idol worship is not a new matter; rather, it has always been and will always be so.

Then, with the phrase: "Say [O Prophet], I do not follow your desires" (قُلْ لا أَتَّبِعُ أَهْواءَكُمْ), He gives a clear answer to their baseless proposal, which is that idolatry has no logical basis and never aligns with the judgment of intellect and reason. This is because the intellect clearly understands that a human being is more noble than inanimate matter; how could it be possible for a human to bow down before another creature, or even before an inferior being? Furthermore, idols were often crafted and fashioned by humans themselves; how could something that is a human's own creation become his deity and the solver of his problems? Therefore, the source of idolatry is nothing but blind imitation and the following of superstitions and whims.

In the final phrase, for further emphasis, He says: "If I were to do such a thing, I would surely have gone astray and would not be among the guided" (قَدْ ضَلَلْتُ إِذاً وَ ما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُهْتَدِينَ).

In the next verse, He gives them another answer: "I stand upon a clear evidence (Bayyinah) from my Lord, though you have denied it" (قُلْ إِنِّي عَلى‌ بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي وَ كَذَّبْتُمْ بِهِ). "Bayyinah" originally refers to something that creates a distance and separation between two things such that they no longer have any connection or mixing; subsequently, it has been applied to a clear and manifest proof because it completely separates truth from falsehood.

In jurisprudential terminology, although "Bayyinah" refers to the testimony of two just witnesses, its linguistic meaning is quite broad, and the testimony of two just witnesses is merely one of its instances. If miracles are called "Bayyinah," it is for this same reason—that they separate truth from falsehood. And if the verses and divine laws are called "Bayyinah," it is again as an instance of this broad meaning.

In short, in this verse as well, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) is commanded to emphasize this point: "My evidence regarding the issue of worshipping God and the struggle against idols is completely clear and manifest, and your denial and rejection do not diminish its importance."

Then He points to another one of their excuses, which was their saying: "If you are on the truth, then bring sooner the punishments with which you threaten us." In response to them, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) says: "That which you seek to hasten is not in my power" (ما عِنْدِي ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ).

"All affairs and commands are in the hand of Allah alone" (إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ).

And then, as an emphasis, He says: "It is He who separates the truth from falsehood, and He is the best of those who distinguish [between truth and falsehood]" (يَقُصُّ الْحَقَّ وَ هُوَ خَيْرُ الْفاصِلِينَ).

It is obvious that the one who can best separate truth from falsehood is the one whose knowledge is greater than all others, and for whom the recognition of truth and falsehood is perfectly clear. Additionally, He must have sufficient power to implement His knowledge, and these two attributes (knowledge and power) exist in an unlimited and infinite manner only for the Pure Essence of Allah; therefore, He is the best of those who separate truth from falsehood.

In the next verse, He commands the Prophet that in the face of the demand for punishment and retribution from this stubborn and ignorant group, "Say to them: If that which you seek to hasten from me were in my grasp of power and I were to act upon your request, the matter between me and you would have been settled [and you would have been destroyed]" (قُلْ لَوْ أَنَّ عِنْدِي ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ لَقُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ بَيْنِي وَ بَيْنَكُمْ). But so they do not imagine that their punishment has been forgotten, He says at the end: "And Allah knows best the wrongdoers and will give them their retribution at the proper time" (وَ اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِالظَّالِمِينَ).

Key Points

  1. It is understood from the verses of the Quran that many past nations made this same request of their prophets: "If you are telling the truth, then why do you not send upon us the punishment you expect for us?!"

The people of Nuh (peace be upon him) made such a request of him and said: "O Nuh! Why do you speak and argue with us so much? If you are truthful, bring upon us that which you threaten us with" (قَالُوا يَا نُوحُ قَدْ جَادَلْتَنَا فَأَكْثَرْتَ جِدَالَنَا فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ) (Hud: 32). A similar request was made by the people of Salih (A'raf: 77).

Likewise, the people of 'Ad made such a proposal to their prophet, Hud (A'raf: 70). It is also understood from Surah Al-Isra that this request was repeatedly made of the Prophet of Islam; they even said: "We will not believe in you until you do one of several things, including causing the sky to fall upon us in pieces!" (أَوْ تُسْقِطَ السَّماءَ كَما زَعَمْتَ عَلَيْنا كِسَفاً) (Al-Isra: 92).

These unreasonable requests were made either as mockery and ridicule or truly as a demand for a miracle. In both cases, it was a foolish act; because in the second case, it would lead to their destruction, leaving no room to benefit from the miracle, and in the first case, with the clear proofs and signs the prophets brought, at least the possibility of their truthfulness would be manifest to any observer. How is it possible that with such a possibility, a person would request his own destruction or take it as a joke? But prejudice and stubbornness are a great calamity that blocks all thought and logic.

  1. The phrase "إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ" (Command belongs only to Allah) has a clear meaning: every kind of command in the world of creation (Takwin) and in the world of religious laws (Tashri') is in the hand of Allah. Therefore, if the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) receives a mission, that too is by His command.

And if Prophet Isa, for example, raises the dead, that too is by His permission. Likewise, any position, whether it be divine leadership, judgeship, or arbitration, that is entrusted to someone, is also from the side of the Lord.

Unfortunately, however, this very clear phrase has been repeatedly misused throughout history. Sometimes the Kharijites, to object to the issue of appointing "arbitrators" (Hakamayn) during the Battle of Siffin—which had been done at the suggestion of people like themselves—clung to this phrase. As Ali (peace be upon him) said, they took a word of truth and used it for a false meaning, and gradually the phrase "La hukma illa lillah" became a slogan for them.

They were so ignorant and foolish that they imagined if someone gained arbitration in a matter by the command of Allah and the instruction of Islam, it contradicted the phrase "إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ." Although they read the Quran a lot, they understood very little, for even the Quran, regarding the subject of Islamic family courts, explicitly mentions the selection of an arbitrator from the side of the woman and an arbitrator from the side of the man (فَابْعَثُوا حَكَماً مِنْ أَهْلِهِ وَ حَكَماً مِنْ أَهْلِها) (An-Nisa: 35). Others—as Fakhr al-Razi mentions in his Tafsir—have taken this phrase as evidence for the school of determinism (Jabr), because when we accept that all commands in the world of creation are in the hand of Allah, no choice remains for anyone.

This is while we know that the free will of the servants and their choice is also by the command of the Lord. It is Allah who wills for them to be autonomous and free in their actions so that, in the light of choice and freedom, He may place duty and responsibility upon them and they may be refined.

  1. The word "yaqussu" (يَقُصُّ) linguistically means "to cut" or "to sever" something [^2]. When we read in the above verse "yaqussu al-haqq" (He cuts the truth), it means Allah severs the truth—meaning He completely separates and distinguishes it from falsehood. Therefore, the subsequent phrase "وَ هُوَ خَيْرُ الْفاصِلِينَ" (and He is the best of those who distinguish) is considered an emphasis on this subject.

It should be noted that the word "yaqussu" is not from the root "qissah" meaning to relate a story or narrative, as some commentators have imagined.

[^1]: The use of the word "alladhina" (الَّذِينَ), which is for the plural masculine rational, for idols is because He is speaking to them from the perspective of their own thinking. [^2]: Al-Qamus says: "Qassa al-sha'r wa al-zufr: he cut some of them with the miqass, i.e., the shears." The Arabs call the trimming of hair and nails with shears or scissors "qass," and the shears "miqass" (with a kasrah on the mim and a fathah on the qaf).

Ayahs

۞ وَعِندَهُۥ مَفَاتِحُ ٱلْغَيْبِ لَا يَعْلَمُهَآ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ ۚ وَمَا تَسْقُطُ مِن وَرَقَةٍ إِلَّا يَعْلَمُهَا وَلَا حَبَّةٍ فِى ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا رَطْبٍ وَلَا يَابِسٍ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ (٥٩)
With Him are the treasures of the Unseen; no one knows them except Him. He knows whatever there is in land and sea. No leaf falls without His knowing it, nor is there a grain in the darkness of the earth, nor anything fresh or withered but it is in a manifest Book. (59)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يَتَوَفَّىٰكُم بِٱلَّيْلِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا جَرَحْتُم بِٱلنَّهَارِ ثُمَّ يَبْعَثُكُمْ فِيهِ لِيُقْضَىٰٓ أَجَلٌ مُّسَمًّى ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٦٠)
It is He who takes your souls by night, and He knows what you do by day, then He reanimates you therein so that a specified term may be completed. Then to Him will be your return, whereat He will inform you concerning what you used to do. (60)
وَهُوَ ٱلْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ وَيُرْسِلُ عَلَيْكُمْ حَفَظَةً حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنَا وَهُمْ لَا يُفَرِّطُونَ (٦١)
He is the All-dominant over His servants, and He sends guards to [protect] you. When death approaches anyone of you, Our messengers take him away and they do not neglect [their duty]. (61)
ثُمَّ رُدُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَوْلَىٰهُمُ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ أَلَا لَهُ ٱلْحُكْمُ وَهُوَ أَسْرَعُ ٱلْحَـٰسِبِينَ (٦٢)
Then they are returned to Allah, their real master. Look! All judgement belongs to Him, and He is the swiftest of reckoners. (62)

Commentary

Secrets of the Unseen!

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the knowledge and power of Allah and the vast scope of His rule and command. In these verses, what was mentioned briefly in the previous ones is explained in detail.

First, addressing the subject of Allah’s knowledge, it says:

"And with Him are the treasuries of the Unseen (or the keys to the Unseen); none knows them but He" (وَعِنْدَهُ مَفَاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لَا يَعْلَمُهَا إِلَّا هُوَ).

"Mafatih" (مفاتح) is the plural of "miftah" (مفتح), meaning "key," and it could also be the plural of "maftah" (مفتح), meaning "treasury" or a center for keeping something.

In the first case, the meaning of the verse would be that all the keys to the Unseen are in His hand, and in the second case, all the treasuries of the Unseen.

It is also possible that both meanings are intended in one expression, and as we have established in the principles of jurisprudence (Ilm al-Usul), there is no obstacle to using one word for multiple meanings. In any case, these two are inseparable, for wherever there is a treasury, there is a key.

However, it seems more likely that "mafatih" means keys rather than treasuries, because the objective here is to describe Allah’s knowledge, and that is more compatible with the concept of a key, which is the means of gaining awareness of various stores. In the two other instances where the word "mafatih" is used in the Quran, it also refers to keys [^1]. Then, for further explanation and emphasis, it says: "And He knows what is on the land and in the sea" (وَيَعْلَمُ مَا فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ).

"Barr" (بر) means a vast place and is usually applied to dry land, and "bahr" (بحر) originally means a vast place where a large amount of water is collected, usually applied to seas and sometimes to great rivers.

In any case, Allah’s awareness of what is on the land and in the seas means His knowledge encompasses everything. Reflecting on the breadth of the meaning of the above sentence (He knows what is on the land and in the seas) actually clarifies a corner of His vast knowledge.

That is, He knows of the movement of billions of living creatures, small and large, in the depths of the seas. Of the trembling of tree leaves in all forests and mountains. Of the definitive history of the blooming of every bud and the opening of petals. Of the flow of the waves of the breeze in the deserts and the curves of the valleys. Of the actual number of cells in every human body and the blood corpuscles. Of the mysterious movements of all electrons in the heart of atoms. And finally, of all the thoughts that pass through the layers of our brains and penetrate to the depths of our souls... Yes, He is equally aware of all of these.

Again, in the next sentence, to emphasize the encompassment of Allah’s knowledge, it refers to a specific instance and says: "And not a leaf falls but He knows it" (وَمَا تَسْقُطُ مِنْ وَرَقَةٍ إِلَّا يَعْلَمُهَا).

That is, the number of these leaves, the moment of their separation from the branches, their rotation in mid-air, and the moment they settle on the ground—all of these are clear before His knowledge.

"Similarly, not a grain is in the darkness of the earth" but that He knows all its characteristics (وَلَا حَبَّةٍ فِي ظُلُماتِ الْأَرْضِ).

In fact, the finger has been placed on two sensitive points that no human—even if millions of years of his life pass and he achieves astonishing industrial and evolutionary progress—can encompass.

Who knows what seeds the winds separate from plants every day and night across the entire globe and to which point they scatter them—seeds that may sometimes remain hidden in the depths of the earth for long years until they obtain sufficient water for growth and development?

Who knows how many seeds of what kind and in which part of the earth are sown every hour by insects or by humans?

What electronic brain can calculate the number of leaves that separate from the branches of forest trees in a single day?

Looking at the scenery of a forest, especially in the autumn season, and particularly following a rain shower or a gale, and the exquisite sight created by the successive falling of leaves, well proves this reality: that such knowledge can never be accessible to man.

In reality, the falling of leaves is the moment of their death, and the falling of seeds into the hiding places of the earth is the first step of their life and existence. It is He who is aware of the system of this death and life; even the various steps a seed takes toward full life and blossoming are manifest before His knowledge at every moment and every hour.

The statement of this subject has a "philosophical" effect and an "educational" effect.

As for its philosophical effect, it negates the notion of those who limit Allah’s knowledge to universals and believe that God is not aware of the particulars of this world, and it explicitly states that Allah has full knowledge of all universals and particulars [^1]. As for its educational effect, it is clear, for faith in this vast, expansive knowledge tells man: all the secrets of your existence, your actions and words, your intentions and thoughts, are all manifest to His Pure Essence. With such faith, how is it possible for a man not to be mindful of his state and not to control his actions, words, and intentions?

And at the end of the verse, He says: "And there is nothing wet or dry but it is recorded in a Clear Book" (وَلَا رَطْبٍ وَلَا يَابِسٍ إِلَّا فِي كِتَابٍ مُبِينٍ).

This sentence, with a short phrase, expresses the infinite vastness of Allah’s knowledge over all beings. And nothing will be excluded from it, for the meaning of "wet" and "dry" is not their literal sense; rather, this expression is usually a metaphor for universality.

Regarding the "Clear Book" (Kitabun Mubin), various possibilities have been given by commentators, but it seems more likely that the "Clear Book" refers to the station of the Lord’s knowledge; that is, all beings are recorded in His infinite knowledge. Interpreting it as the "Preserved Tablet" (Lawh Mahfuz) is also applicable to this same meaning, as it is not unlikely that the Preserved Tablet is itself the page of Allah’s knowledge.

[^1]: "Indeed, its keys were a burden to a company of strong men" (Surah Al-Qasas, Verse 76)... "or those whose keys you possess" (Surah An-Nur, Verse 61).

The possibility also exists in the meaning of "Clear Book" that it refers to the world of creation and existence and the chain of cause and effect, in which everything is written.

In several narrations reaching us through the Ahl al-Bayt علیهم السلام, "leaf" (waraqah) has been interpreted as a miscarried fetus, "grain" (habbah) as a child, "darkness of the earth" (zulumat al-ard) as the wombs of mothers, "wet" (ratb) as that which remains alive from the sperm, and "dry" (yabis) as that which perishes [^1]. There is no doubt that this interpretation does not align with a rigid adherence to the literal meanings of these words, for "waraqah" means leaf, "habbah" means grain, "zulumat al-ard" means the darknesses of the earth, "ratb" means wet, and "yabis" means dry. However, the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt علیهم السلام actually intended with this interpretation to make Muslims realize that they must look at the verses of the Quran with a broad and expansive vision and not remain rigid only on the literal word when understanding their meaning; rather, when clues indicate an expansion of meaning, they should look at the breadth of the meaning. The above narration actually points out that the concept of the verse is not limited to plant seeds but even includes the seeds of human sperm.

In the next verse, the discussion is drawn to Allah’s encompassment of human actions—which is the primary goal—and also defines Allah’s overwhelming power, so that people may derive the necessary educational results from the sum of this discussion. First, it says: "And He is the one who takes your souls by night, and He knows what you have committed and earned by day" (وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ بِاللَّيْلِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا جَرَحْتُمْ بِالنَّهارِ).

[^1]: For more explanation, refer to the book How to Know God. [^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 528.

"Tawaffi" (توفى) literally means to take back, and the fact that sleep is introduced as a kind of taking back of the soul is because sleep—as is well known—is the brother of death. Death is the complete cessation of the human brain's apparatus and the absolute severance of the bond between soul and body, while sleep is only the cessation of a part of the brain's apparatus and the weakening of this bond. Therefore, sleep is considered a small stage of death [^1]. "Jarahtum" (جرحتم) is from the root "jarh" and here means acquisition and obtaining something. That is, you are under the umbrella of Allah’s power and knowledge night and day; He who is aware of the nurturing of plant seeds in the heart of the soil and of the falling and death of leaves in every place and every time is also aware of your actions.

Then it says: this system of sleep and wakefulness repeats; you sleep at night "and He raises you up therein so that an appointed term may be fulfilled" (ثُمَّ يَبْعَثُكُمْ فِيهِ لِيُقْضَى أَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى) [^2]. Finally, it states the ultimate conclusion of the discussion: "Then to Him is your return, then He will inform you of what you used to do" (ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِمَا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ).

In the next verse, again for further explanation of Allah’s scientific encompassment of the servants' actions and the precise keeping of their accounts for the Day of Resurrection, it says: "He is the Supreme over His servants, and He sends over you guardians and watchers to precisely keep the account of your deeds" (وَهُوَ الْقاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبادِهِ وَيُرْسِلُ عَلَيْكُمْ حَفَظَةً).

As we have previously pointed out, "Qahiriyyah" (قاهريت) means victory and complete dominance over something such that there is no ability to resist on the opposing side. According to some, this word is usually used where the opposing side possesses intellect, whereas the word "ghalabah" (victory) has neither of these two specificities; rather, its meaning is completely broad.

"Hafazah" (حفظة) is the plural of "hafiz" and here means the angels who are commissioned to keep the account of human deeds, just as we read in Surah Al-Infitar, verses 10 to 13: "And indeed, over you are guardians, noble writers, they know whatever you do" (إِنَّ عَلَيْكُمْ لَحافِظِينَ. كِراماً كاتِبِينَ. يَعْلَمُونَ ما تَفْعَلُونَ).

Some commentators believe they are not guardians of human deeds, but rather their mission is to protect the human himself from accidents and calamities until the arrival of the appointed term, and they consider the phrase "until when death comes to one of you" (حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدُكُمُ الْمَوْتُ), mentioned after "hafazah," as a clue for this, and verse 11 of Surah Ar-Ra'd may also be taken as evidence for this statement [^1]. However, scrutiny of the entirety of the verse under discussion shows that the meaning of "hifz" (guarding) here is the guarding of deeds, and regarding the angels commissioned to protect humans, we will discuss this, God willing, in the commentary of Surah Ar-Ra'd.

Then it says: the keeping of this account continues until the moment of the end of life and the arrival of death (حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدُكُمُ الْمَوْتُ).

"And at this time, Our messengers who are commissioned to take the souls take his soul" (تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا) [^1]. And at the end, it adds that these angels do not neglect, fall short, or fail in any way in performing their mission; they neither advance the taking of the soul by a moment nor delay it (وَهُمْ لا يُفَرِّطُونَ).

The possibility also exists that this attribute pertains to the angels who are guardians of the account of human deeds, meaning they do not have the slightest negligence or shortcoming in preserving and keeping the account of deeds, and the emphasis of the speech in the verse under discussion is also on this part.

In the next verse, it points to the final stage of man's affair, saying: "Human beings, after passing through their era with these organized files in which everything is recorded, return on the Day of Resurrection to the Lord who is their true Master" (ثُمَّ رُدُّوا إِلَى اللَّهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِّ). And in that court, "judgment, decree, and adjudication belong exclusively to the Pure Essence of Allah" (أَلا لَهُ الْحُكْمُ).

And with all those deeds and files that human beings have had throughout their tumultuous history, He settles all their accounts with speed (وَهُوَ أَسْرَعُ الْحاسِبِينَ).

To the extent that it has been narrated in some traditions: "Indeed, He, the Glorified, settles the account of all His servants in the time it takes to milk a sheep!" (انه سبحانه يحاسب جميع عباده على مقدار حلب شاة!) [^2]

[^1]: We also spoke about this in Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, p. 431. [^2]: The pronoun "fihi" refers to "nahar" (day), and "ba'th" here means raising from sleep and waking up, and "ajal musamma" refers to the lifespan decreed for everyone. [^1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 7, p. 134. [^1]: Regarding the taking of souls and the seizing of human life, please refer to Volume 4 of Tafsir Nemouneh, p. 85. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 3, p. 313.

As we said in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 202, the speed of settling the servants' accounts is such that He can determine all their accounts even in a single moment; even the mention of the time to milk a sheep in the above narration is to show the brevity of time. Hence, we read in another narration: "Indeed, Allah the Almighty settles the account of all creatures in the duration of a blink of an eye!" (ان اللَّه تعالى يحاسب الخلائق كلهم فى مقدار لمح البصر) [^1]. The reason for this is what passed in the commentary of the above verse: that human actions leave an effect on his being and on the beings around him; that is, exactly like machines that show the amount of their movement and performance on devices with numbers! To put it more clearly, if there were precise devices, one could read in the human eye the number of treacherous glances he has made, and study on the human tongue the number of lies, slanders, sharp words, and incorrect speech. In short, each of the human body parts, in addition to his soul, has a counting and accounting device within itself, and with a moment of examination, its account becomes clear.

And if we read in some narrations that for individuals with great responsibility and great wealth, their accounting on that day becomes long, it is not actually because of a delay in settling the basis of the account, but rather they must answer the questions asked regarding their deeds. That is, the weight of the burden of responsibility and the necessity of answering and the completion of proof (itmam al-hujjah) makes their time in court long.

The sum of these verses is a complete educational lesson for the servants of Allah: Allah’s awareness of the smallest particles of this world, His scientific encompassment of everything, His power and supremacy over the servants, His knowledge of all human deeds, the keeping of the account of deeds by precise scribes, the taking of his life at a specific moment, his being raised in the Resurrection, and then the precise and rapid settling of all the affairs of this human.

Who is it that has faith in the sum of these matters and does not become mindful of his deeds? Who would commit injustice and oppression without account, tell lies without reason, and transgress against others without cause? Can these actions ever be reconciled with faith, belief, and attention to the above principles?!

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 298.

Ayahs

قُلْ مَن يُنَجِّيكُم مِّن ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ تَدْعُونَهُۥ تَضَرُّعًا وَخُفْيَةً لَّئِنْ أَنجَىٰنَا مِنْ هَـٰذِهِۦ لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (٦٣)
Say, ‘Who delivers you from the darkness of land and sea, [when] You invoke Him suppliantly and secretly: ‘‘If He delivers us from this, we will surely be among the grateful’’?’ (63)
قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يُنَجِّيكُم مِّنْهَا وَمِن كُلِّ كَرْبٍ ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ (٦٤)
Say, ‘It is Allah who delivers you from them and from every agony, [but] then you ascribe partners [to Him].’ (64)

Commentary

A Light That Shines in the Darkness

Once again, the Quran takes the polytheists by the hand and leads them into the depths of their own primordial nature (fitrah), revealing to them the light of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the oneness of God within that mysterious sanctuary. It commands the Prophet to speak to them thus: "Who delivers you from the darkness of the land and the sea?" (قُلْ مَنْ يُنَجِّيكُمْ مِنْ ظُلُماتِ الْبَرِّ وَ الْبَحْرِ؟) It is worth noting that darkness sometimes has a physical aspect and sometimes a spiritual one. Physical darkness occurs when light is completely cut off or becomes so weak that a person cannot see anything or sees only with great difficulty. Spiritual darkness refers to those problems, hardships, and anxieties whose outcomes are dark and unseen. Ignorance is darkness; social and economic chaos, intellectual disorders, deviations, and moral corruptions—whose ominous consequences are unpredictable or result in nothing but misery and distress—are all forms of darkness.

Darkness is inherently terrifying and provocative of illusions, for the attacks of many dangerous animals, thieves, and criminals take place under the cover of night. Everyone has memories in this regard; therefore, when trapped in darkness, whims and fancies come to life, and terrifying ghosts and monsters emerge from the corners of the imagination, plunging ordinary people into fear and dread.

Darkness is a branch of non-existence ('adam), and man inherently flees from and fears non-existence; for this reason, he is usually afraid of the dark.

If this darkness is mixed with terrifying real-life events—for instance, if a person is surrounded during a sea voyage by "a dark night, the fear of waves, and a daunting whirlpool"—the terror is many degrees greater than problems that arise during the day. This is because the paths to a solution are usually closed to man in such conditions. Similarly, if one loses their way in a desert on a dark night and the terrifying sounds of predatory animals seeking prey in the heart of the night reach their ears from near and far, it is in such moments that man forgets everything else. He forgets all but himself and a brilliant light that shines in the depths of his soul, calling him toward an Origin who alone can solve such problems. Such states are windows into the world of Monotheism and the recognition of God.

Therefore, in the next sentence, it says: "In such a state, you seek help from His infinite grace, sometimes openly with supplication and humility, and sometimes secretly within your heart and soul, you call upon Him" (تَدْعُونَهُ تَضَرُّعاً وَ خُفْيَةً).

"And in such a state, you immediately make a covenant and a promise with that Great Origin that if He delivers us from the jaws of danger, we will certainly be among those who are grateful for His blessings and will set our hearts on none but Him" (لَئِنْ أَنْجانا مِنْ هذِهِ لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الشَّاكِرِينَ).

"But O Prophet, tell them: Allah delivers you from these darknesses and from every other grief and distress (and has delivered you many times), yet after being rescued, you again follow the same path of polytheism and disbelief" (قُلِ اللَّهُ يُنَجِّيكُمْ مِنْها وَ مِنْ كُلِّ كَرْبٍ ثُمَّ أَنْتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ).

Key Points

  1. The mention of "tadarru'" (تضرع), which means open supplication, and "khufyah" (خفية), which is secret prayer, may be because problems differ from one another. Sometimes, when a problem has not reached a peak of intensity, it invites man to secret prayer; but when it reaches a severe stage, he openly raises his hands in prayer and sometimes cries out and wails. The point is that Allah solves both your severe and your minor problems.

  2. Some believe that the verse refers to four psychological states of man, each of which is a type of reaction during the onset of problems: the state of "supplication and need," the state of "humility and submission," the state of "sincerity," and the state of "commitment to gratitude upon being saved from problems." Unfortunately, for many of these individuals, these precious states appear like a fleeting flash of lightning and almost in a state of desperation in the face of hardships. However, because they are not mixed with deep awareness, they fade away once the hardships are removed. Therefore, these states, even if fleeting, can serve as a proof for those who have drifted far from the recognition of God.

  3. "Karb" (کرب - rhyming with harb) originally means to turn over the soil or to dig it. It also refers to a tight knot tied in the rope of a bucket. Subsequently, it has been used for the grief and sorrow that turns over a person's heart and sits upon it like a knot. Therefore, the mention of the word "karb" in the above verse—which has a broad and expansive meaning and includes any significant problem—after the mention of "the darknesses of the land and sea"—which refers to a specific part of hardships—is an instance of mentioning a general concept after a specific one (take note).

In this context, it is appropriate to recall a Hadith mentioned under this verse in some Islamic commentaries. It is narrated from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) that he said: "The best supplication is the one done secretly (and with utmost sincerity), and the best provision is that which is sufficient" (not the accumulation of wealth that causes the deprivation of others and becomes a heavy burden on one's shoulders).

And in the continuation of this same Hadith, we read: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) passed by a group of people who had raised their voices in supplication. He said: 'You are not calling upon one who is deaf, nor one who is absent; rather, you are calling upon One who is All-Hearing and Near!'" [1] From this Hadith, it is understood that if prayer is quiet and mixed with more attention and sincerity, it is better.

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan and Nur al-Thaqalayn, under the aforementioned verse.

Ayah

قُلْ هُوَ ٱلْقَادِرُ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابًا مِّن فَوْقِكُمْ أَوْ مِن تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِكُمْ أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعًا وَيُذِيقَ بَعْضَكُم بَأْسَ بَعْضٍ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَفْقَهُونَ (٦٥)
Say, ‘He is able to send upon you a punishment from above you or from under your feet, or confound you as [hostile] factions, and make you taste one another’s violence.’ Look, how We paraphrase the signs variously so that they may understand! (65)

Commentary

Diverse Punishments

In the preceding verses, while explaining innate monotheism (Tawhid al-Fitri), there was effectively a form of encouragement and an expression of affection toward the servants—showing how Allah accepts them into His sanctuary during hardships and difficulties and grants their requests.

In this verse, to complete the various methods of upbringing (tarbiyati), the focus shifts to the issue of threatening with Divine torment and punishment. That is to say, just as Allah is the Most Merciful of the merciful and the refuge of the helpless, He is also the Subduer (al-Qahhar) and the Avenger against the rebellious and the defiant.

In this verse, the Prophet is commanded to threaten the criminals with three types of punishment: torments from above, torments from below, and the punishment of discord and the outbreak of war and bloodshed. Thus, He says:

"Say: He is the One Capable of sending upon you a punishment from above you or from beneath your feet" (قُلْ هُوَ الْقادِرُ عَلى‌ أَنْ يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذاباً مِنْ فَوْقِكُمْ أَوْ مِنْ تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِكُمْ‌).

"Or to confuse you into [separate] factions and make you taste the violence of one another" (أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعاً وَ يُذِيقَ بَعْضَكُمْ بَأْسَ بَعْضٍ‌).

And at the end of the verse, He adds: "See how We diversify the signs and proofs for them so that perhaps they may understand and return to the Truth" (انْظُرْ كَيْفَ نُصَرِّفُ الْآياتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَفْقَهُونَ‌).

Here, several points must be noted

1- There is discussion among commentators regarding what is meant by "punishment" from "above" and "below." However, it appears that these two words (fawq and taht) have a very broad meaning. They encompass both physical directions—meaning punishments such as lightning, dangerous downpours, and storms from above, and earthquakes, devastating fissures in the earth, and the flooding of rivers and seas from below.

Furthermore, it includes the painful torments that rain down upon certain nations from the ruling class and the upper strata of society, as well as the distress and suffering caused by irresponsible workers, which are sometimes no less than the first category of punishments.

It is also possible that the terrifying weapons of war in our era—which crush human life from the air and the ground in a horrific manner, turning the most prosperous cities into piles of ash through aerial bombardments, ground attacks, mining, and submarines in a short period—fall within the broad concept of this verse.

2- يَلْبِسَكُمْ (yalbisakum) is derived from the root labs (rhyming with habs), meaning confusion and mixing, not from the root libs (rhyming with qurs), which means wearing clothes. Therefore, the meaning of the sentence becomes: He is able to mix you together as different groups and factions [^1]. This expression shows that the issue of discord and fragmentation among a population is so dangerous that it is placed alongside heavenly punishments, lightning, and earthquakes. And indeed it is so; in fact, sometimes the destruction resulting from discord and fragmentation is degrees greater than the destruction caused by lightning and earthquakes. It has been seen repeatedly that prosperous countries have been led to absolute ruin under the ominous shadow of hypocrisy and division. This sentence is a warning to all the Muslims of the world!

There is also another possibility in the interpretation of this sentence: that Allah has mentioned two other punishments alongside the heavenly and earthly ones. One is the difference in belief and thought (which is effectively like punishments from above), and the other is differences in the realm of action and social methods that lead to war and bloodshed (which is similar to punishments from below). Thus, four types of natural punishments and two types of social punishments are alluded to in the verse.

3- Let there be no mistake: when the verse under discussion says Allah casts division among you, it does not mean that Allah afflicts people with hypocrisy and discord without reason. Rather, this is the result of people's evil deeds, selfishness, egoism, and personal greed, the consequence of which manifests as hypocrisy and division. Attributing it to Allah is because He is the One who placed such an effect within these ugly deeds.

[^1]: "Shi'an" is the plural of "Shi'ah," meaning a group.

4- Given that the address in these verses is to the polytheists and idolaters, we conclude the following: a polytheistic society that has deviated from the path of monotheism and the worship of the One God will be afflicted by both the tyranny and oppression of the upper classes and the irresponsibility of the lower classes, as well as differences in belief and bloody social conflicts. Just as the materialist societies of today's world, who prostrate only before the idols of industry and wealth, are afflicted by all these great calamities and are struggling within them.

We also know of nations that claim monotheism and the worship of God but are practically polytheists and idolaters; such nations will meet the same fate as the polytheists. The fact that we read in some reports that Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام said: "كل هذا فى اهل القبلة" ("All of this occurs among the people of the Qibla [Muslims]") may be an allusion to this very point.
