That is, whenever Muslims deviate from the path of monotheism, and selfishness and egoism replace Islamic brotherhood, and personal interests are prioritized over public interests, and everyone thinks only of themselves while Allah's commands are forgotten—they too will be afflicted by such a fate!

Ayahs

وَكَذَّبَ بِهِۦ قَوْمُكَ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ ۚ قُل لَّسْتُ عَلَيْكُم بِوَكِيلٍ (٦٦)
Your people have denied it, though it is the truth. Say, ‘It is not my business to watch over you.’ (66)
لِّكُلِّ نَبَإٍ مُّسْتَقَرٌّ ۚ وَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (٦٧)
For every prophecy there is a [preordained] setting, and soon you will know. (67)

Commentary

These two verses are, in fact, a completion of the discussion regarding the invitation toward Allah, the Resurrection, the truths of Islam, and the fear of divine punishment that passed in the preceding verses.

First, it says: "Your people and group—meaning the Quraysh and the people of Makkah—denied your teachings while they are all the truth, and various proofs from the path of reason, fitrah (innate nature), and sense confirm them" (وَ كَذَّبَ بِهِ قَوْمُكَ وَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ) [^1]. Therefore, their denial and rejection do not diminish the importance of these truths, no matter how numerous the opponents and deniers may be.

Then, He commands: "Tell them, my duty is only to convey the message, and I am not a guarantor of your acceptance" (قُلْ لَسْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِوَكِيلٍ).

From several verses in which this expression and its likes appear (such as Al-An'am 107, Yunus 108, Az-Zumar 41, and Ash-Shura 6), it is understood that the meaning of "Wakil" (guardian/trustee) in these instances is someone who is responsible for practical guidance and is a guarantor for others. In this way, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله announces to them: it is you who must make the final decision regarding the acceptance or rejection of the truth and embrace guidance; I am only commissioned to convey the message and the divine invitation.

In the next verse, with a short and meaningful sentence, He warns them and invites them to be careful in choosing the correct path, saying: "Every news that Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله have given you eventually has a place of settlement in this world or the next, and it will finally take place at the appointed time, and soon you will come to know" (لِكُلِّ نَبَإٍ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَ سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ) [^1].

[^1]: Some have referred the pronoun "به" (it) back to the Quran, and some specifically to the punishment mentioned in the previous verse; however, it appears that it refers to all of these and all the teachings of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله which were subject to the denial and rejection of the Prophet's enemies, and the final sentence of the verse is also a witness to this meaning.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخُوضُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَخُوضُوا۟ فِى حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِۦ ۚ وَإِمَّا يُنسِيَنَّكَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ فَلَا تَقْعُدْ بَعْدَ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٦٨)
When you see those who gossip impiously about Our signs, avoid them until they engage in some other discourse; but if Satan makes you forget, then, after remembering, do not sit with the wrongdoing lot. (68)
وَمَا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ مِنْ حِسَابِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ وَلَـٰكِن ذِكْرَىٰ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ (٦٩)
Those who are Godwary are in no way accountable for them, but this is merely for admonition’s sake, so that they may beware. (69)

Occasion of Revelation

In Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that when the first verse was revealed and the Muslims were prohibited from associating with the disbelievers and those who mock the Divine signs,

[^1]: "Mustaqarr" (مستقر) may be a mim-infinitive meaning "establishment," or it may refer to time and place, meaning "the place of establishment." In the first case, it informs of the actual realization of Divine promises, and in the second case, it informs of the time and place of these promises.

A group of Muslims said: "If we wish to act upon this command everywhere, we must never go to al-Masjid al-Haram or perform the circumambulation (Tawaf) of the House of Allah (because they are scattered in the corners of the Mosque and are engaged in vain talk regarding the Divine signs, and in every corner of al-Masjid al-Haram where we might stop briefly, it is possible that their words reach our ears)." At this point, the second verse was revealed and commanded the Muslims to advise them in such situations and to engage in guiding and directing them as much as lies within their power.

Mentioning the occasion of revelation for the above verse—as we have previously pointed out—does not contradict the fact that the entire Surah was revealed at once. This is because various events may occur in the lives of Muslims, and then a Surah is revealed all at once, even though several of its verses may address specific parts of those events.

Commentary

Avoiding the Gatherings of the People of Falsehood

Since the discussions in this Surah mostly address the condition of the polytheists and idolaters, these two verses point to another issue related to them. First, it says to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "When you see the stubborn and illogical opponents who mock the signs of Allah, turn away from them until they desist from this and engage in other talk" (وَ إِذا رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ يَخُوضُونَ فِي آياتِنا فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّى يَخُوضُوا فِي حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِ) [^1]

[^1]: "Khawd" (خوض), as Raghib says in the book al-Mufradat, originally means entering into water and walking (and swimming) in it, but later it was also applied to entering into other matters. However, in the Holy Quran, this word is mostly applied to entering into false and baseless matters.

Although the address in this sentence is to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, this ruling is certainly not exclusive to him and includes all believers. The philosophy of this ruling is also clear; for if the Muslims participated in their gatherings, they would continue their false and improper speech to take revenge and cause distress. However, when they are passed by with indifference, they will naturally fall silent and turn to other matters, because their entire goal was to distress the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims.

Then it adds that this matter is of such importance that "if Satan causes you to forget and you inadvertently sit with such persons, as soon as you become aware of the matter, immediately rise from that gathering and do not sit with these wrongdoers" (وَ إِمَّا يُنْسِيَنَّكَ الشَّيْطانُ فَلا تَقْعُدْ بَعْدَ الذِّكْرى‌ مَعَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ) [^1]

Two Questions

Two questions arise here. First: Is it possible for Satan to gain mastery over the Prophet and cause him to forget? In other words, despite the station of infallibility (Ismah) and protection from error even in mundane subjects, is it possible for the Prophet to be afflicted by mistake and forgetfulness?! In response to this question, it can be said that although the address in the verse is to the Prophet,

[^1]: It perhaps does not need mentioning that the meaning of "do not sit" (لا تقعد) is not that only sitting with such persons is forbidden; rather, the goal is participating in their assembly, whether it be in the form of sitting, standing, or while walking.

In reality, the intended audience is his followers, who, if they are afflicted by forgetfulness and participate in the sin-filled gatherings of the disbelievers, must rise and leave as soon as they realize. Similar to this discussion is seen in our daily conversations and in the literature of various languages, where a person addresses someone but his goal is for others to hear, according to the famous Arabic proverb:

"I mean you, but listen, O neighbor." (اياك اعنى و اسمعى يا جاره)

Some commentators, such as Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan and Abu al-Futuh in his famous Tafsir, have given another answer, the summary of which is: oversight and forgetfulness regarding the laws and in the station of messengership from Allah is not permissible for the Prophets, but in mundane subjects, provided it does not lead to the misguidance of the people, there is no obstacle. However, this answer does not align with what is famous among our theologians—that the Prophets and Imams are infallible and protected from mistake in ordinary subjects in addition to the religious laws.

Another Question

Some Sunni scholars have taken this verse as evidence for the impermissibility of Taqiyyah (precautionary dissimulation) for religious leaders, because the verse explicitly says: do not practice Taqiyyah before the enemies, and even if you are in their gathering, rise from it.

Answer

This objection is also clear, for the Shi'a never say that Taqiyyah must be practiced everywhere; rather, Taqiyyah is definitely forbidden in some cases. Its obligation is limited to cases where Taqiyyah and the omission of declaring the truth have benefits greater than declaring it, or where it leads to averting a general harm or danger.

In the next verse, one case is exempted, saying: "If the God-fearing individuals participate in their gatherings for the sake of forbidding evil, and remind them in the hope of their piety and return from sin, there is no obstacle, and their sins will not be written against such persons, because in any case, their intention was service and the fulfillment of duty" (وَ ما عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ مِنْ حِسابِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ وَ لكِنْ ذِكْرى‌ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ). Another interpretation has been mentioned for this verse, but what we have said is more consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse and also with its occasion of revelation. Additionally, it must be noted that only those can benefit from this exception who, according to the expression of the verse, possess the station of Taqwa and piety, and not only are they not influenced by them, but they are also able to influence them.

Under verse 140 of Surah an-Nisa, we also had a theme similar to the above verse, and other points were explained in that regard. [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 4, page 172.

Ayah

وَذَرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ لَعِبًا وَلَهْوًا وَغَرَّتْهُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَذَكِّرْ بِهِۦٓ أَن تُبْسَلَ نَفْسٌۢ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ لَيْسَ لَهَا مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِىٌّ وَلَا شَفِيعٌ وَإِن تَعْدِلْ كُلَّ عَدْلٍ لَّا يُؤْخَذْ مِنْهَآ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُبْسِلُوا۟ بِمَا كَسَبُوا۟ ۖ لَهُمْ شَرَابٌ مِّنْ حَمِيمٍ وَعَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ (٧٠)
Leave alone those who take their religion for play and diversion and whom the life of this world has deceived, and admonish with it, lest any soul should perish because of what it has earned: It shall not have any guardian besides Allah, nor any intercessor; and though it should offer every kind of ransom, it shall not be accepted from it. They are the ones who perish because of what they have earned; they shall have boiling water for drink and a painful punishment because of what they used to defy. (70)

Commentary

Those Who Have Taken the True Religion as a Plaything

This verse, in fact, completes the discussion of the previous verse and commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Leave those who have taken their religion as a play and amusement, and for whom a handful of games and pastimes count as religion, and whom the life of this world and its material possibilities have deceived" (وَ ذَرِ الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا دِينَهُمْ لَعِباً وَ لَهْواً وَ غَرَّتْهُمُ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا). It is evident that the command to leave such individuals does not contradict the issue of Jihad, for Jihad has its own conditions, and indifference toward the disbelievers has other conditions and circumstances; each of these two must be carried out in its proper place. Sometimes it is necessary to crush the opponents with indifference, and sometimes through struggle, Jihad, and drawing weapons. The notion held by some that the verses of Jihad have abrogated the above verse is completely baseless.

In reality, the above verse points out that their creed is hollow and vain in terms of content, and they have placed the name of "religion" upon a collection of actions that are more akin to the play of children and the amusements of adults. Such individuals are not worthy of discussion or debate; therefore, He commands to turn away from them and pay no heed to them or their hollow religion.

From what we have stated, it becomes clear that "their religion" (دِينَهُمْ) refers to the creed of polytheism and idol-worship that they held. The possibility that it refers to the "True Religion," and that the attribution of the religion to them is because religion is innate (fitrah), seems very unlikely.

There is another possibility in the interpretation of the verse: that the Quran is referring to a group of disbelievers who viewed even their own creed as a game and amusement and never thought about it as a serious matter. That is, they were faithless even in their lack of faith and were not loyal even to the foundations of their own baseless religion.

In any case, the verse is not exclusive to the disbelievers; it includes all those who make the sanctities and divine laws a plaything to achieve material and personal goals, making religion a tool for the world and the command of Allah a toy for personal motives.

Then, He commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to "warn them through it [the Quran] against these actions, for a day will come when every soul shall be held hostage by its own deeds and will have no way to escape from their grasp" (وَ ذَكِّرْ بِهِ أَنْ تُبْسَلَ نَفْسٌ بِما كَسَبَتْ) 1.

1: "Tubsal" (تُبْسَلَ) is originally derived from the root "basl" (بسل), meaning to hold back or prevent something through force and dominance. For this reason, forcing someone to surrender is called "ibsal," and it is also applied to punishment or taking a hostage. "Jayshun basil" (لشگر باسل), meaning a "brave army," is also related to this, as it forces others to surrender through power and dominance. In the above verse, it means the surrender of a person and their entrapment in the clutches of their own evil deeds.

"And on that day, besides Allah, it shall have neither protector nor helper, nor any intercessor" (لَيْسَ لَها مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ وَلِيٌّ وَ لا شَفِيعٌ).

Their situation on that day is so difficult and painful, and they are so entangled in the chains of their own deeds, that "even if it offers every [possible] ransom (supposing they had it and could pay it) to save itself from punishment, it will not be accepted from it" (وَ إِنْ تَعْدِلْ كُلَّ عَدْلٍ لا يُؤْخَذْ مِنْها) 1. This is because "they have been held hostage by their own deeds" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ أُبْسِلُوا بِما كَسَبُوا); neither is the path of compensation open on that day, nor is it the time for repentance. For this reason, no way of salvation can be imagined for them.

Then, He points to a part of their painful punishments, saying: "They shall have a drink of boiling water and a painful torment because of their rejection of the truth and reality" (لَهُمْ شَرابٌ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ بِما كانُوا يَكْفُرُونَ).

They shall burn from within by the boiling water and from without by the fire! A point that must be noted is that the sentence "Those are the ones who are held hostage by what they earned" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ أُبْسِلُوا بِما كَسَبُوا) is, in fact, the reason and cause for the non-acceptance of ransom and the absence of any protector or intercessor for them. That is, their punishment is not due to an external factor that could be repelled in some way; rather, it stems from within their own essence, attributes, and actions. They are prisoners of their own ugly deeds; for this reason, liberation is impossible for them, because separation from one's deeds and their effects is like separation from one's own self.

However, it must be noted that this intensity, severity, and the absence of a way back or intercession is specific to those who insisted on disbelief and continued in it, as is understood from the phrase "because they used to disbelieve" (بِما كانُوا يَكْفُرُونَ), since the present continuous verb (mudari') denotes the continuation of an action.

Ayahs

قُلْ أَنَدْعُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُنَا وَلَا يَضُرُّنَا وَنُرَدُّ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْقَابِنَا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ كَٱلَّذِى ٱسْتَهْوَتْهُ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ حَيْرَانَ لَهُۥٓ أَصْحَـٰبٌ يَدْعُونَهُۥٓ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَى ٱئْتِنَا ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ هُوَ ٱلْهُدَىٰ ۖ وَأُمِرْنَا لِنُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٧١)
Say, ‘Shall we invoke besides Allah that which can neither benefit us nor harm us, and turn back on our heels after Allah has guided us, like someone seduced by the devils and bewildered on the earth, who has companions that invite him to guidance, [saying,] ‘‘Come to us!’’?’ Say, ‘Indeed it is the guidance of Allah which is [true] guidance. And we have been commanded to submit to the Lord of all the worlds, (71)
وَأَنْ أَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَٱتَّقُوهُ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (٧٢)
and that ‘‘Maintain the prayer and be wary of Him, and it is He toward whom you will be gathered.’’ ’ (72)

Commentary

In response to the insistence of the polytheists inviting Muslims to disbelief and idolatry, this verse commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to answer them with a crushing proof and to ask them in the form of a rhetorical question: "Do you say that we should take as a partner to Allah something that neither benefits us—such that we would seek it for its profit—nor harms us—such that we would fear its injury?!" (قُلْ أَنَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنْفَعُنَا وَلَا يَضُرُّنَا).

This sentence, in reality, points to the fact that human actions usually stem from one of two sources: either to gain a benefit (whether spiritual or material) or to repel a harm (whether spiritual or material). How can a rational person perform an act in which neither of these two factors exists?

Then, he employs another argument against the polytheists, saying: "If we return to idolatry and step onto the path of polytheism after Divine guidance, we would be turning back on our heels," and this is contrary to the law of evolution, which is the general law of the world of life (وَنُرَدُّ عَلَىٰ أَعْقَابِنَا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَانَا اللَّهُ) [^1]. Afterward, he clarifies the matter with an example, stating that returning from monotheism to polytheism "is like one whom the devils (or desert ghouls, according to the superstition of the pre-Islamic Arabs who imagined they lay in wait on the roads to lure travelers into the wilderness!) have caused to lose the way to his destination, leaving him bewildered and wandering in the desert" (كَالَّذِي اسْتَهْوَتْهُ الشَّيَاطِينُ فِي الْأَرْضِ حَيْرَانَ).

"While he has companions who call him to guidance and the main road, shouting: 'Come to us!'" but he is so bewildered and wandering that it is as if he does not hear their words or is unable to make a decision (لَهُ أَصْحَابٌ يَدْعُونَهُ إِلَى الْهُدَى ائْتِنَا) [^1]. At the end of the verse, He commands the Prophet to say explicitly: "Guidance is only the guidance of Allah, and we are commanded to submit only to the Lord of the worlds" (قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى اللَّهِ هُوَ الْهُدَىٰ وَأُمِرْنَا لِنُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ).

This sentence is, in fact, another proof for the rejection of the polytheists' religion, because one must only submit to Him who is the Owner, Creator, and Nurturer of the universe, not to idols who have no role in the creation or the will of this world.

[^1]: "A'qab" is the plural of "Aqib" (on the weight of khashin), meaning the heel of the foot. Returning on one's heels means a backward turn and is a metaphor for deviation and retreating from the goal; it is the same thing that is referred to today as "reactionism" (irtija').

[^1]: "Istahwat-hu" is derived from the root "hawa," and this sentence means compelling someone to follow whims and desires. "Hayran" linguistically means coming and going and is usually a metaphor for bewilderment, because bewildered individuals walk a bit and then return. Therefore, the above verse likens those who return from faith to polytheism to bewildered followers of whims who receive their life programs from Satan.

Question

A question arises here: Was the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله a follower of the polytheists' religion before his mission that he says: "Should we return on our heels" (نُرَدُّ عَلَىٰ أَعْقَابِنَا)? While we know that he never prostrated before an idol, and no history has recorded such a thing; fundamentally, the station of Infallibility (Ismah) does not permit such a thing.

Answer

This sentence is actually spoken on behalf of the community of Muslims, not from the tongue of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله personally; hence, it is expressed with plural forms and pronouns.

In the next verse, He describes the continuation of the Divine call as follows: that beyond monotheism, "we are commanded to establish prayer and be mindful of Him (attain Taqwa)" (وَأَنْ أَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَاتَّقُوهُ). And finally, He concludes this discussion by pointing to the issue of the Resurrection and that your gathering and return is toward Allah (وَهُوَ الَّذِي إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ).

In truth, in these few short sentences, the program to which the Prophet was inviting—which stems from the judgment of reason and the command of Allah—is presented as a four-point program: its beginning is monotheism, its end is the Resurrection, and its intermediate stages are strengthening Divine bonds and abstaining from every kind of sin.

Ayah

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَيَوْمَ يَقُولُ كُن فَيَكُونُ ۚ قَوْلُهُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۚ وَلَهُ ٱلْمُلْكُ يَوْمَ يُنفَخُ فِى ٱلصُّورِ ۚ عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ (٧٣)
It is He who created the heavens and the earth with reason, and the day He says [to something], ‘Be!’ it is. His word is the truth, and to Him belongs all sovereignty on the day when the Trumpet will be blown. Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, He is the All-wise, the All-aware. (73)

Commentary

This verse is, in fact, a proof for the matters discussed in the previous verse and a reason for the necessity of submission to the Lord and following His leadership. Therefore, it first states: "He is the One who created the heavens and the earth in truth" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِ‌).

Only such a Being, who is the origin of the universe, is worthy of leadership, and one must submit only to His command, for He has created everything for a correct purpose.

The meaning of "truth" (حق) in the above sentence refers to the purpose, result, benefits, and wisdom; meaning He created everything for the sake of a goal, a result, and a benefit. In fact, this sentence is similar to what appears in Surah Sad, verse 27, where He says: وَ ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما باطِلًا: "And We did not create the heaven and the earth and that between them aimlessly."

Then He states: Not only is He the origin of the universe, but the Resurrection and Judgment Day also take place by His command: "And the day He says, 'Be,' and it is" (وَ يَوْمَ يَقُولُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ‌) [^1]. Some have suggested that this sentence refers to the beginning of creation and the origin of the universe, where everything was brought into existence by His command. However, considering that

[^1]: Regarding what "Yawm" (Day)—which according to grammatical rules is an adverb of time—relates to, there is discussion among commentators. Some relate it to the verb "Khalaqa" (created) and some to the omitted verb "Udhkuru" (remember), but it is not unlikely that it relates to "Yakunu" (it is/becomes), and the meaning of the sentence would be: "The Resurrection shall be on the day Allah says to it, 'Be'."

"Yaqulu" (He says) is a present/future tense verb, and that the beginning of creation was mentioned prior to this sentence, and also considering the subsequent sentences, it can be said that this sentence pertains to the Resurrection and the Divine command regarding it. As we mentioned in Volume 1, page 299 (under verse 117 of Al-Baqarah), the meaning of the phrase "Kun Fayakun" (Be, and it is) is not that Allah issues a verbal command such as "exist"; rather, it means that whenever His will pertains to the creation of something, His will is implemented without the need for any other factor. If He wills for it to exist all at once, it will exist all at once, and if He wills for it to exist gradually, its gradual program will begin.

Then it adds that "His word is the truth," meaning that just as the beginning of creation was based on purpose, result, and benefit, the Resurrection will be the same (قَوْلُهُ الْحَقُ‌).

"And on the day the Trumpet is blown and the Resurrection is established, the sovereignty and ownership belong specifically to His Pure Essence" (وَ لَهُ الْمُلْكُ يَوْمَ يُنْفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ).

It is true that Allah's ownership and sovereignty over the entire universe have existed from the beginning of the world and will continue until the end of the world and into the realm of the Hereafter, and it is not exclusive to the Day of Resurrection. However, since in this world a series of factors and causes are effective in advancing goals and performing tasks, these factors and causes sometimes make man heedless of Allah, who is the Cause of causes. But on that day, when all these causes cease to function, His ownership and sovereignty become more manifest and clear than ever before. This is exactly like another verse that says: لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ: "To whom belongs sovereignty this Day? To Allah, the One, the Prevailing" (Surah Ghafir, verse 16).

Regarding what is meant by the "Trumpet" (Sur) that is blown into, and how Israfil (عليه السلام) blows into the Trumpet so that the inhabitants of the world die, and blows into it again so that all are resurrected and the Day of Judgment begins, we will discuss this in detail, God willing, under verse 68 of Surah Az-Zumar, as it is more appropriate for that verse.

And at the end of the verse, it refers to three of Allah's attributes, saying: "He is the Knower of the unseen and the witnessed" (عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَ الشَّهادَةِ).

"And all His actions are based on wisdom, and He is aware of all things" (وَ هُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْخَبِيرُ).

In verses related to the Resurrection, these attributes of Allah are often mentioned—that He is both All-Knowing and All-Powerful and Wise. This means that by virtue of His knowledge, He knows the deeds of His servants, and by virtue of His power and wisdom, He gives everyone the appropriate recompense.

Ayah

۞ وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ لِأَبِيهِ ءَازَرَ أَتَتَّخِذُ أَصْنَامًا ءَالِهَةً ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكَ وَقَوْمَكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٧٤)
When Abraham said to Azar, his father, ‘Do you take idols for gods? Indeed I see you and your people in manifest error.’ (74)

Commentary

Since this Surah has an aspect of combating polytheism and idol-worship, and its discourse is primarily addressed to idolaters—utilizing various methods to awaken them—it refers here to a portion of the life of Ibrahim (peace be upon him), the hero of idol-breaking, and recalls his powerful logic in crushing idols within several verses.

It is noteworthy that the Quran relies on this narrative in many discussions regarding Monotheism (Tawhid) and the struggle against idols, because Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was respected by all groups, especially the Arab polytheists.

First, it states that Ibrahim (peace be upon him) reproached his father (uncle) and said to them: "Have you taken these worthless idols and lifeless beings as your gods?" (وَ إِذْ قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ لِأَبِيهِ آزَرَ أَ تَتَّخِذُ أَصْناماً آلِهَةً).

"Undoubtedly, I see you and your followers and associates in manifest error." What error could be more manifest than for a human to make his own creation his deity, to consider a lifeless and senseless being as his refuge, and to seek the solution to his problems from them? (إِنِّي أَراكَ وَ قَوْمَكَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ).

Was Azar the Father of Ibrahim?

The word "Ab" (أب) in the Arabic language is most often applied to the biological father, and as we shall see, it is sometimes applied to the maternal grandfather, the uncle, as well as the guardian, teacher, and those who have undergone trouble for a person's upbringing. However, there is no doubt that when this word is used without any accompanying context, "father" is the first meaning that comes to mind.

Now the question arises: Does the above verse truly say that the idol-worshipping man (Azar) was Ibrahim's father? And can an idol-worshipper and idol-maker be the father of an Ulul-'Azm (Arch-Prophet)? Furthermore, would heredity not leave an undesirable effect on the child's disposition?

A group of Sunni commentators have answered the first question affirmatively and consider Azar to be Ibrahim's biological father. In contrast, all Shia commentators and scholars believe that Azar was not Ibrahim's father; some have considered him his maternal grandfather, and many have considered him Ibrahim's uncle.

The evidences that support the view of Shia scholars are several:

  1. In none of the historical sources is the name of Ibrahim's father listed as "Azar"; rather, they have all written "Terah" (تارخ). This same name appears in the books of the Bible. Interestingly, those who insist that Ibrahim's father was Azar have resorted to justifications here that are by no means acceptable, such as claiming that Ibrahim's father's name was Terah and his title was Azar! This is while this title is also not mentioned in historical sources.

Alternatively, they suggest that Azar was an idol that Ibrahim's father worshipped, but this possibility is entirely inconsistent with the apparent meaning of the verse above, which says his Ab was Azar, unless we assume a word or phrase is omitted, which is contrary to the apparent meaning (Zahir).

  1. The Holy Quran states that Muslims do not have the right to seek forgiveness for polytheists, even if they are their relatives and kin. Then, so that no one uses Ibrahim's seeking of forgiveness for Azar as a pretext, it says: وَ ما كانَ اسْتِغْفارُ إِبْراهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ إِلَّا عَنْ مَوْعِدَةٍ وَعَدَها إِيَّاهُ فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ أَنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ لِلَّهِ تَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُ (Surah At-Tawbah, Verse 114): "And Ibrahim's seeking forgiveness for his father was only because of a promise he had made to him (where he said: سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي—Surah Maryam, Verse 47, meaning: 'I will soon seek forgiveness for you from my Lord'—hoping that with this promise he would be encouraged and turn away from idol-worship). But when it became clear to him that he was an enemy to Allah, he disassociated himself from him."

From this verse, it is clearly understood that after losing hope in Azar, Ibrahim never again sought forgiveness for him, nor was it appropriate to do so. All evidence indicates that this event occurred during Ibrahim's youth and the time he lived in the city of Babylon and struggled against idolaters.

However, other verses of the Quran show that Ibrahim, at the end of his life and after completing the construction of the Kaaba, sought forgiveness from Allah for his father (of course, in these verses, as will be shown, the term "Ab" is not used for the father, but rather the term "Walid" (والد), which explicitly conveys the concept of the biological father).

Where He says: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي وَهَبَ لِي عَلَى الْكِبَرِ إِسْماعِيلَ وَ إِسْحاقَ إِنَّ رَبِّي لَسَمِيعُ الدُّعاءِ ... رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لِي وَ لِوالِدَيَّ وَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْحِسابُ "Praise be to Allah, who has granted me in old age Ishmael and Isaac. Indeed, my Lord is the Hearer of supplication... Our Lord! Forgive me and my parents and the believers on the Day the account is established." [^1]

[^1]: Surah Ibrahim, Verses 39 and 41.

By joining this verse with the verse from Surah At-Tawbah—which warns Muslims against seeking forgiveness for polytheists and describes Ibrahim as refraining from such an act except for a limited period for a sacred purpose—it is clearly understood that the meaning of "Ab" in the verse under discussion is not the biological father, but rather an uncle or maternal grandfather or the like. In other words, "Walid" is explicit in the meaning of biological father, whereas "Ab" is not.

In the verses of the Quran, the word "Ab" has also been used for an uncle, such as in Verse 133 of Surah Al-Baqarah: قالُوا نَعْبُدُ إِلهَكَ وَ إِلهَ آبائِكَ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ إِسْماعِيلَ وَ إِسْحاقَ إِلهاً واحِداً: "The children of Ya'qub said to him: 'We will worship your God and the God of your fathers, Ibrahim, Isma'il, and Ishaq—the One God.'" And we know that Isma'il was the uncle of Ya'qub, not his father.

  1. This subject can also be inferred from various Islamic narrations, as it is mentioned in a famous Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"لم يزل ينقلنى اللَّه من اصلاب الطاهرين الى ارحام المطهرات حتى اخرجنى فى عالمكم هذا لم يدنسنى بدنس الجاهلية"

"Allah continued to transfer me from the loins of pure men to the wombs of pure women until He brought me forth into this world of yours; He never defiled me with the defilements of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah)." [^1] There is no doubt that the most obvious defilement of the Age of Ignorance is polytheism and idol-worship, and those who have restricted it solely to the defilement of adultery have no evidence for their claim, especially since the Quran says: إِنَّمَا الْمُشْرِكُونَ نَجَسٌ: "The polytheists are indeed unclean." [^1]

Tabari, who is among the Sunni scholars, narrates in his commentary Jami' al-Bayan from the famous commentator Mujahid, who explicitly states: "Azar was not Ibrahim's father." [^2] Alusi, another Sunni commentator, says in the commentary Ruh al-Ma'ani under this same verse: "Those who say that the belief that Azar was not Ibrahim's father is exclusive to the Shia do so out of their lack of information, for many scholars believe that Azar was the name of Ibrahim's uncle." [^3] Suyuti, the famous Sunni scholar, in the book Masalik al-Hunafa, quotes from Fakhr al-Razi in the book Asrar al-Tanzil that the father, mother, and ancestors of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله were never polytheists, and he argued using the Hadith we quoted above from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Then Suyuti himself adds that we can prove this reality by considering two sets of Islamic narrations: first, narrations stating that the fathers and ancestors of the Prophet up to Adam were each the best individual of their time (he quoted these Hadiths from Sahih Bukhari, Dala'il al-Nubuwwah by Bayhaqi, and the like).

And narrations stating that in every age and time, monotheistic and God-worshipping individuals have existed. By combining these two sets of narrations, it is proven that the ancestors of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, including (Ibrahim's father), were certainly monotheists. [^4]

[^1]: This narration has been quoted by many Shia and Sunni commentators, such as the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Nishaburi in Ghara'ib al-Quran, Fakhr al-Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir, and Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani. [^2]: Jami' al-Bayan, Vol. 7, p. 158. [^3]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 7, p. 169. [^4]: Masalik al-Hunafa, p. 17 (as quoted in the footnotes of Bihar al-Anwar, new edition, Vol. 15, p. 118 onwards).

Considering what has been said, it becomes clear that the above interpretation for the verse under discussion is an interpretation based on clear evidences from the Quran itself and various Islamic narrations, not an interpretation based on personal opinion (Tafsir bi-al-Ra'y), as some biased Sunni writers like the author of Al-Manar have claimed.

Ayahs

وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُرِىٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُوقِنِينَ (٧٥)
Thus did We show Abraham the dominions of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certitude. (75)
فَلَمَّا جَنَّ عَلَيْهِ ٱلَّيْلُ رَءَا كَوْكَبًا ۖ قَالَ هَـٰذَا رَبِّى ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَفَلَ قَالَ لَآ أُحِبُّ ٱلْـَٔافِلِينَ (٧٦)
When night darkened over him, he saw a star and said, ‘This is my Lord!’ But when it set, he said, ‘I do not like those who set.’ (76)
فَلَمَّا رَءَا ٱلْقَمَرَ بَازِغًا قَالَ هَـٰذَا رَبِّى ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَفَلَ قَالَ لَئِن لَّمْ يَهْدِنِى رَبِّى لَأَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (٧٧)
Then, when he saw the moon rising, he said, ‘This is my Lord!’ But when it set, he said, ‘Had my Lord not guided me, I would surely have been among the astray lot.’ (77)
فَلَمَّا رَءَا ٱلشَّمْسَ بَازِغَةً قَالَ هَـٰذَا رَبِّى هَـٰذَآ أَكْبَرُ ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَفَلَتْ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ (٧٨)
Then, when he saw the sun rising, he said, ‘This is my Lord! This is bigger!’ But when it set, he said, ‘O my people, indeed I disown what you take as [His] partners.’ (78)
إِنِّى وَجَّهْتُ وَجْهِىَ لِلَّذِى فَطَرَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ حَنِيفًا ۖ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (٧٩)
‘Indeed I have turned my face toward Him who originated the heavens and the earth, as a Hanif, and I am not one of the polytheists.’ (79)

Commentary

Evidences of Monotheism in the Heavens

Following the reproach Abraham (peace be upon him) gave regarding the idols and the invitation he extended to Azar to abandon idol worship, in these verses, Allah refers to Abraham’s logical struggles against various groups of idolaters and explains how he arrived at the principle of Monotheism (Tawhid) through clear intellectual arguments.

First, He says: "Just as We made Abraham aware of the harms of idol worship, We also showed him the absolute ownership and dominion of the Lord over all the heavens and the earth" (وَ كَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ) [^1]. Malakut is originally from the root mulk (on the pattern of hukm), meaning "governance and ownership." The letters "waw" and "ta" are added to it for emphasis and exaggeration; therefore, what is meant here is the absolute sovereignty of Allah over the entire universe.

This verse seems to be a summary of the details mentioned in the subsequent verses regarding the observation of the sun, moon, and stars, and deducing their status as created beings from their setting. That is, the Quran first mentions a summary of those events and then proceeds to explain them; in this way, the meaning of showing the malakut of the heavens and the earth to Abraham becomes clear.

At the end of the verse, He says: "Our goal was that Abraham might be of those who possess certainty" (وَ لِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ). There is no doubt that Abraham had intellectual and innate certainty in the oneness of God, but through studying the secrets of creation, this certainty reached the level of perfection—just as he had faith in the Resurrection, but by witnessing the slaughtered birds coming back to life, his faith reached the stage of "Eye of Certainty" (Ayn al-Yaqin).

In the following verses, this subject is explained in detail, clarifying Abraham’s reasoning from the fading and setting of the stars and the sun against their divinity. First, it says: "When the dark curtain of night covered the world, a star manifested itself before his eyes. Abraham called out: 'Is this my Lord?!' But when it set, he said with total decisiveness: 'I love not those that set,'" and I do not consider them worthy of worship or Lordship (فَلَمَّا جَنَّ عَلَيْهِ اللَّيْلُ رَأى‌ كَوْكَباً قالَ هذا رَبِّي فَلَمَّا أَفَلَ قالَ لا أُحِبُّ الْآفِلِينَ).

Once again, he cast his gaze upon the expanse of the sky; this time, the silver disc of the moon appeared with its pleasant glow and brilliance. "When he saw the moon rising, Abraham called out: 'Is this my Lord?' But eventually, the moon met the same fate as the star and drew its face behind the curtain of the horizon. The seeker, Abraham, said: 'If my Lord does not guide me to Himself, I shall surely be among the people who are astray!'" (فَلَمَّا رَأَى الْقَمَرَ بازِغاً قالَ هذا رَبِّي فَلَمَّا أَفَلَ قالَ لَئِنْ لَمْ يَهْدِنِي رَبِّي لَأَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الضَّالِّينَ).

At this time, the night had come to an end, gathering its dark curtains and fleeing from the scene of the sky. The sun emerged from the eastern horizon, spreading its beautiful and delicate light like a golden fabric over the mountains, plains, and deserts. "As soon as Abraham’s truth-seeking eye fell upon its dazzling light, he called out: 'Is this my God?! This is greater and more radiant!' But with the setting of the sun and the sinking of its disc into the mouth of the monster of night, Abraham uttered his final word and said: 'O my people, I am free from all these fabricated deities that you associate with Allah'" (فَلَمَّا رَأَى الشَّمْسَ بازِغَةً قالَ هذا رَبِّي هذا أَكْبَرُ فَلَمَّا أَفَلَتْ قالَ يا قَوْمِ إِنِّي بَرِي‌ءٌ مِمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ).

"Now that I have understood that beyond these changing, limited creatures—captives in the grip of the laws of nature—there is a God Who is Powerful and Sovereign over the system of the universe, I turn my face toward Him Who created the heavens and the earth, and I allow not the slightest polytheism into this belief of mine; I am a pure monotheist and I am not of the polytheists" (إِنِّي وَجَّهْتُ وَجْهِيَ لِلَّذِي فَطَرَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ حَنِيفاً وَ ما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ).

Regarding the interpretation of this verse and the subsequent ones—and how Abraham, the monotheist, pointed to a star in the sky saying, "This is my Lord"—commentators have discussed much. Among all interpretations, two are most noteworthy, each chosen by some great commentators, and there are evidences for them in Hadith sources:

First: Abraham personally wanted to contemplate the knowledge of God and find the Deity he felt in the depths of his soul through his pure primordial nature (fitrah). He had known God through the light of fitrah and general intellectual reasoning, and all his expressions show he had no doubt in His existence. However, he was searching for His true manifestation—or rather, he knew His true manifestation but wanted to reach the stage of "Truth of Certainty" (Haqq al-Yaqin) through clearer intellectual arguments. This event occurred before his prophethood, likely at the beginning of maturity or before it.

In some narrations and histories, we read that this was the first time Abraham’s eyes fell upon the stars, seeing the indigo expanse of the night sky with its bright lamps, because his mother had raised him inside a cave since infancy out of fear of the tyrant Nimrod’s regime. However, it seems very unlikely that a human would live inside a cave for years without stepping out even once on a dark night. Perhaps some favored this possibility because of the phrase رَأى‌ كَوْكَباً (he saw a star), implying he had not seen a star until then. But this expression by no means carries such a meaning; rather, it means that although he had seen the stars, moon, and sun many times before, this was the first time he looked at them as a researcher of Monotheism, contemplating the connection between their fading and setting and the negation of their divinity. In truth, Abraham had seen them many times, but not with this eye.

Therefore, when Abraham says hadha rabbi (this is my Lord), it is not as a definitive statement, but as a hypothesis and assumption for reflection and thought. It is exactly like when we want to investigate the cause of an event: we study all possibilities and hypotheses one by one and examine the implications of each to find the true cause. Such a thing is neither disbelief nor does it even indicate a lack of faith; rather, it is a way for further research, better identification, and reaching higher stages of faith—just as in the case of the "Resurrection," Abraham sought further investigation to reach the stage of witnessing and the tranquility resulting from it.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 5, p. 312

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, it is narrated from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Imam al-Baqir or al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "Abraham said this as an investigation and his words were never disbelief; and whoever among the people says such a thing for the purpose of reflection and investigation is in the same position as Abraham." [^1] Two other narrations on this subject are also cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn.

The second interpretation is that Abraham spoke these words during a debate with star-worshippers and sun-worshippers. This likely occurred after his fierce struggles against idolaters in Babylon and his departure from that land toward Sham (Levant), where he encountered these groups. Abraham, having tested the stubbornness of ignorant people in their wrong ways in Babylon, first harmonized his voice with them to draw the attention of the worshippers of the sun, moon, and stars. He said to the star-worshippers: "You say this star (Venus) is my Lord; very well, let it be so that I may eventually explain this belief to you." It was not long before the radiant face of the star was hidden behind the dark curtain of the horizon. Here, the strong weapon fell into Abraham’s hands, and he said: "I can never accept such a deity."

Therefore, the phrase "this is my Lord" means "according to your belief, this is my Lord," or he said it as a question: "Is this my Lord?" In this regard, a Hadith is also narrated in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn and other commentaries from Uyun Akhbar al-Rida عليه السلام.

Abraham’s Method of Reasoning for Monotheism

Now the question arises: how did Abraham reason from the setting of the sun, moon, and stars to negate their Lordship? This reasoning could be from three paths:

  1. The Lord and Nurturer of beings (as understood from the word Rabb) must always have a close connection with His creatures and not be separated from them for even a moment. Therefore, how can a being that sets, withdrawing its light and blessing for hours and becoming completely estranged from many creatures, be their Lord and Rabb?!

  2. A being that has rising and setting is a captive in the grip of laws. How can something that is itself subject to these laws be the ruler over them and their owner? It is itself a weak creature, submissive to their command, and lacks the ability for the slightest deviation or violation of them.

  3. A being that possesses motion must certainly be a "contingent/originated" (hadith) being. As explained in detail in philosophy, motion everywhere is evidence of origination, because motion itself is a type of originated existence. Something subject to changes—meaning it possesses motion—cannot be an eternal and everlasting existence (reflect on this).

Several points should be noted here

  1. In the first verse discussed, the word kadhalika (thus/in this way) draws attention. Its meaning is: just as We clarified the harms of idol worship for Abraham’s intellect and reason, We also showed him the sovereignty and ownership of God over the heavens and the earth. Some commentators have said it means: just as We showed you the signs of Our power and governance over the heavens, We showed them to Abraham as well, so that through them he might become more acquainted with God.

  2. Janna (from the root j-n-n) means to cover something. In the verse under discussion, the meaning of the phrase is: "when the night covered the faces of beings from Abraham..." The reason a madman is called majnun is that it is as if a veil has been drawn over his intellect. The term jinn for unseen beings is for the same reason. A janin (fetus) is so called because it is hidden inside the mother's womb. A jannat (paradise/garden) is so called because its ground is covered beneath trees. The heart is called janan because it is hidden within the chest or because it keeps human secrets hidden.

  3. There is discussion among commentators as to which star was meant by kawkaba (a star). Most commentators mention Venus or Jupiter. Some histories indicate that both these stars were worshipped in ancient times and were considered among the alihah (gods). However, in a Hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام in Uyun al-Akhbar, it is explicitly stated that this star was Venus. In Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim, this is also narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام. [^1] Some commentators have said that the people of Chaldea and Babylon, where Abraham began his struggle against idolaters, recognized each of the planets as the creator or "Lord of the Species" (Rabb al-Naw') of certain beings: "Mars" as the lord of war, "Jupiter" as the lord of justice and knowledge, "Mercury" as the lord of ministers... and the "Sun" as the king of all! [^2]

  4. Bazigh is from the root bazgh, which originally means to slit and cause blood to flow; hence, the surgery performed by a veterinarian (bitar) is called bazgh. Applying this word to the rising of the sun or moon is actually a beautiful metaphor, for the sun and moon, upon rising, seem to slit the curtain of darkness. Furthermore, they create a faint redness near the horizon that is not unlike the color of blood.

  5. Fatara is from the root futur, meaning to split. As we wrote under verse 14 of this same Surah, applying this word to the creation of the heavens and the earth is perhaps because, according to modern science, the world was a single mass on the first day and then split apart, and the celestial bodies came into existence one after another (for more explanation, refer to the commentary of the aforementioned verse).

  6. Hanif means pure/sincere, as explained under verse 67 of Surah Al-Imran in Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 461.

[^1]: In the verse, there is an omission and implication that becomes clear from the preceding verses. In reality, the content of the verse is: "Just as We showed Abraham the ugliness of what his people were upon regarding idol worship, so We show Abraham the malakut of the heavens and the earth" (reflect on this). [^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 738. [^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, pp. 735 and 737. [^2]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh, Vol. 4, p. 467 (footnote).

Ayahs

وَحَآجَّهُۥ قَوْمُهُۥ ۚ قَالَ أَتُحَـٰٓجُّوٓنِّى فِى ٱللَّهِ وَقَدْ هَدَىٰنِ ۚ وَلَآ أَخَافُ مَا تُشْرِكُونَ بِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ رَبِّى شَيْـًٔا ۗ وَسِعَ رَبِّى كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًا ۗ أَفَلَا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٨٠)
His people argued with him. He said, ‘Do you argue with me concerning Allah, while He has guided me for certain? I do not fear what you ascribe to Him as [His] partners, excepting anything that my Lord may wish. My Lord embraces all things in [His] knowledge. Will you not then take admonition? (80)
وَكَيْفَ أَخَافُ مَآ أَشْرَكْتُمْ وَلَا تَخَافُونَ أَنَّكُمْ أَشْرَكْتُم بِٱللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ عَلَيْكُمْ سُلْطَـٰنًا ۚ فَأَىُّ ٱلْفَرِيقَيْنِ أَحَقُّ بِٱلْأَمْنِ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٨١)
How could I fear what you ascribe [to Him] as [His] partners, when you do not fear ascribing to Allah partners for which He has not sent down any authority to you? So [tell me,] which of the two sides has a greater right to safety, if you know? (81)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوٓا۟ إِيمَـٰنَهُم بِظُلْمٍ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمُ ٱلْأَمْنُ وَهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ (٨٢)
Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be safety, and they are the [rightly] guided.’ (82)
وَتِلْكَ حُجَّتُنَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ ۚ نَرْفَعُ دَرَجَـٰتٍ مَّن نَّشَآءُ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ (٨٣)
This was Our argument that We gave to Abraham against his people. We raise in rank whomever We wish. Indeed your Lord is all-wise, all-knowing. (83)

Commentary

Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding Ibrahim’s monotheistic arguments, these verses refer to the debate and dialogue between Ibrahim and his idol-worshipping people. First, it says: "And his people argued with him" (وَ حاجَّهُ قَوْمُهُ‌).

In response to them, Ibrahim said: "Do you argue with me concerning Allah while He has guided me [to the path of monotheism through logical and clear proofs]?" (قالَ أَ تُحاجُّونِّي فِي اللَّهِ وَ قَدْ هَدانِ‌).

It is clearly understood from this verse that the idol-worshipping population among Ibrahim’s people exerted every effort to turn him away from his belief at any cost and draw him toward the religion of idolatry; however, he resisted with utmost courage and answered everyone's words with logical reasons.

As for what logic they resorted to against Ibrahim, nothing is explicitly mentioned in these verses. However, from Ibrahim’s response, it becomes generally clear that they threatened him with the punishment and wrath of the gods and idols, and frightened him regarding his opposition to them. This is because, in the continuation of the verse, we read from the tongue of Ibrahim: "I do not fear those you associate with Him [for they have no power to harm anyone]" (وَ لا أَخافُ ما تُشْرِكُونَ بِهِ‌).

"No one and nothing can cause me harm unless my Lord wills" (إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ رَبِّي شَيْئاً) [1]. It seems that with this phrase, Ibrahim intends to make a preemptive point, saying that if, for instance, an accident were to happen to me in the midst of these struggles, it has no connection to the idols; rather, it pertains to the will of the Lord. This is because a mindless and lifeless idol does not possess power over its own benefit or harm, let alone the benefit or harm of another.

Then he says: "The knowledge of my Lord is so vast and expansive that it encompasses all things" (وَسِعَ رَبِّي كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عِلْماً).

This sentence is, in fact, a reason for the preceding statement: that idols can never be the source of benefit or harm because they possess no knowledge or awareness. The first condition for delivering benefit or harm is knowledge, consciousness, and awareness. Only the God whose knowledge encompasses all things can be the source of benefit and harm; so why should I fear the wrath of anyone other than Him?

Finally, to stimulate their thought and reflection, he addresses them saying: "Will you not then remember [and wake up]?" (أَ فَلا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ‌).

In the next verse, another logic and argument from Ibrahim is expressed, where he says to the idol-worshipping crowd: "How could I fear the idols and allow dread into my heart from your threats, while I see no sign of intellect, consciousness, or power in these idols? Yet you, despite believing in the existence of God and knowing His power and knowledge, and despite Him having sent down no authority to you regarding the worship of idols,

[1]: In reality, the above exception is similar to a "disconnected exception" (istithna' munqati'), because the power over benefit and harm is completely negated for the idols and affirmed for Allah. Although commentators have suggested other possibilities for the meaning of this phrase, what we have stated above appears to be the most correct.

...despite all this, you do not fear His wrath. How then should I fear the wrath of idols?" (وَ كَيْفَ أَخافُ ما أَشْرَكْتُمْ وَ لا تَخافُونَ أَنَّكُمْ أَشْرَكْتُمْ بِاللَّهِ ما لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِ عَلَيْكُمْ سُلْطاناً) [1]. For we know that the idolaters did not deny the existence of Allah as the Creator of the heavens and the earth; rather, they made idols partners in His worship and imagined them to be intercessors at His threshold.

"Now, be fair: which of the two parties has more right to feel secure [and at peace]?" (فَأَيُّ الْفَرِيقَيْنِ أَحَقُّ بِالْأَمْنِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ‌).

In reality, Ibrahim’s logic here is a rational one based on this fact: you threaten me with the wrath of idols while their existential influence is imaginary, yet you have no fear of the wrath of the Great God whom both you and I have accepted, and whose commands we must follow—and no command has come from Him regarding the worship of idols. Have you abandoned a certain matter and clung to an imaginary one?

In the next verse, an answer is quoted from Ibrahim to the question he himself posed in the previous verse (this is an interesting method in scholarly arguments where the arguer sometimes asks a question on behalf of the opposing side and immediately proceeds to answer it, indicating that the matter is so clear that everyone should know the answer).

[1]: "Sultan" means superiority and victory. Since evidence and proof lead to victory, they are also sometimes called "sultan," and in the above verse, it carries this meaning—that is, there is no evidence whatsoever for the permission to worship idols. This was a point that no idolater could deny, as such a command must be announced through reason or revelation and prophecy, and neither of these exists.

He says: "Those who believe and do not mix their faith with injustice (zulm)—security is for them, and they are the [rightly] guided" (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ لَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَ هُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ‌).

In a narration reported from Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام, it is also emphasized that this statement is a continuation of Ibrahim’s dialogue with the idolaters [1]. Some commentators have suggested that this sentence might be a Divine statement rather than Ibrahim’s words, but the first possibility—besides being mentioned in narrations—aligns better with the context and arrangement of the verses. However, the possibility that this sentence is the speech of the idolaters who woke up after hearing Ibrahim’s words seems very unlikely.

What is meant by "Zulm" (Injustice) here?

The well-known view among commentators is that it means "Shirk" (polytheism). They take what is mentioned in Surah Luqman (Verse 13)—إِنَّ الشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ: "Indeed, polytheism is a great injustice"—as evidence for this meaning. In a narration from Ibn Mas'ud, it is reported that when this verse (the verse under discussion) was revealed, it weighed heavily on the people. They said: "O Messenger of Allah, who among us has not at least wronged himself?" (implying everyone is included in this verse). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"It is not as you think. Have you not heard the words of the righteous servant of God (Luqman) when he said: 'O my son, do not associate partners with Allah, for indeed polytheism is a great injustice'?" [2]. However, since the verses of the Quran in many cases encompass two or more meanings—where one might be broader and more general than the other—this possibility also exists in

[1] and [2]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse.

...this verse, that "security" is general, encompassing security from the punishment of the Lord or security from painful social events.

That is to say, wars, transgressions, corruptions, crimes, and even mental peace and security are only achieved when two principles govern human societies: faith and social justice. If the foundations of faith in God are shaken and the sense of responsibility before the Lord vanishes, and social justice is replaced by oppression and injustice, security will not exist in such a society. For this reason, despite all the efforts made by a group of world thinkers to dismantle various forms of insecurity in the world, the distance between the people of the world and true peace and security increases day by day. The reason for this situation is exactly what is pointed out in the above verse: the foundations of faith are trembling and injustice has taken the place of justice.

Specifically, the influence of faith on mental peace and security is beyond doubt for anyone, just as the distress of conscience and the deprivation of psychological peace due to committing injustice is hidden from no one.

In some narrations from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated: "The meaning of the above verse is those who believe in the command of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the Wilayah (guardianship) and leadership of the Islamic Ummah after him, and do not mix it with the Wilayah and leadership of others; security belongs to them" [1]. This interpretation actually looks at the criterion and the spirit of the matter in the noble verse, because in this verse, the talk is about the leadership and Wilayah of Allah and not mixing it with the leadership of others. Since the leadership of Ali عليه السلام—according to the verse إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللَّهُ وَ رَسُولُهُ—is a

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 740.

...reflection of the leadership of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, whereas leaderships not designated by Allah are not so, the above verse, with a broad view, includes them all. Therefore, the purpose of this hadith is not that the concept of the verse is exclusively this, but rather this interpretation is a ray from the original concept of the verse.

Hence, in another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that this verse includes the Kharijites, who exited the Wilayah of the Wali of Allah and placed themselves under the Wilayah and leadership of Satan [1].

The next verse makes a general reference to all the previous discussions regarding monotheism and the struggle against polytheism narrated from Ibrahim, saying: "And that was Our argument which We gave to Ibrahim against his people" (وَ تِلْكَ حُجَّتُنا آتَيْناها إِبْراهِيمَ عَلى‌ قَوْمِهِ‌). It is true that these arguments had a logical aspect, and Ibrahim reached them through the power of reason and primordial inspiration (fitrah), but since this power of reason and that primordial inspiration are all from Allah, He considers all these arguments as His own gifts reflected in prepared hearts like Ibrahim’s.

It is noteworthy that "tilka" in the Arabic language is a demonstrative pronoun for the distant, but sometimes the importance of a subject and its high status causes even a near subject to be mentioned with a distant demonstrative pronoun, as we read at the beginning of Surah al-Baqarah: ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ: "That [great] Book, there is no doubt in it."

Then, to complete this discussion, He says: "We raise in degrees whom We will" (نَرْفَعُ دَرَجاتٍ مَنْ نَشاءُ) [2].

[1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 538. [2]: Regarding "degree" (darajah) and its difference from "level" (dark), we discussed this in Vol. 4, p. 180, under Verse 145 of Surah an-Nisa.

However, so that no mistake arises where one might think Allah practices discrimination in this raising of degrees, He says: "Indeed, your Lord is All-Wise, All-Knowing." The degrees He grants are based on His knowledge of their worthiness and in accordance with the standards of wisdom; until someone is worthy, they will not benefit from it (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ‌).

Ayahs

وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ ۚ كُلًّا هَدَيْنَا ۚ وَنُوحًا هَدَيْنَا مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِهِۦ دَاوُۥدَ وَسُلَيْمَـٰنَ وَأَيُّوبَ وَيُوسُفَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٨٤)
And We gave him Isaac and Jacob and guided each of them. And Noah We had guided before, and from his offspring, David and Solomon, Job, Joseph, Moses and Aaron—thus do We reward the virtuous— (84)
وَزَكَرِيَّا وَيَحْيَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَإِلْيَاسَ ۖ كُلٌّ مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٨٥)
and Zechariah, John, Jesus and Ilyas—each of them among the righteous— (85)
وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَٱلْيَسَعَ وَيُونُسَ وَلُوطًا ۚ وَكُلًّا فَضَّلْنَا عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨٦)
and Ishmael, Elisha, Jonah and Lot—each We graced over all the nations— (86)
وَمِنْ ءَابَآئِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِهِمْ وَإِخْوَٰنِهِمْ ۖ وَٱجْتَبَيْنَـٰهُمْ وَهَدَيْنَـٰهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٨٧)
and from among their fathers, their descendants and brethren—We chose them and guided them to a straight path. (87)

Commentary

In these verses, reference is made to a portion of the blessings that Allah bestowed upon Ibrahim (Abraham), which is the blessing of righteous children and a worthy, flourishing progeny—considered one of the greatest divine favors.

First, He says: "And We bestowed upon him Ishaq (Isaac) and Ya'qub (Jacob, the son of Isaac)" (وَ وَهَبْنا لَهُ إِسْحاقَ وَ يَعْقُوبَ).

If there is no mention here of Ibrahim's other son, Isma'il (Ishmael), and he is instead mentioned later in the discussion, it is perhaps because the birth of Ishaq from a barren mother like Sarah, and that too in old age, was an extremely wondrous matter and an unexpected blessing.

Then, to demonstrate that the honor of these two did not lie solely in being the children of a Prophet, but that they personally, in the light of correct thought and righteous deeds, had allowed the light of guidance to dwell in their hearts, He says: "Each of them We guided" (كُلًّا هَدَيْنا).

Following that, so it is not imagined that there were no standard-bearers of monotheism in the eras before Ibrahim and that this matter began with him, He adds: "And Nuh (Noah) We guided before that" (وَ نُوحاً هَدَيْنا مِنْ قَبْلُ).

And we know that Nuh was the first of the Ulul-'Azm (Arch-Prophets) who possessed a divine law (Shari'ah) and is the head of the lineage of the Arch-Prophets.

In truth, by referring to the status of Nuh, who is among the ancestors of Ibrahim, and the status of a group of Prophets who are from his lineage and offspring, He defines the distinguished position of Ibrahim in terms of "inheritance and roots" and the "fruit" of his existence.

Following this, He lists the names of a large group of Prophets who were from the lineage of Ibrahim. First, He says: "And of his progeny, Dawud (David), Sulayman (Solomon), Ayyub (Job), Yusuf (Joseph), Musa (Moses), and Harun (Aaron)" (وَ مِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِهِ داوُدَ وَ سُلَيْمانَ وَ أَيُّوبَ وَ يُوسُفَ وَ مُوسى‌ وَ هارُونَ).

Then, with the phrase "Thus do We reward the doers of good," He clarifies that their rank and position were in the light of their actions and deeds (وَ كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ).

Regarding whom the pronoun in min dhurriyyatihi ("of his progeny") refers to—Ibrahim or Nuh—there is much discussion among commentators. However, the majority of commentators have returned it to Ibrahim, and apparently, there should be no doubt that the referent of the pronoun is Ibrahim, because the discussion of the verse is about Allah's blessings upon Ibrahim, not about the Prophet Nuh. Furthermore, this point is also derived from numerous narrations that we will cite later.

The only thing that caused some commentators to return the pronoun to Nuh is the mention of the names "Yunus" (Jonah) and "Lut" (Lot) in the subsequent verses, as it is well-known in histories that Yunus was not from the descendants of Ibrahim, and Lut was Ibrahim's nephew (brother's or sister's son).

However, regarding Yunus, historians do not have a consensus; some have considered him from the lineage of Ibrahim [1], and some have counted him among the Prophets of the Children of Israel [2]. Additionally, historians usually preserve lineage through the father; what is to prevent Yunus—like 'Isa (Jesus), whose name we also read in the above verses—from tracing back to Ibrahim through his mother?

[1] Tafsir al-Alusi, Vol. 7, p. 184. [2] Da'irat al-Ma'arif Farid Wajdi, Vol. 10, p. 1055 (under the entry 'Yunus').

As for Lut, although he was not the son of Ibrahim, he was from his family and kin. Just as in the Arabic language the paternal uncle is sometimes called ab (father), the nephew is also referred to as dhurriyyah (progeny) and child. Thus, we cannot abandon the apparent meaning of the verses which are about Ibrahim and return the pronoun to Nuh, who is not the subject of the discourse here.

In the next verse, He mentions the names of "Zakariyya (Zechariah), Yahya (John), 'Isa (Jesus), and Ilyas (Elias), and adds that all of them were of the righteous," meaning their ranks were not merely ceremonial or forced, but they gained personality and greatness in the presence of Allah in the light of righteous deeds (وَ زَكَرِيَّا وَ يَحْيى‌ وَ عِيسى‌ وَ إِلْياسَ كُلٌّ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ).

In the following verse, the names of four other Prophets and divine leaders are mentioned, and He says: "And Isma'il, al-Yasa' (Elisha), Yunus, and Lut, and each of them We preferred over the worlds" (وَ إِسْماعِيلَ وَ الْيَسَعَ وَ يُونُسَ وَ لُوطاً وَ كلًّا فَضَّلْنا عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ). There is discussion among commentators and Arabic linguists regarding what kind of name "al-Yasa'" is and which Prophet it refers to. Some consider it a Hebrew name that was originally "Yusha'" (Joshua), then the alif and lam were added and the shin changed to sin. Some believe it is an Arabic name derived from yasa' (the present tense verb from the root wus'at - vastness). The possibility has also been given that it was the name of one of the previous Prophets in this very form. In any case, he is among the Prophets who are from the lineage of Ibrahim.

In the final verse, He makes a general reference to the righteous fathers, children, and brothers of the aforementioned Prophets whose names are not mentioned in detail here, saying: "And from among their fathers and their progeny and their brothers—We chose them and guided them to a straight path" (وَ مِنْ آبائِهِمْ وَ ذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ وَ إِخْوانِهِمْ وَ اجْتَبَيْناهُمْ وَ هَدَيْناهُمْ إِلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ).

Here, several matters should be noted

1. The Children of the Prophet

In the above verses, 'Isa is counted among the children of Ibrahim (and possibly among the children of Nuh), even though we know he is related to them only through his mother. This is proof that the lineage proceeds equally through both the father and the mother, and for this reason, both paternal and maternal grandchildren are considered the dhurriyyah and offspring of a person.

On this basis, the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, who all reach the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله through his daughter, are called Abna' Rasul Allah (the children of the Messenger of Allah).

Although during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), when no importance was given to women, they considered lineage only through the father, Islam drew a line of cancellation over this ignorant thought. Unfortunately, however, some writers who did not have a proper affection for the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt tried to deny this matter and refrained from calling them Ibn Rasul Allah, thereby reviving the customs of Jahiliyyah.

Coincidentally, this issue was raised during the time of the Imams themselves, and they responded using this very verse, which is considered a crushing proof.

Among these instances, it is narrated in al-Kafi and in Tafsir al-Ayyashi from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "Allah the Almighty, in the Glorious Quran, has stated the lineage of 'Isa, which reaches Ibrahim through his mother, as dhurriyyah (offspring)." Then he recited the verse: "And of his progeny, Dawud and Sulayman..." until the end, and the next verse up to the word "'Isa."

Also, in Tafsir al-Ayyashi, it is narrated from Abu al-Aswad who said: One day, Hajjaj sent someone to Yahya ibn Ma'mar, who was a lover of the Prophet's family صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "I have heard that you consider Hasan and Husayn to be the children of the Messenger of Allah and you argue this using verses of the Quran, while I have read the Quran from beginning to end and have not come across such a verse." Yahya ibn Ma'mar replied to him: "Have you come across this verse in Surah al-An'am which says: 'And of his progeny, Dawud and Sulayman... and Yahya and 'Isa'?" He said: "Yes, I have read it." Yahya said: "Is it not the case that in these verses 'Isa is counted as the progeny of Ibrahim, even though he did not reach him through a father?!"

In Uyun al-Akhbar, within a long hadith regarding the conversation of Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام with Harun al-Rashid and Musa ibn Mahdi, it is narrated that he (Harun) said to Imam al-Kadhim عليه السلام: "How do you say 'We are the progeny of the Prophet,' while the Prophet had no son, and lineage is through the son, not the daughter, and you are the children of his daughter?" In response, the Imam asked him to overlook this question, but Harun insisted and said: "I will by no means overlook it, for you believe that everything is in the Glorious Quran and you must bring a verse from the Quran regarding this." The Imam said: "I seek refuge in Allah from the accursed Satan, in the name of Allah, the Beneficent, the Merciful: 'And of his progeny, Dawud and Sulayman and Ayyub and Yusuf and Musa and Harun; thus do We reward the doers of good, and Zakariyya and Yahya and 'Isa...'" Then he asked: "O Harun! Who was the father of 'Isa?" He said: "'Isa had no father." The Imam said: "Therefore, if he is joined to the progeny of the Prophets, it is through Maryam; likewise, we are joined to the progeny of the Messenger of Allah through our mother Fatimah." [1] It is interesting to note that some biased scholars of the Ahl al-Sunnah have also included this matter in their commentaries under this same verse. Among them, Fakhr al-Razi in his Tafsir al-Kabir says: "This verse indicates that Hasan and Husayn are from the progeny of the Prophet, because Allah counted 'Isa from the progeny of Ibrahim even though he is related to him only through his mother." [2]

[1] Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 743. [2] Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 13, p. 66.

The author of al-Manar, who in his bias regarding certain specific religious discussions is no less than Fakhr al-Razi, after quoting Fakhr al-Razi's words, says: "On this subject, a hadith is narrated from 'Abu Bakr' in Sahih al-Bukhari from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he pointed to Imam Hasan عليه السلام and said: 'Indeed, this son of mine is a leader' (meaning he applied the word 'son' to Imam Hasan), whereas among the (ignorant) Arabs, the word ibn (son) was not applied to a daughter's child... Then he adds: For this reason, the people considered the children of Fatimah عليه السلام to be the children of the Messenger, his 'itrat (progeny), and his Ahl al-Bayt."

In any case, there is no doubt that grandchildren through both the daughter and the son are considered a person's children, and there is no difference in this regard. This matter is not exclusive to our Prophet, and opposition to this issue has no source other than bias or ignorant thoughts. Therefore, in all Islamic rulings such as marriage, inheritance, and the like, there is no difference between the two. The only exception to this subject is the issue of Khums, which is based on the title of Siyadat (being a Sayyid), and for a specific reason mentioned in the books of Khums in jurisprudence, this matter has been excepted.

2. Why are the names of these Prophets mentioned in three groups in three verses?

Some commentators have suggested that the first group—namely Dawud, Sulayman, Ayyub, Yusuf, Musa, and Harun—these six were among the Prophets who, in addition to the station of Prophethood and Messengership, also possessed governance and leadership. Perhaps the phrase "Thus do We reward the doers of good" which comes after mentioning their names was because of the abundant good they did for the people during their reign.

As for the second group—namely Zakariyya, Yahya, 'Isa, and Ilyas—they were among the Prophets who were examples of asceticism and indifference to the world, in addition to the station of Prophethood and Messengership. The phrase "All were of the righteous" after mentioning their names could be an allusion to this reality.

Regarding the third group—namely Isma'il, al-Yasa', Yunus, and Lut—they had the distinction of undertaking extensive migrations and implementing the program of Hijrah (migration) to consolidate Allah's religion. The mention of the phrase "And each of them We preferred over the worlds" (assuming it refers to these four and not to all the Prophets mentioned in these three verses) could also be an allusion to their travels in the world and among various nations.

3. The Importance of Righteous Children in Defining a Person's Character

Another subject derived from the above verses is this very issue. For, to introduce the high station of Ibrahim, the idol-breaking hero, Allah describes in detail the great human personalities who emerged from his lineage in different eras. So much so that out of the 25 Prophets whose names appear in the entire Quran, the names of 16 of Ibrahim's children and relatives and the name of one of his ancestors appear in these verses. This is, in fact, a great lesson for all Muslims to know that the character of their children and lineage is considered part of their own character, and the educational and human issues related to them are of extraordinary importance.

4. Answer to an Objection

Some might infer from the last verse which says: "And from among their fathers and their progeny and their brothers—We chose them and guided them to a straight path," that the fathers of the Prophets were not all believers and that there were non-monotheists among them—as some Sunni commentators have said under this verse. However, considering that "We chose them and guided them" (ajtabaynahum wa hadaynahum), by the context of the expression used in this series of verses, refers to the station of Prophethood and Messengership, the problem is solved. That is, the meaning of the verse will be that We chose some of them for the station of Prophethood, and this does not contradict the others being monotheists.

In verse 90 of this same Surah (a few verses after this one), "guidance" is also applied to the station of Prophethood. [1]

Ayahs

ذَٰلِكَ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ يَهْدِى بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۚ وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا۟ لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٨٨)
That is Allah’s guidance: with it, He guides whomever He wishes of His servants. But were they to ascribe any partners [to Allah], what they used to do would not avail them. (88)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحُكْمَ وَٱلنُّبُوَّةَ ۚ فَإِن يَكْفُرْ بِهَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنَا بِهَا قَوْمًا لَّيْسُوا۟ بِهَا بِكَـٰفِرِينَ (٨٩)
They are the ones whom We gave the Book, judgement and prophethood. So if these disbelieve in them, We have certainly entrusted them to a people who will never disbelieve in them. (89)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ هَدَى ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَبِهُدَىٰهُمُ ٱقْتَدِهْ ۗ قُل لَّآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا ۖ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرَىٰ لِلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٩٠)
They are the ones whom Allah has guided. So follow their guidance. Say, ‘I do not ask you any recompense for it. It is just an admonition for all the nations.’ (90)

Commentary

Three Important Merits

Following the mention of various groups of Divine prophets in the preceding verses, the text here refers to the general and primary outlines of their lives. First, It states: "That is the guidance of Allah with which He guides whomever He wills of His servants" (ذلِكَ هُدَى اللَّهِ يَهْدِي بِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ).

This means that although they were righteous men who walked the path of guidance with the power of intellect, reflection, and their entire being, nevertheless, if Divine success (tawfiq) had not encompassed them and His merciful hand had not supported them, the possibility of slipping existed and exists for all of them and for everyone.

Then, so that no one imagines they were forced onto this path, nor that Allah held a specific, exceptional, and baseless bias toward them, He says: "And if they had associated others [with Allah], then surely all that they used to do would have been rendered null and void" (وَ لَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

This implies that they too are subject to the same Divine laws that are applied to others, and there is no discrimination involved.
