In the next verse, reference is made to three important merits that formed the foundation of all the prophets' distinctions, stating: "Those are the ones to whom We gave the Book, the authority (judgment), and prophethood" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَ الْحُكْمَ وَ النُّبُوَّةَ). Of course, the intention is not that every single one of them possessed a scripture; rather, since the discourse concerns them as a collective, the "Book" is attributed to the group. This is exactly like saying: "In such-and-lesser book, the scholars and their books are introduced," meaning the books of those among them who authored a book.

Furthermore, regarding what is meant by "hukm" (authority/judgment), there are three possibilities:

1- Judgment in the sense of intellect, understanding, and perception; meaning that in addition to placing the Divine Book at their disposal, We granted them the power to perceive and understand it, for the existence of a book without the presence of a strong and perfect understanding would have no effect.

2- The station of adjudication; meaning that in the light of the heavenly laws derived from the Divine Book, they were able to judge among the people, and all the requirements of a just judge and arbiter were fully gathered within them.

3- Governance and leadership; because in addition to the station of prophethood and messengership, they also held the station of governance.

The evidence for the above meanings—besides the fact that the linguistic meaning of hukm aligns with all these definitions—is that in various verses of the Quran, hukm has been used in these senses [^1], and there is no obstacle to hukm in the above verse being used in a comprehensive sense that includes all three aforementioned concepts. This is because hukm originally—as

[^1]: In Surah Luqman, verse 12, it means knowledge and understanding; in Surah Sad, verse 22, it means adjudication; and in Surah Al-Kahf, verse 26, it means governance.

Raghib says in the book Al-Mufradat—means "prevention" or "restraint." Since intellect prevents mistakes and wrongdoings, just adjudication prevents oppression and tyranny, and just governance prevents the illegitimate rule of others, it is used in each of these three meanings.

Of course, as we noted, not all prophets held all these stations, but when certain attributes are ascribed to a group, it is not necessary for every individual in that group to possess all those attributes. Rather, it is possible that some of them possess only some of those attributes. Therefore, the subject of the Divine Book, which existed only for a number of the mentioned prophets, creates no difficulty for us in understanding the above verse.

Then He says: "But if these people—meaning the polytheists, the people of Makkah, and their like—disbelieve in them, your call will not remain unanswered, for We have entrusted them to a people who are not disbelievers therein; a people who do not walk the path of disbelief and are submissive to the Truth" (فَإِنْ يَكْفُرْ بِها هؤُلاءِ فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنا بِها قَوْماً لَيْسُوا بِها بِكافِرِينَ).

In the commentaries of Al-Manar and Ruh al-Ma'ani, it is narrated from some commentators that this "people" refers to the Persians (who soon accepted Islam and strove with all their might for its advancement, and whose scholars authored numerous books in various Islamic sciences) [^1].

[^1]: There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that "these" (ha'ula') refers to the prophets themselves; meaning that if, by an impossible assumption, these great Divine prophets had refused to fulfill the message, the Divine mission would still not have remained on the ground, and another group would have been commissioned to deliver it to the worlds. Similar expressions are seen in the Quran, such as where we read elsewhere: "If you associate others [with Allah], your work will surely be rendered null" (Az-Zumar: 65).

In the final verse, the program of these great prophets is introduced as a supreme model of guidance for the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "Those are the ones whom Allah has guided, so follow their guidance" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَدَى اللَّهُ فَبِهُداهُمُ اقتَدِهْ) [^1]. This verse emphasizes once again that the principles of the call of all Divine prophets are one, even if they had significant differences in terms of specific features and characteristics according to the various needs of each era, and subsequent religions were more complete than previous ones—like educational and training classes—until reaching the final one, which is the ultimate program: Islam.

Regarding what is meant by this "guidance" that must serve as a model for the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, some commentators have suggested it refers to patience and steadfastness in the face of difficulties, while others have said it means monotheism and the propagation of the message. However, it appears that "guidance" has a broad meaning that includes monotheism and other principles of belief, as well as patience, perseverance, and other principles of ethics, education, and training.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that the above verse in no way contradicts the fact that Islam abrogates previous religions and laws, because abrogation only includes a portion of the rulings,

[^1]: It should be noted that the "h" in "iqtadih" is not a pronoun but rather the "ha of silence" (ha al-sakt) which is attached to the word when pausing on a vocalized letter, similar to the hamzat al-wasl brought at the beginning of a word to avoid starting with a silent letter. Therefore, just as the hamzat al-wasl drops when connecting speech, the ha al-sakt should also drop. However, since this "h" is written in the script of the Quran, to respect the appearance of the script, caution has been exercised to pause here so that the "h" is pronounced.

not the general principles of their call.

Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is commanded to "Tell the people: I ask of you no reward for my message, just as the previous prophets made no such request; I follow this eternal tradition of the prophets and emulate them" (قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً).

Not only does emulating the prophets and their eternal tradition necessitate not demanding a reward, but since this pure religion I have brought for you is a Divine trust placed at your disposal, the concept of a reward for delivering a Divine trust to you is meaningless.

Furthermore, this Quran, the message, and the guidance are a wake-up call and a reminder to all the worlds (إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرى‌ لِلْعالَمِينَ).

And such a general and universal blessing is like sunlight, airwaves, and rainfall, which have a public and global nature and are never bought or sold, nor does anyone take a reward for them. This guidance and message likewise do not have a private or exclusive aspect for which a reward could be considered. (Given what was said in the interpretation of this sentence, their connection to each other and to the preceding verses becomes perfectly clear).

Additionally, from the final phrase, it is well understood that the religion of Islam does not have an ethnic or regional character; it is a global and universal religion.

Ayah

وَمَا قَدَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِۦٓ إِذْ قَالُوا۟ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ بَشَرٍ مِّن شَىْءٍ ۗ قُلْ مَنْ أَنزَلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ٱلَّذِى جَآءَ بِهِۦ مُوسَىٰ نُورًا وَهُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ ۖ تَجْعَلُونَهُۥ قَرَاطِيسَ تُبْدُونَهَا وَتُخْفُونَ كَثِيرًا ۖ وَعُلِّمْتُم مَّا لَمْ تَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنتُمْ وَلَآ ءَابَآؤُكُمْ ۖ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ ۖ ثُمَّ ذَرْهُمْ فِى خَوْضِهِمْ يَلْعَبُونَ (٩١)
They did not regard Allah with the regard due to Him when they said, ‘Allah has not sent down anything to any human.’ Say, ‘Who had sent down the Book that was brought by Moses as a light and guidance for the people, which you make into parchments that you display, while you conceal much of it, and [by means of which] you were taught what you did not know, [neither] you nor your fathers?’ Say, ‘Allah!’ Then leave them to play around in their impious gossip. (91)

Circumstances of Revelation: The God-Unaware

It has been narrated from Ibn Abbas that a group of Jews said: "O Muhammad! Has Allah truly sent down a book upon you?!" The Prophet replied: "Yes." They said:

"By Allah, Allah has never sent down any book from heaven!" [^1] Other narrations have also been cited regarding the circumstances of the revelation of this verse, and as we shall see later, the one mentioned above is the most fitting and appropriate.

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Abu al-Futuh Razi, and al-Manar, under the commentary of this verse.

Commentary

There is a discussion among commentators as to whether this verse concerns the Jews or the polytheists. Since the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not have such dialogues with the Jews in Mecca—as those occurred in Medina—and on the other hand, Surah al-An'am (which contains this verse) is Meccan, some believe that this verse was revealed in Medina as an exception. By the Prophet's command, it was placed in the middle of this Meccan Surah due to a specific relevance; such instances are numerous in the Quran.

To clarify the reality of the matter, we must first understand a brief commentary of the verse and then discuss the people it addresses and its objective. First, it says: "They did not recognize Allah with the recognition due to Him when they said:

'Allah has not revealed anything to any human'!" (وَمَا قَدَرُوا اللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ إِذْ قَالُوا مَا أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ عَلَىٰ بَشَرٍ مِنْ شَيْءٍ).

Allah commands His Prophet to say in response to them: "Say, who revealed the Book which Moses brought as a light and guidance for the people?" (قُلْ مَنْ أَنْزَلَ الْكِتَابَ الَّذِي جَاءَ بِهِ مُوسَىٰ نُورًا وَهُدًى لِلنَّاسِ).

"The very Book which you have turned into scattered pages, disclosing some of it which benefits you and concealing much of it which you find to your disadvantage" (تَجْعَلُونَهُ قَرَاطِيسَ تُبْدُونَهَا وَتُخْفُونَ كَثِيرًا).

"And in this Heavenly Book, you were taught things that neither you nor your forefathers knew, and which you could not have known without Divine instruction" (وَعُلِّمْتُمْ مَا لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا أَنْتُمْ وَلَا آبَاؤُكُمْ). At the end of the verse, He commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to "mention only Allah, then leave them to play in their vain talk and stubbornness," for they are a people who have taken the Divine Book and His signs as a game. (قُلِ اللَّهُ ۖ ثُمَّ ذَرْهُمْ فِي خَوْضِهِمْ يَلْعَبُونَ).

Now, if this verse was revealed in Medina and the address is to the Jews, its meaning is that a group of Jews denied the revelation of any Heavenly Book to any prophet. Is such a thing possible? That the Jews and followers of the Torah would deny the revelation of a Heavenly Book? If you are not surprised, yes; and by considering one point, the nuance of this matter becomes clear. If we carefully examine the books of the New Testament (the Gospels) and the Old Testament (the Torah and its related books), we will see that none of these books possess a Heavenly tone. That is, the aspect of Allah addressing humanity is not present in them. Rather, it is clearly understood from them that they were written by disciples and non-disciples among the followers of the religions of Moses عليه السلام and Jesus عليه السلام in the form of history and biographies. Apparently, contemporary Jews and Christians do not deny this either, as the stories of the deaths of Moses and Jesus and many events related to times after them are mentioned in these books—not as prophecies, but as reports of the past. Is it possible for such a book to have been revealed to Moses and Jesus?

However, Christians and Jews believe that because these books were written by humans who were aware of Heavenly revelation, they are considered holy, reliable, and free from error.

With this point in mind, it becomes clear why they were surprised by the tone of the Quran, which is in the form of Allah's address to the Prophet and His servants. In the circumstances of revelation mentioned above, we read that they asked the Prophet in surprise: "Has Allah revealed a Heavenly Book?" and then denied the matter entirely, saying that no book from Allah's side has been revealed to any human, even Moses.

But in response to them, Allah points to the fact that you yourselves believe that tablets and teachings were revealed to Moses. That is, even if what is in your hands is not the Heavenly Book itself, you at least accept that such a thing was revealed by God, part of which you disclose and much of which you conceal. In this way, no problem remains as to how it is possible for the Jews to have denied the revelation of a Heavenly Book (reflect on this).

And if the verse, like other verses of this Surah, is about the polytheists, its meaning would be that they denied any kind of Heavenly Book in order to reject the call of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. However, Allah argues against them: how can they make such a claim when Allah revealed the Torah to Moses? Although the polytheists did not accept the Jewish religion, they likely accepted previous prophets like Abraham and even Moses as prophets for a specific region and era, and they considered themselves followers of the religion of Abraham. Therefore, when the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله appeared, they went to the People of the Book to search for his signs and asked them to check their books to see if they gave news of such a prophet. If they did not accept these books at all, how could they make such a request? Thus, after questioning the Jews, they would disclose what was in their favor and hide what was to their disadvantage (such as the signs of the Prophet mentioned in the previous books). In this way, the verse can also be applicable to the words of the polytheists of Mecca.

However, the first interpretation seems more consistent with the tone of the verse, the circumstances of revelation, and the pronouns used in the verse.

Here, several points must be noted

  1. Qaratis (قراطيس) is the plural of qirtas (قرطاس). As some have said, its origin is taken from Greek, and its meaning, as Raghib says in the book al-Mufradat, is "anything upon which one writes." Therefore, it includes ordinary paper, animal skins, trees, and the like, upon which letters and books were written in ancient times, and it is not limited to ordinary paper.

  2. It might be asked why the verse censures the Jews for having written the Heavenly revelation on papers and the like, as there is no blame in that.

In response, we say: the censure is not for this reason, but rather because they had written the contents of the Torah on scattered papers and the like so that they could show people what was in their favor and hide what was to their disadvantage.

  1. The phrase "They did not recognize Allah with the recognition due to Him" (وَمَا قَدَرُوا اللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ) (and did not comprehend His attributes) is actually an allusion to the point that anyone who truly recognizes Allah cannot deny that leaders and guides accompanied by Heavenly Books have been sent by Him for humanity. This is because Allah's wisdom necessitates, firstly, helping man toward the goal for which he was created (the goal of perfection) in the winding path he faces; otherwise, it would be a contradiction of purpose. This goal is impossible without sending revelation, a Heavenly Book, and correct teachings free from any error or mistake.

Secondly, how is it possible for the station of Allah's general and specific mercy to allow Him to leave man alone on the path of happiness—where he faces thousands of obstacles and many pitfalls lie in wait—and not send leaders with comprehensive teachings to take their hands and guide them? (Therefore, both His wisdom and His mercy necessitate the sending of Heavenly Books).

There is no doubt that knowing the essence of Allah's Being and the essence of His attributes is not possible for anyone, and the above verse does not refer to this at all. Rather, it intends to say that if that amount of knowledge of Allah and His attributes which is possible for man is attained, no doubt will remain that such a Lord would not leave His servants without a guardian and a Heavenly Book.

Ayah

وَهَـٰذَا كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مُبَارَكٌ مُّصَدِّقُ ٱلَّذِى بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَلِتُنذِرَ أُمَّ ٱلْقُرَىٰ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهَا ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ ۖ وَهُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَاتِهِمْ يُحَافِظُونَ (٩٢)
Blessed is this Book, which We have sent down, confirming what was [revealed] before it, so that you may warn the Mother of Cities and those around it. Those who believe in the Hereafter believe in it, and they are watchful of their prayers. (92)

Commentary

Following the discussion regarding the Jewish scripture in the previous verse, reference is made here to the Quran, which is another Heavenly Book. In fact, mentioning the Torah serves as a preamble to mentioning the Quran, so that people do not feel astonishment or dread regarding the revelation of a Heavenly Book upon a human being.

First, It says: "And this is a Book which We have sent down" (وَ هذا كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ‌).

"A Book full of blessings, for it is the source of all kinds of goodness, virtues, and victories" (مُبارك).

"Furthermore, it confirms the scriptures that were revealed before it" (مُصَدِّقُ الَّذِي بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ‌).

The meaning of the Quran confirming previous holy books is that all the signs mentioned in them align with it.

In this way, two signs of the Quran's truthfulness are expressed in the two preceding phrases: one is the existence of signs foretold in previous books, and the other is the content of the Quran itself, which contains every kind of goodness, blessing, and means of prosperity. Therefore, its signs of truthfulness are manifest both in terms of content and in terms of historical documents and evidence.

Then, He explains the purpose of the Quran's revelation: that We sent it "so that you may warn the Mother of Cities (Mecca) and all those around it, and make them aware of their responsibilities and duties" (وَ لِتُنْذِرَ أُمَّ الْقُرى‌ وَ مَنْ حَوْلَها) [1]. Since "warning" (inzar) means drawing attention to responsibilities and frightening one against the neglect of duties—which is the most important program of the Quran, especially against the rebellious and tyrannical—only this part is mentioned.

At the end of the verse, It says: "Those who believe in the Day of Resurrection, the Account, and the reward of deeds will believe in this Book, and they will be mindful of their prayers" (وَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ وَ هُمْ عَلى‌ صَلاتِهِمْ يُحافِظُونَ‌).

Here, several points must be noted

[1]: There is a discussion among commentators as to what "li-tundhira" (that you may warn) is coordinated (atf) with, but it seems most likely that it is coordinated with a deleted phrase such as "li-tubashshira" (that you may give glad tidings) and the like.

1- Islam is a Universal Religion

Various verses of the Quran clearly testify that Islam is a universal religion. Expressions such as: "that I may warn you thereby and whomever it reaches" (لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَ مَنْ بَلَغَ) (Al-An'am: 19); "It is naught but a reminder to the worlds" (إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرى‌ لِلْعالَمِينَ) (Al-An'am: 90); and "Say: O mankind! Indeed I am the Messenger of Allah to you all" (قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنِّي رَسُولُ اللَّهِ إِلَيْكُمْ جَمِيعاً) (Al-A'raf: 158), and similar verses which are abundant in the Quran, bear witness to this reality. Interestingly, many of these verses were revealed in Mecca, at a time when Islam had not yet transcended the environment of that city.

However, considering the verse under discussion, the question arises: how is the goal of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) mission stated as warning and guiding the people of Mecca and those around it? Does this not contradict the universality of Islam?

Coincidentally, this objection has been cited from some Jews and followers of other religions, who believe they have found a strong weapon against the universality of Islam, claiming it is limited to a specific region (namely Mecca and its surroundings) [1].

Answer

This objection is completely clarified by considering two points; not only does this verse not contradict the universality of Islam, but it can be said to be one of the proofs of its universality:

[1]: This objection is cited from some Orientalists in Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 7, p. 621, and Fi Zilal al-Quran, Vol. 3, p. 305.

1- The word "Qaryah" (city/village) in the language of the Quran refers to any inhabited place, whether it is a large city, a small one, or a village. For example, in Surah Yusuf, we read from the brothers addressing their father: "And ask the city (al-qaryah) in which we were" (وَ سْئَلِ الْقَرْيَةَ الَّتِي كُنَّا فِيها) [1]. We know this was said after their return from the capital of Egypt following the detention of their brother Benjamin by the administration of the Aziz of Egypt. Similarly, we read: "And if the people of the cities (al-qura) had believed and been God-wary, We would have opened for them blessings from the heaven and the earth" (وَ لَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى‌ آمَنُوا وَ اتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَ الْأَرْضِ) [2]. It is obvious that the intention here is not specifically villages, but rather all inhabited points of the world.

On the other hand, we read in numerous narrations that the dry lands of the earth were spread out from beneath the Kaaba, referred to as "Dahw al-Ardh" (the expansion of the earth).

We also know that in the beginning, due to torrential rains, the entire globe was covered with water. The waters gradually receded and settled in the low points of the earth, and the dry lands gradually emerged from under the water. According to Islamic narrations, the first point to emerge from under the water was the land of Mecca.

If the elevation of this land is not currently the highest in the world, it does not contradict this statement at all, because hundreds of millions of years have passed since then, and the state of the earth's surface has completely changed; some mountains are now in the depths of oceans, and some ocean depths have turned into mountain peaks. This is among the established facts of geology and physical geography.

[1]: Yusuf: 82. [2]: Al-A'raf: 96.

2- The word "Umm" (Mother), as mentioned previously, means the root, foundation, origin, and beginning of anything.

Given what has been said, it becomes clear that if Mecca is called "Umm al-Qura" (Mother of Cities), it is because it is the origin and beginning of the emergence of all dry lands on earth. Therefore, "and those around it" (وَ مَنْ حَوْلَها) includes all the people on the face of the earth.

The previous verses regarding the universality of Islam also support this interpretation. Furthermore, the numerous letters the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) wrote to the great rulers of the world, such as Khosrow and Caesar—the details of which were mentioned in Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 452—serve as another witness to this subject.

2- The Connection Between Faith in the Quran and Faith in the Hereafter

In the above verse, we read: "Those who believe in the Hereafter believe in the Quran." This means they know this world is a preamble to another world, and is like a "farm," a "university," or a "marketplace." In any case, without a series of laws, programs, regulations, and the sending of Prophets, reaching that lofty goal and preparing for that Day is impossible.

In other words, since Allah sent man for perfection in this world and his original abode is the next world, if He did not send Prophets and Heavenly Books for him, it would be a contradiction of purpose (naqd al-gharadh). Thus, from faith in God and the Resurrection, faith in the mission of the Prophets and the Heavenly Books is concluded. (Reflect on this).

3- The Importance of Prayer

In the above verse, among all religious instructions, only prayer (Salah) is mentioned. As we know, prayer is the manifestation of the bond with God and communication with Him; for this reason, it is superior to and higher than all other acts of worship. According to some, at the time of the revelation of these verses, the only Islamic obligation was this very prayer [1].

Ayah

وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَوْ قَالَ أُوحِىَ إِلَىَّ وَلَمْ يُوحَ إِلَيْهِ شَىْءٌ وَمَن قَالَ سَأُنزِلُ مِثْلَ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ فِى غَمَرَٰتِ ٱلْمَوْتِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ بَاسِطُوٓا۟ أَيْدِيهِمْ أَخْرِجُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمُ ۖ ٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذَابَ ٱلْهُونِ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ غَيْرَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَكُنتُمْ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ (٩٣)
Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or says, ‘It has been revealed to me,’ while nothing was revealed to him, and he who says, ‘I will bring the like of what Allah has sent down?’ Were you to see when the wrongdoers are in the throes of death, and the angels extend their hands [saying]: ‘Give up your souls! Today you shall be requited with a humiliating punishment because of what you used to attribute to Allah untruly, and for your being disdainful towards His signs.’ (93)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the occasion of revelation for this verse, several narrations have been cited in hadith sources and books of tafsir. Among them is that the verse was revealed concerning a person named "Abdullah ibn Sa'd," who was one of the scribes of revelation but later committed treachery and was expelled by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Subsequently, he claimed, "I can produce the like of the verses of the Quran." A group of commentators has also stated that the verse, or a portion of it, was revealed regarding "Musaylimah al-Kadhdhab" (the Liar), who was one of the false claimants to prophethood. However, considering that the story of Musaylimah occurred toward the end of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life, and this Surah is among the Meccan Surahs, proponents of this occasion of revelation believe that this verse, like several other verses in this Surah, was revealed in Medina and placed among the verses of this Surah by the command of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

In any case, like other verses of the Quran revealed under specific circumstances, its content and message are general and universal, encompassing all claimants to prophethood and those like them.

Commentary

Following the previous verses which referred to the statements of the Jews regarding the denial of the revelation of any Heavenly Book to anyone, this verse speaks of other sinners who stand at the opposite extreme, claiming that divine revelation has been sent down to them while they are lying.

In truth, three groups of such individuals are mentioned in the verse under discussion.

First, it says: "And who is more unjust than one who invents a lie against Allah, or distorts a verse and changes a word from the words of God" (وَ مَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً).

The second group are "those who claim prophethood and revelation while they are not prophets and no revelation has been sent down to them" (أَوْ قالَ أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ وَ لَمْ يُوحَ إِلَيْهِ شَيْ‌ءٌ).

The third group are those who, by way of denying the prophethood of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله or out of mockery, say: "We too can reveal the like of these verses," while they are lying and possess not the slightest power to do so (وَ مَنْ قالَ سَأُنْزِلُ مِثْلَ ما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ‌).

Indeed, all of these are wrongdoers, and none is more unjust than them, for they block the path of truth to the servants of Allah and leave them wandering in the wilderness. They fight against the leadership of the true leaders; they are both misguided themselves and lead others into misguidance. What injustice is greater than individuals who lack the competence for leadership claiming leadership—and that too, a Divine and Heavenly leadership?

Although the verse pertains to claimants of prophethood and revelation, its spirit encompasses all those who falsely claim a position for which they are not worthy.

Then, it describes the painful punishment of such individuals as follows: "If you, O Prophet, could see these wrongdoers when they are submerged in the agonies of death and the throes of passing away, while the angels of death have stretched out their hands saying to them, 'Deliver up your souls,' you would see that their state is extremely painful and deplorable" (وَ لَوْ تَرى‌ إِذِ الظَّالِمُونَ فِي غَمَراتِ الْمَوْتِ وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ باسِطُوا أَيْدِيهِمْ أَخْرِجُوا أَنْفُسَكُمُ‌) [1]. At this moment, the angels of punishment say to them: "Today you will be recompensed with a humiliating punishment because of two actions: first, because you spoke lies against Allah, and second, because you did not submit to His verses" (الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذابَ الْهُونِ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَقُولُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ غَيْرَ الْحَقِّ وَ كُنْتُمْ عَنْ آياتِهِ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ‌).

Here, several points should be noted

  1. False claimants to prophethood and fraudulent leaders are introduced in the verse as the worst of wrongdoers. In reality, there is no greater injustice than stealing someone's thoughts, destroying their beliefs, blocking their path to salvation, and making them an intellectual colony of one's own.

  2. The phrase باسِطُوا أَيْدِيهِمْ (stretching out their hands) may mean that the angels of death are ready to seize their souls with open hands, or it may mean stretching out their hands to begin their punishment.

  3. أَخْرِجُوا أَنْفُسَكُمُ‌ ("Deliver up your souls"): This is actually a form of humiliation from the angels of death toward these wrongdoers. Otherwise, surrendering the soul is not the act of the wrongdoers themselves but the work of the angels. It is similar to when a criminal is told at the moment of execution, "Now die!" In any case, this humiliation is seemingly in response to the humiliation they showed toward the verses of Allah, His prophets, and His servants. Furthermore, this verse is another piece of evidence for the independence of the soul and its separation from the body.

Additionally, it is inferred from this verse that the punishment for this type of sinner begins from the very moment of death and the passing of the soul.

[^1]: Ghamarat is the plural of ghamrah (on the pattern of darbah), which originally means erasing the traces of something. It later came to mean abundant water that covers the entire surface of something, and it is also applied to the hardships and calamities that swallow a person.

Ayah

وَلَقَدْ جِئْتُمُونَا فُرَٰدَىٰ كَمَا خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَتَرَكْتُم مَّا خَوَّلْنَـٰكُمْ وَرَآءَ ظُهُورِكُمْ ۖ وَمَا نَرَىٰ مَعَكُمْ شُفَعَآءَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ أَنَّهُمْ فِيكُمْ شُرَكَـٰٓؤُا۟ ۚ لَقَد تَّقَطَّعَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنكُم مَّا كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ (٩٤)
‘Certainly you have come to Us alone, just as We created you the first time, and left behind whatever We had bestowed on you. We do not see your intercessors with you—those whom you claimed to be [Our] partners in [deciding] you[r] [fate]. Certainly all links between you have been cut, and what you used to claim has forsaken you!’ (94)

Occasion of Revelation

In Tafsir al-Mizan, Tafsir al-Tabari, and Tafsir al-Alusi, it is narrated that one of the polytheists named Nadr bin al-Harith said: "Lat and Uzza (two great and famous idols of the Arabs) will intercede for me on the Day of Resurrection." The above verse was revealed and provided an answer to him and those like him.

Commentary

The Lost Ones

In the previous verse, a portion of the states of the wrongdoers at the threshold of death was mentioned. In this verse, the words that Allah says to them at the time of death or upon entering the scene of Resurrection are reflected.

At the beginning, He says: "And truly you have come to Us alone, just as We created you the first time" (وَ لَقَدْ جِئْتُمُونا فُرادى‌ كَما خَلَقْناكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ).

"And you have left behind your backs all that We had bestowed upon you" which were your supports in life, and you have come empty-handed (وَ تَرَكْتُمْ ما خَوَّلْناكُمْ وَراءَ ظُهُورِكُمْ‌) [1]. Furthermore, "We do not see with you your intercessors whom you claimed were partners [with Allah] in your affairs" (وَ ما نَرى‌ مَعَكُمْ شُفَعاءَكُمُ الَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ أَنَّهُمْ فِيكُمْ شُرَكاءُ).

In reality, "The bond between you has been severed, and all ties have been cut off from you" (لَقَدْ تَقَطَّعَ بَيْنَكُمْ‌).

"And all that you used to claim and rely upon has gone astray from you and is lost" (وَ ضَلَّ عَنْكُمْ ما كُنْتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ‌).

The Arab polytheists and idolaters relied on three things: the "tribe and clan" to which they belonged, the "wealth and riches" they had gathered for themselves, and the "idols" they imagined to be partners with Allah in determining human destiny and intercessors before Him. In each of the three phrases of the verse, one of these three subjects is addressed, showing how at the time of death, all of them bid farewell to man and leave him completely alone.

Here, two points must be noted:

1- Based on the placement of this verse following the previous verse—which described the dialogue of the angels of death at the time of passing—and also considering the phrase "you have left your wealth behind your backs," it is inferred that this address is also spoken to them at the time of death, but from Allah. However, it is understood from some narrations that this address will take place upon entering the scene of Resurrection; of course, there is not much difference regarding the primary objective of the verse.

2- Although this verse was revealed concerning the Arab polytheists, it is certainly not exclusive to them.

On that day, generally, all material bonds and interests, all imaginary and fabricated deities, and all the supports that man has built for himself in this world—thinking they would be his helpers on the day of misfortune—will be separated from him. He remains with his deeds, he remains with his God, and the rest will vanish, and in the expression of the Quran, they "go astray" (become lost), meaning they will be so insignificant, base, and unrecognizable that they will not even be seen!

[1]: Khawwalnakum is derived from the root khawl (on the pattern of 'amal), which originally means something that requires guardianship, management, and administration. It is usually applied to the various properties and blessings that Allah bestows upon man.

Ayahs

۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ فَالِقُ ٱلْحَبِّ وَٱلنَّوَىٰ ۖ يُخْرِجُ ٱلْحَىَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَيِّتِ وَمُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَيِّتِ مِنَ ٱلْحَىِّ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُؤْفَكُونَ (٩٥)
Indeed Allah is the splitter of the grain and the pit. He brings forth the living from the dead and He brings forth the dead from the living. That is Allah! Then where do you stray? (95)
فَالِقُ ٱلْإِصْبَاحِ وَجَعَلَ ٱلَّيْلَ سَكَنًا وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ حُسْبَانًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ (٩٦)
Splitter of the dawn, He has made the night for rest, and the sun and the moon for calculation. That is the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing. (96)

Commentary

The Splitter of the Dawn

Once again, the discourse turns toward the polytheists, explaining the proofs of Monotheism (Tawhid) through eloquent expressions and vivid examples of the mysteries of the universe, the system of creation, and the wonders of existence.

In the first verse, reference is made to three of these wonders found on Earth, and in the second verse, to three celestial phenomena.

First, He says: "Indeed, Allah is the splitter of the grain and the date-stone" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ فالِقُ الْحَبِّ وَ النَّوى‌).

Faliq is derived from the root falq (rhyming with farq), meaning to split something and separate one part from another. [^1] Habb and habbah refer to edible grains and food seeds, such as wheat and barley, and that which is harvestable, [^2] though it is sometimes applied to the seeds of other plants as well.

Nawa means the stone or pit (of a fruit). The fact that some have said it specifically refers to the date-stone is perhaps because the Arabs, due to their specific environmental conditions, would think of the date-stone whenever this word was used.

Now let us see what subtle point lies within this expression.

It should be noted that the most critical moment in the life of a plant is the moment the grain or stone splits, which, like the birth of a child, is considered the time of transition from one world to another; the most significant transformation in its life occurs at this very instant.

Interestingly, the grains and stones of plants are often extremely hard. A look at a date-stone or the pits of fruits like peaches and nectarines, or the hard seeds of certain legumes, shows how that vital embryo—which is in reality a small sapling or tree—is besieged within an extraordinarily strong fortress. Yet, the system of creation grants such a quality of submission and softness to this impenetrable fortress, and such power and strength to that extremely delicate and fine sprout developing inside the stone or grain, that it can split its wall and rise up from within it. Truly, this event in the plant kingdom is a magnificent phenomenon upon which the Quran has placed its finger as a sign of Monotheism.

Then He says: He brings forth the living from the dead, and He is the bringer forth of the dead from the living (يُخْرِجُ الْحَيَّ مِنَ الْمَيِّتِ وَ مُخْرِجُ الْمَيِّتِ مِنَ الْحَيِ‌).

[^1] and [^2]: Mufradat al-Raghib, pages 385 and 105.

In reality, this sentence—the likes of which are frequently seen in the Quran—refers to the system of life and death and the conversion of one into the other. Sometimes, from lifeless organic matter, He brings forth various forms of life in the heart of the oceans, the depths of forests, and the reaches of deserts and wildernesses, creating life-sustaining substances from combinations of materials that are each individually a deadly poison. Conversely, with a slight alteration, He transforms powerful and mighty living beings into lifeless matter.

The issue of life and the existence of living beings, whether plant or animal, is among the most complex matters that human science and knowledge have yet to unveil or penetrate—how natural elements and organic matter, with a great leap, transform into a living being.

It is possible that one day humans, using various natural compounds under extremely complex conditions, might create a living being in the manner of assembling the parts of a pre-made machine. However, neither the current inability of man nor his potential future capability in this regard can diminish the importance of the subject of life and how its complex system testifies to an All-Knowing and All-Powerful Originator.

Therefore, we see that the Quran repeatedly relies on this issue to prove the existence of God. Great Prophets such as Ibrahim and Musa (peace be upon them), when facing tyrants like Nimrod and Pharaoh, argued through the phenomenon of life and its testimony to the existence of the Powerful and Wise Originator of the world.

Ibrahim says to Nimrod: "My Lord is He who gives life and causes death" (رَبِّيَ الَّذِي يُحْيِي وَ يُمِيتُ‌) (Al-Baqarah: 258). And Musa says before Pharaoh: "And He sent down water from the sky, and thereby We brought forth pairs of various plants" (وَ أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَخْرَجْنا بِهِ أَزْواجاً مِنْ نَباتٍ شَتَّى‌) (Taha: 53).

Of course, it must not be forgotten that the emergence of living beings from lifeless matter did not occur only at the beginning of life on Earth. Even now, through the absorption of water and other substances into the cells of living beings, the garment of life is effectively placed upon the frame of these lifeless materials. Thus, the established law in modern natural sciences—which states that under current terrestrial conditions, no lifeless matter transforms into a living being and that every living being must come from the egg or seed of another living being—does not contradict what we have said (reflect carefully).

From the narrations that have reached us from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام regarding the interpretation of this verse or similar verses, it is understood that life and death are not only physical but also encompass spiritual life and death. [^1] We see believers born to faithless fathers, and we observe wicked, tainted, and faithless individuals who descend from pure lineages, overriding the law of heredity through their own will and choice. This itself is another sign of the Creator's greatness—that He has granted such power of will to man.

Another point necessary to note here is that yukhriju (He brings forth), which is a present/future tense verb, like mukhrij (the bringer forth), which is an active participle, denotes continuity. That is, the system of life emerging from dead matter and the emergence of the dead from living beings is a permanent and universal system in the world of creation.

At the end of the verse, as an emphasis and consolidation of the matter, He says: "That is Allah, your Lord; so how are you deluded away from the truth?" (ذلِكُمُ اللَّهُ فَأَنَّى تُؤْفَكُونَ‌).

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Volume 2, Chapter on the Nature (Tinah) of the Believer and the Disbeliever; Tafsir al-Burhan, Volume 1, page 543.

In the second verse, as we mentioned, reference is made to three blessings from the atmospheric and celestial graces. First, He says: "He is the splitter of the dawn" (فالِقُ الْإِصْباحِ‌).

Falaq (rhyming with khalaq) originally means splitting, and the dawn is called falaq for this very reason. Isbah and subh both carry the same meaning.

The above expression is one of the most beautiful metaphors used here, for the darkness of night is likened to a thick curtain that the light of dawn tears asunder and splits open. This applies to both the "true dawn" (subh-e sadiq) and the "false dawn" (subh-e kadhib). The false dawn refers to the faint light that appears vertically in the east at the end of the night, appearing like a crack in the dark and black tent of the night from east to west. The true dawn, which rises after it, is like a beautiful, bright white ribbon that first appears across the eastern horizon, as if it splits the black veil of night from the bottom along the north-south axis and advances, gradually rising to cover the entire sky.

In addition to repeatedly emphasizing the blessing of light and darkness, and night and day, the Quran here emphasizes the phenomenon of the rising dawn, which is one of the Lord's great blessings. We know that this celestial phenomenon is a result of the Earth's atmosphere (the thick layer of air surrounding this globe). If there were no atmosphere around the Earth, like the Moon, there would be neither the twilight of dawn nor the glow of dusk. Instead, the sun would rise like an uninvited guest without any introduction from the eastern horizon, immediately splashing its blinding light into eyes accustomed to the darkness of night. At sunset, like a fleeing criminal, it would suddenly vanish from sight, and in an instant, a terrifying darkness would cover everywhere. However, the existence of the Earth's atmosphere and the interval between the darkness of night and the light of day during sunrise and sunset gradually prepares man to receive each of these two contrasting phenomena. The transition from light to darkness and from darkness to light occurs gradually, gently, and in a completely pleasant and tolerable manner. We have often seen in a brightly lit room at night how uncomfortable it feels when the light is suddenly turned off; and if this darkness lasts an hour, when the light is turned on without warning, another state of discomfort occurs. The blinding light of the lamp hurts the eyes, and we struggle to see surrounding objects. If this were repeated, it would certainly be harmful to the eyes. The Splitter of the Dawn has solved this problem for humanity in a most excellent way. [^1]

However, so that it is not imagined that the splitting of the dawn implies that the darkness of night is something undesirable or a punishment or deprivation of blessing, He immediately says: "And He made the night for rest" (وَ جَعَلَ اللَّيْلَ سَكَناً).

It is an established fact that in the presence of light, humans tend toward effort and activity; blood flow moves toward the surface of the body, and all cells become ready for action. For this reason, sleep in the presence of light is not very restful. The darker the environment, the deeper and more restful the sleep, because in darkness, blood moves toward the interior of the body, and cells generally fall into a state of calm and rest. For this reason, in the natural world, not only animals but even plants fall asleep during the darkness of night and begin their hustle and bustle with the first rays of morning. This is contrary to the industrial world, where people stay awake until after midnight and remain asleep for a long time after sunrise, thereby losing their vitality and health.

In the hadiths narrated through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, we read instructions that are all consistent with the spirit of this matter. Among them, it is narrated from Ali عليه السلام in Nahj al-Balagha that he instructed one of his companions: "Never continue your journey at the beginning of the night, for Allah has made the night for rest and has made it a time for staying, not for departing; keep your body calm at night and rest." [^1] In a hadith narrated in al-Kafi from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "Perform marriage ceremonies at night, for He has made the night for rest" (just as marriage and proper sexual intimacy are also sources of tranquility). [^2] We also read in al-Kafi that Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام would instruct his servants never to slaughter animals at night before the break of dawn, saying: "Indeed, Allah has made the night a source of rest for everything." [^3]

Then He points to the third blessing and sign of His greatness: "And [He made] the sun and the moon for calculation" (وَ الشَّمْسَ وَ الْقَمَرَ حُسْباناً).

[^1]: Astronomers say: Dawn begins when the sun reaches 18 degrees below the horizon in the east, and the darkness of night covers everywhere and the twilight disappears when the sun reaches 18 degrees below the horizon in the west. [^1], [^2], and [^3]: Tafsir al-Safi, under the verse.

Husban (rhyming with Luqman) is a verbal noun from the root hisab, meaning to calculate. Here, it may mean that the regular rotation and orderly movement of these two celestial bodies (referring to their movement as seen by us, which results from the Earth's motion) allows you to bring the various programs of your life under a system and calculation.

Some commentators have also suggested that the above phrase means that these two celestial bodies themselves are under a system, calculation, and program.

Thus, in the first case, it refers to one of Allah's blessings for mankind, and in the second case, it refers to one of the signs of Monotheism and proofs for the existence of God. It is possible that both meanings are intended.

In any case, it is very noteworthy that for millions of years, the Earth has orbited the sun and the moon has orbited the Earth. As a result, the solar disk appears to us on Earth to move through the twelve zodiacal constellations, and the lunar disk appears with its regular crescent and gradual, orderly fluctuations. This rotation is so calculated that it does not deviate by even a moment. If we consider the length of the Earth's path around the sun—moving in an elliptical orbit with an average radius of 150 million kilometers under the sun's immense gravitational force—and likewise the moon, which completes its circular path every month with an average radius of 384,000 kilometers while the Earth's immense gravity constantly pulls it, we will realize what a precise balance has been established between the gravitational force of these spheres on one hand and their centrifugal force on the other, such that no pause or variation occurs in their orderly movement. This is not possible except under the shadow of an Infinite Knowledge and Power that both designs the plan and executes it precisely.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, He says: "That is the determination of the All-Mighty, the All-Knowing" (ذلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ الْعَزِيزِ الْعَلِيمِ‌).

Ayah

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلنُّجُومَ لِتَهْتَدُوا۟ بِهَا فِى ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ ۗ قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (٩٧)
It is He who made the stars for you, so that you may be guided by them in the darkness of land and sea. We have certainly elaborated the signs for a people who have knowledge. (97)

Commentary

Following the previous verse which referred to the system of the sun and moon's rotation, here it points to another of the Lord's blessings, saying: "It is He who has placed the stars for you so that you may find your way by them in the darkness of the land and the sea, during dark nights" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ النُّجُومَ لِتَهْتَدُوا بِها فِي ظُلُماتِ الْبَرِّ وَ الْبَحْرِ). At the end of the verse, He says: "We have detailed the signs for a people who know" (قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ). For thousands of years, man has been familiar with the stars of the sky and their system; although the more human science and knowledge have advanced, the deeper he has entered into the understanding of this system, he has nonetheless always been more or less familiar with their positions. Therefore, for finding directions in sea and land travels, these very stars were his best means.

Especially in the vast oceans where there is no sign to find the way to the destination, and at a time when the compass had not yet been invented, there was no reliable means except the stars of the sky. It was they that saved millions of humans from wandering and drowning, leading them to their intended destination.

Continuous observation of the sky over several consecutive nights shows that the arrangement of the stars is uniform everywhere; it is as if the stars are like pearls sewn onto a black cloth, and this cloth is pulled from the east toward the west from the beginning of the night, and they all move with it, rotating around the Earth's axis without their distances changing. The only exception to this general rule is a number of stars called planets, which have their own independent and specific movements. The total number of these does not exceed eight, five of which are visible to the naked eye (Mercury, Venus, Saturn, Mars, and Jupiter), while the other three planets (Uranus, Neptune, and Pluto) can only be observed with astronomical telescopes (of course, considering that Earth is also one of the planets orbiting the sun, their total number reaches nine).

Perhaps even prehistoric humans were familiar with the state of the "fixed stars" and "planets," for there is no sight more interesting and enchanting for man than the view of the sky on a dark, starry night. For this reason, it is not unlikely that they also used the stars to find their path.

From some narrations transmitted through the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, it is understood that the above verse has another interpretation as well: that the "stars" refer to the Divine leaders and the guides of the path of salvation, namely the Imams, through whom people are saved from wandering in the darkness of life. As we have stated many times, such spiritual interpretations do not contradict the outward and physical interpretation of the verse, and it is possible that the verse refers to both aspects. [^1]

Ayahs

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ فَمُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمُسْتَوْدَعٌ ۗ قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَفْقَهُونَ (٩٨)
It is He who created you from a single soul, then there is the [enduring] abode and the place of temporary lodging. We have certainly elaborated the signs for a people who understand. (98)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَخْرَجْنَا بِهِۦ نَبَاتَ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ فَأَخْرَجْنَا مِنْهُ خَضِرًا نُّخْرِجُ مِنْهُ حَبًّا مُّتَرَاكِبًا وَمِنَ ٱلنَّخْلِ مِن طَلْعِهَا قِنْوَانٌ دَانِيَةٌ وَجَنَّـٰتٍ مِّنْ أَعْنَابٍ وَٱلزَّيْتُونَ وَٱلرُّمَّانَ مُشْتَبِهًا وَغَيْرَ مُتَشَـٰبِهٍ ۗ ٱنظُرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ ثَمَرِهِۦٓ إِذَآ أَثْمَرَ وَيَنْعِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكُمْ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٩٩)
It is He who sends down water from the sky, and brings forth with it every kind of growing thing. Then from it We bring forth vegetation from which We produce the grain, in clusters, and from the palm-tree, from the spathes of it, low-hanging clusters [of dates], and gardens of grapes, olives and pomegranates, similar and dissimilar. Look at its fruit as it fructifies and ripens. Indeed, there are signs in that for a people who have faith. (99)

Commentary

In these verses, the proofs of monotheism and the knowledge of God are further pursued. For this purpose, the Quran sometimes takes man on a journey through the horizons and distant worlds, and at other times invites him to journey within his own being, explaining the signs and tokens of the Lord within his body and soul, so that he may see God everywhere and in everything.

First, It says: "He is the One Who created you from a single soul."

(وَ هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْشَأَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ)

That is to say, despite all these diverse faces, different tastes and thoughts, and the vast variety in all aspects of existence, you were all created from a single individual. This demonstrates the ultimate greatness of the Creator—how He brought forth so many different countenances from a single origin.

It is noteworthy that in this sentence, the creation of man is expressed with the term "insha’" (انشاء). As understood from linguistic texts, this word means creation and innovation intertwined with nurturing and upbringing. This implies that not only did Allah create you without any prior precedent, but He also took upon Himself your nurturing and development. It is certain that if a creator creates something and then abandons it, he has not displayed much power; however, if he keeps it constantly under his protection and never neglects its development and nurturing for a single moment, he has fully demonstrated his greatness and mercy.

Furthermore, one should not fall into the misconception from the above sentence that "Eve," our first mother, was created from Adam (as stated in the Torah, Genesis, Chapter 2). Rather, since Adam and Eve, according to Islamic narrations, were both created from the same clay and are of the same genus and species, the term "single soul" (نفس واحدة) is applied to them (we also discussed this at the beginning of Surah An-Nisa).

Then He says: "Some of the human race are settled (mustaqarr) and some are deposited (mustawda’)." (فَمُسْتَقَرٌّ وَ مُسْتَوْدَعٌ)

Mustaqarr is originally derived from the root qarr (rhyming with harr), meaning coldness. Since extreme cold causes humans and other creatures to stay indoors, this word has come to mean stillness, stopping, and settling. Thus, mustaqarr means stable and enduring.

Mustawda’ is derived from the root wad’ (rhyming with man’), meaning to leave. Since unstable things soon leave their place, this word is also used to mean transient or unstable. A "deposit" (wadi’ah) is called such because it must leave its place and return to its original owner.

From the sum of what has been said, we conclude that the above verse says some humans are stable and some are unstable. There is much discussion among commentators as to what these two expressions mean here, but among them, several interpretations—which are not mutually exclusive and can all be accepted as explanations of the verse—seem closer to the truth:

First, mustaqarr refers to those humans whose creation is complete and who have stepped into the "settlement" of the mother's womb or onto the earth, while mustawda’ refers to individuals whose creation is not yet finished and are in the form of sperm in the loins of their fathers.

Second, mustaqarr refers to the human soul, which is a stable and enduring entity, while mustawda’ refers to the human body, which is unstable and perishable.

In some narrations, a spiritual interpretation for these two terms has also been mentioned: mustaqarr refers to those humans who possess stable faith, and mustawda’ refers to those whose faith is unstable [1].

There is also the possibility that the two expressions refer to the primary components forming the human embryo. As we know, the human embryo is formed from two parts: the "ovum" (female gamete) and the "sperm" (male gamete). The female gamete is relatively fixed and settled (mustaqarr) in the womb, while the male gametes move toward it with speed as mobile living entities, and the first sperm to reach the ovum merges with it, pushing the others back, forming the first human seed.

At the end of the verse, He says once more: "We have detailed the signs for a people who understand." (قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَفْقَهُونَ)

By referring to linguistics, it is understood that fiqh is not just any kind of knowledge or understanding, but rather deducing unknown information from present knowledge [2]. Therefore, paying attention to the creation of man with all these different faces and various physical and spiritual countenances is worthy of reflection by those who are perceptive, so they may know their God through it.

The second verse is the final verse in this series of discussions that invites us to the wonders of the world of creation and the recognition of God through them.

At the beginning, it points to one of the most important and fundamental blessings of the Lord, which can be considered the root and mother of all other blessings: the emergence and growth of plants and trees under its light. It says: "He is the One Who sent down water from the sky (for you)." (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً)

The reason He says "from the sky" (meaning from above, as "sky" in Arabic linguistics refers to anything situated above) is that all water resources on earth—including springs, rivers, aqueducts, and deep wells—ultimately lead back to rainwater. Thus, a lack of rain affects all of them, and if drought continues, they all dry up.

Then, pointing to the prominent effect of rainfall, He says: "By means of it, We brought forth the growth of every thing." (فَأَخْرَجْنا بِهِ نَباتَ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ)

Commentators have mentioned two possibilities in interpreting "the growth of every thing" (nabata kulli shay’): First, it refers to the various types and species of plants that are all irrigated by the same water and nurtured from the same earth and soil. This is among the wonders of creation: how all these types of plants, with their completely different and sometimes contradictory properties, and various shapes, are all nurtured in one land with one water.

Second, it refers to the plants needed by every thing; that is, each of the birds, quadrupeds, insects, and animals of the sea and desert has a share of these plants. Interestingly, Allah has provided the food needed by all from one earth and one water. This is a great masterpiece—for example, preparing thousands of types of food for various tastes and temperaments from one specific substance in a kitchen.

Even more interesting is that not only do the plants of the desert and land grow by the blessing of rainwater, but even the tiny plants that grow amidst the waves of the sea—which are the primary food for fish—grow under the light of the sun and the drops of rain. I do not forget a resident of the Persian Gulf islands who was complaining about the lack of catch; explaining the reason, he said: the shortage of fish was due to the drought, and he believed the life-giving effect of raindrops in the sea is even greater than on land!

Then, elaborating on this sentence, He points out important instances of plants and trees nurtured by rainwater. First, He says: "By means of it, We brought forth green stalks of plants from the earth, and from a small, dry seed, We created a fresh and green stalk whose delicacy and beauty dazzle the eye." (فَأَخْرَجْنا مِنْهُ خَضِراً) [1] "And from that green stalk, We bring forth grains arranged in layers (like ears of wheat and corn)." (نُخْرِجُ مِنْهُ حَبًّا مُتَراكِباً) [2]

"Also, by means of it, We sent forth from the date-palm, from its spathe, clusters of dates hanging low, which emerge after the splitting of the beautiful thin strands that carry the date fruits and lean downward due to their weight." (وَ مِنَ النَّخْلِ مِنْ طَلْعِها قِنْوانٌ دانِيَةٌ)

Tal’ (طلع) refers to the closed cluster of the date palm wrapped in a beautiful green sheath; after it splits, thin strands emerge from its center, which later form the date clusters. Qinwan (قنوان) is the plural of qinu, referring to these thin and delicate strands.

Daniyah (دانية) means near or close, and it may refer to the proximity of these strands to one another or to their leaning downward due to being heavily laden.

"Also, We nurtured gardens of grapes, olives, and pomegranates." (وَ جَنَّاتٍ مِنْ أَعْنابٍ وَ الزَّيْتُونَ وَ الرُّمَّانَ)

Then, pointing to another masterpiece of creation in these trees, He says: "They are both similar to one another and not similar." (مُشْتَبِهاً وَ غَيْرَ مُتَشابِهٍ)

With reference to verse 141 of this same Surah, which mentions the description of "similar and not similar" for olives and pomegranates, it is understood that in the verse under discussion, this description also pertains to these two trees [1]. These two trees are very similar in terms of their outward appearance and the structure of their branches and leaves, while they are very different in terms of fruit, taste, and properties. One contains a potent and powerful fatty substance, and the other contains acidic or sugary substances, which are completely different from each other. Moreover, these two trees sometimes grow in the exact same soil and are irrigated by the same water—meaning they are both very different and yet similar.

There is also the possibility in interpreting the verse that the above phrase refers to the various types and species of trees and fruits: some fruits and trees are similar to one another, and others are different. (That is, each of these two is a quality for a group of trees and fruits; however, according to the first interpretation, both were qualities for the same thing).

Then, from among all the parts of the tree's body, He focuses the discussion on the fruit, saying: "Look at the structure of its fruit when it bears fruit and also look at how the fruits ripen; indeed, in these are clear signs of Allah's power and wisdom for people who have certainty."

(انْظُرُوا إِلى‌ ثَمَرِهِ إِذا أَثْمَرَ وَ يَنْعِهِ إِنَّ فِي ذلِكُمْ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ)

Considering what we read today in botany regarding how fruits emerge and ripen, the reason for this special importance the Quran gives to fruit becomes clear. The emergence of fruits is exactly like the birth of offspring in the animal world: male gametes are separated from special sacs by specific means (wind or insects, etc.) and are placed upon the female part of the plant. After the process of fertilization and combination with each other, the first egg and seed are formed, and various types of nutrients, like flesh, surround it. These nutrients are very diverse in structure and exceptionally different in taste, nutritional properties, and medicinal qualities. Sometimes a single fruit (like a pomegranate or grape) contains hundreds of seeds, each of which is considered an embryo and the seed of a tree, possessing a very complex and layered structure.

Explaining the structure of all fruits and their nutritional and medicinal substances is beyond the scope of this discussion, but it is not out of place to mention, as an example, the structure of the pomegranate fruit, which the Quran specifically points to in the above verse.

If we split a pomegranate and take one small seed of it, holding it against the sun or a lamp and looking closely, we see it is composed of smaller parts that are arranged like tiny bottles containing pomegranate juice. In one small seed of pomegranate, there are perhaps hundreds of these tiny bottles. Then, they are enclosed by a delicate skin, which is the transparent skin of a pomegranate seed. Then, to make this packaging more complete, stronger, and further from danger, a number of pomegranate seeds are arranged on a base with a specific order, and a relatively thick white membrane is wrapped around them. Finally, a thick and strong skin, which has a special glaze on both sides, is drawn over all of them to prevent the penetration of air and microbes, to protect them against impacts, and to significantly reduce the evaporation of water within the seeds. This delicate packaging is not exclusive to pomegranate seeds but is also seen in other fruits like oranges and lemons, though in pomegranates and grapes, it is much more delicate and fascinating.

It is as if man used this same principle to send liquids from one point to another: first arranging small bottles in a small carton and filling the space between them with soft material, then placing the smaller cartons in a larger carton and transporting the whole as a large load to the destination! The way pomegranate seeds are positioned on the internal bases and receive their share of water and nutrients from them is even more strange and fascinating. Furthermore, these are things we see with the naked eye; if we were to place the particles of these fruits under microscopes, a bustling world with strange, amazing, and exceptionally calculated structures would be visualized before our eyes. How is it possible for someone to look at a fruit with the eye of truth and believe that its maker had no knowledge or wisdom?! And the fact that we see the Quran commanding us with the phrase unzhuru (look) to be precise in this part of the plant is to draw attention to these very truths.

This is on one hand; on the other hand, the various stages a fruit goes through from when it is unripe until it is fully ripe are very noteworthy. The internal laboratories of the fruit are constantly at work, regularly changing its chemical composition until it reaches the final stage and its chemical structure becomes stabilized. Each of these stages is itself a sign of the greatness and power of the Creator.

However, it must be noted that, as the Quran expresses, only people of faith—that is, those who see the truth and seekers of reality—see these matters; otherwise, with the eye of enmity and stubbornness, or with indifference and negligence, it is impossible to see any of these truths.

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 750.

[2]: Mufradat al-Raghib, p. 384.

[1]: Khadir (خضر) means akhdar (green), and thus it includes all greenery, even the buds of trees; however, since the above verse is followed by a reference to food grains, it is understood that it specifically refers to crops.

[2]: Mutarakib is from the root rukub, meaning riding; that is, grains that are "riding" on top of one another, which is mostly the case with food grains.

Ayahs

وَجَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ شُرَكَآءَ ٱلْجِنَّ وَخَلَقَهُمْ ۖ وَخَرَقُوا۟ لَهُۥ بَنِينَ وَبَنَـٰتٍۭ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ (١٠٠)
They make the jinn partners of Allah, though He has created them, and carve out sons and daughters for Him, without any knowledge. Immaculate is He and exalted above what they allege [concerning Him]! (100)
بَدِيعُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌ وَلَمْ تَكُن لَّهُۥ صَـٰحِبَةٌ ۖ وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (١٠١)
The originator of the heavens and the earth—how could He have a child when He has had no spouse? He created all things and He has knowledge of all things. (101)
ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ خَـٰلِقُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَكِيلٌ (١٠٢)
That is Allah, your Lord, there is no god except Him, the creator of all things; so worship Him. He watches over all things. (102)
لَّا تُدْرِكُهُ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱللَّطِيفُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ (١٠٣)
The sights do not apprehend Him, yet He apprehends the sights, and He is the All-attentive, the All-aware. (103)

Commentary

He is the Creator of All Things

In these verses, reference is made to a portion of the incorrect beliefs and superstitions of the polytheists and followers of false religions, followed by a logical response to them.

First, it says: "They attributed partners to Allah from among the jinn" (وَ جَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ الْجِنَّ). Regarding whether "jinn" here refers to its literal meaning—hidden beings concealed from human senses—or specifically to the race of jinn that the Quran frequently discusses (which we will refer to soon), commentators have offered two possibilities.

According to the first possibility, the verse may refer to those who worshipped angels or any other unseen beings. According to the second possibility, the verse refers to those who considered the race of jinn as partners of Allah or His spouses.

"Kalbi" narrates in the book Al-Asnam that a tribe of Arabs called "Banu Malih," a branch of the "Khuza'ah" tribe, worshipped the jinn [^1]. It is even said that the worship of jinn and belief in their divinity existed among the superstitious religions of ancient Greece and India [^2]. As inferred from verse 158 of Surah As-Saffat—وَ جَعَلُوا بَيْنَهُ وَ بَيْنَ الْجِنَّةِ نَسَباً (And they claimed a kinship between Him and the jinn)—there were those among the Arabs who attributed a type of kinship between the jinn and Allah.

[^1]: Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 3, p. 326 (footnote). [^2]: Tafsir Al-Manar, Vol. 8, p. 648.

As many commentators have narrated, the Quraysh believed that Allah had married the jinn and that the angels were the fruit of this marriage! [^1] Then, the verse responds to this superstitious notion, saying: "Even though Allah created them (i.e., the jinn)" (وَ خَلَقَهُمْ).

That is to say, how can a creature be a partner to its Creator? Partnership implies being of the same kind or on the same level, whereas a creature can never be on the level of the Creator.

Another superstition is that "they attributed to Him sons and daughters out of ignorance" (وَ خَرَقُوا لَهُ بَنِينَ وَ بَناتٍ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ).

In truth, the best evidence for the falsehood of such superstitious beliefs is found in the phrase "without knowledge" (بغير علم); meaning they had no evidence or sign for these delusions.

It is noteworthy that "kharaqu" is derived from the root "kharq," which originally means tearing something without a plan or calculation—the exact opposite of "khalq," which is the creation of something based on calculation. These two words (khalq and kharq) are sometimes used regarding fabricated and false matters; however, calculated lies that are constructed with study are called "khalq and ikhtilaq," while uncalculated and "outlandish" lies are called "kharq and ikhtiraq."

This means they fabricated these lies without studying the aspects of the matter and without considering its implications.

As for which groups attributed sons to Allah, the Quran mentions two groups in other verses: one is the Christians, who believed Jesus is the son of God, and the other is the Jews, who considered Ezra (Uzayr) His son.

As can be inferred generally from verse 30 of Surah At-Tawbah, and as a group of contemporary researchers have discovered by studying the common roots of Christianity and Buddhism—especially regarding the issue of the Trinity—the belief in the existence of a son for God was not limited to Christians and Jews, but also existed among earlier superstitious religions.

Regarding the belief in the existence of daughters for God, the Quran itself clarifies the matter in other verses, saying: وَ جَعَلُوا الْمَلائِكَةَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ عِبادُ الرَّحْمنِ إِناثاً: "And they made the angels, who are servants of the Most Merciful, females" (Az-Zukhruf, verse 19).

As we mentioned above, it is stated in commentaries and histories that the Quraysh tribe believed the angels were children born from the marriage of Allah with the jinn!

At the end of this verse, the Quran draws a red line through all these superstitious matters and baseless delusions, negating all these falsehoods with an eloquent and awakening phrase: "Glory be to Him! He is high above what they describe" (سُبْحانَهُ وَ تَعالى‌ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ).

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan and other commentaries under the verse.

In the following verse, it proceeds to answer these superstitious beliefs, first saying: "Allah is the One who originated and created the heavens and the earth" (بَدِيعُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ). Has anyone else done such a thing or does anyone have the power to do so, such that they should be considered His partner in worship? No, all are creatures, submissive to His command and in need of His Pure Essence.

Furthermore, how could He have a son when He has no spouse? (أَنَّى يَكُونُ لَهُ وَلَدٌ وَ لَمْ تَكُنْ لَهُ صاحِبَةٌ).

Fundamentally, what need does He have for a spouse? Moreover, who could possibly be His spouse when all are His creatures? Beyond all this, His Holy Essence is pure and sanctified from physical attributes, and having a spouse and child is one of the clear physical and material attributes.

Once again, it emphasizes His status as the Creator of everything and everyone, and His scientific encompassment of all of them, saying: "He created everything and He is All-Knowing of everything" (وَ خَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ وَ هُوَ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عَلِيمٌ).

In the third verse under discussion, after mentioning His creation of everything, His origination of the heavens and the earth, His being sanctified from physical attributes, spouses, and children, and His scientific encompassment of every deed and every thing, it concludes: "Such is Allah, your Lord; since no one else possesses such attributes, no one besides Him is worthy of worship. He is the Lord and He is the Creator; therefore, He alone can be the One worshipped. So worship Him" (ذلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ فَاعْبُدُوهُ).

At the end of the verse, to cut off any hope in anyone other than Allah and to burn the root of any polytheism or general reliance on others, it says: "He is the Guardian, Protector, and Disposer of all things" (وَ هُوَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ وَكِيلٌ).

Therefore, the key to solving your problems is only in His hand, and no one besides Him has the power to do this, because everyone else is in need and has their eyes fixed on His grace. In such a state, it makes no sense for someone to take their problems to another and seek their solution from them.

It is noteworthy that here it says عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ وَكِيلٌ ('ala kulli shay'in wakil) rather than li-kulli shay'in wakil. The difference between the two is clear: the use of the word "'ala" (upon/over) is evidence of His authority and the influence of His command, whereas the use of the word "lam" (for) would indicate following or subordination.

In other words, the first expression means guardianship and preservation, while the second expression would mean representation.

In the last verse under discussion, to prove His sovereignty and guardianship over everything, and also to prove His distinction from all beings, it says: "Vision perceives Him not, but He perceives all vision; and He is the Bestower of all kinds of blessings, Aware of all subtleties, and Cognizant of everything." He knows the interests of His servants, is aware of their needs, and treats them according to His grace (لا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصارُ وَ هُوَ يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصارُ وَ هُوَ اللَّطِيفُ الْخَبِيرُ).

In truth, whoever wishes to be the Protector, Sustainer, and Refuge of all things must possess these attributes.

Furthermore, this sentence is proof that He differs from all beings in the world; for some of them both see and are seen, like humans; some neither see nor are seen, like our internal traits; others are seen but do not see anyone, like inanimate objects. The only One who is not seen but sees everything and everyone is His Pure Essence.

Key Points

1- Eyes Do Not See Allah Intellectual proofs testify that Allah will never be seen with the eye, because the eye only sees bodies—or more accurately, some of their qualities—and that which is not a body and does not possess the qualities of a body will never be observed with the eye. In other words, if something is seen with the eye, it must certainly possess a place, a direction, and matter, whereas He is above all these. He is an infinite existence and for this reason is higher than the material world, because in the material world, everything is limited.

In many verses of the Quran, including those regarding the Children of Israel and their request to see Allah, the possibility of seeing Allah is denied with complete clarity (as the explanation of this will come, God willing, in the commentary of verse 143 of Surah Al-A'raf).

It is strange that many of the Sunnis believe that if Allah is not seen in this world, He will be seen in the world of Resurrection! According to the author of Tafsir Al-Manar: "This is the school of the People of the Sunnah and the scholars of Hadith" [^1]. Even stranger is that even their contemporary researchers and so-called intellectuals show an inclination toward this subject and sometimes even stand by it stubbornly! This is while the falsehood of this belief is so clear that it needs no discussion, for this world and the hereafter (considering the physical resurrection) will have no difference in this matter. Will Allah, who is an existence beyond matter, turn into a material existence in the Resurrection and decline from that infinite status to a limited one? Will He on that day turn into a body or the attributes of a body? And do the intellectual proofs for the impossibility of seeing Allah make any distinction between this world and the hereafter? This is while the judgment of intellect in this field is unchangeable.

The excuse some of them have brought—that it is possible for man to gain a different perception and vision in the other world—is a completely unjustified excuse. For if by this perception and vision they mean intellectual and rational perception, that exists in this world as well, and we observe the beauty of God with the eye of the heart and the power of intellect. But if they mean something with which a body can be seen, such a thing is impossible regarding Allah, whether in this world or in another world. Therefore, the aforementioned statement—that man does not see God in this world but the believers will see God in the Resurrection—is an illogical and unacceptable statement.

The only thing that has caused them to generally defend this belief is that in some hadiths narrated in their famous books, the possibility of seeing Allah in the Resurrection is mentioned. But would it not be better to consider the falsehood of this subject by the judgment of intellect as proof of the fabrication of those narrations and the lack of credibility of the books in which such narrations appear? Unless we interpret these narrations to mean observation with the eye of the heart. Is it correct to bid farewell to the judgment of reason and intellect for the sake of such hadiths?
