And if in some verses of the Quran there are expressions that at first glance suggest the issue of seeing Allah, such as: (وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ ناضِرَةٌ إِلى‌ رَبِّها ناظِرَةٌ) [^1]: "Faces on that day will be radiant, looking toward their Lord"—these expressions are like يَدُ اللَّهِ فَوْقَ أَيْدِيهِمْ: "The hand of Allah is above their hands" [^2], which are metaphorical, for we know that no verse of the Quran will ever be contrary to the judgment and command of reason.

[^1]: Tafsir Al-Manar, Vol. 7, p. 653. [^1]: Surah Al-Qiyamah, verses 22 and 23. [^2]: Surah Al-Fath, verse 10.

Interestingly, in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, this superstitious belief is strongly denied, and its adherents are criticized with crushing expressions. For instance, one of the famous companions of Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام named "Hisham" says: I was with Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام when Mu'awiyah ibn Wahb (another friend of the Imam) entered and said: "O son of the Prophet, what do you say regarding the report narrated about the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله that he saw Allah? In what form did he see Him?! And also in another report narrated from him that the believers will see their Lord in Paradise, in what form will they see Him?!" Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام smiled (bitterly) and said: "O Mu'awiyah ibn Wahb! How ugly it is for a man to live seventy or eighty years, live in the kingdom of Allah and eat His bounty, yet not know Him correctly. O Mu'awiyah! The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله never observed Allah with this eye. Observation is of two kinds: observation with the eye of the heart and observation with the physical eye. Whoever speaks of observation with the eye of the heart has spoken the truth, and whoever speaks of observation with the physical eye has lied and disbelieved in Allah and His verses. For the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: Whoever likens Allah to the creation is a disbeliever" [^1]. In another narration in the book At-Tawhid by Saduq, narrated from Isma'il ibn Fadl, he says: I asked Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, "Will Allah be seen in the Resurrection?" He said: "Glory be to Allah from such a thing, and He is greatly exalted... Eyes do not perceive except that which has color and quality, while Allah is the Creator of colors and qualities" [^2].

[^1]: Ma'ani al-Akhbar, as cited in Al-Mizan, Vol. 8, p. 268. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 753.

Interestingly, in this hadith, emphasis is specifically placed on the word "color" (lawn). Today, it is clear to us that the body itself is never seen; rather, its color is always seen. If a body had no color, it would never be seen.

(In Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 156, under verse 46 of Surah Al-Baqarah, we have also discussed this field).

2- Allah's Creatorship of All Things

Some Sunni commentators who follow the school of predestination (jabr) have used the above verse, which states Allah's creatorship of all things, to argue for the path of predestination. They say: our actions and deeds are also among the "things" of this world, for "thing" (shay') is said of any kind of existence, whether material or non-material, whether essence or attribute. Therefore, when we say Allah is the Creator of all things, we must accept that He is also the Creator of our actions, and this is nothing but predestination.

However, proponents of free will and choice have a clear answer to such arguments: Allah's creatorship, even regarding our actions, does not contradict our choice. Our actions can be attributed to us and also to Allah. If we attribute them to Allah, it is because He has placed all the prerequisites at our disposal; He is the One who gave us power, strength, will, and choice. Therefore, since all prerequisites are from Him, our deeds can be attributed to Him and He can be considered their Creator. But from the perspective that the final decision is ours—we are the ones who use the God-given power and choice and choose one of the two sides: action or omission—from this aspect, the actions are attributed to us and we are responsible for them.

In philosophical terms, there are not two creators and two causes on the same level here; rather, they are in a vertical sequence (tuli). The existence of two complete causes on the same level makes no sense, but if they are vertical, there is no obstacle. Since our actions are the necessary result of the prerequisites God gave us, these results can be attributed to Him and also to the one who performed the actions.

This statement is exactly like a person who, to test his employees, leaves them free in their work, gives them full authority, and provides all the prerequisites for the work. It is obvious that the works they perform are considered the work of their boss from one perspective, but this does not strip the employees of their freedom and choice; rather, they are responsible for their own work. Regarding the belief in predestination and free will, it will be discussed in detail under the appropriate verses, God willing [^1].

3- What Does "Badi'" Mean?

As we mentioned above, the word "Badi'" means the bringer into existence of something without precedent; meaning Allah created the heavens and the earth without any prior matter or design or plan.

Here, some object: how is it possible for something to come into existence from nothing? We have discussed the answer to this objection in detail under verse 117 of Surah Al-Baqarah (Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 300). The summary is: when we say Allah brought beings into existence from non-existence, the concept is not that "non-existence" is the material forming the beings of the world, like saying a carpenter made a table from wood. Such a thing is certainly impossible, for "non-existence" cannot be the matter of "existence."

Rather, the meaning is that the beings of this world previously were not, then they came to be. This subject has no problem, and we gave examples for it in the first volume. We add here that in the environment of our mind and thought, we can bring beings into existence that were not in our mind at all before. There is no doubt that these mental beings have a type of existence for themselves, even if they are not like external existence; but ultimately, they exist in the horizon of our mind. If the existence of something after non-existence were impossible, what would be the difference between mental existence and external existence? Therefore, just as we create existences in our mind that were not there before, Allah does such a thing in the external world. A little reflection on this example or the examples given in the first volume can solve this problem.

[^1]: You may also refer to the book How to Know God?, the chapter on Predestination and Free Will.

4- What Does "Latif" Mean?

In the above verses, one of the attributes of Allah is mentioned as "Latif," which is from the root "lutf." When used regarding bodies, it means lightness as opposed to heaviness. When used regarding movements (harakat latifah), it means a small and fleeting movement. Sometimes it is said regarding very precise and subtle beings and works that cannot be perceived by the senses. If we describe Allah as Latif, it is in this sense: He is the Creator of unseen things and possesses actions that are beyond the reach of hearing power; He is very subtle-sighted and exceptionally precise.

An interesting hadith in this field is narrated from "Fath ibn Yazid Jurjani" from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, which is considered a scientific miracle. The hadith is that the Imam says: "The reason we say Allah is Latif is because He created subtle creatures and because He is aware of subtle, delicate, and unseen things. Do you not see the traces of His craftsmanship in subtle and non-subtle plants? And also in small creatures, animals, tiny insects, and things even smaller than that—beings that the eye can never encompass, and which are so small that their male and female, new and old, are not recognized. When we observe such matters... and consider what exists in the oceans and inside the bark of trees and in the deserts and plains... and that there are beings our eyes never see, and we cannot touch them with our hands, from all this we understand that their Creator is Latif" [^1]. This hadith, which refers to microbes and microscopic animals and was stated centuries before the birth of Pasteur, clarifies the interpretation of Latif. There is also the possibility in the interpretation of this word that Allah being Latif means His Pure Essence is such that it is never perceived by anyone's senses; therefore, He is Latif because no one is aware of His Essence, and He is Khabir because He is aware of everything.

This meaning is also referred to in some narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام [^2], and it should be noted that there is no obstacle to both meanings being intended by this word.

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, p. 93. [^2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 548.

Ayahs

قَدْ جَآءَكُم بَصَآئِرُ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ فَمَنْ أَبْصَرَ فَلِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَنْ عَمِىَ فَعَلَيْهَا ۚ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ عَلَيْكُم بِحَفِيظٍ (١٠٤)
[Say,] ‘Certainly insights have come to you from your Lord. So whoever sees, it is to the benefit of his own soul, and whoever remains blind, it is to its detriment, and I am not a keeper over you.’ (104)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ وَلِيَقُولُوا۟ دَرَسْتَ وَلِنُبَيِّنَهُۥ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (١٠٥)
Thus do We paraphrase the signs variously, lest they should say, ‘You have received instruction,’ and so that We may make it clear for a people who have knowledge. (105)
ٱتَّبِعْ مَآ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٠٦)
Follow that which has been revealed to you from your Lord, there is no god except Him, and turn away from the polytheists. (106)
وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَآ أَشْرَكُوا۟ ۗ وَمَا جَعَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَفِيظًا ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْهِم بِوَكِيلٍ (١٠٧)
Had Allah wished they would not have ascribed partners [to Him]. We have not made you a caretaker for them, nor is it your duty to watch over them. (107)

Commentary

Your Duty is Not to Compel

These verses are, in fact, a kind of summary and conclusion drawn from the preceding verses.

First, it says: "Clear proofs and signs regarding Monotheism, the knowledge of God, and the negation of any kind of polytheism—which are the source of insight and vision—have come to you from your Lord" (قَدْ جاءَكُمْ بَصائِرُ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ).

"Basa'ir" (بصائر) is the plural of "Basirah" (بصيرة), derived from the root "Basar" (بصر), meaning to see. However, it is usually used for intellectual and rational insight, and sometimes it is applied to all matters that lead to the understanding and comprehension of a subject. In the above verse, it carries the meaning of proof, witness, and evidence, encompassing the collection of arguments mentioned in the previous verses regarding the knowledge of God; rather, the entirety of the Quran is included within it.

Then, to clarify that these proofs reveal the truth sufficiently and possess a logical dimension, it says: "Those who behold the face of truth through these proofs have stepped toward their own benefit, and those who, like the blind, deprive themselves of observing it, have acted to their own loss" (فَمَنْ أَبْصَرَ فَلِنَفْسِهِ وَ مَنْ عَمِيَ فَعَلَيْها).

At the end of the verse, it speaks from the tongue of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "I am not a guardian or a keeper over you" (وَ ما أَنَا عَلَيْكُمْ بِحَفِيظٍ).

Regarding the meaning of this sentence, commentators have offered two possibilities: First, that I am not the keeper of your deeds, nor am I the observer or responsible for your actions; rather, Allah preserves everything and will give everyone their reward or punishment. My duty is only to convey the message and to strive as much as possible in the path of guiding the people.

The second is: I am not commissioned as your guardian to invite you to faith through compulsion and force; rather, my only duty is the logical expression of truths, and the final decision rests with you.

There is no obstacle to both meanings being intended by this word.

In the next verse, to emphasize the point that the final decision in choosing the path of truth or falsehood lies with the people themselves, it says: "Thus do We explain the signs and proofs in various forms and different appearances" (وَ كَذلِكَ نُصَرِّفُ الْآياتِ) [1]. However, a group rose in opposition and, without study or any proof, said: "You have learned these lessons from others (from the Jews, Christians, and their books)" (وَ لِيَقُولُوا دَرَسْتَ) [2]. But "another group, who possess the readiness to accept the truth, and are people of insight, knowledge, and awareness, see the face of truth through it and accept it" (وَ لِنُبَيِّنَهُ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ).

The accusation against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding his learning teachings from the Jews and Christians is a matter that was raised many times by the polytheists, and stubborn opponents still pursue it.

[1]: "Nusarrif" (نصرف) is from the root "Tasrif" (تصريف), meaning to transform and bring into different forms. This refers to the fact that the verses of the Quran were revealed with different tones and by utilizing all means of psychological influence for people who are at different levels in terms of thought, belief, and other social and psychological aspects.

[2]: The "Lam" in "Liyaqulu" (ليقولوا) is what is termed the "Lam of Consequence" (Lam al-Aqibah), which is brought to express the end result and outcome of something, though it was not the primary objective. "Darasta" (درست) is from the root "Dars" (درس), meaning to learn, and this was an accusation the polytheists leveled against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

While fundamentally, in the environment of the Arabian Peninsula, there were no schools, lessons, or sciences for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to learn, and the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله travels outside the peninsula were so brief that there is no room for such a possibility. Furthermore, the knowledge of the Jews and Christians residing in the Hijaz environment was so meager and mixed with superstitions that it was not comparable at all to the Quran and the teachings of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. Further explanation on this subject will be noted, God willing, under verse 103 of Surah An-Nahl.

Then, it defines the duty of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the face of the stubbornness, malice, and accusations of the opponents, saying: "Your duty is to follow what is revealed to you from your Lord—the God besides whom there is no deity" (اتَّبِعْ ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ). And also: "Your duty is not to pay heed to the polytheists and their undue attributions and baseless words" (وَ أَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْمُشْرِكِينَ).

In reality, this verse is a kind of consolation and spiritual strengthening for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, so that in the face of such opponents, not the slightest weakness occurs in his firm and iron resolve.

From what we have said, it becomes clearly evident that the phrase "Turn away from the polytheists" (and do not pay them heed) does not contradict in any way the command to invite them to Islam or to engage in Jihad against them; rather, the meaning is that he should not pay attention to their baseless words and accusations and should remain steadfast in his path.

In the last verse under discussion, it confirms once more the truth that Allah does not want to force them into faith by compulsion, and "If He had willed, they would not have associated partners with Him, and no one would have become a polytheist" (وَ لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ ما أَشْرَكُوا). It also emphasizes: "You are not responsible for their deeds, and you were not sent to compel them toward faith" (وَ ما جَعَلْناكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَفِيظاً).

Just as "You do not have the duty to force them toward good deeds" (وَ ما أَنْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِوكِيلٍ).

The difference between "Hafiz" (حفيظ) and "Wakil" (وكيل) is that "Hafiz" refers to someone who guards a person or thing and protects them from loss and harm, whereas "Wakil" refers to someone who strives and makes an effort to secure benefits for someone.

Perhaps it is unnecessary to mention that the negation of these two attributes (Hafiz and Wakil) from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله means the negation of compelling them to repel harm or attract benefit; otherwise, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, through preaching and inviting toward good deeds and leaving evil deeds, performs these two duties regarding them in a voluntary manner.

The tone of these verses is very noteworthy from the perspective that faith in God and the foundations of Islam cannot have any aspect of imposition; rather, it must advance through logic, reasoning, and influence over the thoughts and souls of individuals. This is because compulsory faith has no value; what matters is that people understand the truths and accept them by their own will and choice.

The Quran has emphasized this truth many times in various verses, declaring the alienation of Islam from violent acts such as those of the Church in the Middle Ages [1] and the Inquisition and the like. And God willing, at the beginning of Surah Al-Bara'ah (At-Tawbah), the reasons for Islam's strictness toward polytheists will be discussed.

[1]: The "Middle Ages" refers to a thousand-year period that began in the 6th century AD and ended in the 15th century. This period is one of the darkest eras that passed over Europe and Christianity, and it is interesting that the Golden Age of Islam is situated exactly in the middle of the Middle Ages.

Ayah

وَلَا تَسُبُّوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ فَيَسُبُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَدْوًۢا بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ زَيَّنَّا لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ عَمَلَهُمْ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِم مَّرْجِعُهُمْ فَيُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٠٨)
Do not abuse those whom they invoke besides Allah, lest they should abuse Allah out of hostility, without any knowledge. That is how to every people We have made their conduct seem decorous. Then their return will be to their Lord and He will inform them concerning what they used to do. (108)

Commentary

Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding the logical nature of Islamic teachings and the necessity of inviting others through reasoning rather than compulsion, this verse emphasizes: "Never insult the idols and deities of the polytheists, for this action will cause them to likewise treat the Holy Presence of Allah with the same disrespect out of injustice, oppression, and ignorance" (وَ لا تَسُبُّوا الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ فَيَسُبُّوا اللَّهَ عَدْواً بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ).

As derived from certain narrations, a group of believers, due to their intense distress over the issue of idolatry, would sometimes lash out at the idols of the polytheists and insult them. The Quran explicitly forbade this matter and considers the observance of the principles of etiquette, chastity, and decency in speech to be necessary, even when facing the most superstitious and worst of religions.

The reason for this is clear: one cannot turn someone away from a wrong path through insults and vitriol. On the contrary, the intense prejudice mixed with ignorance found in such individuals causes them to become stubborn and more entrenched in their false creed. Worse yet, they may open their tongues to slander and insult the Holy Presence of the Lord. This is because every group and nation possesses a sense of attachment to its own beliefs and actions, as the Quran states in the following phrase: "Thus We have made fair-seeming to every community their deeds" (كَذلِكَ زَيَّنَّا لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ عَمَلَهُمْ).

At the end of the verse, He says: "Then to their Lord is their return, and He will inform them of what they used to do" (ثُمَّ إِلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ مَرْجِعُهُمْ فَيُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

Here, three points must be noted

1- In the above verse, the "making fair-seeming" (beautifying) of the good and bad deeds of every person in their own eyes is attributed to Allah. This might be a source of surprise for some: is it possible for Allah to make the bad deeds of someone appear beautiful to them?! The answer to this question is what we have stated many times: such expressions refer to the inherent property and effect of the action itself. That is, when a human being performs an act repeatedly, its vileness and ugliness gradually vanish in their eyes, and it may even appear attractive to them. Since Allah is the Cause of causes, the Originator of means, and the Creator of all things, and all effects ultimately trace back to Him, such consequences are sometimes attributed to Him in the language of the Quran (reflect deeply).

To put it more clearly, the phrase زَيَّنَّا لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ عَمَلَهُمْ means that We left them to face the evil consequences of their actions until the ugly appeared beautiful in their sight.

From this, it becomes clear that the fact that in some verses of the Quran the beautifying of deeds is attributed to Satan does not contradict this point. This is because Satan whispers to them to perform the evil deed, and they submit to Satan's whispers, eventually becoming entangled in the grim consequences of their actions. In scientific terms, the "causality" is from Allah's side (as the law of nature), but the "creation of the cause" is by these individuals and through satanic whispers (reflect deeply). [^1]

2- In Islamic narrations, the logic of the Quran regarding the abandonment of insulting the misguided and deviant has been followed. The great leaders of Islam commanded Muslims to always rely on logic and reasoning and not to resort to the futile weapon of insulting the beliefs of opponents. In Nahj al-Balagha, we read that Ali عليه السلام said to a group of his companions who were insulting the followers of Mu'awiyah during the Battle of Siffin: "I dislike for you to be cursers. Rather, if you were to describe their deeds and mention their state, it would be more correct in speech and more effective in providing an excuse (establishing proof)." [^2]

[^1]: In the verses of the Quran, the beautifying of evil and bad deeds is attributed to Satan in 8 instances; in 10 instances it appears in the passive form (zuyyina - "was made fair-seeming"); and in two instances it is attributed to Allah. Given what was explained above, the meaning of all three becomes clear.

[^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 206, Subhi al-Salih edition.

3- Sometimes the objection is raised: how is it possible for idolaters to speak ill of Allah when they generally believed in "Allah" (God) and considered the idols to be intercessors before Him?

However, if we examine the condition of the stubborn and prejudiced masses, we will see that this is not so surprising. When such individuals become upset, they try to hurt the opposing party at any cost, even if it is through insulting beliefs that both parties hold in common. "Alusi," the famous Sunni scholar, narrates in the commentary Ruh al-Ma'ani that a certain ignorant commoner, upon seeing some Shiites insulting the Shaykhayn (the first two Caliphs), became enraged and began to insult the status of Ali عليه السلام. When he was asked, "Why do you insult Ali عليه السلام, whom you respect?"

He replied: "I wanted to upset the Shiites, for I found nothing more distressing to them than this!" Later, they compelled him to repent for this act. [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Alusi, Vol. 7, p. 218.

Ayahs

وَأَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ لَئِن جَآءَتْهُمْ ءَايَةٌ لَّيُؤْمِنُنَّ بِهَا ۚ قُلْ إِنَّمَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَا يُشْعِرُكُمْ أَنَّهَآ إِذَا جَآءَتْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٠٩)
They swear by Allah with solemn oaths that were a sign to come to them they would surely believe in it. Say, ‘These signs are only from Allah,’ and what will bring home to you that they will not believe even if they came? (109)
وَنُقَلِّبُ أَفْـِٔدَتَهُمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرَهُمْ كَمَا لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَنَذَرُهُمْ فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (١١٠)
We transform their hearts and their visions as they did not believe in it the first time, and We leave them bewildered in their rebellion. (110)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of commentators have narrated regarding the occasion of revelation for this verse that a number of the Quraysh came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "You narrate extraordinary feats and significant miracles for Moses and Jesus, as well as for other prophets; perform similar acts for us so that we may believe." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked: "What would you like me to do for you?" They replied: "Ask God to turn Mount Safa into gold, and let some of our deceased ancestors be resurrected so we may question them regarding the truthfulness of your mission. Also, show us the angels so they may bear witness to you, or bring Allah and the angels before us all together!..."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked: "If I perform some of these things, will you believe?"

They said: "By Allah, we shall do so." When the Muslims saw the insistence of the polytheists in this regard, they requested the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to do so, hoping they might believe. Just as the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله prepared to pray to ask God for some of these requests (as some of them were irrational and impossible), the messenger of divine revelation descended. He brought the message that if you wish, your request will be granted, but in that case (since the proof will be completed in every respect and the matter will take on a sensory and witnessed form), if they do not believe, they will all be severely punished (and destroyed). However, if their request is not granted and you leave them to themselves, it is possible that some of them may repent in the future and follow the path of truth. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله accepted, and the above verses were revealed.

Commentary

In the preceding verses, several logical arguments regarding Monotheism (Tawhid) were mentioned, which were sufficient to prove the oneness of God and negate polytheism and idolatry. Nevertheless, a group of stubborn and prejudiced polytheists did not submit and began making excuses. Among these, they demanded strange and extraordinary feats from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—some of which were fundamentally impossible—and falsely claimed that their goal was to see such miracles and then believe. The Quran narrates their situation in the first verse as follows:

"And they swore by Allah their strongest oaths that if a miracle came to them, they would surely believe in it" (وَ أَقْسَمُوا بِاللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ لَئِن جاءَتْهُمْ آيَةٌ لَيُؤْمِنُنَّ بِها) [^1]. In response to them, the Quran states two truths: First, it declares to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to tell them that this matter is not in my hands to perform whatever suggestion they make; rather, "Miracles are only from Allah and by His command" (قُلْ إِنَّمَا الْآياتُ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ).

Then, addressing the simple-hearted Muslims who were influenced by the heavy and intense oaths of the polytheists, it says: "You do not realize that they are lying, and even if these miracles and signs they requested were performed, they still would not believe" (وَ ما يُشْعِرُكُمْ أَنَّها إِذا جاءَتْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ) [^2]. The various scenes of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله encounters with them also testify to the fact that this group was not seeking the truth; rather, their goal was to keep the people occupied with excuses and to sow seeds of doubt and hesitation in their hearts.

In the following verse, the reason for their stubbornness is explained: that due to their insistence on deviance, ignorant prejudices, and refusal to submit to the truth, they have lost their healthy perception and vision, and they live in a state of confusion and misguidance. It says:

"And We will turn their hearts and their eyes away, just as they did not believe in it the first time" (وَ نُقَلِّبُ أَفْئِدَتَهُمْ وَ أَبْصارَهُمْ كَما لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا بِهِ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ).

Here too, this action is attributed to Allah, similar to what appeared in previous verses. This is, in reality, the reaction and result of their own deeds. Attributing it to Allah is because He is the Cause of causes and the Source of the universe, and every property in everything exists by His will. In other words, Allah has created this effect in stubbornness, deviance, and blind prejudice—that it gradually disables a person's perception and vision.

At the end of the verse, He says: "And We leave them in their transgression, wandering blindly" (وَ نَذَرُهُمْ فِي طُغْيانِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ) [^1].

May Allah protect us all from falling into such confusion, which is born of our own reckless actions, and grant us perfect perception and vision so that we may see the face of truth as it truly is.

[^1]: "Jahd" (جهد) means effort and striving to perform any task, and here it refers to striving for emphasized oaths.

[^2]: There is much discussion among commentators regarding whether "ma" (ما) in the above sentence is interrogative or negative, and also regarding the structure of the sentence. Some have taken "ma" as a rhetorical interrogation, though if this were the case, the meaning would be: "How do you know that if a miracle comes, they will not believe?" implying they might believe, which is contrary to the purpose of the verse. Therefore, some have taken "ma" to be negative (which is closer to the intended meaning). Thus, the meaning becomes: "You do not know that even if these miracles are performed, they will not believe." In this case, the subject of "yush'ir" (يشعر) is the word "shay'" (thing) which is implied, and "yush'ir" has two objects: "kum" (you) as the first object and "annaha..." as the second object (take note).

[^1]: "Ya'mahun" (يعمهون) is from the root "amah" (عمه), meaning wandering and bewilderment.

Beginning of the Eighth Part (Juz') Verse 111 - Surah Al-An'am

Beginning of the Eighth Part of the Quran

Ayah

۞ وَلَوْ أَنَّنَا نَزَّلْنَآ إِلَيْهِمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ وَكَلَّمَهُمُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَحَشَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ قُبُلًا مَّا كَانُوا۟ لِيُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ يَجْهَلُونَ (١١١)
Even if We had sent down angels to them, and the dead had spoken to them, and We had gathered before them all things manifestly, they would [still] not believe unless Allah wished. But most of them are ignorant. (111)

Commentary

Why Stubborn Individuals Do Not Follow the Path

This verse is related to the preceding verses; they all pursue a single truth. The objective of these few verses is to show that a group of those requesting strange and extraordinary miracles are not sincere in their requests, and their goal is not to accept the truth. Therefore, some of their demands (such as God appearing before them!) are fundamentally impossible.

In their own imagination, they seek to destabilize the thoughts of the believers and confuse and preoccupy the minds of truth-seekers by raising these requests and strange miracles.

In the above verse, the Quran explicitly states: "Even if We (as they had requested) had sent down the angels to them, and the dead had come and spoken to them, and in short, if We had gathered everything they wanted before them, they still would not have believed" (وَ لَوْ أَنَّنا نَزَّلْنا إِلَيْهِمُ الْمَلائِكَةَ وَ كَلَّمَهُمُ الْمَوْتى‌ وَ حَشَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قُبُلًا ما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا) [^1]. Then, to emphasize the matter, He says: "There is only one case in which they might believe, and that is if Allah, through His compulsory Will, forces them to accept faith." It is obvious that such faith would have no educational benefit or evolutionary effect (إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللَّهُ‌). At the end of the verse, He adds that "most of them are ignorant and unaware" (وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ يَجْهَلُونَ‌).

Regarding who the pronoun "them" refers to in this sentence, there is discussion among the commentators: it may refer to the believers who were insistent that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله fulfill the demands of this group of disbelievers and bring whatever miracle they suggested.

This is because most of these believers were unaware of the reality and did not realize that those people were not sincere in their requests. However, Allah knew that these claimants were lying; for this reason, He did not fulfill their demands. Nevertheless, since the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mission cannot be without miracles, He manifested various miracles through his hands in specific instances.

There is also the possibility that the pronoun "them" refers back to the "requesting disbelievers."

[^1]: The meaning of "حَشَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ" is that everything and all their demands be fulfilled, because hashr originally means gathering and collecting, and qubulan means face-to-face and in front. There is also the possibility that qubulan is the plural of qabil, meaning that groups upon groups of angels and the dead, etc., would appear before them.

That is, most of them are unaware of the reality that Allah has the power over every kind of extraordinary act, but they seemingly consider His power limited. Therefore, whenever the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله showed a miracle, they attributed it to sorcery or an optical illusion, as we read in another verse:

وَ لَوْ فَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ باباً مِنَ السَّماءِ فَظَلُّوا فِيهِ يَعْرُجُونَ لَقالُوا إِنَّما سُكِّرَتْ أَبْصارُنا بَلْ نَحْنُ قَوْمٌ مَسْحُورُونَ‌: "And even if We opened for them a gate to the heaven and they were to continue ascending through it, they would surely say, 'Our eyes have been dazzled; rather, we are a people bewitched!'" (Surah Al-Hijr, Verses 14 and 15). Therefore, they are an ignorant and stubborn group to whom no attention should be paid, nor to their words.

Ayahs

وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَا لِكُلِّ نَبِىٍّ عَدُوًّا شَيَـٰطِينَ ٱلْإِنسِ وَٱلْجِنِّ يُوحِى بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ زُخْرُفَ ٱلْقَوْلِ غُرُورًا ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ رَبُّكَ مَا فَعَلُوهُ ۖ فَذَرْهُمْ وَمَا يَفْتَرُونَ (١١٢)
That is how for every prophet We appointed as enemy the devils from among humans and jinn, who inspire each other with flashy words, deceptively. Had your Lord wished, they would not have done it. So leave them with what they fabricate, (112)
وَلِتَصْغَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ أَفْـِٔدَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَلِيَرْضَوْهُ وَلِيَقْتَرِفُوا۟ مَا هُم مُّقْتَرِفُونَ (١١٣)
so that toward it may incline the hearts of those who do not believe in the Hereafter, and so that they may be pleased with it and commit what they commit. (113)

Commentary

Satanic Whispers

In this verse, it is explained that the existence of such stubborn and persistent enemies against the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), as mentioned in the previous verses, was not unique to him; rather, "for every prophet, there have been enemies from among the devils of mankind and jinn" (وَ كَذلِكَ جَعَلْنا لِكُلِّ نَبِيٍّ عَدُوًّا شَياطِينَ الْإِنْسِ وَ الْجِنِ‌).

And their work was such that "they would inspire one another with flowery speech in order to deceive, through mysterious and sometimes whispered means" (يُوحِي بَعْضُهُمْ إِلى‌ بَعْضٍ زُخْرُفَ الْقَوْلِ غُرُوراً).

However, let there be no mistake: "had your Lord willed, He could have forcibly prevented them all" so that no devil or devilish person could place the slightest obstacle in the path of the prophets and their mission (وَ لَوْ شاءَ رَبُّكَ ما فَعَلُوهُ‌).

But Allah did not do this, because He willed for people to be free so that there would be a field for their trial, perfection, and development, whereas compulsion and the deprivation of freedom would not align with this goal. Furthermore, the existence of such stubborn and persistent enemies (even though their actions were by their own will and choice) not only does no harm to true believers but indirectly aids in their perfection. This is because perfection often arises through conflict, and the presence of a powerful enemy is effective in mobilizing human forces and strengthening the will.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, He commands His Prophet not to pay any attention to such devilry "and leave them and their fabrications alone" (فَذَرْهُمْ وَ ما يَفْتَرُونَ‌).

Here, several points should be noted

  1. In the above verse, Allah attributes the existence of human and jinn devils against the prophets to Himself, saying: وَ كَذلِكَ جَعَلْنا (And thus We have made). There is discussion among commentators regarding the meaning of this phrase, but as we have previously mentioned, all human actions can be attributed to Allah from one perspective, for whatever anyone possesses is from Him; their power is from Him, just as their choice and free will are from Him. However, the concept of such expressions is never predestination or the deprivation of choice—that Allah created some in such a way that they must rise in enmity against the prophets.

For if it were so, they should bear no responsibility for their enmity and hostility; rather, their work would be considered the fulfillment of a mission, whereas we know this is not the case.

Of course, it cannot be denied that the existence of such enemies, even if by their own choice, can have a constructive effect on believers indirectly. To put it better, true believers can derive a positive effect from the existence of any enemy and use them as a means to raise their level of awareness, readiness, and resistance, because the presence of an enemy causes the mobilization of human forces.

  1. Shayatin (devils), the plural of Shaytan, has a broad meaning and refers to any rebellious, defiant, and harmful creature. Therefore, in the Quran, the word "Shaytan" is also applied to base, wicked, and rebellious humans, just as in the above verse, the word Shaytan is applied to both human devils and non-human devils who are hidden from our sight. However, "Iblis" is the proper name for the specific Shaytan who stood against Adam (عليه السلام) and is, in fact, the leader of all devils. Thus, Shaytan is a common noun and Iblis is a proper noun. [1]

  2. Zukhruf al-qawl refers to deceptive speech that has an attractive appearance but an ugly and evil interior, and ghurur means to deceive or mislead. [2]

  3. The use of the term "revelation" (wahy) in the above verse is a subtle reference to the fact that they have mysterious plans in their devilish speech and actions, which they secretly convey to one another so that people do not become aware of their work and their schemes can be fully implemented; for one of the literal meanings of wahy is quiet and whispered speech.

In the next verse, He describes the result of the deceptive suggestions and propaganda of the devils as follows: "The end result of their work will be that those who do not believe—that is, those who do not believe in the Day of Resurrection—will lend their ears to their speech and their hearts will incline toward it" (وَ لِتَصْغى‌ إِلَيْهِ أَفْئِدَةُ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ). [3]

[1]: We also had a discussion on this subject in the first volume of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 135.

[2]: It should be noted that zukhruf originally means ornament and also gold, which is a means of ornament; subsequently, deceptive speech that has a beautiful appearance is also called zukhruf and muzakhraf.

[3]: Regarding the structure of the verse and what the phrase وَ لِتَصْغى‌ is coordinated with, there is discussion among commentators. What is most consistent with the meaning of the verse is that this phrase is coordinated with yuhi (inspire) and the "lam" is the "lam of consequence" (lam al-aqibah), meaning the end result of the devils' work will be that they speak deceptive words to one another and the faithless will incline toward them. It is also possible that it is coordinated with the position of ghururan, which is an object of purpose (maf'ul la-ajlih), meaning "so that they may be deceived and so that they may incline" (because in the first stage, a person is deceived and then develops an inclination) (Reflect on this).

Litasgha is derived from the root saghw, meaning to incline toward something, but it is mostly used for an inclination that occurs through hearing and by means of the ear. If someone listens to another's speech with approval, it is called saghw and isgha.

Then He says: "The end of this inclination will be complete satisfaction with the devilish programs" (وَ لِيَرْضَوْهُ‌).

And the end of it all will be the commission of various sins and ugly, undesirable acts (وَ لِيَقْتَرِفُوا ما هُمْ مُقْتَرِفُونَ‌).

Ayahs

أَفَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَبْتَغِى حَكَمًا وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مُفَصَّلًا ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهُۥ مُنَزَّلٌ مِّن رَّبِّكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُمْتَرِينَ (١١٤)
[Say,] ‘Shall I seek a judge other than Allah, while it is He who has sent down to you the Book, well-elaborated?’ Those whom We have given the Book know that it has been sent down from your Lord with the truth; so do not be one of the skeptics. (114)
وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ صِدْقًا وَعَدْلًا ۚ لَّا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (١١٥)
The word of your Lord has been fulfilled in truth and justice. Nothing can change His words, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (115)

Commentary

This verse is, in fact, the conclusion of the preceding verses, and it asks: with all these clear signs regarding Monotheism (Tawhid) that have passed, who should be accepted as the judge? "Should I seek a judge other than Allah?" (أَ فَغَيْرَ اللَّهِ أَبْتَغِي حَكَماً) [^1] "while it is He who has sent down to you this great Heavenly Book, in which all human educational needs are contained, and which has distinguished between truth and falsehood, light and darkness, and disbelief and faith?" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ إِلَيْكُمُ الْكِتابَ مُفَصَّلًا).

Then it states: not only do you and the Muslims know that this Book is from Allah, but "those to whom We gave the Scripture (the Jews and Christians), who have seen the signs of this Heavenly Book in their own scriptures, know that it is sent down from your Lord in truth" (وَ الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ يَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهُ مُنَزَّلٌ مِنْ رَبِّكَ بِالْحَقِ‌).

Therefore, there is no room for any kind of doubt or hesitation regarding it, "so do not be among the doubters" (فَلا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُمْتَرِينَ‌).

The question here is the same as what we have mentioned in similar cases: the addressee is actually the people, but to emphasize and reinforce the matter, Allah addresses His Prophet so that others may realize their own responsibility.

In the next verse, He says: "The Word of your Lord has been fulfilled in truth and justice; none can change His words, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing."

[^1]: "Hakam" (rhyming with Qalam) means judge, arbitrator, and ruler. Some have considered it synonymous with "Hakim" in meaning, but a group of commentators, including Sheikh Tusi in Al-Tibyan, believe that "Hakam" refers to one who judges only by the truth, whereas "Hakim" can be applied to both. Others, such as the author of Al-Manar, believe that "Hakam" is someone chosen by the two parties of a dispute, while "Hakim" refers to any kind of judge.

(وَ تَمَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ صِدْقاً وَ عَدْلًا لا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِماتِهِ وَ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ‌).

"Kalimah" (Word) linguistically means speech and any kind of sentence, and it is even applied to detailed and lengthy discourses. If we see that it is sometimes used to mean "promise," such as: "And the good word of your Lord was fulfilled for the Children of Israel because of what they had patiently endured" (Surah Al-A'raf, Verse 137), it is from this perspective, because when a person makes a promise, they utter a sentence that contains the promise.

Furthermore, "Kalimah" sometimes means religion, creed, decree, or command, all of which trace back to the same root.

Regarding what is meant by "Kalimah" in the above verse—whether it is the Quran, the religion of Allah, or the promise of victory given to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—various possibilities have been suggested. These are not mutually exclusive, and it is possible that the verse refers to all of them. However, since the preceding verses discussed the "Quran," this meaning is more appropriate.

In reality, the verse says: there is no room for doubt or hesitation regarding the Quran because it is perfect and flawless from every aspect; its histories and reports are all truth (sidq), and its rulings and laws are all justice ('adl).

It is also possible that "Kalimah" refers to the same promise mentioned in the subsequent phrase, "none can change His words" (لا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِماتِهِ), because similar phrases are seen in other verses of the Quran, such as: "And the word of your Lord has been fulfilled: 'I will surely fill Hell with jinn and men all together'" (Surah Hud, Verse 119). Or in another verse we read:

"And Our word has already preceded for Our servants, the messengers, that they would be the victors" (Surah As-Saffat, Verses 171 and 172).

In such verses, the subsequent phrase explains the promise alluded to by the mention of "Kalimah" in the preceding phrase.

Therefore, the interpretation of the verse becomes: "Our promise has been fulfilled with truth and justice, that no one has the power to change the commands and decrees of the Lord."

As we mentioned, the verse can refer to all of these meanings.

It is also necessary to mention that if the verse refers to the Quran, it does not contradict the fact that the entire Quran had not been revealed at that time, because the perfection of the Quranic verses means that whatever had been revealed was without flaw or defect and was perfect from every aspect.

Some commentators have used this verse as evidence for the impossibility of distortion (tahrif) entering the Quran, because the phrase "none can change His words" indicates that no one can effect any change or alteration—neither in terms of wording, nor in terms of reports, nor in terms of rulings—in the Quran. This Heavenly Book, which must be a guide for the worlds until the end of time, will always be protected and preserved from the interference of traitors and distorters.

Ayahs

وَإِن تُطِعْ أَكْثَرَ مَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يُضِلُّوكَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ وَإِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ (١١٦)
If you obey most of those on the earth, they will lead you astray from the way of Allah. They follow nothing but conjectures and they do nothing but surmise. (116)
إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ مَن يَضِلُّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ ۖ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ (١١٧)
Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way; and He knows best those who are guided. (117)

Commentary

We know that the verses of this Surah were revealed in Makkah, and at that time, Muslims were in a severe minority. Occasionally, their minority status and the overwhelming majority of idolaters and opponents of Islam might have created the illusion for some that if their religion is false and baseless, why does it have so many followers? And if we are on the path of truth, why are we so few? In this verse, to dispel this illusion—which might arise following the mention of the Quran's truthfulness in the preceding verses—Allah addresses His Prophet, saying: "And if you obey most of those on the earth, they will mislead and divert you from the path of truth!" (وَ إِنْ تُطِعْ أَكْثَرَ مَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ يُضِلُّوكَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ‌).

In the following sentence, He explains the reason for this, stating: The cause is that they do not act based on logic and sound thought; "their guide is a handful of conjectures tainted by whims and desires, and a collection of lies, deceptions, and guesswork."

(إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ‌) [1]

Since the implication of the previous verse is that the majority alone cannot point to the path of truth, the conclusion is that the path of truth must be taken only from Allah, even if the supporters of the truth are in the minority. Therefore, in the second verse, He clarifies the reason for this: "Your Lord, who is aware and informed of all things and in whose infinite knowledge not the slightest error finds its way, knows best which is the path of deviation and which is the path of guidance, and He knows best the misguided and the guided." (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ مَنْ يَضِلُّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ وَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُهْتَدِينَ‌) [2] Here, a question arises: Do others know the path of guidance and deviation without God's guidance such that the verse says: Allah knows better than others?! The answer to the question is that through his intellect, man undoubtedly perceives certain truths and understands the path of guidance and deviation to some extent; however, the light of the lamp of intellect and its radiance is certainly limited, and many matters may remain hidden from the view of the intellect. Furthermore, man is prone to error in his knowledge, and for this reason, he needs

[^1]: Khars (rhyming with tars) originally means estimation and was first used regarding the estimation of the amount of fruit on trees during the leasing of an orchard and the like. Subsequently, it was applied to any kind of guess or estimation. Since estimation is sometimes consistent with reality and sometimes contrary to it, this word has also been used in the sense of lying. In the above verse, it can carry both meanings.

[^2]: Usually, the "elative noun" (comparative/superlative) is made transitive by the preposition "bi" (ب); therefore, it should have said "a'lamu bi-man" (اعلم بمن), but the "bi" has been omitted, and "man yadillu" is, in grammatical terms, "mansub bi-naz' al-khafid" (accusative due to the removal of the preposition).

Divine leaders and guides. Therefore, the phrase "Allah is more knowing" (better knows) is correct, even though human knowledge is not comparable to Allah's knowledge.

Numerical Majority is of No Importance

Contrary to what is certain in the view of some—that numerical majorities always follow the correct path—the Quran negates this subject in numerous verses and does not grant importance to numerical majority. In reality, it considers the "qualitative" majority to be the criterion, not the "quantitative" one. The reason for this is clear: although modern societies have found no way to manage society other than relying on the majority, it must not be forgotten that this issue, as we have said, is a kind of necessity and a dead-end. This is because in a material society, there is no criterion for decision-making and passing laws that is free from flaws and objections. Thus, many scholars, while confessing to the truth that the opinion of the majority of society is often erroneous, have been forced to accept it because the other ways have even more defects.

However, a society that believes in the mission of the Prophets sees no compulsion in following the opinion of the majority in passing laws, because the programs and laws of the true Prophets are free from any defect, deficiency, or error, and are not comparable to what a fallible majority approves.

A look at the face of the modern world and the governments built upon majorities, and the incorrect laws mixed with whims that are sometimes approved by majorities, shows that numerical majority has not solved any pain. Many wars have been approved by majorities, and many vices have been desired by them.

Colonization and exploitation, wars and bloodshed, the legalization of alcoholic beverages, gambling, abortion, prostitution, and even some heinous acts that are shameful to mention, approved by the majority of representatives in many so-called advanced countries—reflecting the opinion of the majority of their masses—stand as witnesses to this truth.

Scientifically speaking, do the majorities of societies tell the truth? Are the majorities trustworthy? Do the majorities refrain from encroaching upon the rights of others if they are able to? Do the majorities see their own interests and the interests of others with the same eye?

The answers to these questions are self-evident. Therefore, it must be admitted that the modern world's reliance on the majority is, in fact, a kind of environmental compulsion and necessity—a bone stuck in the throat of societies.

Yes, if the thinking minds, the compassionate reformers of human societies, and the purposeful thinkers—who are always in the minority—undertake an all-encompassing effort to enlighten the masses, and if human societies achieve sufficient intellectual, moral, and social growth, then certainly the opinions of such a majority will be very close to the truth. But what problem can an immature, unaware, or corrupt, deviant, and misguided majority remove from its own path or the path of others? Therefore, the majority alone is not sufficient; rather, a guided majority can solve the problems of its society as far as is humanly possible.

And if the Quran criticizes the majority in various verses, there is no doubt that it refers to an immature and unguided majority.

Ayahs

فَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا ذُكِرَ ٱسْمُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ إِن كُنتُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِهِۦ مُؤْمِنِينَ (١١٨)
Eat from that over which Allah’s Name has been mentioned, if you are believers in His signs. (118)
وَمَا لَكُمْ أَلَّا تَأْكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا ذُكِرَ ٱسْمُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ وَقَدْ فَصَّلَ لَكُم مَّا حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِلَّا مَا ٱضْطُرِرْتُمْ إِلَيْهِ ۗ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا لَّيُضِلُّونَ بِأَهْوَآئِهِم بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ (١١٩)
Why should you not eat that over which Allah’s Name has been mentioned, while He has already elaborated for you whatever He has forbidden you, excepting what you may be compelled to [eat in an emergency]? Indeed many mislead [others] by their fancies, without any knowledge. Indeed your Lord knows best the transgressors. (119)
وَذَرُوا۟ ظَـٰهِرَ ٱلْإِثْمِ وَبَاطِنَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْسِبُونَ ٱلْإِثْمَ سَيُجْزَوْنَ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَقْتَرِفُونَ (١٢٠)
Renounce outward sins and the inward ones. Indeed those who commit sins shall be requited for what they used to commit. (120)

Commentary

All Traces of Polytheism Must Be Eradicated:

These verses are, in fact, one of the results of the previous discussions regarding Monotheism (Tawhid) and polytheism (Shirk); therefore, the first verse begins with the particle "fa" (so/then) for derivation, which is usually used to state a conclusion.

To explain: In the preceding verses, through various expressions, the reality of Monotheism was proven, and the falsehood of polytheism and idol worship was made clear. One of the consequences of this matter is that Muslims must refrain from eating the meat of animals slaughtered in the name of "idols," and should only use the meat of animals slaughtered in the name of Allah. This is because one of the acts of worship of the Arab polytheists was sacrificing for idols and eating their meat for blessings (tabarruk), and this practice was a form of idol worship.

Therefore, it first says: "So eat of that over which the name of Allah has been mentioned, if you are believers in His verses" (فَكُلُوا مِمَّا ذُكِرَ اسْمُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ بِآياتِهِ مُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

This means that faith is not merely a claim, speech, or belief; rather, it must manifest within one's actions. One who believes in the One God eats only from such meat.

Of course, the command "kulu" (eat) here does not imply that eating such meat is obligatory (wajib); rather, the intent is to state that these are permissible (mubah) and others are forbidden (haram).

Furthermore, it becomes clear that the prohibition of meat over which the name of Allah was not mentioned at the time of slaughter is not due to hygienic aspects—such that one might ask what effect mentioning a name has—but rather relates to spiritual and moral aspects and the strengthening of the foundation of Monotheism and the worship of the One God.

In the next verse, this same subject is presented with different phrasing accompanied by further reasoning, stating: "And why should you not eat of that over which the name of Allah has been mentioned, while He has explained to you in detail what He has forbidden you?" (وَ ما لَكُمْ أَلَّا تَأْكُلُوا مِمَّا ذُكِرَ اسْمُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ وَ قَدْ فَصَّلَ لَكُمْ ما حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ‌).

Once again, we point out that the reproach and emphasis are not for the sake of abandoning the eating of lawful meat; rather, the goal is that you should eat only from this meat and not use others. In other words, the focus is on the opposite point and the implication of the sentence. This is why it is reasoned with the phrase: "He has explained to you in detail what He has forbidden you" (قَدْ فَصَّلَ لَكُمْ ما حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ‌).

Regarding which Surah and which verse this subject appears in, and where lawful and unlawful meats are explained, one might imagine it refers to Surah Al-Ma'idah or some future verses of this same Surah (Verse 145). However, considering that this Surah was revealed in Makkah and Al-Ma'idah in Madinah, and the future verses of this Surah had not yet been revealed at the time these verses were sent down, it becomes clear that neither of these two possibilities is correct. Rather, it either refers to Verse 115 of Surah Al-Nahl, in which a portion of forbidden meats—especially animals slaughtered for other than Allah—is explicitly mentioned, or it refers to the explanation of the ruling of these meats by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, for he spoke nothing but divine revelation.

Then, He makes one exception, saying: "Except that to which you are compelled" (إِلَّا مَا اضْطُرِرْتُمْ إِلَيْهِ‌).

Whether this compulsion is due to being stranded in a wilderness and facing extreme hunger, or being caught in the clutches of polytheists and being forced by them into this matter.

Then He adds: "And indeed, many lead [others] astray through their desires without knowledge" (وَ إِنَّ كَثِيراً لَيُضِلُّونَ بِأَهْوائِهِمْ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ‌).

Although following whims is often coupled with ignorance and lack of knowledge, He brings them together for further emphasis, saying: (بِأَهْوائِهِمْ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ‌).

Additionally, from this expression, it is well understood that true knowledge is never compatible with following whims and fantasies; where they become compatible, it is ignorance, not knowledge!

It is also necessary to mention that the above sentence could refer to the very superstition that existed among the Arab polytheists, who argued for eating the meat of dead animals (carrion) by saying: "Is it possible that we consider lawful the animals we kill ourselves, but consider forbidden what our God has killed?!" It is obvious that this sophistry was nothing more than a vain fantasy, for Allah did not slaughter or cut the throat of the dead animal so that it could be compared to animals whose throats we have cut. For this reason, it is a center for various diseases and its meat is putrid; thus, Allah did not permit eating it.

At the end of the verse, He says: "Indeed, your Lord is most knowing of the transgressors" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُعْتَدِينَ‌).

Those who, with vain arguments, not only deviate from the path of truth but also strive to lead others astray.

And since some might commit this forbidden act in secret, in the following verse, as a general rule, He says: "And leave outward sin and inward [sin]" (وَ ذَرُوا ظاهِرَ الْإِثْمِ وَ باطِنَهُ‌).

It is said that during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), some believed that an act contrary to chastity (adultery/fornication) was not a problem if done in secret, and was only a sin if done openly! Even now, some have practically accepted this ignorant logic and only fear open sins, yet commit secret sins without feeling any discomfort!

The above verse not only condemns the aforementioned logic but also has a broad meaning that, in addition to what was said, encompasses other interpretations regarding "outward" and "inward" sin. Among these is that outward sins are those performed with the limbs of the body, and inward sin is that which occurs with the heart, intention, and decision.

Then, as a reminder and a threat to sinners regarding the grim fate that awaits them, He says: "Indeed, those who earn sin will be compensated for what they used to commit" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْسِبُونَ الْإِثْمَ سَيُجْزَوْنَ بِما كانُوا يقتَرِفُونَ‌).

The expression "earning sin" (يَكْسِبُونَ الْإِثْمَ‌) is a profound expression showing that human beings in this world are like investors entering a great market. Their capital is intelligence, reason, life, youth, and various God-given powers. Wretched are those who, instead of acquiring felicity and "earning" honor, character, piety, and proximity to Allah, earn sin.

The expression "they will soon be compensated" (سَيُجْزَوْنَ‌) might be an indication that although the Resurrection seems far to some, it is in reality very near, and this world passes quickly and the Resurrection arrives.

Or it may be an indication that most individuals will see a portion of the results of their evil deeds even in this worldly life in the form of individual and social repercussions.

Ayah

وَلَا تَأْكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا لَمْ يُذْكَرِ ٱسْمُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَفِسْقٌ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ لَيُوحُونَ إِلَىٰٓ أَوْلِيَآئِهِمْ لِيُجَـٰدِلُوكُمْ ۖ وَإِنْ أَطَعْتُمُوهُمْ إِنَّكُمْ لَمُشْرِكُونَ (١٢١)
Do not eat [anything] of that over which Allah’s Name has not been mentioned, and that is indeed transgression. Indeed the satans inspire their friends to dispute with you; and if you obey them, you will indeed be polytheists. (121)

Commentary

In the preceding verses, the focus was on the positive aspect of the matter, namely eating from lawful meats. However, in this verse—to emphasize the point as much as possible—the focus shifts to the negative aspect and its implication, stating: "And do not eat of that upon which the name of Allah has not been mentioned" (وَ لا تَأْكُلُوا مِمَّا لَمْ يُذْكَرِ اسْمُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ‌).

Then, with a short phrase, it once again condemns this act, saying:

"Indeed, this is disobedience (fisq), sin, and a departure from the path and custom of servitude and obedience to the command of Allah" (وَ إِنَّهُ لَفِسْقٌ‌).

And so that some simple-hearted Muslims would not fall under the influence of their satanic whisperings, it adds: "Indeed, the devils inspire their allies with whispering arguments to dispute with you" (وَ إِنَّ الشَّياطِينَ لَيُوحُونَ إِلى‌ أَوْلِيائِهِمْ لِيُجادِلُوكُمْ‌).

But be alert: "And if you obey them, you will indeed be polytheists (mushrikun)" (وَ إِنْ أَطَعْتُمُوهُمْ إِنَّكُمْ لَمُشْرِكُونَ‌).

This dispute and whispering perhaps refer to the same logic that the polytheists used to suggest to one another (and some have said the Arab polytheists learned it from the Magians):

That if we eat the meat of a dead animal (carrion), it is because Allah has killed it, and it is better than the animal we kill ourselves; meaning, not eating carrion is a kind of indifference toward Allah's work!

They were oblivious to the fact that whatever dies a natural death, in addition to often being diseased, has not been slaughtered; thus, the foul blood remains within the tissues of its meat, dies, putrefies, and contaminates the meat. For this reason, Allah has commanded to eat only the meat of animals that have been slaughtered under specific conditions and whose blood has been drained.

Furthermore, it is understood from these verses that animals slaughtered by non-Muslims are forbidden (haram) because—among other reasons—they do not commit themselves to mentioning the name of Allah at the time of slaughter.

Ayahs

أَوَمَن كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَـٰهُ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُۥ نُورًا يَمْشِى بِهِۦ فِى ٱلنَّاسِ كَمَن مَّثَلُهُۥ فِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ لَيْسَ بِخَارِجٍ مِّنْهَا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٢٢)
Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people, like one who dwells in a manifold darkness which he cannot leave? To the faithless is thus presented as decorous what they have been doing. (122)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَا فِى كُلِّ قَرْيَةٍ أَكَـٰبِرَ مُجْرِمِيهَا لِيَمْكُرُوا۟ فِيهَا ۖ وَمَا يَمْكُرُونَ إِلَّا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ (١٢٣)
Thus have We installed in every town its major criminals that they may plot therein. Yet they do not plot except against their own souls, but they are not aware. (123)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the occasion of revelation for the first verse, it is narrated that: "Abu Jahl," who was among the fiercest enemies of Islam and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, severely harassed the Prophet one day. "Hamzah," the brave uncle of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, who had not yet accepted Islam and was still studying and reflecting upon his religion, had gone to the desert for hunting that day as was his custom. When he returned from the desert and was informed of the incident involving Abu Jahl and his nephew, he became deeply enraged. He went straight to Abu Jahl and struck him so hard on his head—or nose—that blood flowed. Despite all the influence Abu Jahl possessed among his tribe and kin, and even among the people of Mecca, he refrained from reacting out of fear of Hamzah's extraordinary bravery.

Subsequently, Hamzah went to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and accepted Islam. From 그날 forward, he officially served as a gallant officer of Islam, defending this heavenly religion until his final breath.

The above verse was revealed concerning this incident, distinguishing the state of Hamzah's faith and Abu Jahl's persistence in disbelief and corruption.

It is also understood from some narrations that the verse was revealed regarding the conversion of Ammar ibn Yasir and Abu Jahl's insistence on disbelief. In any case, this verse, like other verses of the Quran, is not restricted to its specific occasion of revelation; rather, it possesses a broad concept that applies to every true believer and every stubborn disbeliever.

Commentary

Faith and Insight:

The connection between these verses and the preceding ones lies in the fact that the previous verses referred to two groups: the sincere believer and the stubborn disbeliever who not only refuses to believe but also strives hard to mislead others. Here, the condition of these two groups is visualized through two striking and clear parables.

First, it likens those who were in misguidance and then changed their path by accepting the truth and faith to a dead person who has been brought to life by the will and command of Allah (أَ وَ مَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ‌).

Frequently in the Quran, "death" and "life" are used to mean spiritual death and life, or disbelief and faith. This expression clearly shows that faith is not a dry and empty belief or ceremonial words; rather, it is like a soul breathed into the lifeless body of faithless individuals, affecting their entire being. Their eyes gain vision and light, their ears gain the power of hearing, their tongues gain the ability to speak, and their hands and feet gain the power to perform every kind of positive deed. Faith transforms individuals, influences their entire lives, and manifests the signs of life in all their affairs.

From the phrase فَأَحْيَيْناهُ‌ (We gave him life), it is understood that although faith must involve effort from the human side, these efforts lead nowhere unless there is an attraction from Allah's side! Then He says: "And We made for him a light by which he walks among the people" (وَ جَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ‌).

Although commentators have suggested various possibilities regarding the meaning of this "light," it appears that it does not refer solely to the Quran and the teachings of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Rather, in addition to these, faith in Allah grants man a new insight and understanding, bestowing upon him a specific clear-sightedness. It expands his horizon of vision beyond the limited material life and the four walls of the physical world, immersing him in an extraordinarily vast realm.

And since it invites him to self-purification, it removes the veils of selfishness, vanity, prejudice, stubbornness, and whims from before the eye of his soul, allowing him to see truths he was never able to perceive before.

In the radiance of this light, he can find his path of life among the people and remain protected and safe from many mistakes that others fall into due to greed, limited material thinking, or the dominance of selfishness and passion.

And what we read in Islamic narrations: "The believer looks with the light of Allah" (المؤمن ينظر بنور اللَّه‌) refers to this very reality. Nevertheless, the specific insight that a person of faith acquires cannot be fully described by pen or speech; rather, its taste must be experienced and its existence felt!

Then, He compares such a living, active, luminous, and effective individual with stubborn faithless persons, saying: "Is such a person like one who is plunged into waves of darkness and shadows from which he can never emerge?" (كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها).

It is noteworthy that He does not say "like one who is in darkness," but rather says "like one whose likeness is in darkness." Some have said the purpose of this expression was to prove that such individuals are so deeply immersed in darkness and misery that their condition has become a proverb known to all wise people.

However, it is possible that this expression refers to a more subtle meaning: that nothing remains of the existence of such individuals except a ghost, a mold, a likeness, and a statue. They have a body without a soul and a mind that has ceased to function! It is also necessary to mention that the guide of the believers is mentioned as "light" (in the singular form) and the environment of the disbelievers as "darkness" (in the plural form), because faith is nothing but one single truth and is the secret of unity and oneness, while disbelief and lack of faith are the source of scattering, division, and discord.

At the end of the verse, He points to the cause of this grim fate, saying: "Thus the deeds of the disbelievers are made to seem fair to them" (كَذلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِلْكافِرِينَ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌).

As we have previously mentioned, this is the characteristic of repeating an ugly act: its ugliness gradually diminishes in one's eyes, and it even reaches a point where it appears as a good deed. It becomes like a chain upon his hands and feet, not allowing him to escape this trap. A simple study of the state of criminals clearly illuminates this reality.

And since the protagonist of this story on the negative side was "Abu Jahl," and he was considered one of the leaders of the polytheists of Mecca and the Quraysh, the second verse refers to the state of these misguided leaders and chiefs of disbelief and corruption, saying: "And thus We have placed in every city great ones who are its criminals, to plot therein" (وَ كَذلِكَ جَعَلْنا فِي كُلِّ قَرْيَةٍ أَكابِرَ مُجْرِمِيها لِيَمْكُرُوا فِيها).

We have repeatedly stated that the attribution of such actions to Allah is because He is the Cause of causes and the source of all powers. Anyone who performs any action does so using the facilities that Allah has placed at his disposal, although some use them well and others misuse them.

The phrase "to plot" (لِيَمْكُرُوا) refers to the end result and consequence of their actions, not the purpose of their creation [1]. That is, the ultimate result of their frequent disobedience and sin was that they became highwaymen of the path of truth and diverted Allah's servants from the way. This is because "makr" (plotting) originally means twisting and winding, and subsequently, it is applied to any deviant act accompanied by concealment and secrecy.

[1]: In technical terms, the "Lam" is not the "Lam of Purpose" (Ghayat) but the "Lam of Consequence" (Aqibat), of which there are several examples in the Quran.

At the end of the verse, He says: "But they plot not except against themselves, and they perceive it not" (وَ ما يَمْكُرُونَ إِلَّا بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ وَ ما يَشْعُرُونَ‌).

What plot or deception is greater than this: that they employ the assets of their existence—including intellect, intelligence, initiative, life, time, and wealth—in a path that not only brings them no benefit but also burdens their backs with a heavy load of responsibility and sin, while they think they have achieved victories! Furthermore, it is clearly understood from this verse that the corruptions and miseries that afflict societies originate from the "great ones" and the leaders of nations; it is they who, with various types of ruses and plots, distort the way of Allah and hide the face of truth from the people.

Ayah

وَإِذَا جَآءَتْهُمْ ءَايَةٌ قَالُوا۟ لَن نُّؤْمِنَ حَتَّىٰ نُؤْتَىٰ مِثْلَ مَآ أُوتِىَ رُسُلُ ٱللَّهِ ۘ ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ حَيْثُ يَجْعَلُ رِسَالَتَهُۥ ۗ سَيُصِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا۟ صَغَارٌ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَعَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَمْكُرُونَ (١٢٤)
When a sign comes to them, they say, ‘We will not believe until we are given the like of what was given to Allah’s apostles.’ Allah knows best where to place His apostleship! Soon the guilty will be visited by a degradation and severe punishment from Allah because of the plots they used to devise. (124)

Occasion of Revelation

The late Tabarsi writes in Majma' al-Bayan: This verse was revealed concerning Walid ibn Mughirah (who was one of the famous leaders of the idolaters and was considered their so-called "think tank"). He used to say to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "If prophethood is true, I am more deserving of attaining this position than you, for I am both older than you and possess more wealth!"

Some have also said it was revealed regarding Abu Jahl, because he imagined that the matter of prophethood should also be a center for rivalries. He would say: "We and the tribe of Bani Abd-Manaf (the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله tribe) competed in everything and moved forward neck-and-neck like two racing horses, until they claimed that a prophet has risen from among them to whom revelation is sent. But it is impossible for us to believe in him unless revelation is sent to us just as it is sent to him!!"

Commentary

The Choice of the Prophet is in Allah's Hand:

In this verse, a brief and meaningful reference is made to the way of thinking and the ridiculous claims of these leaders of falsehood and "great criminals," stating: "And when a sign comes to them for their guidance, they say: 'We will never believe until we are given the like of what was given to the messengers of Allah'" (وَ إِذا جاءَتْهُمْ آيَةٌ قالُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ حَتَّى نُؤْتى‌ مِثْلَ ما أُوتِيَ رُسُلُ اللَّهِ‌).

As if attaining the station of messengership and leadership of the people depends on age and wealth, or on the childish rivalries of tribes, and as if Allah is also obligated to observe these ridiculous and baseless rivalries and endorse them—rivalries that stem from the ultimate intellectual decline and a lack of understanding of the concept of prophethood and the leadership of creation.

The Quran gives them a clear answer, saying: It is not necessary for you to teach Allah how He should dispatch His prophets and messengers and from among which individuals He should choose them! For "Allah knows best where to place His message" (اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ حَيْثُ يَجْعَلُ رِسالَتَهُ‌).

It is clear that messengership has no connection to age or wealth, nor to the status of tribes; rather, its condition, before anything else, is spiritual readiness, purity of conscience, authentic human virtues, lofty thought and powerful intellect, and finally, extraordinary piety and God-wariness at the level of Infallibility (Ismah). The existence of these qualities, especially the readiness for the station of Infallibility, is something that none but Allah knows. How vast is the difference between these conditions and what they imagined!

The successor of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله also possesses all his qualities and programs, except for revelation and legislation; meaning he is both the protector of the Law (Shar') and the Sharia, the guardian of the school of thought and its laws, and the spiritual and material leader of the people. Therefore, he too must possess the station of Infallibility and immunity from error and sin so that he can bring his mission to fruition and become an obeyed leader and a trusted role model.

For this very reason, his selection is also in the hand of Allah, and Allah knows where to place this station, not the creation of Allah, and not through the election of the people or a consultative council (Shura).

At the end of the verse, it describes the fate that awaits such criminals and pretentious leaders of falsehood, saying: "Soon those who committed crimes will be afflicted with humiliation and debasement before Allah and a severe punishment for the plots they used to devise to mislead the people" (سَيُصِيبُ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا صَغارٌ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ وَ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما كانُوا يَمْكُرُونَ‌) [1]. These self-centered individuals wanted to preserve their position and greatness through their wrongdoings, but Allah will "humiliate" them in such a way that they will feel the most painful spiritual torments. Furthermore, because their clamor in the way of falsehood was great and their efforts were "intense," their retribution and punishment will also be "severe" and resounding.

[1]: "Ijram" (crime/sin) is derived from the root "Jurm," which originally means "to cut." Since sinful individuals cut ties and separate themselves from obeying Allah's command, this word is also applied to "sin." In this, there is a subtle allusion to the fact that every human being, in their essence, has a connection with Truth, purity, and justice, and becoming contaminated with sin is, in reality, a separation from this divine nature (Fitrah).

Ayahs

فَمَن يُرِدِ ٱللَّهُ أَن يَهْدِيَهُۥ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُۥ لِلْإِسْلَـٰمِ ۖ وَمَن يُرِدْ أَن يُضِلَّهُۥ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُۥ ضَيِّقًا حَرَجًا كَأَنَّمَا يَصَّعَّدُ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَجْعَلُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرِّجْسَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٢٥)
Whomever Allah desires to guide, He opens his breast to Islam, and whomever He desires to lead astray, He makes his breast narrow and straitened as if he were climbing to a height. Thus does Allah lay [spiritual] defilement on those who do not have faith. (125)
وَهَـٰذَا صِرَٰطُ رَبِّكَ مُسْتَقِيمًا ۗ قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (١٢٦)
This is the straight path of your Lord. We have already elaborated the signs for a people who take admonition. (126)
۞ لَهُمْ دَارُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمِ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ ۖ وَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٢٧)
For them shall be the abode of peace near their Lord and He will be their guardian because of what they used to do. (127)

Commentary

Divine Assistance

Following the previous verses which discussed true believers and stubborn disbelievers, these verses describe the great blessings awaiting the first group and the lack of success that afflicts the second group.
