First, it states: "Whomever Allah wills to guide, He expands his breast to receive the truth; and whomever He wills to leave astray, He makes his breast so tight and constricted as if he were climbing up into the sky" (فَمَنْ يُرِدِ اللَّهُ أَنْ يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ وَ مَنْ يُرِدْ أَنْ يُضِلَّهُ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقاً حَرَجاً كَأَنَّما يَصَّعَّدُ فِي السَّماءِ).

To emphasize this matter, it adds: "Thus does Allah place defilement and filth upon those who do not believe," and misery and the deprivation of success will envelop them entirely (كَذلِكَ يَجْعَلُ اللَّهُ الرِّجْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

Several points must be noted here

1- We have repeatedly stated that "guidance" and "misguidance" from Allah refer to providing or removing the prerequisites for guidance in the case of those who have proven their readiness or lack thereof to accept the truth through their own deeds and conduct.

For those who are seekers of the path of truth and are thirsty for the pure water of faith, Allah places bright lamps along their path so they do not get lost in the darkness while seeking this water of life. However, those who have proven their indifference toward these truths are deprived of this Divine assistance; they face a multitude of difficulties in their path, and the success of guidance is taken away from them.

Therefore, neither is the first group forced to traverse this path, nor is the second group compelled in their actions; Divine guidance and misguidance are, in reality, the completion of what they themselves have willed and chosen.

2- The term "breast" (صدر) here refers to the soul and thought, a metaphor used in many instances. "Expansion" (شرح) refers to the vastness of the soul, the loftiness of thought, and the broadening of the horizon of human intellect. This is because accepting the truth requires significant sacrifices of personal interests, for which only those with expansive souls and lofty thoughts are prepared.

3- "Haraj" (حرج) means extreme tightness and severe limitation. This is the state of stubborn and faithless individuals whose thinking is very short-sighted and whose souls are exceptionally small and weak, showing the least amount of selflessness in life.

4- A Scientific Miracle of the Quran:

Comparing such individuals to someone who wants to climb into the sky is because ascending to the heavens is an exceptionally difficult task, and accepting the truth is similarly difficult for them.

Just as in everyday speech we sometimes say, "This task is as difficult for so-and-so as if he wanted to go to the sky," or we say, "Going to the sky is easier than this task."

Of course, in those days, flying to the sky was nothing more than a concept for mankind. But even today, when space travel has become a reality, it remains an exhausting task, and astronauts always face a multitude of severe difficulties.

However, a more subtle meaning for the verse also comes to mind which completes the previous discussion: today it is proven that the air surrounding the Earth is quite dense near the planet and suitable for human respiration. However, the higher we move, the thinner the air becomes and the lower the oxygen level drops, to the point that if we move a few kilometers up from the Earth's surface (without an oxygen mask), breathing becomes increasingly difficult every moment. If we continue further, shortness of breath and lack of oxygen lead to unconsciousness. The expression of this comparison in an era when this scientific reality had not yet been proven is, in fact, considered one of the scientific miracles of the Quran.

5- What is "Sharh al-Sadr" (Expansion of the Breast)?

In the above verse, "S'at al-Sadr" (expansiveness of the breast) is counted as one of the great blessings, and "Diq al-Sadr" (tightness of the breast) as one of the Divine punishments. Similarly, when Allah mentions a great blessing to His Prophet, He says: "Have We not expanded for you your breast?" (أَ لَمْ نَشْرَحْ لَكَ صَدْرَكَ‌) [^1].

This is a subject that can be clearly observed by studying the states of individuals. Some have souls so open and expansive that they are ready to accept any reality—no matter how great. Conversely, some have souls so narrow and limited that it seems there is no way or space for any truth to penetrate them. Their intellectual horizon is limited to daily life, sleep, and food; if these are provided, everything is fine, but if the slightest change occurs, it is as if everything has ended and the world has collapsed!

When the above verse was revealed, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was asked: "What is Sharh al-Sadr?"

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "It is a light that Allah casts into the heart of whomever He wills, and in its light, his soul becomes vast and expansive."

They asked: "Does it have a sign by which it can be recognized?"

He replied: "Yes, turning toward the eternal abode, withdrawing from the abode of deception, and preparing for death before death descends" [^2]. (Through faith, righteous deeds, and striving in the way of truth).

[^1]: Surah Ash-Sharh, Verse 1. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 4, p. 363.

In the next verse, as an emphasis on the previous discussion, it says: "This matter—that Divine assistance reaches the seekers of truth and the deprivation of success comes to the enemies of truth—is a direct, fixed, and unchangeable Divine Sunnah (tradition)" (وَ هذا صِراطُ رَبِّكَ مُسْتَقِيماً). There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that "this" (هذا) refers to Islam and the Quran, as it is the straight path and the balanced way.

At the end of the verse, it emphasizes again: "We have detailed Our signs and verses for those who have a receptive heart and a listening ear" (قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَذَّكَّرُونَ‌).

In the following verse, it mentions two of the greatest blessings given to those who are awakened and seek the truth. First: "For them is the Home of Peace with their Lord" (لَهُمْ دارُ السَّلامِ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ‌). And second: "And He is their Guardian, Protector, and Helper" (و هو وليهم).

"And all of this is because of the good deeds they used to do" (بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌).

What honor is higher than for Allah to take charge of a person's affairs and be his Protector, Friend, and Helper?

And what blessing is greater than "Dar al-Salam"—meaning the Home of Peace and Security—awaiting man? A place where there is no war, no bloodshed, no conflict, no dispute, no violence, no exhausting and deadly rivalries, no clash of interests, no lies, no slander, no accusations, no envy, no malice, and no grief; a place that is coupled with tranquility in every respect.

However, the verse says these are not given to anyone through mere words, but in exchange for action—yes, in exchange for action!

Ayahs

وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعًا يَـٰمَعْشَرَ ٱلْجِنِّ قَدِ ٱسْتَكْثَرْتُم مِّنَ ٱلْإِنسِ ۖ وَقَالَ أَوْلِيَآؤُهُم مِّنَ ٱلْإِنسِ رَبَّنَا ٱسْتَمْتَعَ بَعْضُنَا بِبَعْضٍ وَبَلَغْنَآ أَجَلَنَا ٱلَّذِىٓ أَجَّلْتَ لَنَا ۚ قَالَ ٱلنَّارُ مَثْوَىٰكُمْ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ (١٢٨)
On the day He will gather them all together, [He will say], ‘O company of jinn! You claimed many of the humans.’ Their friends from among the humans will say, ‘Our Lord, we used each other, and we completed our term which You had appointed for us.’ He will say, ‘The Fire is your abode, to remain in it [forever], except what Allah may wish.’ Indeed your Lord is all-wise, all-knowing. (128)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُوَلِّى بَعْضَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بَعْضًۢا بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (١٢٩)
That is how We make the wrongdoers one another’s friends because of what they used to earn. (129)

Commentary

In these verses, the Quran returns once again to the fate of the misguided criminals and those who misguide others, completing the discussions of the previous verses.

It reminds them of the Day when they will stand face-to-face with the devils from whom they took inspiration. Both these followers and those leaders will be questioned—a questioning to which they have no answer, resulting in nothing but regret and sorrow. These warnings are given so that they do not look only at these few days of life but also consider the end of the matter.

First, It says: "On the day He will gather them all together," He will first say: "O assembly of jinn and seductive devils! You misled a great many humans" (وَ يَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً يا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ قَدِ اسْتَكْثَرْتُمْ مِنَ الْإِنْسِ) ^1. The word "jinn" here refers to the devils, because in the original linguistic meaning, as we have mentioned before, "jinn" refers to any hidden being. In verse 50 of Surah Al-Kahf, regarding the chief of the devils, "Iblis," we read: "He was of the jinn" (كانَ مِنَ الْجِنِ).

The previous verses, which spoke of the cryptic whisperings of the devils with the phrase "Indeed, the devils inspire their allies" (إِنَّ الشَّياطِينَ لَيُوحُونَ إِلى‌ أَوْلِيائِهِمْ), as well as the subsequent verse which speaks of the leadership of some oppressors over others, can be an allusion to this subject.

However, the seductive devils seemingly have no answer to this statement and remain silent, but "their allies among mankind will say: 'Our Lord, we profited from each other, and we have reached our term which You appointed for us'" (وَ قالَ أَوْلِياؤُهُمْ مِنَ الْإِنْسِ رَبَّنَا اسْتَمْتَعَ بَعْضُنا بِبَعْضٍ وَ بَلَغْنا أَجَلَنَا الَّذِي أَجَّلْتَ لَنا).

They were pleased to have submissive followers and to rule over them, and we too were pleased with the glitz and glamour of the world and its unconditional, fleeting pleasures, which appeared charming and delightful due to the whisperings of the devils!

There is discussion among commentators regarding what "term" (ajal) refers to in this verse—whether it is the end of life or the Day of Resurrection. However, it apparently refers to the end of life, as "ajal" is used in this sense in many verses of the Quran.

Then, Allah addresses all these corrupt and corrupting leaders and followers, saying: "The Fire is your residence, wherein you will abide eternally, except as Allah wills" (قالَ النَّارُ مَثْواكُمْ خالِدِينَ فِيها إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللَّهُ).

The exception with the phrase "except as Allah wills" (إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللَّهُ) is either an indication that the eternity of punishment and penalty in such cases does not strip the Lord of His power; whenever He wills, He can change it, even if He keeps it fixed for a certain group.

Or, it refers to those individuals who do not deserve eternal punishment or have the capacity to be included in Divine forgiveness, who must be excepted from the ruling of immortality and eternity of punishment.

At the end of the verse, He says: "Indeed, your Lord is All-Wise, All-Knowing" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ).

Both His punishment is based on a precise account, and so are His pardon and forgiveness, and He is well aware of their instances.

In the next verse, He refers to a perpetual Divine law regarding such people, saying: "Just as the oppressors and tyrants in this world were supporters and backers of one another, and were leaders and guides for each other, cooperating closely in wrong paths," in the other world too, We shall leave them to one another, and this is because of the deeds they used to perform in this world (وَ كَذلِكَ نُوَلِّي بَعْضَ الظَّالِمِينَ بَعْضاً بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ).

For, as we have stated in discussions related to the Resurrection, the scene of the Resurrection is a scene of reactions and consequences on a massive scale, and what exists there is a reflection and echo of our deeds in this world. ^1 In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim al-Qummi, it is narrated from the Imam عليه السلام that he said:

"We will turn over everyone to those they took as allies, so they will be with them on the Day of Resurrection." (نولى كل من تولى اوليائهم فيكونون معهم يوم القيامة)

It is noteworthy that all these groups are introduced as "oppressors" (zalimin) in the verse, and there is no doubt that oppression, in its broad sense, includes all of them. What oppression is greater than a human removing himself from the guardianship (wilayah) of Allah by accepting the leadership of devilish characters, only to be placed under their guardianship in the other world as well?

Furthermore, this expression and the phrase "because of what they used to earn" (بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ) show that this wretchedness and misery are due to their own actions. This is a Divine Sunnah (tradition) and a law of creation: those who travel dark paths will have no end but falling into the pit and valley of misery.

^1: For further explanation, please refer to the valuable book "Resurrection and the World After Death" (Ma'ad va Jahan-e Pas az Marg).

Ayahs

يَـٰمَعْشَرَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِّنكُمْ يَقُصُّونَ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِى وَيُنذِرُونَكُمْ لِقَآءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هَـٰذَا ۚ قَالُوا۟ شَهِدْنَا عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِنَا ۖ وَغَرَّتْهُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَشَهِدُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ كَـٰفِرِينَ (١٣٠)
‘O company of jinn and humans! Did there not come to you apostles from yourselves, recounting to you My signs and warning you of the encounter of this Day?’ They will say, ‘We testify against ourselves.’ The life of this world had deceived them, and they will testify against themselves that they had been faithless. (130)
ذَٰلِكَ أَن لَّمْ يَكُن رَّبُّكَ مُهْلِكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ بِظُلْمٍ وَأَهْلُهَا غَـٰفِلُونَ (١٣١)
This is because your Lord would never destroy the towns unjustly while their people were unaware. (131)
وَلِكُلٍّ دَرَجَـٰتٌ مِّمَّا عَمِلُوا۟ ۚ وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ (١٣٢)
For everyone there are ranks in accordance with what they have done; and your Lord is not oblivious of what they do. (132)

Commentary

The Completion of Proof:

In the preceding verses, the fate of the tyrannical, devil-like individuals on the Day of Resurrection was described. To ensure it is not assumed that they committed such acts in a state of ignorance, these verses clarify that sufficient warnings were given to them and the proof was completed. Therefore, on the Day of Judgment, He says to them: "O assembly of jinn and mankind! Did there not come to you messengers from among you, reciting to you My verses and warning you of the meeting of this Day of yours?" (يَا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ وَ الْإِنْسِ أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِنْكُمْ يَقُصُّونَ عَلَيْكُمْ آيَاتِي وَ يُنْذِرُكُمْ لِقَاءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هَذَا).

The word "ma'shar" is originally derived from "'asharah" meaning the number ten. Since the number ten is a complete number, the word "ma'shar" is applied to a complete assembly that encompasses various classes and groups. There is a discussion among commentators as to whether the messengers of the "jinn" are of their own kind or of the human species. However, what is clearly understood from the verses of Surah Al-Jinn is that the Quran and Islam were revealed for everyone, even them, and the Prophet of Islam was sent to all. Nevertheless, there is no obstacle to messengers and representatives from among themselves being commissioned by the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) to invite them.

(Further explanation in this regard, as well as the scientific meaning of "jinn," will be provided, God willing, in the commentary of Surah Al-Jinn in the 29th part of the Holy Quran).

However, it should be noted that the word "minkum" (from among you) is not proof that the prophets of each group will be of their own kind; for when a group is told "some individuals from among you...", these individuals may be from one tribe or from all tribes.

Then it says: Since the Day of Resurrection is not a day of concealment and the signs of everything are manifest and no one can hide anything, "they will all declare in response to this Divine question: We bear witness against ourselves and confess that such messengers came and delivered Your messages to us, but we opposed them" (قَالُوا شَهِدْنَا عَلَى أَنْفُسِنَا).

Yes, sufficient proofs from the Lord were at their disposal, and they could distinguish the path from the pit, "but the deceptive life of the world and its alluring glitter deceived them" (وَ غَرَّتْهُمُ الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا).

This sentence clearly conveys that the great barrier on the path to human salvation is excessive attachment and unconditional surrender to the manifestations of the material world—attachments that drag man into the chains of captivity and invite him toward every kind of injustice, oppression, transgression, selfishness, and rebellion.

Once again, the Quran emphasizes that "they explicitly bear witness against themselves that they followed the path of disbelief and stood in the ranks of the deniers of truth" (وَ شَهِدُوا عَلَى أَنْفُسِهِمْ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا كَافِرِينَ).

In the next verse, the same theme of the previous verse is recounted, but in the form of a general law and a perpetual Divine tradition: "This is because your Lord would never destroy the cities and settlements for their wrongdoings while their people were unaware," unless He sends messengers to them to make them aware of the ugliness of their deeds and says what needs to be said (ذَلِكَ أَنْ لَمْ يَكُنْ رَبُّكَ مُهْلِكَ الْقُرَى بِظُلْمٍ وَ أَهْلُهَا غَافِلُونَ).

The phrase "bi-zhulmin" (for/by wrongdoing) can mean that Allah does not punish anyone for their wrongdoings while they are in a state of unawareness before sending prophets. It can also mean that Allah does not punish unaware individuals out of injustice, for punishing them in such a state would be an act of injustice, and Allah is far above committing injustice against anyone [1].

Finally, the next verse summarizes their state, saying: "Each of these groups—the righteous and the wicked, the obedient and the lawbreakers, the truth-seekers and the oppressors—has degrees and ranks according to their deeds there, and your Lord is never unaware of their deeds; rather, He knows them all and gives everyone what they deserve" (وَ لِكُلٍّ دَرَجَاتٌ مِمَّا عَمِلُوا وَ مَا رَبُّكَ بِغَافِلٍ عَمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ).

This verse once again emphasizes the reality that all positions, "degrees" (upward ranks), and "ranks" (downward levels) are born of man's own deeds and nothing else.

[^1]: In the first case, the subject of "wrongdoing" (zhulm) is the disbelievers, and in the second case, where injustice is negated, the subject is Allah.

Ayahs

وَرَبُّكَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ذُو ٱلرَّحْمَةِ ۚ إِن يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَيَسْتَخْلِفْ مِنۢ بَعْدِكُم مَّا يَشَآءُ كَمَآ أَنشَأَكُم مِّن ذُرِّيَّةِ قَوْمٍ ءَاخَرِينَ (١٣٣)
Your Lord is the All-sufficient dispenser of mercy. If He wishes, He will take you away, and make whomever He wishes succeed you, just as He produced you from the descendants of another people. (133)
إِنَّ مَا تُوعَدُونَ لَـَٔاتٍ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ (١٣٤)
Indeed what you are promised will surely come, and you will not be able to thwart it. (134)
قُلْ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِكُمْ إِنِّى عَامِلٌ ۖ فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَن تَكُونُ لَهُۥ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلدَّارِ ۗ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (١٣٥)
Say, ‘O my people, act according to your ability; I too am acting. Soon you will know in whose favour the outcome of that abode will be. Indeed the wrongdoers will not be felicitous.’ (135)

Commentary

The first verse is, in fact, an argument for what was stated in the preceding verses regarding the absence of injustice from the Lord. The verse says: "Your Lord is both Self-Sufficient and Merciful." Therefore, there is no reason for Him to permit even the slightest injustice toward anyone, for injustice is committed by one who is either needy or harsh and cold-hearted (وَ رَبُّكَ الْغَنِيُّ ذُو الرَّحْمَةِ).

Furthermore, He has no need for your obedience, nor does He fear your sins, because "if He wills, He can take you away and replace you with whomever He wills, just as He produced you from the descendants of other people who differed from you in many characteristics" (إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَ يَسْتَخْلِفْ مِنْ بَعْدِكُمْ ما يَشاءُ كَما أَنْشَأَكُمْ مِنْ ذُرِّيَّةِ قَوْمٍ آخَرِينَ).

Thus, He is simultaneously Self-Sufficient, Merciful, and All-Powerful; consequently, the notion of injustice regarding Him is impossible.

And considering His infinite power, it is clear that: "Indeed, what you are promised regarding the Resurrection, reward, and punishment will surely come to pass, and there is not the slightest deviation in it" (إِنَّ ما تُوعَدُونَ لآتٍ). "And you can never escape the realm of His sovereignty or flee from the grasp of His justice" (وَ ما أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ) [1].

Then, He commands the Prophet to threaten them, saying: "O people! Do whatever is in your power; I too will do what my Lord has commanded me. But soon you will know who will have the good end and the final victory. Certainly, the wrongdoers and oppressors will not be successful and will never see the face of prosperity" (قُلْ يا قَوْمِ اعْمَلُوا عَلى‌ مَكانَتِكُمْ إِنِّي عامِلٌ فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ تَكُونُ لَهُ عاقِبَةُ الدَّارِ إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ).

[1]: "Mu‘jizīn" is derived from the root "I‘jāz," meaning to render another person incapable. The verse says you cannot render Allah incapable of the Resurrection and the execution of justice; in other words, you cannot resist His power.

Here, we see once again that instead of "disbelief" (kufr), the term "injustice" (zulm) is used. This indicates that disbelief and the denial of truth are a form of manifest injustice—injustice to oneself and injustice to society. Since injustice is contrary to the universal justice of creation, it is ultimately destined for defeat.

Ayah

وَجَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ مِمَّا ذَرَأَ مِنَ ٱلْحَرْثِ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ نَصِيبًا فَقَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا لِلَّهِ بِزَعْمِهِمْ وَهَـٰذَا لِشُرَكَآئِنَا ۖ فَمَا كَانَ لِشُرَكَآئِهِمْ فَلَا يَصِلُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَا كَانَ لِلَّهِ فَهُوَ يَصِلُ إِلَىٰ شُرَكَآئِهِمْ ۗ سَآءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ (١٣٦)
They dedicate a portion to Allah out of what He has created of the crops and cattle, and say, ‘This is for Allah,’ so do they maintain, ‘and this is for our partners.’ But what is for their partners does not reach Allah, and what is for Allah reaches their partners. Evil is the judgement that they make. (136)

Commentary

Once again, to uproot idolatrous thoughts from the minds, the Quran addresses the beliefs, customs, traditions, and superstitious rituals of the polytheists, clarifying their superstitious nature with eloquent expression.

First, it states: "The disbelievers of Mecca and other polytheists would assign a portion of their crops and cattle to Allah and a portion to the idols, saying: 'This portion is for Allah'—according to their claim—'and this is for our partners (the idols)'" (وَ جَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ مِمَّا ذَرَأَ مِنَ الْحَرْثِ وَ الْأَنْعامِ نَصِيباً فَقالُوا هذا لِلَّهِ بِزَعْمِهِمْ وَ هذا لِشُرَكائِنا).

Although the verse initially mentions only the portion for Allah, it is understood from the subsequent phrases that they would set aside one portion for Allah and another for the idols. It is mentioned in narrations that the portion assigned to Allah was spent on children and guests; however, the portion of crops and cattle assigned to the idols was reserved for the servants and custodians of the idols and temples, sacrificial ceremonies, and their own personal use [^1]. The expression "لِشُرَكائِنا" (our partners) regarding the idols is because they considered them partners in their wealth, capital, and lives.

The expression "مِمَّا ذَرَأَ" (from what He created) is, in fact, an allusion to the invalidity of their belief. Since all these assets were created by Allah, how could they assign one portion of them to Allah and another to the idols?

Then, it points to their strange judgment in this matter, saying: "The portion they assigned to their partners (idols) would never reach Allah, but the portion assigned to Allah would reach their partners" (فَما كانَ لِشُرَكائِهِمْ فَلا يَصِلُ إِلَى اللَّهِ وَ ما كانَ لِلَّهِ فَهُوَ يَصِلُ إِلى‌ شُرَكائِهِمْ‌).

There is discussion among commentators regarding the meaning of this sentence, but they all essentially return to one reality: whenever an accident occurred and a part of the portion assigned to Allah from the crops or cattle was damaged or destroyed, they would say, "It does not matter, Allah is Self-Sufficient." However, if something from the idols' portion was lost, they would replace it with Allah's portion, saying, "The idols have a greater need."

Similarly, if water from a field designated as Allah's portion seeped into a field designated for the idols, they would say, "There is no harm, Allah is Self-Sufficient." But if the situation was reversed, they would block it, saying the idols had a greater need.

At the end of the verse, with a short phrase, it condemns this superstitious belief, saying: "Evil is what they judge" (ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ‌).

The evidence for the vileness of their action—besides the fact that idolatry is fundamentally corrupt and baseless—lies in several points:

  1. Despite everything being created by Allah, being His absolute property, and Him being the Ruler, Disposer, and Protector of all beings, they only allocated a portion of it to Allah, as if they were the original owners and the right of distribution was solely in their hands! (As we mentioned, the phrase "مِمَّا ذَرَأَ" points to this reality).

  2. In this division, they prioritized the side of the idols. Thus, any damage to Allah's portion was considered insignificant, but damages to the idols' portion were compensated using Allah's portion, which was taken and given to the idols, as alluded to in the verse. This was a form of preference and superiority given to the idols over Allah!!

  3. It is understood from some narrations that they placed a special importance on the idols' portion; therefore, the custodians, servants of the idols, and the idolaters themselves would eat from that portion, while Allah's portion was given only to children and guests. Evidence suggests that the fat, healthy sheep and the best grains belonged to the idols so they could, so to speak, feast heartily.

All of this indicates that in their division, they did not value Allah even as much as the idols. What judgment could be more hideous and shameful than for a human to consider a piece of stone or worthless wood higher than the Creator of the universe? Can any intellectual degradation be imagined greater than this?!

[^1]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 8, p. 122.

Ayah

وَكَذَٰلِكَ زَيَّنَ لِكَثِيرٍ مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ قَتْلَ أَوْلَـٰدِهِمْ شُرَكَآؤُهُمْ لِيُرْدُوهُمْ وَلِيَلْبِسُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ دِينَهُمْ ۖ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَا فَعَلُوهُ ۖ فَذَرْهُمْ وَمَا يَفْتَرُونَ (١٣٧)
That is how to most of the polytheists the slaying of their children is presented as decorous by those whom they ascribe as partners [to Allah], that they may ruin them and confound their religion for them. Had Allah wished, they would not have done it. So leave them with what they fabricate. (137)

Commentary

In this verse, the Quran points to another of the pagans' evil deeds and their shameful crimes, stating: "Just as their division [of offerings] between Allah and the idols seemed fair to them—and they considered this ugly, superstitious, and even ridiculous act as a commendable deed—likewise, their partners had made the killing of their children seem fair to many of the polytheists," to the extent that they considered killing their own children a type of "honor" or "worship" (وَ كَذلِكَ زَيَّنَ لِكَثِيرٍ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ قَتْلَ أَوْلادِهِمْ شُرَكاؤُهُمْ).

The term "partners" (شركاء) here refers to the idols, for whose sake they would sometimes sacrifice their children or make vows that if they were granted a child, they would sacrifice it for the idol, as has been mentioned in the history of ancient idolaters. Therefore, the attribution of "beautifying" (تزيين) to the idols is because their affection and love for the idol compelled them toward this criminal act. Based on this interpretation, the "killing" mentioned in the above verse is not related to the issue of burying daughters alive or killing sons out of fear of poverty.

There is also the possibility that the beautification of this crime by the idols means that the servants and custodians of the idol temples encouraged the people to do this, presenting themselves as the voice of the idol. It is narrated that the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) would seek permission from "Hubal" (their great idol) for important journeys and other affairs. Their method of seeking permission was to shake arrow shafts kept in a special bag adjacent to Hubal—on some of which were written "Do it" (افعل) and on others "Do not do it" (لا تفعل)—and draw one out. Whatever was written on it was regarded as the message of Hubal! This shows that they discovered the "opinion" of the idols through this method; it is not at all unlikely that the matter of sacrificing for the idol was also introduced by the custodians as a message from the idol.

Another possibility is that the burying of daughters alive—which, according to historical accounts, became common among the tribe of "Bani Tamim" as a way to remove a perceived stain of disgrace—was introduced as a message from the idols. We read in history that "Nu'man ibn Mundhir" attacked a group of Arabs and took their women captive, among whom was the daughter of "Qays ibn Asim." Later, peace was established between them, and every woman returned to her tribe except for the daughter of Qays, who preferred to stay among the enemy tribe, perhaps to marry one of their youths. This matter was very heavy for Qays, and he swore (by the idols) that whenever another daughter was born to him, he would bury her alive. It did not take long before this act became a tradition among them, where under the mask of defending honor, they committed the greatest crime: killing innocent children [1]. Therefore, the burying of daughters alive may also be included in the above verse.

One more possibility in the interpretation of the verse comes to mind, although commentators have not mentioned it: the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance held such importance for the idols that they spent their precious wealth on the idols and their powerful, high-income custodians and servants, becoming poor and destitute themselves to the point that they would sometimes slaughter their children due to hunger and poverty. This love for the idol had beautified such an ugly act in their eyes.

However, the first interpretation—namely, sacrificing children for the idols—is most consistent with the text of the verse.

Then the Quran says: The result of such evil deeds was that the idols and the idol-servants would cast the polytheists into destruction and obscure the true religion and creed for them, depriving them of reaching a pure faith (لِيُرْدُوهُمْ وَ لِيَلْبِسُوا عَلَيْهِمْ دِينَهُمْ).

The Quran states: "But despite all this, if Allah had willed, He could have forcibly prevented them." However, compulsion is contrary to Allah's Sunnah (way). Allah willed for His servants to be free so that the path of education and perfection remains open, for in compulsion, there is neither education nor perfection (وَ لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ ما فَعَلُوهُ).

[^1]: Some have imagined that the word "children" (اولاد) in the above verse is not compatible with this interpretation, but it should be noted that "awlad" has a broad meaning that applies to both sons and daughters, just as we read in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 233: "وَ الْوالِداتُ يُرْضِعْنَ أَوْلادَهُنَّ حَوْلَيْنِ كامِلَيْنِ" ("Mothers shall suckle their children for two whole years").

In conclusion, it says: Now that this is the case, and they are immersed in such ugly and shameful superstitious acts and do not even perceive their foulness—and worst of all, they sometimes attribute them to Allah—"Leave them and their fabrications alone, and attend to the education of ready and receptive hearts" (فَذَرْهُمْ وَ ما يَفْتَرُونَ).

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أَنْعَـٰمٌ وَحَرْثٌ حِجْرٌ لَّا يَطْعَمُهَآ إِلَّا مَن نَّشَآءُ بِزَعْمِهِمْ وَأَنْعَـٰمٌ حُرِّمَتْ ظُهُورُهَا وَأَنْعَـٰمٌ لَّا يَذْكُرُونَ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهَا ٱفْتِرَآءً عَلَيْهِ ۚ سَيَجْزِيهِم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (١٣٨)
And they say, ‘These cattle and tillage are a taboo: none may eat them except whom we please,’ so they maintain, and there are cattle whose backs are forbidden and cattle over which they do not mention Allah’s Name, fabricating a lie against Him. Soon He will requite them for what they used to fabricate. (138)
وَقَالُوا۟ مَا فِى بُطُونِ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ خَالِصَةٌ لِّذُكُورِنَا وَمُحَرَّمٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَزْوَٰجِنَا ۖ وَإِن يَكُن مَّيْتَةً فَهُمْ فِيهِ شُرَكَآءُ ۚ سَيَجْزِيهِمْ وَصْفَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ (١٣٩)
And they say, ‘That which is in the bellies of these cattle is exclusively for our males and forbidden to our wives. But if it be still-born, they will all share it.’ Soon He will requite them for their allegations. Indeed, He is all-wise, all-knowing. (139)

Commentary

In these verses, several parts of the superstitious rulings of the idolaters are mentioned, which reflect the shallowness of their thinking and complete the discussion of the previous verses.

First, it states that the idolaters would say: "This portion of the cattle and crops which is dedicated to the idols and is their share is strictly forbidden to everyone, except those whom we wish," and according to their imagination, it was only lawful for this specific group and not for others (وَ قالُوا هذِهِ أَنْعامٌ وَ حَرْثٌ حِجْرٌ لا يَطْعَمُها إِلَّا مَنْ نَشاءُ بِزَعْمِهِمْ).

Their intention was the servants and custodians of the idols and the idol temples; only this group, in their imagination, had the right to eat from the share of the idols.

From this statement, we conclude that this part of the verse actually refers to the manner of consuming the share that they allocated for the idols from the crops and cattle, the details of which were mentioned in the two preceding verses.

The word "hijr" (rhyming with she'r) originally means "to prohibit" or "to forbid." As Raghib has stated in the book Al-Mufradat, it is not unlikely that it is derived from the root "hijarah" meaning "stone," because when they wanted to declare an area forbidden or restricted, they would surround it with stones. The reason the "Hijr Ismail" is called by this name is that it is separated from other parts of Masjid al-Haram by a specific stone wall. For the same reason, the "intellect" is sometimes called "hijr" because it prevents a person from committing wrong acts. Whenever someone is under the supervision and protection of another, it is said they are in their "hijr" (lap/custody), and a "mahjur" is someone who is prohibited from disposing of their own property. [^1] Then, it points to the second thing they had prohibited, saying:

"They believed that there were certain cattle whose backs were forbidden to be ridden" (وَ أَنْعامٌ حُرِّمَتْ ظُهُورُها).

Apparently, these were the same animals described in verse 103 of Surah Al-Ma'idah as "Sa'ibah," "Bahirah," and "Ham" (for more information, refer to the commentary of the aforementioned verse).

Then, it explains the third part of their unjust rulings, saying: "They would not mention the name of Allah over a portion of the cattle" (وَ أَنْعامٌ لا يَذْكُرُونَ اسْمَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْهَا).

This sentence might refer to animals over which only the name of an idol was mentioned at the time of slaughter, or animals they had forbidden for riding during the Hajj pilgrimage, as narrated from some commentators in Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir al-Kabir, Al-Manar, and Qurtubi. In either case, it was a baseless and superstitious ruling.

The strange thing is that they were not content with these superstitious rulings, but rather "they would fabricate lies against Allah and attribute them to Him" (افْتِراءً عَلَيْهِ).

At the end of the verse, after mentioning these fabricated rulings, it says: "Allah will soon recompense them for these fabrications" (سَيَجْزِيهِمْ بِما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ).

Indeed, when humans attempt to legislate rulings and laws based on their own deficient intellect,

[^1]: It should be noted that "hijr" in the above verse has a descriptive meaning, used in the sense of "prohibited," and it is the same for both masculine and feminine.

every tribe might create rulings and regulations according to their own whims and desires, and unnecessarily forbid Allah's blessings upon themselves or make their own ugly and wrong actions lawful. This is why we say that the Legislator must only be Allah, who knows everything, is aware of the benefits of all matters, and is free from any whims or desires.

In the next verse, it points to another of their superstitious rulings regarding the meat of animals, stating: "They said: Whatever is in the bellies of these cattle is exclusively for our males and forbidden to our wives; but if it is born dead, then they are all partners in it" (وَ قالُوا ما فِي بُطُونِ هذِهِ الْأَنْعامِ خالِصَةٌ لِذُكُورِنا وَ مُحَرَّمٌ عَلى‌ أَزْواجِنا وَ إِنْ يَكُنْ مَيْتَةً فَهُمْ فِيهِ شُرَكاءُ). It should be noted that the phrase "هذِهِ الْأَنْعامِ" (these cattle) refers to the same animals mentioned in the previous verse.

Some commentators have suggested the possibility that the phrase "ما فِي بُطُونِ هذِهِ الْأَنْعامِ" (what is in the bellies of these cattle) also includes the milk of these animals. However, considering the phrase "وَ إِنْ يَكُنْ مَيْتَةً" (if it be dead), it becomes clear that the verse only discusses the fetus; if it was born alive, they considered it exclusive to men, and if it was born dead—which was not very desirable to them—they considered everyone equal in it!

This ruling, firstly, had no philosophy or reason behind it; secondly, regarding the fetus born dead, it was very foul and repulsive, as the meat of such an animal is often spoiled and harmful; and thirdly, it was a type of blatant discrimination between the male and female genders, because whatever was good was reserved for men, and whatever was bad, women were given a share of it.

Following this ignorant ruling, the Quran concludes the matter with this sentence: "Soon He will recompense them for their [false] descriptions" (سَيَجْزِيهِمْ وَصْفَهُمْ).

The expression "wasf" (description) refers to the description they made of Allah, attributing the prohibition of these foods to Him, although it may also refer to the quality and state that overtakes a sinner due to repeated sin, making them deserving of punishment. At the end of the verse, it says: "Indeed, He is All-Wise, All-Knowing" (إِنَّهُ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ).

He is both aware of their actions, words, and unjust accusations, and He punishes them based on a precise account.

Ayah

قَدْ خَسِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَتَلُوٓا۟ أَوْلَـٰدَهُمْ سَفَهًۢا بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَحَرَّمُوا۟ مَا رَزَقَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱفْتِرَآءً عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُهْتَدِينَ (١٤٠)
They are certainly losers who slay their children foolishly without knowledge, and forbid what Allah has provided them, fabricating a lie against Allah. Certainly, they have gone astray and are not guided. (140)

Commentary

Following the previous few verses which discussed some of the superstitious rulings and the ugly, disgraceful customs of the pre-Islamic era of ignorance (Jahiliyyah)—including the killing of children as sacrifices for idols, or burying daughters alive to preserve tribal and family "honor," as well as the prohibition of some lawful blessings—this verse severely condemns all these actions and rulings. Using "seven different expressions" in short yet very eloquent and striking sentences, it clarifies their status.

First, it says: "Those who killed their children out of folly and ignorance have indeed lost." They suffered loss from a human and moral perspective, an emotional perspective, and a social perspective, and above all, they suffered spiritual loss in the other world: (قَدْ خَسِرَ الَّذِينَ قَتَلُوا أَوْلادَهُمْ سَفَهاً بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ‌). In this sentence, their action is introduced as a type of "loss" (khusran), then "folly and light-headedness" (safahah), and then an "ignorant act" (bi-ghayri 'ilm). Each of these three expressions alone is sufficient to demonstrate the ugliness of their deed. What intellect allows a person to kill their own child with their own hands? Furthermore, is it not folly and light-headedness to not feel shame for this act, but rather to consider it a source of pride or an act of worship? What science or knowledge allows a person to accept such an act as a tradition or law in their society?! It is here that we recall the statement of Ibn Abbas, who used to say: "If anyone wishes to know the extent of the backwardness of the ignorant tribes, let them read the verses of Surah Al-An'am (meaning the above verses)."

Then the Quran says: "These people prohibited for themselves what Allah had provided for them as sustenance and had made permissible and lawful, and they fabricated lies against Allah, claiming that He had forbidden them" (وَ حَرَّمُوا ما رَزَقَهُمُ اللَّهُ افْتِراءً عَلَى اللَّهِ‌). In this sentence, their actions are condemned with two other expressions. Firstly, they prohibited for themselves a blessing that Allah had "provided" for them—something even necessary and essential for the continuation of their lives—and thus trampled upon Allah's law. Secondly, they "fabricated lies" against Allah, claiming He had given such a command, while it was never so.

At the end of the verse, it condemns them with two more expressions. First, it says they have certainly gone astray (قَدْ ضَلُّوا), and then it adds that they were never on the path of guidance (وَ ما كانُوا مُهْتَدِينَ‌).

Ayah

۞ وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَ جَنَّـٰتٍ مَّعْرُوشَـٰتٍ وَغَيْرَ مَعْرُوشَـٰتٍ وَٱلنَّخْلَ وَٱلزَّرْعَ مُخْتَلِفًا أُكُلُهُۥ وَٱلزَّيْتُونَ وَٱلرُّمَّانَ مُتَشَـٰبِهًا وَغَيْرَ مُتَشَـٰبِهٍ ۚ كُلُوا۟ مِن ثَمَرِهِۦٓ إِذَآ أَثْمَرَ وَءَاتُوا۟ حَقَّهُۥ يَوْمَ حَصَادِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تُسْرِفُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (١٤١)
It is He who produces gardens trellised and without trellises, and palm-trees and crops of diverse produce, olives and pomegranates, similar and dissimilar. Eat of its fruits when it fructifies, and give its due on the day of harvest, and do not be wasteful; indeed, He does not like the wasteful. (141)

Commentary

A Great Lesson in Monotheism

In this verse, several subjects are mentioned, each of which is, in fact, a result of the other.

First, it says: "It is Allah who has created various gardens and crops with diverse trees; some are trellised, which, with their exquisite and delightful appearance, draw the eyes toward them and sweeten the human palate with delicious and blessed fruits; and some stand upright without the need for trellises, spreading their shade over human beings and serving human nutrition with various fruits" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْشَأَ جَنَّاتٍ مَعْرُوشاتٍ وَ غَيْرَ مَعْرُوشاتٍ).

Commentators have offered three possibilities regarding the interpretation of the words "ma'rushat" (trellised) and "ghayra ma'rushat" (untrellised):

  1. The same as mentioned above: trees that do not stand on their own and require a trellis, and trees that stand on their own without needing a trellis. (Since "arsh" linguistically means to raise up or any raised structure, and for this reason, a ceiling or a high-legged throne is called an arsh).

  2. "Ma'rushat" refers to domestic trees protected by walls and the like in gardens, while "ghayra ma'rushat" refers to wild trees of the desert, forest, and mountains.

  3. "Ma'rushat" is a tree that stands upright or rises above the ground, while "ghayra ma'rushat" is a tree that creeps and spreads along the ground.

However, the first meaning seems more appropriate, and perhaps the mention of "ma'rushat" at the beginning of the discourse is due to the strange and wonderful structure of these types of trees. A brief look at the grapevine and its winding stem and branches, equipped with special hooks that cling to surrounding objects to straighten its back, is a witness to this claim.

Then, referring to two parts of the gardens and jannat, it says: "And [He created] the date palms and crops" (وَ النَّخْلَ وَ الزَّرْعَ).

The specific mention of these two is because of their special importance in human life and nutrition (note that "jannat" refers to both gardens and lands covered with crops).

Then it adds that "these trees differ from one another in terms of fruit and taste; that is, despite growing from the same soil, each has its own specific taste, fragrance, and property not found in the other" (مُخْتَلِفاً أُكُلُهُ) [^1]. Then it points to two other types of fruits that are exceptionally useful and possess vital value, saying: "And [He created] the olive and the pomegranate" (وَ الزَّيْتُونَ وَ الرُّمَّانَ). The selection of these two is apparently because these two trees, while resembling each other in appearance, are very different in terms of fruit and nutritional properties. Therefore, it immediately says: "resembling [each other] and yet not resembling" (مُتَشابِهاً وَ غَيْرَ مُتَشابِهٍ) [^2]. After mentioning all these various blessings, the Lord says: "Eat of their fruit when they come to fruition, but do not forget that at the time of harvest, you must pay its due" (كُلُوا مِنْ ثَمَرِهِ إِذا أَثْمَرَ وَ آتُوا حَقَّهُ يَوْمَ حَصادِهِ).

And at the end, He commands: "Do not be extravagant, for Allah does not love the extravagant" (وَ لا تُسْرِفُوا إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُسْرِفِينَ).

"Israf" (extravagance) means exceeding the limits of moderation, and this sentence could refer to not being extravagant in eating or not being extravagant in giving; for some individuals are so open-handed that they give away everything they have to others, leaving themselves and their children in hardship.

[^1]: Ukul (with a damma on the alif and a sukun or damma on the kaf) means that which is eaten (from the root akl meaning to eat).

[^2]: An explanation regarding this was also provided under verse 99 of this same Surah in Volume 5.

Key Points

  1. The Connection of the Verse with Previous Verses: In the preceding verses of this Surah, the discussion was about the superstitious rulings of the polytheists, who would set aside a portion of crops and livestock for Allah and believed these shares must be consumed in a specific way. They would forbid riding the backs of certain livestock and would sacrifice their children for certain idols.

The above verse and the verse following it are, in fact, a response to all these superstitious rulings, for it explicitly states:

The Creator of all these blessings is Allah; He is the one who created all these trees, livestock, and crops, and He is the one who commanded to benefit from them and not to be extravagant. Therefore, no one other than Him has the right to "forbid" or "permit."

  1. Regarding what purpose the phrase idha athmara (when it bears fruit) pursues by mentioning the word thamarihi (its fruit) before it, there is discussion among commentators. However, it appears the goal of this phrase is that as soon as the fruit appears on the trees, or the ears and grains appear in the crops, benefiting from them becomes permissible and lawful, even if the right of the needy has not yet been paid. This right must only be paid at the time of picking the fruit and during the harvest (yawm al-hasad) (take note).
  1. What is meant by this "right" that must be paid at the time of harvest? Some believe it is the obligatory Zakat, i.e., one-tenth or one-twentieth. However, considering that this Surah was revealed in Makkah and the ruling of Zakat was revealed in the second year of the Hijrah or later in Madinah, such a possibility seems very unlikely.

In numerous narrations reaching us from the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, as well as in many Sunni narrations, this right is introduced as something other than Zakat. It refers to what is given to the needy person present at the time of picking the fruit or harvesting the crop, and it has no fixed or specific limit [^1]. In this case, is this ruling obligatory or recommended? Some believe it was an obligatory ruling required of Muslims before the legislation of the Zakat ruling, but after the revelation of the Zakat verse, this ruling was abrogated, and the Zakat ruling with its specific limits took its place.

However, it is understood from the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام that this ruling has not been abrogated and remains in force as a recommended (mustahabb) ruling.

  1. The expression using the word "yawm" (day) may indicate that picking fruits and harvesting crops is better done during the day, so that the needy may be present and a portion given to them, rather than some stingy individuals doing this work at night so that no one becomes aware. This subject has also been emphasized in narrations reaching us through the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام [^2].

[^1]: Narrations related to this subject are cited by the author of Wasa'il in the Book of Zakat, Chapters on Zakat of Grains, Chapter 13, and by Bayhaqi in the book al-Sunan, Vol. 4, p. 132.

[^2]: Regarding this, refer to the book Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Book of Zakat, Chapters on Zakat of Grains, Chapter on the Dislike of Harvesting and Picking... at Night (Vol. 6, p. 136).

Ayahs

وَمِنَ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ حَمُولَةً وَفَرْشًا ۚ كُلُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (١٤٢)
Of the cattle [some] are for burden and [some] for slaughter. Eat of what Allah has provided you and do not follow in Satan’s footsteps; he is indeed your manifest enemy. (142)
ثَمَـٰنِيَةَ أَزْوَٰجٍ ۖ مِّنَ ٱلضَّأْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ وَمِنَ ٱلْمَعْزِ ٱثْنَيْنِ ۗ قُلْ ءَآلذَّكَرَيْنِ حَرَّمَ أَمِ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ أَمَّا ٱشْتَمَلَتْ عَلَيْهِ أَرْحَامُ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ ۖ نَبِّـُٔونِى بِعِلْمٍ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٤٣)
Eight mates: two of sheep, and two of goats. Say, ‘Is it the two males that He has forbidden or the two females, or what is contained in the wombs of the two females? Inform me with knowledge, should you be truthful.’ (143)
وَمِنَ ٱلْإِبِلِ ٱثْنَيْنِ وَمِنَ ٱلْبَقَرِ ٱثْنَيْنِ ۗ قُلْ ءَآلذَّكَرَيْنِ حَرَّمَ أَمِ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ أَمَّا ٱشْتَمَلَتْ عَلَيْهِ أَرْحَامُ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ ۖ أَمْ كُنتُمْ شُهَدَآءَ إِذْ وَصَّىٰكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِهَـٰذَا ۚ فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا لِّيُضِلَّ ٱلنَّاسَ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٤٤)
And two of camels and two of oxen. Say, ‘Is it the two males that He has forbidden or the two females, or what is contained in the wombs of the two females? Were you witnesses when Allah enjoined this upon you?’ So who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah to mislead the people without any knowledge? Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (144)

Commentary

As previously mentioned, these verses aim to negate the superstitious rulings of the polytheists regarding agriculture and livestock. While the preceding verse discussed various types of crops and God-given fruits, these verses speak of lawful (halal) animals and the services they provide.

First, it states: "Allah is the One Who created for you from the livestock large, burden-bearing animals and small animals" (وَ مِنَ الْأَنْعامِ حَمُولَةً وَ فَرْشاً) [^1]. Hamulah (حَمُولَةً) is a collective noun and, as linguists have noted, has no singular form of its own kind; it refers to large animals used for carrying loads, such as camels, horses, and the like.

Farsh (فَرْشاً) carries its common meaning (carpet/spread), but here it is interpreted as sheep and similar small animals. The point seems to be that such animals are very close to the ground and, in comparison to large burden-bearing animals, are considered like a carpet. If one looks from afar at a field where sheep are grazing, they appear exactly like a carpet spread across the earth, whereas a herd of camels never presents such a view from a distance.

The contrast between Hamulah and Farsh also supports this meaning.

Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that this word refers to the carpets and floorings people produce from these animals—meaning many animals are used both for transport and for providing materials for carpets—but the first interpretation is closer to the meaning of the verse.

[^1]: The "Waw" at the beginning of this verse is a conjunction, and what follows it is conjoined to "Jannat" (gardens) mentioned in the previous verse.

Then it concludes: now that all of these are the creation of Allah and their ruling is in His hand, He commands you: "Eat of what Allah has provided for you" (كُلُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللَّهُ‌).

The reason it does not say "eat of these animals" but rather "eat of what He has provided for you" is that lawful animals are not limited to livestock; there are other animals whose meat is lawful which were not mentioned in the preceding phrase.

To emphasize this point and to invalidate the superstitious rulings of the polytheists, He says: "And do not follow the footsteps of Satan, for he is an open enemy to you" (an enemy who has declared war against you since the beginning of Adam's creation) (وَ لا تَتَّبِعُوا خُطُواتِ الشَّيْطانِ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ‌).

This is an indication that such baseless rulings and regulations, which stem purely from raw imagination, whims, and ignorance, are nothing but a series of satanic whispers that lead you away from the truth step by step and leave you wandering in error.

An interesting explanation regarding this phrase was provided under verse 168 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

In the following verse, by way of explanation, He describes a portion of the lawful animals and those that are both burden-bearers and usable for human nutrition, saying: "Allah created eight pairs of livestock for you: of the sheep, a pair (male and female), and of the goats, a pair (male and female)" (ثَمانِيَةَ أَزْواجٍ‌ [^1] مِنَ الضَّأْنِ اثْنَيْنِ وَ مِنَ الْمَعْزِ اثْنَيْنِ‌).

[^1]: Azwaj (أَزْواجٍ) is the plural of Zawj (زَوْجٍ), which linguistically means "pair." However, it should be noted that it is sometimes applied to the set of two (male and female) and sometimes to each individual of the pair; hence the set is called Zawjayn. The reference to "eight pairs" in the verse refers to four types of male animals and four types of female animals. Another possibility suggested for the verse is that it refers to pairs of domestic and wild animals—meaning the male and female of domestic sheep and wild sheep, and so on.

After mentioning these four pairs, He immediately commands His Prophet to ask them explicitly: "Say: Is it the two males He has forbidden or the two females?" (قُلْ آلذَّكَرَيْنِ حَرَّمَ أَمِ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ‌) "Or that which the wombs of the two females contain?" (أَمَّا اشْتَمَلَتْ عَلَيْهِ أَرْحامُ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ‌). Then He adds: "Inform me with knowledge if you are truthful" (نَبِّئُونِي بِعِلْمٍ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ‌)—meaning if you have any proof based on knowledge for the prohibition of any of these animals, then inform me.

In the next verse, He mentions the other four pairs, saying: "And of the camels, two (male and female), and of the cattle, two (male and female). Say: Is it the two males He has forbidden or the two females, or that which the wombs of the two females contain?" (وَ مِنَ الْإِبِلِ اثْنَيْنِ وَ مِنَ الْبَقَرِ اثْنَيْنِ قُلْ آلذَّكَرَيْنِ حَرَّمَ أَمِ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ أَمَّا اشْتَمَلَتْ عَلَيْهِ أَرْحامُ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ‌).

Since the ruling on the lawfulness or prohibition of these animals lies solely with Allah—Who is their Creator and the Creator of mankind and the entire universe—anyone who claims something is lawful or prohibited must either have the testimony of reason, or have received personal revelation, or have been present when such a command was issued by a Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

The previous verse explicitly stated that the polytheists possessed no scientific or rational evidence for the prohibition of these animals. Since they did not claim prophethood or revelation either, only the third possibility remains: that they claim to have been present and witnessed when this command was issued by the Divine Prophets. Therefore, He says: "Or were you witnesses when Allah enjoined this upon you?" (أَمْ كُنْتُمْ شُهَداءَ إِذْ وَصَّاكُمُ اللَّهُ بِهذا).

And since the answer to this question is also negative, it is proven that they had no basis for this other than slander and fabrication.

Thus, at the end of the verse, He adds: "Who then is more unjust than one who invents a lie against Allah to mislead people without knowledge? Indeed, Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" (فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً لِيُضِلَّ النَّاسَ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ، إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ‌) [^1]. From this verse, it is understood that inventing lies against Allah is one of the greatest injustices—an injustice against the Holy Station of the Lord, an injustice against the servants of Allah, and an injustice against oneself. As we have said before, the expression "most unjust" in such cases is "relative"; therefore, there is no contradiction in this same expression being used for some other major sins.

Furthermore, it is understood from this verse that Divine guidance and misguidance are not matters of compulsion; rather, their factors and preliminaries begin with the human being himself. When someone commits oppression and injustice, Allah withholds His support from him, and he will wander lost in the paths of error.

[^1]: Regarding what the phrase "without knowledge" (بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ) relates to, several possibilities have been suggested. However, it is not unlikely that it relates to the verb "mislead" (يضل); meaning that due to their own ignorance, they lead people into misguidance.

Ayah

قُل لَّآ أَجِدُ فِى مَآ أُوحِىَ إِلَىَّ مُحَرَّمًا عَلَىٰ طَاعِمٍ يَطْعَمُهُۥٓ إِلَّآ أَن يَكُونَ مَيْتَةً أَوْ دَمًا مَّسْفُوحًا أَوْ لَحْمَ خِنزِيرٍ فَإِنَّهُۥ رِجْسٌ أَوْ فِسْقًا أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ بِهِۦ ۚ فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ بَاغٍ وَلَا عَادٍ فَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٤٥)
Say, ‘I do not find in what has been revealed to me that anyone be forbidden to eat anything except carrion or spilt blood, or the flesh of swine—for that is indeed unclean—or an impiety offered to other than Allah.’ But should someone be compelled, without being rebellious or aggressive, indeed your Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (145)

Commentary

A Portion of Prohibited Animals

Subsequently, to clarify the Divine prohibitions in contrast to the innovations that the polytheists had introduced into the true religion, Allah commands the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in this verse to explicitly tell them: "Say, 'I do not find within that which has been revealed to me [any food] forbidden to one who would eat it'" (including men and women, young and old) (قُلْ لا أَجِدُ فِي ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ مُحَرَّماً عَلى‌ طاعِمٍ يَطْعَمُهُ‌).

"Except if it be a dead animal" (إِلَّا أَنْ يَكُونَ مَيْتَةً).

"Or spilled blood" (meaning the blood that flows out of the animal's body, not the blood that remains within the capillaries inside the meat after the jugular veins are cut and a large amount of blood has exited) (أَوْ دَماً مَسْفُوحاً).

"Or the flesh of swine" (أَوْ لَحْمَ خِنزِيرٍ).

Because "all of these are rijs (impurity)" and a cause of aversion for a healthy human nature, a source of various contaminations, and a spring of diverse harms (فإنه رجس).

Although the pronoun innahu (it is) is singular, many commentators believe it refers back to all three categories (carrion, blood, and swine flesh), and the meaning of the sentence is: "All that has been mentioned is impurity." [^1] This is also consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse, as there is no doubt that carrion and blood are impure just like the flesh of swine.

Then, referring to the fourth type, He says: "Or an abomination over which a name other than that of Allah has been invoked" (أَوْ فِسْقاً أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ اللَّهِ بِهِ‌). [^2] Interestingly, instead of using the word "animal," the word fisq (abomination/disobedience) is used. As we have previously noted, fisq means departing from the path of servitude and obedience to Allah's command; hence, every type of sin is called fisq. However, mentioning this word in contrast to rijs—which was used for the three previous prohibited items—might indicate that prohibited meats are generally of two types: meats whose prohibition is due to impurity, natural aversion, and physical harm, which are labeled as rijs; and meats that are neither impure nor hygienically harmful, but from a moral and spiritual perspective, they signify alienation from God and distance from the school of Monotheism (Tawhid).

[^1]: In fact, the word "innahu" carries the meaning of "innama dhukira" (that which was mentioned).

[^2]: "Uhilla" is derived from the root "ihlal," which originally comes from "hilal" (crescent moon). it means to raise one's voice upon sighting the crescent moon. Subsequently, it was applied to any loud sound, including the first cry of a newborn. Since the polytheists used to invoke the names of idols loudly while slaughtering animals, the term "uhilla" was employed.

And for this very reason, they have been prohibited.

Therefore, one should not expect prohibited meats to always possess hygienic harms; rather, they are sometimes prohibited due to their spiritual and moral harms. From this, it becomes clear that the conditions for Islamic slaughter are also of two types: some, like cutting the four main vessels and draining the animal's blood, have a hygienic aspect; while others, such as facing the Qibla, saying "Bismillah," and the slaughterer being a Muslim, have a spiritual aspect.

At the end of the verse, those who are compelled by necessity and desperation—finding no other food to save their lives—and consume these prohibited meats are exempted. He says: "But whoever is forced by necessity, neither desiring [it] nor transgressing [its limit], then indeed, your Lord is Forgiving and Merciful" (فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ باغٍ وَ لا عادٍ فَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌). [^1] In reality, these two conditions are set so that individuals do not use "necessity" as a pretext to violate the boundaries of Divine laws or exceed the limits of Allah's regulations under the guise of desperation.

However, in some narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام), such as a narration cited in Tafsir al-Ayyashi from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read:

al-Baghi is the oppressor, and al-Adi is the usurper (الباغى الظالم و العادى الغاصب‌).

And in another narration from the Imam (عليه السلام), it is stated:

al-Baghi is the one who revolts

[^1]: "Baghin" or "Baghi" is from the root "baghy," meaning seeking/desiring, and "adin" or "adi" is from the root "adw," meaning transgression.

against a just Imam, and al-Adi is the thief (الباغى الخارج على الامام و العادى اللص‌).

These narrations and their likes indicate that the necessity to consume prohibited meats usually occurs during travels. If someone embarks on a journey for the purpose of oppression, tyranny, usurpation, or theft and encounters a food shortage, eating prohibited meats is not permissible for him. Although he is obligated to use those meats to save his life, he will still bear the punishment for this sin, as he himself provided the preliminaries for such a forbidden journey. In any case, these narrations are fully consistent with the general concept of the verse.

Answer to a Question

A question arises here: How is it that all Divine prohibitions regarding food are restricted to four things, while we know that prohibited foods are not limited to these? The meat of predatory animals, marine animals (except for fish with scales), and the like are all prohibited, yet no mention of them is made in the verse, and the prohibitions are counted as only four.

Some have answered this question by saying that at the time these verses were revealed in Makkah, the ruling for the prohibition of other forbidden foods had not yet been revealed. However, this answer does not seem correct. The evidence for this is that the exact same expression or something similar is also seen in some Medinan Surahs, such as verse 173 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

The apparent reality is that this verse only aims to negate the superstitious rulings of the polytheists and is what is termed "relative restriction" (hasr idafi). In other words, the verse says: The Divine prohibitions are these, not what you have fabricated.

To clarify this point, it is helpful to provide an example: Someone asks us, "Did both Hasan and Husayn come?" We reply, "No, only Hasan came." Here, our intention is to negate the coming of the second person, Husayn. However, there is no obstacle to other people—who were not the subject of our discussion at all—having come. Such a restriction is called a relative (or comparative) restriction.

However, it must be noted that the appearance of a restriction is usually a "real restriction" (hasr haqiqi) unless there is a contextual clue involved, as is the case in the verse under discussion.

Ayahs

وَعَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ حَرَّمْنَا كُلَّ ذِى ظُفُرٍ ۖ وَمِنَ ٱلْبَقَرِ وَٱلْغَنَمِ حَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ شُحُومَهُمَآ إِلَّا مَا حَمَلَتْ ظُهُورُهُمَآ أَوِ ٱلْحَوَايَآ أَوْ مَا ٱخْتَلَطَ بِعَظْمٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ جَزَيْنَـٰهُم بِبَغْيِهِمْ ۖ وَإِنَّا لَصَـٰدِقُونَ (١٤٦)
To the Jews We forbade every animal having an undivided hoof, and of oxen and sheep We forbade them their fat, except what is borne by their backs or the entrails, or what is attached to the bones. We requited them with that for their rebelliousness, and We indeed speak the truth. (146)
فَإِن كَذَّبُوكَ فَقُل رَّبُّكُمْ ذُو رَحْمَةٍ وَٰسِعَةٍ وَلَا يُرَدُّ بَأْسُهُۥ عَنِ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (١٤٧)
But if they deny you, say, ‘Your Lord is dispenser of an all-embracing mercy, but His punishment will not be averted from the guilty lot.’ (147)

Commentary

Prohibitions upon the Jews

In the preceding verses, the prohibited animals were limited to four things. However, these verses point to a portion of the prohibitions imposed upon the Jews so it may become clear that the baseless and superstitious decrees of the idolaters are compatible neither with the religion of Islam, nor with the religion of the Jews (nor with the religion of Christ, which usually follows the Jewish faith in its rulings).

Furthermore, these verses explicitly state that this portion of the prohibitions upon the Jews also served as a punishment and penalty; had they not committed crimes and transgressions, these matters would not have been made forbidden to them. Therefore, it is fitting to ask the idolaters: where did you bring these fabricated rulings from?! Thus, He first says: "And to those who are Jews We prohibited every animal with claws" (وَعَلَى الَّذِينَ هَادُوا حَرَّمْنَا كُلَّ ذِي ظُفُرٍ).

"Zufur" (ظفر) originally means "nail," but it is also applied to the hooves of solid-hoofed animals (those like the horse, which have hooves that are not cloven, unlike sheep and cattle which possess cloven hooves), because their hooves resemble nails. It is also applied to the foot of the camel, as the tip of its foot is solid and not split.

Therefore, it is understood from the above verse that all animals that are not "cloven-hoofed," whether they be quadrupeds or birds, were prohibited to the Jews.

The same meaning is generally understood from the current Torah, the Book of Leviticus, Chapter 11, where it says:

"Whatever parts the hoof and is generous-cloven-footed and chews the cud among the animals, you may eat. Nevertheless, among those that chew the cud or part the hoof, you shall not eat these: The camel, because it chews the cud but does not part the hoof, is unclean to you."

From the subsequent phrase in the verse above, which mentions only cattle and sheep, it can also be inferred that the camel was entirely prohibited for the Jews (take note).

Then He says: "And of the cattle and the sheep We prohibited to them their fat" (وَمِنَ الْبَقَرِ وَالْغَنَمِ حَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ شُحُومَهُمَا).

Following that, He excludes three cases: first, "the fat which their backs carry" (إِلَّا مَا حَمَلَتْ ظُهُورُهُمَا).

"And the fat found in the flanks and among the intestines" (أَوِ الْحَوَايَا) [^1] "and that which is mixed with the bone" (أَوْ مَا اخْتَلَطَ بِعَظْمٍ). But at the end of the verse, He clarifies that these were not originally prohibited to the Jews, "but because of the rebellion and injustice they committed, they were deprived by Allah's decree of these types of meat and fats which they desired" (ذَلِكَ جَزَيْنَاهُمْ بِبَغْيِهِمْ).

And for emphasis, He adds: "And indeed, We are truthful" (وَإِنَّا لَصَادِقُونَ).

Two points must be noted here

1- Regarding what injustice and rebellion the Children of Israel had committed that caused Allah to prohibit some of the blessings they desired, there is discussion among the commentators. However, what is understood from verses 160 and 161 of Surah An-Nisa is that the cause of the said prohibition was several things:

Injustice and oppression against the weak, preventing the call of the Prophets in the path of guiding the people, usury (Riba), and consuming the wealth of people unjustly, where He says:

"For the wrongdoing of those who were Jews, We made unlawful to them [certain] good foods which had been lawful to them, and for their averting from the way of Allah many [people], and [for] their taking of usury while they had been forbidden from it, and their consuming of the people's wealth unjustly..." (فَبِظُلْمٍ مِنَ الَّذِينَ هادُوا حَرَّمْنا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّباتٍ أُحِلَّتْ لَهُمْ وَ بِصَدِّهِمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ كَثِيراً وَ أَخْذِهِمُ الرِّبَوا وَ قَدْ نُهُوا عَنْهُ وَ أَكْلِهِمْ أَمْوالَ النَّاسِ بِالْباطِلِ).

2- The phrase "And indeed, We are truthful" (وَإِنَّا لَصَادِقُونَ) at the end of the verse may point to the fact that the truth and reality regarding the prohibition of these foods is what We have said, not what the Jews claimed in some of their statements, attributing it as a command for prohibition from Israel (Jacob عليه السلام). For Jacob عليه السلام, as stated in verse 93 of Surah Al-Imran, never decreed these things as prohibited; this was a slander the Jews attributed to him (for more explanation, refer to Tafsir Nemouneh under the aforementioned verse, Volume 3, Page 6).

Since the stubbornness of the Jews and the polytheists was evident, and it was possible they would persist and deny the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, in the following verse Allah commands His Prophet: "If they deny you, say: Your Lord is the possessor of vast and encompassing mercy" (فَإِنْ كَذَّبُوكَ فَقُلْ رَبُّكُمْ ذُو رَحْمَةٍ وَاسِعَةٍ), and He does not punish you quickly but gives you respite, so that perhaps you may turn back from your mistakes, regret your actions, and return to Allah.

But if you continue to abuse the divine respite and persist in your false slanders, know that Allah's punishment is certain and will eventually overtake you, because "His punishment cannot be repelled from the criminal people" (وَلَا يُرَدُّ بَأْسُهُ عَنِ الْقَوْمِ الْمُجْرِمِينَ).

This verse clearly illustrates the greatness of the Quranic teachings; after describing all these transgressions of the Jews and polytheists, it still does not immediately threaten them with punishment.

Rather, it first opens the way for their return with expressions full of affection such as "Your Lord" (رَبُّكُمْ), "Possessor of mercy" (ذُو رَحْمَةٍ), and "Vast" (وَاسِعَةٍ), so that if there is the slightest readiness within them for this matter, they may be encouraged and return to the Truth. However, so that the vast divine mercy does not lead to audacity, insolence, and rebellion, and so they might cease their stubbornness, He threatens them in the final phrase with Allah's certain punishment.

[^1]: "Hawaya" (حوايا) is the plural of "Hawiyah" (حاوية), meaning the collection of the contents of the animal's belly which is like a sphere, within which the intestines are located.

Ayahs

سَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ لَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَآ أَشْرَكْنَا وَلَآ ءَابَآؤُنَا وَلَا حَرَّمْنَا مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ كَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ حَتَّىٰ ذَاقُوا۟ بَأْسَنَا ۗ قُلْ هَلْ عِندَكُم مِّنْ عِلْمٍ فَتُخْرِجُوهُ لَنَآ ۖ إِن تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ وَإِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا تَخْرُصُونَ (١٤٨)
The polytheists will say, ‘Had Allah wished we would not have ascribed any partner [to Him], nor our fathers, nor would we have forbidden anything.’ Those who were before them had denied likewise until they tasted Our punishment. Say, ‘Do you have any [revealed] knowledge that you can produce before us? You follow nothing but conjectures, and you do nothing but surmise.’ (148)
قُلْ فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْحُجَّةُ ٱلْبَـٰلِغَةُ ۖ فَلَوْ شَآءَ لَهَدَىٰكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (١٤٩)
Say, ‘To Allah belongs the conclusive argument. Had He wished, He would have surely guided you all.’ (149)
قُلْ هَلُمَّ شُهَدَآءَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَشْهَدُونَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ حَرَّمَ هَـٰذَا ۖ فَإِن شَهِدُوا۟ فَلَا تَشْهَدْ مَعَهُمْ ۚ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَهُم بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ (١٥٠)
Say, ‘Bring your witnesses who may testify that Allah has forbidden this.’ So if they testify, do not testify with them, and do not follow the desires of those who deny Our signs, and those who do not believe in the Hereafter and equate [others] with their Lord. (150)

Commentary

Escaping Responsibility Under the Pretext of "Determinism"

Following the statements of the polytheists mentioned in the previous verses, these verses point to some of their baseless arguments and provide the response to them:

First, it says: "Soon the polytheists, in response to your objections regarding shirk (polytheism) and the prohibition of lawful sustenance, will say: 'If Allah had willed, neither we nor our ancestors would have associated partners with Him, nor would we have prohibited anything.'" Thus, they claim that whatever they have done or said is entirely His will (سَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ ما أَشْرَكْنا وَ لا آباؤُنا وَ لا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

Similar expressions are found in two other verses of the Quran: in Surah Al-Nahl, verse 35: وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ نَحْنُ وَ لا آباؤُنا وَ لا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ, and in Surah Al-Zukhruf, verse 20: وقالُوا لَوْ شاءَ الرَّحْمنُ ما عَبَدْناهُمْ. This indicates that the polytheists, like many sinners who wish to escape the responsibility of their wrongdoings by hiding under the label of determinism (jabr), believed in the principle of determinism. They argued: "Whatever we do is the will of God and in accordance with His volition, and if He did not want it, such acts would not have proceeded from us." In reality, they wanted to acquit themselves of all these sins; otherwise, the conscience of every rational human being testifies that man is free in his actions, not compelled. For this reason, if someone commits an injustice against them, they become upset, hold the person accountable, and if "capable," they punish them. All of this shows that they view the offender as free in his action, not compelled, and they are never willing to waive the reaction on the grounds that the person's action was in accordance with God's will. (Reflect on this). Of course, there is also the possibility in the meaning of the verse that they claimed God's silence regarding idolatry and the prohibition of certain animals was proof of His satisfaction; for if He were not satisfied, He should have prevented us from this work in some way.

By mentioning the phrase "nor our ancestors," they intended to give their hollow beliefs a color of antiquity and continuity, saying: "This is not a new thing we are saying; it has always been this way."

However, the Quran addresses them with a decisive argument. First, it says: It is not only these people who fabricate such lies against Allah, "but a group of past nations also told the same lies, until they finally faced the evil consequences of their actions and tasted Our punishment" (كَذلِكَ كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ حَتَّى ذاقُوا بَأْسَنا). ^1 In reality, with these statements, they were both lying and denying the prophets, because the Divine prophets explicitly forbade mankind from idolatry, shirk, and the prohibition of what Allah made lawful. Neither their ancestors listened, nor did they. In such a state, how is it possible to consider God satisfied with these acts? If He were satisfied with these acts, why would He send His prophets to call toward monotheism (Tawhid)? Fundamentally, the call of the prophets itself is the most important evidence for the freedom of human will and choice.

Then it says: "Say to them: Do you truly have any certain and definitive proof for this claim? If you do, why do you not produce it for us?" (قُلْ هَلْ عِنْدَكُمْ مِنْ عِلْمٍ فَتُخْرِجُوهُ لَنا). Finally, it adds: "You certainly have no proof for these claims; you follow nothing but conjectures and idle fancies" (إِنْ تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَ إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا تَخْرُصُونَ‌).

In the next verse, it mentions another reason to invalidate the claim of the polytheists, saying: "Say: Allah has established sound and clear proofs regarding His Oneness and Unity, as well as the rulings of the lawful and the prohibited, both through His prophets and through reason, such that no excuse remains for anyone" (قُلْ فَلِلَّهِ الْحُجَّةُ الْبالِغَةُ).

Therefore, they can never claim that God, through His silence, has endorsed their improper beliefs and actions, nor can they claim they are compelled in their actions. For if they were compelled, the establishment of proof, the sending of prophets, and their invitation and preaching would be futile. The establishment of proof is itself evidence of the freedom of will.

Additionally, it should be noted that "Hujjah" (proof) is originally derived from the root "Hajj," meaning "intent" or "purpose." A road or path that is a person's destination is called "Mahajjah," and a reason or argument is called "Hujjah" because the speaker intends to prove his point to others through it.

Given the meaning of "Balighah" (reaching/profound/conclusive), it becomes clear that Allah's proofs for mankind—through reason and tradition, through knowledge and intellect, as well as the sending of prophets—are clear and conclusive from every aspect, such that no room for doubt remains for individuals. For this reason, God made the prophets infallible (Ma'sum) from any error or mistake to remove any doubt or hesitation from their call.

At the end of the verse, He says: "If Allah willed, He would have guided you all through compulsion" (فَلَوْ شاءَ لَهَداكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ‌).

In reality, this phrase points to the fact that it is entirely possible for Allah to guide all humans by force, such that no one would have the power to oppose. But in that case, neither would such faith have value, nor would the actions performed under the shadow of this forced faith. Rather, the virtue and perfection of man lie in traversing the path of guidance and piety on his own feet and by his own will and choice.
