Therefore, there is no contradiction between this phrase and the previous verse in which determinism was denied. This phrase says: Compelling the servants, which you claim, is possible for Allah, but He will never do so, because it is contrary to human wisdom and interest.

In truth, they had used Allah's power and will as a pretext to choose the school of determinism, whereas Allah's power and will are true, but their result is not determinism. He has willed that we be free and traverse the path of truth by our own choice.

In the book Al-Kafi, it is narrated from Imam al-Kazim عليه السلام that he said: "Indeed, Allah has two proofs against people: an outward proof and an inward proof. As for the outward proof, it is the messengers, the prophets, and the Imams; as for the inward proof, it is the intellects." ^1 And in the Amali of Sheikh Tusi, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he was asked about the interpretation of the above verse (فَلِلَّهِ الْحُجَّةُ الْبالِغَةُ). The Imam said: "Indeed, Allah the Almighty will say to the servant on the Day of Resurrection: 'My servant! Were you knowledgeable (and yet sinned)?' If he says 'Yes,' He will say to him: 'Why then did you not act upon what you knew?' And if he says 'I was ignorant,' He will say to him: 'Why then did you not learn so that you could act?' Thus He defeats him in argument; and that is the 'Conclusive Proof' (al-Hujjah al-Balighah)." ^1 It is obvious that the purpose of the above narration is not that the Conclusive Proof is limited to God's conversation with servants in the Hereafter; rather, Allah has many conclusive proofs, one instance of which is what appeared in the above Hadith, for the scope of Allah's conclusive proofs is vast, both in this world and the Hereafter.

In the next verse, so that the falsehood of their words becomes clearer and the correct principles of judgment and arbitration are observed, He invites them to produce credible witnesses if they have any, who can testify that Allah has prohibited the animals and crops they claim are forbidden. Thus He says: "O Prophet! Say to them: Bring your witnesses who testify that Allah has prohibited this" (قُلْ هَلُمَّ شُهَداءَكُمُ الَّذِينَ يَشْهَدُونَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ حَرَّمَ هذا). Then He adds: If they could not find any credible witnesses (and they certainly will not), "and they settled only for their own testimony and claim, do not join them and do not testify in accordance with their testimony and claim." (فإِنْ شَهِدُوا فَلا تَشْهَدْ مَعَهُمْ‌).

From what has been said, it becomes clear that there is no contradiction in the verse as a whole. The fact that He first demands witnesses from them and then says: "Do not accept their witnesses," does not create a problem, because the meaning is that they are definitely incapable of producing credible witnesses, as they have no document or evidence from the Divine prophets or Heavenly books regarding the prohibition of these matters. Therefore, only they themselves, who are the claimants, testify, and it is obvious that such testimony is rejected.

Furthermore, all evidence testifies that these fabricated rulings stem purely from whims, desires, and blind imitation and have no validity whatsoever.

Therefore, in the next phrase, He says: "Do not follow the whims of those who denied Our signs, and those who do not believe in the Hereafter, and those who ascribe partners to their Lord." (وَ لا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا وَ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ وَ هُمْ بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ‌). ^1 That is, their idolatry, their denial of the Resurrection and the Hereafter, and their superstitions and self-worship are living proof that these rulings of theirs are also fabricated, and their claim regarding the prohibition of these subjects by God is baseless and worthless.

^1: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 776. ^1: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 776. ^1: "Ya'dilun" is derived from the root "Adl" (with the weight of 'Kidhb'), which means "peer," "partner," and "likeness." Therefore, the phrase "وَ هُمْ بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ" means that they ascribe a peer, partner, and likeness to God.

Ayahs

۞ قُلْ تَعَالَوْا۟ أَتْلُ مَا حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا۟ بِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَـٰنًا ۖ وَلَا تَقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَوْلَـٰدَكُم مِّنْ إِمْلَـٰقٍ ۖ نَّحْنُ نَرْزُقُكُمْ وَإِيَّاهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ ٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا وَمَا بَطَنَ ۖ وَلَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلنَّفْسَ ٱلَّتِى حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ وَصَّىٰكُم بِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (١٥١)
Say, ‘Come, I will recount what your Lord has forbidden you. That you shall not ascribe any partners to Him, and you shall be good to the parents, you shall not kill your children due to penury—We will provide for you and for them—you shall not approach indecencies, the outward among them and the inward ones, and you shall not kill a soul [whose life] Allah has made inviolable, except with due cause. This is what He has enjoined upon you so that you may exercise your reason. (151)
وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ مَالَ ٱلْيَتِيمِ إِلَّا بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ ۖ وَأَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ لَا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا ۖ وَإِذَا قُلْتُمْ فَٱعْدِلُوا۟ وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰ ۖ وَبِعَهْدِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْفُوا۟ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ وَصَّىٰكُم بِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (١٥٢)
Do not approach the orphan’s property, except in the best [possible] manner, until he comes of age. And observe fully the measure and the balance with justice.’ We task no soul except according to its capacity. ‘And when you speak, be fair, even if it were a relative; and fulfill Allah’s covenant. This is what He enjoins upon you so that you may take admonition.’ (152)
وَأَنَّ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطِى مُسْتَقِيمًا فَٱتَّبِعُوهُ ۖ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ ٱلسُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ وَصَّىٰكُم بِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (١٥٣)
‘This indeed is my straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you from His way. This is what He enjoins upon you so that you may be Godwary.’ (153)

Commentary

The Ten Commandments!

Following the negation of the fabricated rulings of the polytheists mentioned in the previous verses, these three verses point to the fundamental prohibitions in Islam. They outline the primary major sins in a concise, profound, and striking manner across ten points, inviting people to come and hear the true divine prohibitions and cast aside false taboos.

First, it says: "Say to them: Come, I will recite what your Lord has prohibited to you" (قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ):

1- "That you associate nothing as a partner or equal with Allah" (أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئاً).

2- "Be good to your parents" (وَ بِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً).

3- "Do not kill your children because of poverty and want" (وَ لا تَقْتُلُوا أَوْلادَكُمْ مِنْ إِمْلاقٍ).

For your provision and theirs is all in Our hands, and We provide for everyone (نَحْنُ نَرْزُقُكُمْ وَ إِيَّاهُمْ).

4- "Do not approach shameful and lewd deeds, whether open or hidden" (وَ لا تَقْرَبُوا الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَ ما بَطَنَ).

This means not only should you not perform them, but you should not even go near them.

5- "Do not stain your hands with the blood of the innocent, and do not kill the souls that Allah has made sacred and whose blood is not permissible to shed, unless it is by right according to divine law" (such as if they are murderers) (وَ لا تَقْتُلُوا النَّفْسَ الَّتِي حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ).

Following these five parts, for further emphasis, He says: "This is what He has enjoined upon you, so that you may understand and refrain from committing them" (ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ).

6- "Never approach the property of orphans except with the intention of improvement, until they reach the age of maturity" (وَ لا تَقْرَبُوا مالَ الْيَتِيمِ إِلَّا بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ حَتَّى يَبْلُغَ أَشُدَّهُ).

7- "Do not give short measure or weight; fulfill the right of measure and weight with justice" (وَ أَوْفُوا الْكَيْلَ وَ الْمِيزانَ بِالْقِسْطِ).

And since no matter how much a person is careful with measure and weight, a slight discrepancy may still occur that is impossible to gauge with ordinary scales and measures, He adds following this sentence: "We do not burden any soul beyond its capacity" (لا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها).

8- "When you speak—whether in judgment, testimony, or any other instance—observe justice and do not deviate from the path of truth, even if it concerns your relatives and the truthful judgment or testimony ends up being to their detriment" (وَ إِذا قُلْتُمْ فَاعْدِلُوا وَ لَوْ كانَ ذا قُرْبى).

9- "Fulfill the covenant of Allah and do not break it" (وَ بِعَهْدِ اللَّهِ أَوْفُوا).

Regarding what is meant by the "covenant of Allah" in this verse, commentators have suggested several possibilities, but the concept of the verse encompasses all divine covenants, whether "existential" (takwini) or "legislative" (tashri'i), divine obligations, and any form of vow, pledge, or oath.

And again, for emphasis at the end of these four parts, He says: "This is what He enjoins upon you, so that you may take heed" (ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ).

10- "This is My straight path—the path of monotheism, the path of truth and justice, the path of purity and piety—so follow it, and never step into deviant and scattered paths, for they will divert and scatter you from Allah's path and sow the seeds of hypocrisy and discord among you" (وَ أَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَ لا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ).

And at the end of all these, for the third time, He emphasizes that these are the matters Allah enjoins upon you so that you may become God-fearing and pious (ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ).

Key Points

1- Starting with Monotheism and Ending with the Negation of Discord

It is noteworthy that in these ten commandments, it first begins with the prohibition of polytheism (shirk), which is in fact the primary source of all social corruption and divine prohibitions. It concludes with the negation of discord, which is considered a form of practical polytheism.

This subject clarifies the importance of the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) in all Islamic principles and branches—that Monotheism is not merely a religious principle but the soul of all Islamic teachings.

2- Successive Emphases

At the end of each of these three verses, the phrase "ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ" (This is what He has enjoined upon you) appears as an emphasis, with the difference that the first verse ends with "so that you may understand" (لعلكم تعقلون), the second with "so that you may take heed" (لعلكم تذكرون), and the third with "so that you may become pious" (لعلكم تتقون).

These varied and calculated expressions seemingly point to the fact that the first stage upon receiving a ruling is "intellection" (ta'aqqul) and understanding it; the next stage is the stage of "remembrance" (tadhakkur) and absorbing it; and the third stage, which is the final stage, is the stage of action, implementation, and "piety" (taqwa).

It is true that each of these three phrases comes after mentioning several parts of the ten commandments above, but it is clear that these three stages are not exclusive to specific rulings, as every ruling requires "intellection," "remembrance," and "piety and action." Rather, in reality, the requirements of eloquence and rhetoric necessitated that these emphases be distributed among those ten commandments.

3- Eternal Commandments

It perhaps needs no mention that these ten commandments are not exclusive to the religion of Islam but have existed in all religions, although they have been discussed more extensively in Islam. In truth, all of them are among the commands that reason and logic clearly perceive, and are termed "independent dictates of reason" (mustaqillat al-aqliyyah). Therefore, in the Holy Quran, these rulings are seen more or less in the religions of other prophets as well [1].

4- The Importance of Kindness to Parents

Mentioning kindness to parents immediately after the struggle against polytheism, and before important commands such as the prohibition of murder and the implementation of the principles of justice, is evidence of the extraordinary importance of the rights of parents in Islamic instructions.

This matter becomes clearer when we notice that instead of "the prohibition of bothering parents"—which would be consistent with the other prohibitions in this verse—the subject of "ihsan" (kindness and goodness) is mentioned. This means that not only is causing them distress forbidden, but in addition to that, kindness and goodness toward them are necessary and essential.

More interestingly, the word "ihsan" is made transitive with the preposition "bi" (ب), saying "بالوالدين احسانا". We know that "ihsan" is sometimes mentioned with "ila" (الى) and sometimes with "bi". When mentioned with "ila", its concept is doing good even if it is indirect or through an intermediary. However, when mentioned with "bi", its meaning is doing good directly and without an intermediary. Therefore, the verse emphasizes that the subject of kindness to parents must be given such importance that one should undertake it personally and without an intermediary [2].

5- Killing Children Due to Hunger

It appears from these verses that the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) not only buried their daughters alive due to false prejudices, but also killed their sons—who were considered a great asset in the society of that day—out of fear of poverty and want. In this verse, Allah draws their attention to the vast spread of the Lord's blessings, from which even the weakest creatures receive provision, and forbids them from this act.

With great regret, this ignorant practice is repeated in our age and time in another form; under the pretext of a potential shortage of food resources on earth, innocent children are killed in the embryonic stage through "abortion."

Although today other baseless reasons are also mentioned for abortion, the issue of poverty and food shortage is one of its major reasons.

These and other similar issues show that the Age of Ignorance is being repeated in our time in another form, and the "Jahiliyyah of the twentieth century" is even more terrifying and widespread than the Jahiliyyah before Islam.

6- What is Meant by "Fawahish"?

"Fawahish" is the plural of "fahishah," meaning sins that are extraordinarily ugly and repulsive. Therefore, breaking covenants, giving short measure, polytheism, and the like—though they are major sins—are mentioned separately from "fawahish" because of this difference in conceptual meaning.

7- Do Not Approach These Sins

In the above verses, the expression "do not approach" (la taqrabu) is used in two instances. This subject is repeated in the Quran regarding some other sins as well. It seems that this expression is used for sins that are "tempting," such as "adultery and lewdness" and "the defenseless property of orphans" and the like. Therefore, it warns people not to even go near them so as not to fall under the influence of their intense temptations.

8- Open and Hidden Sins

There is no doubt that the phrase "whether open or hidden" (ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَ ما بَطَنَ) includes every kind of ugly sin, whether manifest or secret. However, in some hadiths narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, he said: "What is open is adultery (zina), and what is hidden is taking illicit secret mistresses." But it is clear that mentioning these items is by way of explaining a clear example, not that it is limited to this case alone.

9- The Ten Commandments of the Jews

In the Torah, in Chapter 20 of the "Book of Exodus," ten rulings are seen which have become famous as the Ten Commandments among the Jews, starting from the second verse and ending at the seventeenth of that chapter.

By comparing those ten commandments and what we read in the above verses, it becomes clear that there is an extraordinary distance between these two programs. Of course, one cannot be certain that the current Torah has not undergone distortion in this part, as in many other parts. However, what is certain is that the Ten Commandments present in the Torah, although containing necessary matters, are at a much lower level than the content of the above verses in terms of scope and moral, social, and doctrinal dimensions.

10- How These Few Verses Changed the Face of Medina!

In Bihar al-Anwar and also in the book "A'lam al-Wara," a striking story is found that recounts the extraordinary impact of the above verses on the souls of the listeners. We bring this account in summary below as narrated in Bihar al-Anwar from Ali ibn Ibrahim:

"As'ad ibn Zurarah" and "Dhakwan ibn Abd Qays," who were from the Khazraj tribe, came to Mecca while there was a long-standing war between the Aws and Khazraj tribes. For a long time, they did not lay down their weapons day or night. Their last battle was the day of "Bu'ath," in which the Aws tribe gained the upper hand over the Khazraj. For this reason, As'ad and Dhakwan came to Mecca to secure a treaty from the people of Mecca against the Aws tribe. When they entered the house of "Utbah ibn Rabi'ah" and told him the story, he replied:

"Our city is far from yours, and we have recently encountered a new trouble that has kept us very busy." As'ad asked, "What trouble? You who live in the Secure Sanctuary (Haram)."

Utbah said: "A man has appeared among us who says: 'I am the messenger of Allah!' He considers our intellects insignificant and speaks ill of our gods; he has corrupted our youth and scattered our assembly!" As'ad asked, "What is his relation to you?" He said: "The son of Abdullah ibn Abd al-Muttalib, and coincidentally he is from our noble families."

As'ad and Dhakwan fell into thought and remembered hearing from the Jews of Medina that a prophet would soon appear in Mecca and would migrate to Medina.

As'ad said to himself: "Could this be the same person the Jews were informing us about?"

Then he asked, "Where is he?"

Utbah said: "He is currently sitting in the Hijr Ismail beside the House of Allah. They are besieged in a mountain valley and only find freedom during the Hajj season and the Umrah of the month of Rajab, when they enter the crowds. But I advise you: do not listen to his words and do not speak a single word to him, for he is a strange sorcerer."

—And this was during the days when the Muslims were besieged in the Shi'b Abi Talib.

As'ad turned to Utbah and said, "Then what should I do? I have entered the state of Ihram and it is necessary for me to circumambulate the Kaaba. You tell me not to go near him?!" Utbah said: "Place some cotton in your ears so that you do not hear his words!" As'ad entered Masjid al-Haram while both his ears were tightly plugged with cotton, and began circumambulating the Kaaba, while the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was sitting with a group of Banu Hashim in the Hijr Ismail beside the Kaaba.

He glanced at the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and passed quickly. During the second round of circumambulation, he said to himself: "No one is more foolish than I! Is it possible for such an important story to be on everyone's lips in Mecca and I not get news of it to inform my people?!" Following this thought, he reached out, pulled the cotton out of his ears, threw it away, and stood before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and asked, "To what do you invite us?"

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "To the testimony of the oneness of Allah and that I am His messenger, and I invite you to these deeds," and then he recited the three verses above which contained the ten commandments.

When As'ad heard these profound and soul-nurturing words that were familiar to his innate nature and soul, he was completely transformed and cried out: "I bear witness that there is no god but Allah and that you are the Messenger of Allah."

"O Messenger of Allah, may my father and mother be sacrificed for you! I am from Yathrib, from the Khazraj tribe. Our connection with our brothers from the Aws tribe has been severed due to long wars; perhaps Allah will re-establish this broken bond through your help."

"O Prophet! We had heard your description from the Jewish tribe, and they were always giving us glad tidings of your appearance, and we hope that our city becomes your 'place of migration' (hijratgah), for the Jews so informed us from their heavenly books. I thank Allah that He sent me to you. By Allah, I had come to Mecca for nothing other than to strike a war treaty against enemies, but Allah granted me a greater victory."

Then his companion "Dhakwan" also became Muslim, and both requested the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to send a missionary with them to Medina to teach the people the Quran, invite them to Islam, and extinguish the fire of wars. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent "Mus'ab ibn Umayr" with them to Medina, and from 그 time the foundations of Islam were laid in Medina and the face of Medina was transformed [3].

[1]: Surah Ash-Shura, Verse 13. [2]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 8, p. 185. [3]: Bihar al-Anwar, New Edition, Vol. 19, pp. 8, 9, and 10.

Ayahs

ثُمَّ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ تَمَامًا عَلَى ٱلَّذِىٓ أَحْسَنَ وَتَفْصِيلًا لِّكُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لَّعَلَّهُم بِلِقَآءِ رَبِّهِمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٥٤)
Then We gave Moses the Book, completing [Our blessing] on him who is virtuous, and as an elaboration of all things, and as a guidance and mercy, so that they may believe in the encounter with their Lord. (154)
وَهَـٰذَا كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مُبَارَكٌ فَٱتَّبِعُوهُ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (١٥٥)
And this Book that We have sent down is a blessed one; so follow it, and be Godwary so that you may receive [His] mercy. (155)
أَن تَقُولُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أُنزِلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ عَلَىٰ طَآئِفَتَيْنِ مِن قَبْلِنَا وَإِن كُنَّا عَن دِرَاسَتِهِمْ لَغَـٰفِلِينَ (١٥٦)
Lest you should say, ‘The Book was sent down only to two communities before us, and we were indeed unaware of their studies,’ (156)
أَوْ تَقُولُوا۟ لَوْ أَنَّآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ لَكُنَّآ أَهْدَىٰ مِنْهُمْ ۚ فَقَدْ جَآءَكُم بَيِّنَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ ۚ فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن كَذَّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَصَدَفَ عَنْهَا ۗ سَنَجْزِى ٱلَّذِينَ يَصْدِفُونَ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْدِفُونَ (١٥٧)
or [lest] you should say, ‘If the Book had been sent down to us, surely we would have been better-guided than them.’ There has already come to you a manifest proof from your Lord and a guidance and mercy. So who is a greater wrongdoer than him who denies the signs of Allah, and turns away from them? Soon We shall requite those who turn away from Our signs with a terrible punishment because of what they used to evade. (157)

Commentary

A Decisive Response to Excuse-Seekers

In the preceding verses, ten fundamental and principled decrees of Islam were discussed, which in reality constitute the foundation of many Islamic laws. From expressions such as:

أَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ‌ (This is My straight path, so follow it), and the like, it is understood that these decrees were not exclusive to a specific faith or religion, especially since all of them are among the principles that reason clearly confirms and supports. Thus, the content of the previous verses expresses laws that existed not only in Islam but in all religions.

Following that, these verses state: "Then We gave Moses the Scripture, completing My favor upon those who did good and those who were submissive to Our command and followers of the truth" (ثُمَّ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ تَماماً عَلَى الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ‌).

From what we have said, the meaning of the word "ثُمَّ" (then), which in the Arabic language usually denotes "conjunction with a delay," becomes clear. The meaning of the verse would be: first, We conveyed these universal and general commands to the previous prophets, then We gave the Scripture to Moses, and in it, We explained these programs and all necessary programs.

In this way, we will have no need for the various and occasionally weak justifications mentioned by some commentators.

Furthermore, it became clear that "الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ‌" (he who did good) refers to all those who are doers of good and are ready to accept the truth and submit to Allah's command.

"And in it, everything that was needed and had an effect on the path of human perfection was explained" (وَ تَفْصِيلًا لِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

"And also this book, which was revealed to Moses, was a guidance and a mercy" (وَ هُدىً وَ رَحْمَةً).

"All these programs were so that they might believe in the Day of Resurrection and the meeting with their Lord, and that through faith in the Hereafter, their thoughts, words, and deeds might become pure" (لَعَلَّهُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

It might be asked: if the religion of Moses was complete (as the word tamaman—completing—indicates), what was the necessity for the religion of Christ and then the religion of Islam?! However, it must be noted that: every religion is a comprehensive and complete religion for its own time, and it is impossible for a deficient religion to be revealed by Allah. Yet, this same religion which was complete for one time may be incomplete and inadequate for later times, just as a comprehensive and complete program for primary school is inadequate for high school. The reason for sending various prophets with multiple heavenly books, culminating in the Final Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the final decree, is the same. Of course, once humanity attained the readiness for the final decree and the final decree was issued, there was no longer a need for a new religion. Just like graduates who can proceed toward scientific advancements through study based on their existing knowledge, the followers of such a religion will not need a new faith and will derive sufficient momentum from that final religion.

It is also understood from this verse that matters related to the Resurrection were sufficiently present in the original Torah. If we see that in the current Torahs and related books, there is no mention of the Hereafter and Resurrection except in rare cases, it seems this is mostly due to the distortions by the Jews and world-seekers who preferred to speak less and hear less about the Resurrection.

Of course, in the current Torahs, there are brief references to the issue of Resurrection, but they are so few that some wish to say the Jews fundamentally do not believe in the Resurrection; however, this statement is closer to an exaggeration than a reality.

Additionally, it should be noted, as we pointed out in the first volume of Tafsir Nemouneh (page 156), that the "meeting with the Lord" mentioned in the verses of the Quran does not mean a sensory meeting or vision with the eyes. Rather, it is a type of inner witnessing and spiritual meeting that occurs due to human perfection in the Resurrection, or the observation of His rewards and punishments in the other world.

In the next verse, it refers to the revelation of the Quran and its teachings, completing the discussion of the previous verse, saying: "This is a Book which We have revealed, a magnificent and blessed Book, and a source of all kinds of blessings and goodness" (وَ هذا كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ مُباركٌ‌). And "since it is so, follow it completely and practice piety and avoid opposing it, so that you may receive Allah's mercy" (فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَ اتَّقُوا لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ‌).

In the following verse, it closes all paths of escape and excuse-seeking for the polytheists, first saying to them: "We revealed this heavenly Book with these distinctions so that you may not say that the heavenly Book was only revealed to the two previous groups (Jews and Christians), and we were unaware of their study and investigation. And if we disobeyed Your command, it was because Your command was in the hands of others and did not reach us" (أَنْ تَقُولُوا إِنَّما أُنْزِلَ الْكِتابُ عَلى‌ طائِفَتَيْنِ مِنْ قَبْلِنا وَ إِنْ كُنَّا عَنْ دِراسَتِهِمْ لَغافِلِينَ‌) [1]

In the next verse, the same excuse is quoted from them in a more extensive form, mixed with more claim and arrogance. That is, if the Quran had not been revealed to them, they might have claimed they were so ready to carry out the divine command that no nation was as ready as they were: "Or lest you should say: 'If only the Book had been revealed to us, we would surely have been better guided than they'" (أَوْ تَقُولُوا لَوْ أَنَّا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْكِتابُ لَكُنَّا أَهْدى‌ مِنْهُمْ‌). In reality, the previous verse reflected the excuse that if they did not follow the path, it was due to ignorance of the heavenly books, and this ignorance was caused by those books being revealed to others. But this verse reflects their sense of self-superiority and the baseless claim they had regarding the distinction of the Arab race over others. A similar meaning is quoted in Surah Fatir, verse 42, from the polytheists as a definitive matter, not as a conditional proposition, where it says: "The polytheists swore by Allah their most binding oaths that if a warner came to them, they would be more guided than any of the [other] nations."

In any case, the Quran says in response to these claims: Allah has closed all paths of excuse-seeking for you because "clear signs and proofs from your Lord have come to you, mixed with divine guidance and the mercy of the Lord" (فَقَدْ جاءَكُمْ بَيِّنَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَ هُدىً وَ رَحْمَةٌ).

Interestingly, instead of using the term "heavenly Book," the term "Bayanah" (clear proof) is used, indicating that this heavenly Book is clear and clarifying from every aspect and is accompanied by decisive proofs.

"This being the case, who is more unjust than those who deny the signs of Allah and turn away from them?" (فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنْ كَذَّبَ بِآياتِ اللَّهِ وَ صَدَفَ عَنْها). "Sadafa" is from the root sadaf (on the weight of hadhf), meaning to turn away intensely and without thought from something. This indicates that they not only turned away from Allah's signs but distanced themselves from them with intensity, without applying the slightest thought or reflection. Sometimes this word also carries the meaning of preventing and hindering others.

At the end of the verse, it describes the painful punishment of such stubborn and thoughtless individuals who intensely deny truths without studying them and flee from them, even blocking the path for others, in a short and eloquent sentence:

"We shall soon requite those who turn away from Our signs with a severe punishment, and this is because of their irrational and groundless turning away" (سَنَجْزِي الَّذِينَ يَصْدِفُونَ عَنْ آياتِنا سُوءَ الْعَذابِ بِما كانُوا يَصْدِفُونَ‌). Although "سُوءَ الْعَذابِ‌" means "evil punishment," since an evil punishment is one that is inherently severe and exceptionally painful, many commentators have interpreted it as "severe punishment."

The repetition of the phrase "يَصْدِفُونَ‌" (they turn away) when stating the punishment of such persons is to clarify the reality that all their troubles and miseries stem from the fact that they turn away from truths without thought or study. If they had allowed themselves to study, at least as a seeker or someone in a state of doubt, they would never have been afflicted with such painful consequences.

[1]: The phrase "أَنْ تَقُولُوا" means "لئلا تقولوا": "Lest you say..." and there are many similar instances in the Quran or in Arabic literature.

Ayah

هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّآ أَن تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ أَوْ يَأْتِىَ رَبُّكَ أَوْ يَأْتِىَ بَعْضُ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّكَ ۗ يَوْمَ يَأْتِى بَعْضُ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّكَ لَا يَنفَعُ نَفْسًا إِيمَـٰنُهَا لَمْ تَكُنْ ءَامَنَتْ مِن قَبْلُ أَوْ كَسَبَتْ فِىٓ إِيمَـٰنِهَا خَيْرًا ۗ قُلِ ٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مُنتَظِرُونَ (١٥٨)
Do they not consider that the angels may come to them, or your Lord may come, or some of your Lord’s signs may come? The day when some of your Lord’s signs do come, faith shall not benefit any soul that had not believed beforehand and had not earned some goodness in its faith. Say, ‘Wait! We too are waiting!’ (158)

Commentary

Unreasonable and Impossible Expectations!

In the preceding verses, the truth was established that We have completed the proof for the polytheists and sent the Heavenly Book—the Quran—for the guidance of all, so that they may have no excuse to justify their opposition.

This verse states: However, these stubborn individuals are so persistent in their ways that even this clear program does not affect them; it is as if they are waiting for their own destruction, or the passing of the final opportunity, or are awaiting impossible matters.

First, it says: "Do they wait for anything except that the angels [of death] should come to them?" (هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ) "or that your Lord should come to them" and they see Him and then believe!! (أَوْ يَأْتِيَ رَبُّكَ) In reality, they are waiting for something impossible, not that the coming of Allah or seeing Him is possible. This is exactly like saying to a stubborn murderer who, after being presented with sufficient evidence, still denies his crime: "If you do not accept all this evidence, you must be waiting for the victim to come back to life, appear in court, and testify that you killed him!" Then it says: "or that some of the signs of your Lord should come," referring to those events that occur on the threshold of the Resurrection and the end of the world, following which the doors of repentance will be closed (أَوْ يَأْتِيَ بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ).

Therefore, the expression "آياتِ رَبِّكَ" (signs of your Lord), although mentioned in a general and absolute form, can be understood—by the context of the subsequent sentences which will explain it—to mean the signs of the Resurrection, such as terrifying earthquakes, and the darkening of the sun, moon, and stars, and the like.

Alternatively, it could refer to the irrational demands they made of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, including their claims that they would not believe unless stones fell from the sky upon them, or the dry and scorching desert of Hijaz became filled with springs and palm groves!

Following this, it adds: "The day that such signs come, the faith of those who did not believe before, or those who did not perform any good deeds, will not be accepted," and the doors of repentance will be closed to them. This is because faith and repentance at that moment take on a forced and desperate nature, and will lack the value of voluntary faith and repentance (يَوْمَ يَأْتِي بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ لا يَنْفَعُ نَفْساً إِيمانُها لَمْ تَكُنْ آمَنَتْ مِنْ قَبْلُ أَوْ كَسَبَتْ فِي إِيمانِها خَيْراً).

From what we have stated, it becomes clear that the phrase "أَوْ كَسَبَتْ فِي إِيمانِها خَيْراً" (or earned through its faith any good) means that on that day, not only will coming to faith be of no benefit, but even those who had believed but performed no righteous deeds will find that performing a righteous deed on that day will not benefit them. This is because the circumstances will be such that everyone will involuntarily abandon wrongdoings and turn toward righteous deeds out of necessity.

At the end of the verse, in a threatening tone toward these stubborn individuals, it says: "Now that you have such an expectation, then wait; we too are waiting [for your painful punishment]" (قُلِ انْتَظِرُوا إِنَّا مُنْتَظِرُونَ).

Faith Without Action is of No Benefit

One of the interesting points derived from the above verse is that it introduces the path of salvation as faith—specifically, a faith in the light of which "good is earned" and righteous deeds are performed.

The question may arise: Is faith alone not sufficient, even if it is devoid of all righteous deeds?

In response, we say: It is true that believers may have lapses, commit sins, regret their sins, and seek to reform themselves. However, for someone who has never performed a single good deed in their entire life despite having sufficient opportunity, and conversely, has committed every kind of sin and wickedness, it seems very unlikely that they would be among those saved or that their faith alone would be useful. This is because, fundamentally, it is hard to believe that someone could have faith in a school of thought but never once, in their entire life, act upon its programs, and instead trample upon all its instructions. This itself is a clear proof of their lack of faith. Thus, faith must be accompanied by at least some righteous deeds to demonstrate that faith actually exists.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ وَكَانُوا۟ شِيَعًا لَّسْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِى شَىْءٍ ۚ إِنَّمَآ أَمْرُهُمْ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ (١٥٩)
Indeed those who split up their religion and became sects, you will not have anything to do with them. Their matter rests only with Allah; then He will inform them concerning what they used to do. (159)
مَن جَآءَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُۥ عَشْرُ أَمْثَالِهَا ۖ وَمَن جَآءَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ فَلَا يُجْزَىٰٓ إِلَّا مِثْلَهَا وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (١٦٠)
Whoever brings virtue shall receive [a reward] ten times its like; but whoever brings vice shall not be requited except with its like, and they will not be wronged. (160)

Commentary

Alienation from the Sowers of Discord

Following the ten commandments mentioned in the previous verses—at the end of which was the command to follow "God's Straight Path" and to struggle against all forms of hypocrisy and division—this verse serves as an emphasis, commentary, and clarification of that very point.

First, It states: "Indeed, those who have divided their religion and broken into sects, you have no concern with them in anything, and they have no connection to your school of thought" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا دِينَهُمْ وَ كانُوا شِيَعاً لَسْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِي شَيْ‌ءٍ) [^1]. This is because your school is the school of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the Straight Path, and the Straight Path is always but one. Then, as a threat and rebuke to such divisive individuals, It says: "Their affair is only with Allah; then He will inform them of what they used to do" (إِنَّما أَمْرُهُمْ إِلَى اللَّهِ ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَفْعَلُونَ‌).

Here, attention to two points is necessary

1- Who are the targets of this verse?

A group of commentators believe that the verse was revealed concerning the Jews and Christians, who split into various scattered factions and arrayed themselves against one another.

However, others believe the verse refers to the sowers of discord within this Ummah (Muslim community) who, due to prejudice, a spirit of superiority, and a love for leadership—the sources of division and hypocrisy—create rifts among the Muslim people.

Nevertheless, the content of the verse is a general and universal ruling concerning all divisive individuals who sow the seeds of hypocrisy and discord among God's servants by creating various innovations (Bid'ah), whether they were among previous nations or are within this Ummah.

[^1]: Linguistically, "Shi'a" (شِيَع) means sects, groups, and followers of different individuals. Thus, its singular form refers to a group that follows a specific school or person. This is the linguistic meaning of Shi'a. However, terminologically, it has a specific meaning and refers to those who, after the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), followed the school of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him). One should not confuse the linguistic meaning with the terminological one.

If we observe that traditions from the Ahl al-Bayt, as well as from Sunni sources, state that this verse refers to the misguided, the sowers of discord, and the innovators of this Ummah, it is in fact a mention of an "application" (Misdaq) [^1]. For if this application were not mentioned, some might imagine that it refers exclusively to others, thereby projecting the reference onto others and exonerating themselves!

In a narration from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) cited in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim regarding this verse, we read: "They separated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) and became factions." [^2] The narrations reported from the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) regarding the splitting and scattering of this Ummah—as a prophecy—also confirm this reality.

The Ugliness of Division and Hypocrisy

This verse once again reiterates with full emphasis the reality that Islam is a religion of unity and oneness, and is averse to all forms of hypocrisy, division, and scattering. It tells the Prophet that your program and work bear no resemblance to those who sow discord; Allah, the All-Powerful Avenger, will take vengeance upon them and show them the grim consequence of their actions.

Monotheism is not only one of the principles of Islam; rather, all the principles and branches of Islam and all its programs revolve around the axis of Monotheism. Monotheism is the spirit in the body of all Islamic teachings; it is the soul breathed into the frame of Islam.

But with great regret, a religion whose entire being is composed of unity and oneness has today fallen prey to the discord of hypocrites to such an extent that it has completely lost its original face. Every day, a sinister tune, like the hooting of an owl in a ruin, rises from some corner. An adventurous person, or one afflicted with mental illness or poor taste, raises a flag of opposition against one of the Islamic programs and gathers a group of foolish and ignorant individuals around themselves, becoming the source of a new conflict.

The role of the lack of information and ignorance of a segment of the masses in this sowing of discord is as effective as the alertness and awareness of the enemies.

Sometimes, issues whose period of debate passed centuries ago are raised anew, and a foolish clamor is stirred around them to preoccupy people's minds. But as the above verse says, Islam is alienated from them and they are alienated from Islam, and ultimately, the programs of discord-sowing will fail [^1].

The Unjust Attacks of the Author of Al-Manar against the Shi'a

The author of Tafsir al-Manar, who harbors an extraordinary prejudice against the "Shi'a" and is equally ignorant of Shi'a beliefs and history, has spoken under the mask of a call to unity in a section regarding the Shi'a. He accused them of being sowers of discord, opponents of Islam, and those who engaged in anti-Islamic political activities under the veil of religion! It seems the presence of the word Shi'an (شِيَعاً) in the above verse—which has no connection to the issue of Tashayyu' (Shi'ism) and the Shi'a—reminded him of these baseless claims!

His writings are the best response to his own words and the clearest witness to his lack of knowledge regarding Shi'a history and beliefs because:

1- He established a link between the "Shi'a" and "Abdullah ibn Saba," the Jew—whose very existence is historically doubtful and, assuming he existed, played the least role in the history of Shi'ism and Shi'a books. On the other hand, he linked the "Shi'a" with the "Batiniyya," and even between the "Shi'a" and "deviant sects" who are among the greatest enemies of the Shi'a. A brief acquaintance with Shi'a history shows that these are nothing more than illusory claims, rather, they are slanders and accusations. Most strangely, he linked the "Ghulat" (those who exaggerated regarding Ali (peace be upon him) and accepted him as God)—who are recognized in Shi'a jurisprudence as a group whose disbelief (Kufr) is certain and established—to the Shi'a, accusing the Shi'a of worshipping the Ahl al-Bayt and the like!

Certainly, if the author of Al-Manar were not under the influence of incorrect preconceptions and undue prejudices, and if he had allowed himself to hear Shi'a beliefs from the Shi'a themselves and read them in Shi'a books rather than the books of their enemies, he would have known well that these attributions are not only lies and slanders but are also ridiculous and laughable.

And most strangely, they attributed the emergence of the Shi'a to the Persians, while centuries before the Persians became Shi'a, Shi'ism existed in Iraq, Hijaz, and Egypt, and historical documents are a living witness to this reality.

2- The "sin" of the Shi'a is that they acted upon the definitive word of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), which is also recorded in the most authentic Sunni sources, where he said: "I leave among you two weighty things; if you hold fast to them, you will never go astray: the Book of Allah and my progeny ('Itrah)." [^1] The sin of the Shi'a is that they made the Ahl al-Bayt of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family)—who were the most knowledgeable of all regarding his school and religion—their refuge in Islamic problems and derived the truths of Islam from them.

The sin of the Shi'a is that, following logic and reason and inspired by the Quran and Sunnah, they consider the door of "Ijtihad" (independent legal reasoning) to be open, giving dynamism to Islamic jurisprudence. They ask: What reason is there to restrict the understanding of the Quran and Sunnah to "four individuals," and for everyone to be forced to follow them?! Are not the addresses of the Quran and Sunnah directed at all believing individuals in all centuries and eras?

And did the companions of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) follow specific individuals in understanding the Quran and Sunnah? So why should we confine Islam within four dry and ancient walls named the "Four Schools: Hanafi, Maliki, Hanbali, and Shafi'i"?! The sin of the Shi'a is that they say: The companions of the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), like all other individuals, must be evaluated by the scale of their faith and actions. Those whose actions conform to the Book and Sunnah are good, and those who acted contrary to the Book and Sunnah during the Prophet's time or after him must be rejected. The title of being a "companion" should not become a shield for criminals. Individuals like Mu'awiyah, who trampled all Islamic standards and rebelled against the Imam of the time—whose Imamate the entire Muslim Ummah accepted, at least at that time—and shed so much innocent blood, should not be sanctified, nor should some of the mercenary companions who were part of his clique.

Yes, the Shi'a have such "sins" and confess to them. But do you know of any group more oppressed than the Shi'a, whose brilliant points and honors of life and history are portrayed as weaknesses? A world of lies and slanders is attributed to them, and they are not even allowed to spread their correct beliefs among the general Muslims; instead, their beliefs are always taken from their enemies rather than from themselves!

If a group acts according to the command of its Prophet while others do not, are they considered sowers of discord and hypocrisy? Should they be turned back from the path they are traveling so that unity may be established? Or those who are going astray?!

3- The history of Islamic sciences shows that the Shi'a were pioneers in most of these sciences, to the extent that the Shi'a are recognized as the originators of Islamic sciences [^1]. The books that Shi'a scholars have written in the fields of Tafsir, history, Hadith, Fiqh, Usul, Rijal, and Islamic philosophy are not things that can be hidden; they are available in all free libraries (except, of course, most Sunni libraries, which usually do not allow these books entry, while we have allowed their books into our libraries for centuries!). This is a living witness to this claim.

Were those who wrote all these books for the greatness of Islam and the advancement of Islamic teachings enemies of Islam?

Do you know of an enemy who shows so much friendship and love?

Can anyone other than a sincere lover of the Quran and the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) be the source of so much service?

In conclusion, we must say: if you want hypocrisy and division to be eradicated, come and, instead of accusing one another, let us know each other as we truly are. For such unjust attributions not only do not help Islamic unity but strike a heavy blow to the foundations of Islamic unity.

Greater Reward, Lesser Punishment

In the next verse, reference is made to the vast mercy and reward of Allah awaiting the righteous, completing the threats of the previous verse with these encouragements. It says: "Whoever brings a good deed shall have ten times the like thereof to his credit" (مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثالِها) "and whoever brings an evil deed shall be recompensed only with the like thereof" (وَ مَنْ جاءَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ فَلا يُجْزى‌ إِلَّا مِثْلَها).

And for emphasis, It adds this sentence: "and they shall not be wronged," and they will only be punished to the extent of their actions (وَ هُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ‌).

There is discussion among commentators as to what is meant by "good deed" (Hasanah) and "evil deed" (Sayyi'ah) in the above verse. Does it specifically mean "Monotheism" and "Polytheism," or does it have a broader meaning? However, the apparent meaning of the verse includes any "good deed, good thought, and good belief, or bad." There is no reason to limit the meaning of Hasanah and Sayyi'ah.

Here, several points should be noted

1- The meaning of "Brings it" (جاء به)

As understood from the concept of the sentence, it means that one brings the good or bad deed with them. That is, upon entering the court of Divine Justice, a person cannot be empty-handed and alone; they either have a good belief and action with them or evil beliefs and actions. These are always with them, never separating from them, and will be their companion and associate in the eternal life.

In other verses of the Quran, this expression is used with the same meaning. In verse 33 of Surah Qaf, we read: "Who feared the Most Merciful in the unseen and came with a heart turned in repentance" (مَنْ خَشِيَ الرَّحْمنَ بِالْغَيْبِ وَ جاءَ بِقَلْبٍ مُنِيبٍ‌). Paradise is for those who know God through faith in the unseen and fear Him, and bring a repentant heart full of a sense of responsibility with them to the arena of Resurrection.

2- The reward for a good deed is tenfold

In the above verse, we read that the reward for a "Hasanah" is tenfold, while in some other verses of the Quran, it is merely mentioned as "many multiples" (اضعافا كثيره) (such as verse 245 of Surah Baqarah). In other verses, the reward for some actions like charity (Infaq) reaches seven hundred times or even more (verse 261 of Surah Baqarah). In yet other verses, an "accountless" reward is mentioned, saying: "Indeed, the patient will be given their reward without account" (إِنَّما يُوَفَّى الصَّابِرُونَ أَجْرَهُمْ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ‌) (Az-Zumar, verse 10).

It is clear that these verses do not contradict each other. In reality, the minimum reward given to the righteous is tenfold, and it increases according to the importance of the action, the degree of its sincerity, and the efforts and struggles undertaken for it, until it breaks every boundary and none but Allah knows its limit.

For example, charity, which is extraordinarily important in Islam, has a reward that exceeds the usual reward for a good deed (tenfold) and reaches "many multiples" or "seven hundred times" and more. In the case of "patience" (Istiqamah), which is the root of all successes, prosperities, and happiness, and without which no belief or good deed will remain firm, an accountless reward is mentioned.

From this, it becomes clear that if narrations mention rewards of more than tenfold for some good deeds, there is no contradiction with the above verse.

Similarly, if we read in verse 84 of Surah Al-Qasas: "Whoever brings a good deed, he shall have better than it" (مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ خَيْرٌ مِنْها), it does not oppose the above verse such that the possibility of abrogation (Naskh) arises, because "better" has a broad meaning that is perfectly compatible with "tenfold."

3- Why should 60 days be the expiation for one day?

Some might imagine that the obligation of "sixty days" as expiation (Kaffarah) for eating one day of the fast of the holy month of Ramadan, and other such punishments in this world and the hereafter, are not compatible with the above verse which says: for an evil deed, one will only be punished to its extent.

However, with attention to one point, the answer to this objection becomes clear:

The meaning of equality between "sin" and "punishment" is not numerical equality; rather, the quality of the action must also be considered. Breaking one day of the fast of Ramadan, with all its importance, does not have a punishment of only one day of expiation; rather, one must fast so much that it equals the sanctity of that one day of the holy month. For this reason, we read in some narrations that the punishment for sins in the month of Ramadan is more than in other days, just as the reward for good deeds in those days is greater, to the extent that, for example, one completion of the Quran in this month is equal to seventy completions in other months.

4- The Ultimate Grace of the Lord

Another interesting point is that the above verse portrays the ultimate grace and mercy of the Lord toward man. Who do you know who places all the tools of work at man's disposal, gives him all kinds of awareness, and sends infallible leaders for his guidance and direction, so that he may perform a good deed using God-given strength and the leadership of His messengers, and then sets a tenfold reward for his work? Yet, for his slips and errors, He considers only a single crime, and even then opens the way for apology, repentance, and reaching pardon and forgiveness.

Abu Dharr says: The Truthful and Verified one (i.e., the Prophet) said: "Allah the Almighty said: 'Good deeds are ten times or more, and an evil deed is one or I forgive. So woe to him whose ones overcome his tens'" (meaning his sins exceed his acts of obedience). [^1]

Ayahs

قُلْ إِنَّنِى هَدَىٰنِى رَبِّىٓ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ دِينًا قِيَمًا مِّلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ حَنِيفًا ۚ وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٦١)
Say, ‘Indeed my Lord has guided me to a straight path, the upright religion, the creed of Abraham, a Hanif, and he was not one of the polytheists.’ (161)
قُلْ إِنَّ صَلَاتِى وَنُسُكِى وَمَحْيَاىَ وَمَمَاتِى لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٦٢)
Say, ‘Indeed my prayer and my worship, my life and my death are for the sake of Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. (162)
لَا شَرِيكَ لَهُۥ ۖ وَبِذَٰلِكَ أُمِرْتُ وَأَنَا۠ أَوَّلُ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ (١٦٣)
He has no partner, and I have been commanded [to follow] this [creed], and I am the first of those who submit [to Allah].’ (163)

Commentary

This is My Straight Path

These few verses, and the verses that follow which conclude Surah Al-An'am, are in fact a summary of the discussions in this Surah regarding the struggle against polytheism and idol-worship, providing further completion and explanation. Indeed, this Surah begins with a call to Monotheism (Tawhid) and the struggle against polytheism, and it concludes with the same discussion.

First, in response to the illogical beliefs and claims of the polytheists and idolaters, Allah commands His Prophet: "Say: My Lord has guided me to a straight path, which is the nearest of ways" (this straight path is the road of Monotheism and the crushing of the creed of polytheism and idol-worship) (قُلْ إِنَّنِي هَدانِي رَبِّي إِلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ‌).

It is noteworthy that this verse, and a large number of verses before and after it, begin with the word "Qul" (Say). Perhaps in no other Surah of the Quran is this word repeated to such an extent. This reflects the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله intense engagement in this Surah with the logic of the polytheists.

Furthermore, it closes every avenue of excuse for them, as the repetition of the word "Say" indicates that all these discourses are by the command of Allah—in other words, they are the very logic of the Lord, not the personal opinions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

It is clear that the inclusion of the word "Say" in these verses and their like within the text of the Quran is to preserve the authenticity of the Quran and to convey the exact words revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. In other words, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله made no changes to the words revealed to him, even including the word "Say," which was the Lord's address to him.

He then explains this "Straight Path" (صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ‌) in this verse and the two following verses:

First, He says: "It is a firm religion, in the ultimate truth and correctness, eternal and everlasting," upholding the affairs of religion, the world, the body, and the soul (دِيناً قِيَماً) [1]. Since the Arabs showed a special affection for Ibrahim عليه السلام and even presented their own creed as the religion of Ibrahim, He adds: "The true religion of Ibrahim is exactly what I am calling towards," not what you have attributed to him (مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ‌).

The same Ibrahim who "turned away from the superstitious creeds of his time and environment and turned toward the Truth, which is the creed of Monotheism" (حنيفا).

"Hanif" refers to a person or thing that inclines toward a certain direction, but in Quranic terminology, it refers to one who turns away from the false creeds of their time and pays heed to the true religion.

This expression is seemingly a response to the words of the polytheists who criticized the Prophet's opposition to idol-worship, which was the religion of the Arab ancestors. In response to them, the Prophet says: This breaking of tradition and turning one's back on the superstitious beliefs of the environment is not my act alone; Ibrahim, who is respected by all of us, did the same.

Then, for emphasis, He adds: "And he was never of the polytheists or idolaters" (وَ ما كانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ‌). Rather, he was the hero of idol-breaking and a dynamic, persistent warrior against the creed of polytheism.

The repetition of the phrase "Hanifan wa ma kana min al-mushrikin" in several places in the Quran, with or without the word "Musliman," is to emphasize that the sacred station of Ibrahim—in whom the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance took pride—was pure from these false beliefs and actions [1].

In the next verse, He points out: Say that not only in terms of belief am I a Monotheist, but also in terms of action; every good deed I perform, "my prayer and all my acts of worship, and even my death and my life, are all for the Lord of the worlds." I live for Him, I die for Him, and I sacrifice everything I have in His way. My entire goal, my entire love, and my entire existence is Him (قُلْ إِنَّ صَلاتِي وَ نُسُكِي وَ مَحْيايَ وَ مَماتِي لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ‌).

"Nusuk" originally means worship, and thus a worshipping person is called a "nasik." However, this word is most commonly used regarding the rites of Hajj, referred to as "Manasik al-Hajj." Some have suggested that "nusuk" here might mean "sacrifice," but it appears to encompass every form of worship. In fact, first, prayer (Salat) is mentioned as the most important worship, and then all acts of worship are stated generally. That is, both my prayer and all my worship, and even my life and death, are all for Him.

In the following verse, for emphasis and to nullify every form of polytheism and idol-worship, He adds: "The Lord for whom there is no partner or peer" (لا شَرِيكَ لَهُ‌).

Finally, He says: "And of this I have been commanded, and I am the first of the Muslims" (وَ بِذلِكَ أُمِرْتُ وَ أَنَا أَوَّلُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ‌).

How the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was the First Muslim

In the above verse, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is mentioned as the first Muslim. There is discussion among commentators regarding this, because we know that if "Islam" is meant in its broad sense, it includes all heavenly religions. For this reason, the word "Muslim" has been applied to other Prophets as well. Regarding Nuh عليه السلام, we read: "And I have been commanded to be of the Muslims" (Yunus: 72). Regarding Ibrahim the Friend of Allah عليه السلام and his son Isma'il, we read: "Our Lord, and make us both Muslims [submissive] to You" (Al-Baqarah: 128). Regarding Yusuf عليه السلام, it is said: "Take my soul as a Muslim" (Yusuf: 101), and similarly for other Prophets.

Of course, "Muslim" means one who is submissive to the command of Allah, and this meaning applies to all Divine Prophets and their believing nations. Nevertheless, the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله being the "first Muslim" is either in terms of the quality and significance of his Islam—as his degree of submission and Islam was higher than all others—or he was the first individual of this Ummah to accept the creed of the Quran and Islam.

In some narrations, it is also stated that it refers to the first person who, in the realm of souls (Alam al-Arwah), gave a positive response to the Lord's call and His question regarding His Divinity [1]. In any case, the above verses clarify the spirit of Islam and the reality of the Quran's teachings: a call to the straight path, a call to the pure religion of Ibrahim the idol-breaker, and a call to negate every form of polytheism, duality, or plurality—this is from the perspective of belief and faith.

As for action, it is a call to sincerity and purity of intention, performing everything for Allah, living for Him, giving one's life in His way, desiring everything for Him, attaching one's heart to Him and detaching from all else, loving Him and seeking disassociation from all besides Him.

How great is the distance between this explicit call of Islam and the actions of a group of "pseudo-Muslims" who understand and know nothing but ostentation and showing off! In every matter, they think only of the appearance and are indifferent to the inner reality and essence. For this reason, their life, society, population, honor, and freedom are nothing but hollow shells!

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 4, p. 390. [^1]: "Qiyaman" can mean truth and steadfastness, or it can mean enduring and eternal, or it can mean upholding the affairs of religion and the world. [^1]: Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 135; Surah Al-Imran, Verses 67 and 95. [^1]: Tafsir al-Safi, under the verse.

Ayah

قُلْ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَبْغِى رَبًّا وَهُوَ رَبُّ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ ۚ وَلَا تَكْسِبُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ إِلَّا عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۚ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُم مَّرْجِعُكُمْ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ (١٦٤)
Say, ‘Shall I seek a Lord other than Allah, while He is the Lord of all things?’ No soul does evil except against itself, and no bearer shall bear another’s burden; then to your Lord will be your return, whereat He will inform you concerning that about which you used to differ. (164)

Commentary

How significant are the successive emphases and various arguments presented in this Surah regarding Monotheism (Tawhid) and the struggle against polytheism (Shirk).

In this verse, the logic of the polytheists is criticized through another avenue, saying: "Tell them and ask them: Is it appropriate for me to seek a lord other than the One Allah, while He is the Owner, the Nurturer, and the Lord of all things, and His decree and command prevail over every atom of this universe?" (قُلْ أَ غَيْرَ اللَّهِ أَبْغِي رَبًّا وَ هُوَ رَبُّ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

Then, it responds to a group of short-sighted polytheists who came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "Follow us, and your burden shall be upon us if it be an error!" (Follow our creed; if it is wrong, your sin will be on our necks!):

"No soul earns [evil] except against itself, and no bearer of burdens shall bear the burden of another" (وَ لا تَكْسِبُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ إِلَّا عَلَيْها وَ لا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى‌).

And "Ultimately, to your Lord is your return, and He will inform you at that time concerning that over which you used to differ" (ثُمَّ إِلى‌ رَبِّكُمْ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ‌).

Two points must be noted here

1- Sometimes we bear the burden of others' sins!

It might sometimes be imagined that the above verse, which states two established logical principles present in all religions (namely, that no one performs an act except for themselves and no sinner bears the burden of another), does not align with certain other verses of the Quran and some narrations. For instance, in Surah An-Nahl, verse 25, we read: "That they may bear their own burdens in full on the Day of Resurrection and some of the burdens of those whom they misled without knowledge" (لِيَحْمِلُوا أَوْزارَهُمْ كامِلَةً يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَ مِنْ أَوْزارِ الَّذِينَ يُضِلُّونَهُمْ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ‌).

If no one bears the burden of another, how do these misleaders bear the burdens of those they led astray?

Furthermore, the hadiths regarding "Good Sunnah" (Sunnah Hasanah) and "Evil Sunnah" (Sunnah Sayyi'ah), which have been transmitted through both Shi'a and Sunni chains, convey this meaning: If someone establishes a good tradition, they will have the reward of all those who act upon it (without anything being diminished from their own rewards); likewise, someone who establishes an evil tradition will have the sin of those who act upon it recorded for them (without anything being diminished from their sins).

The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Whoever establishes a good tradition (Sunnah Hasanah) shall have its reward and the reward of whoever acts upon it without their rewards being diminished in the least; and whoever establishes an evil tradition (Sunnah Sayyi'ah) shall bear its burden and the burden of whoever acts upon it without their burdens being diminished in the least."

This also seems inconsistent with the concept of the verse under discussion.

However, the answer to this objection is clear: the verse under discussion states that the sin of one person is not recorded for another without cause or connection. But the aforementioned verses and narrations state that if a person is the founder of another's good or evil deed—and is, so to speak, involved in the performance of that deed through "causality" (tasbib)—they will share in its consequences. In reality, it is considered their own deed because its foundation was laid by them.

2- Are the good deeds of others beneficial to us?

Another misconception that might arise from the verse under discussion is that it says "every person's deed only benefits themselves." Therefore, charitable acts performed as proxies or as gifts of reward (thawab) for the deceased, or even sometimes for living individuals, cannot be beneficial to them. This is while we read in numerous narrations transmitted through Shi'a and Sunni sources from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام that such acts are sometimes useful and beneficial—not only the deed of a child for their parents but for others as well.

Additionally, we know that the reward for deeds depends on the effects that the performance of a good deed leaves on the human soul and spirit, influencing their perfection and progress. How can someone who has performed no good deed and was not even involved in its preliminaries attain that spiritual and internal effect?

Some pursue this objection with great fervor, and not only ordinary individuals but even some writers and commentators, such as the author of "Al-Manar," have been influenced by it to the point of disregarding many established hadiths and narrations. However, by considering two points, the answer to such objections becomes clear:

1- It is true that every person's deed leads to their own perfection, and the philosophies and real results of good deeds only accrue to the doer, just as one's "exercise" or "education" leads to the "strengthening" and "perfection" of their own body and soul.

However, when someone performs a good deed for another, it is certainly because of a merit or good quality that existed in that person; perhaps they were a good teacher, a good student, a sincere friend, a loyal neighbor, a serving scholar, or a true believer. In any case, there was at least one bright spot in their life that attracted the attention of others and caused them to perform good deeds on their behalf. Therefore, they are actually receiving the result of that very merit, prominent quality, or bright spot in their own life. In this way, the performance of a good deed by others for them is usually a reflection of their own good deed or intention.

2- The rewards Allah grants to individuals are of two types: rewards that correspond to the state of spiritual perfection and merit of individuals—meaning that by performing good deeds, their souls soar and progress so much that they gain the merit to live in better and higher realms, flying toward the heights of happiness with wings fashioned from belief and good deeds. Certainly, these types of effects are specific to the doer and cannot be transferred to another.

However, since every good deed is an act of obedience to Allah's command, and the obedient person deserves a reward for the obedience they have shown, they can gift this reward to another by their own will.

It is exactly like a professor teaching an important and constructive scientific field at a university. There is no doubt that through teaching, they gain two types of results: they become more perfect and stronger in scientific aspects through the act of teaching, and they also receive a salary. The first result certainly cannot be gifted to another and is specific to them, but the second result can be gifted to their loved ones.

Gifting the reward of deeds for the deceased, and sometimes for the living, is the same way. Thus, any ambiguity regarding the hadiths narrated in this field is resolved.

However, it must be noted that the rewards reaching individuals through this path cannot secure their entire salvation; rather, they will have lesser effects. The primary foundation of human salvation remains their own faith and deeds.

Ayah

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَكُمْ خَلَـٰٓئِفَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَرَفَعَ بَعْضَكُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجَـٰتٍ لِّيَبْلُوَكُمْ فِى مَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْعِقَابِ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌۢ (١٦٥)
It is He who has made you successors on the earth, and raised some of you in rank above others so that He may test you in respect to what He has given you. Indeed your Lord is swift in retribution, and indeed, He is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (165)

Commentary

In this verse, which is the final verse of Surah Al-An'am, reference is made to the importance of the human status and his position in the universe, so that the previous discussions regarding strengthening the foundations of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the struggle against polytheism (Shirk) are completed. That is, man should recognize his own value as the superior being in the world of creation, so that he does not prostrate before stones, wood, and various other idols, nor become their captive; rather, he should be their commander and ruler.

Therefore, in the first sentence, He says: "And He it is who has made you successors (and His representatives) upon the earth" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَكُمْ خَلائِفَ الْأَرْضِ [1]). A human being who is the representative of Allah on earth, with all the resources of this world placed at his disposal, and whose decree of authority over all these beings has been issued by the Lord, must not degrade himself to the point of becoming lower than inanimate matter and prostrating before it.

Then, referring to the difference in talents and the variation in physical and spiritual blessings among people, and the purpose of this difference and variation, He says: "and He has raised some of you above others in degrees, that He may try you by means of what He has bestowed upon you" (وَ رَفَعَ بَعْضَكُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجاتٍ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ فِي ما آتاكُمْ).

At the end of the verse, while pointing to human freedom in choosing the path of prosperity or misery, He describes the result of these trials as follows: Your Lord is "swift in retribution" (toward those who emerge blackened from the crucible of these trials), and toward those who seek to reform and compensate for their mistakes, He is All-Forgiving and Merciful (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ سَرِيعُ الْعِقابِ وَ إِنَّهُ لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Differences Among Humans and the Principle of Justice

There is no doubt that among human beings, there exists a series of artificial differences which are the result of the injustices and oppression of some humans toward others. For example, a group possesses immeasurable wealth while another group sits on the black dust of poverty; a group remains ignorant and illiterate due to the lack of educational means, while another group reaches the highest academic levels because all kinds of facilities were provided; some are infirm and ill due to malnutrition and lack of health facilities, while others live in ultimate health because all possibilities are available to them.

These types of differences—wealth and poverty, knowledge and ignorance, health and sickness—are often born of colonialism, exploitation, various forms of slavery, and overt or covert injustices.

Certainly, these cannot be attributed to the system of creation, and there is no reason to defend the existence of such groundless differences.

However, at the same time, it cannot be denied that no matter how much the principles of justice are observed in human society, all humans will still not be identical in terms of talent, intelligence, thought, various tastes, and even physical structure.

But does the existence of such differences contradict the principle of justice, or on the contrary, does real justice—meaning placing everything in its proper place—require that everyone not be identical?

If all members of human society were like fabric or vessels coming out of a single factory—identical in shape, uniform, with equal and similar talents—human society would be a dead, soulless society, devoid of movement and lacking evolution.

Look closely at the anatomy of a plant: the strong and coarse roots, with stems that are more delicate yet relatively firm, and branches that are even more tender, and finally the leaves, buds, and petals, each more delicate than the other, have joined hands to create a beautiful flower bush. The cells of each are completely different according to their missions, and their talents are varied in proportion to their duties.

This same subject is seen in the world of humanity. Human individuals, taken together, also form a large and fruitful tree, where each group—rather, each individual—bears a specific mission within this great body, and possesses a structure suited to it. This is why the Quran says these differences are a means of testing you, because as we have said before, "testing" in the context of Divine programs means "training and nurturing." In this way, the answer to any objection raised due to a misunderstanding of the verse's concept becomes clear.

Human Succession on Earth

Another interesting point is that the Quran repeatedly introduces man as the "Khalifa" (successor) and "representative of Allah on earth." This expression, while clarifying the status of man, also expresses the reality that the properties, wealth, talents, and all the blessings Allah has given to man belong, in truth, to the Original Owner (Allah), and man is merely a representative, authorized and permitted by Him. It is obvious that any representative is not independent in his dealings; rather, his actions must be within the limits of the permission and authorization of the Original Owner.

From here, it becomes clear that, for example, in the issue of ownership, Islam distances itself from both the "Communist" camp and the "Capitalist" camp. The first group considers ownership exclusive to society, and the second group places it in the hands of the individual. However, Islam says: ownership belongs neither to the individual nor to society, but in reality, it belongs to Allah, and humans are His trustees and representatives. For this reason, Islam supervises both the manner of individuals' income and the manner of their consumption, and has established constraints and conditions for both, which positions Islamic economics as a distinct school of thought compared to others.

End of Surah Al-An'am.

Surah Al-A'raf

This Surah is among the Meccan Surahs, except for the verses from "And ask them about the town..." until "...for the defiance they used to practice," which were revealed in Medina. The number of verses in this Surah is 206, and according to some, 205.

An Overview of the Topics in This Surah

As we know, most Surahs of the Quran (80 to 90 Surahs) were revealed in Mecca. Considering the environment of Mecca and the condition of Muslims during those 13 years, as well as studying the history of Islam after the Migration (Hijrah), it becomes completely clear why the tone of "Meccan" Surahs differs from "Medinan" ones.

In Meccan Surahs, the discussion often revolves around the Origin (Mabda') and the Resurrection (Ma'ad), proving Monotheism and the Court of Resurrection, struggling against polytheism and idol-worship, and establishing the status and position of man in the universe. This is because the Meccan period was a time for building the Muslims' beliefs and strengthening the foundations of faith as the infrastructure for a "deep-rooted movement."

During the "Meccan period," the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) had the duty to wash away superstitious idol-worshipping thoughts from minds and replace them with the spirit of Monotheism, God-worship, and a sense of responsibility.

He had to acquaint humans—who had been humiliated and defeated during the era of idol-worship—with the greatness of their own status and position, and consequently transform that lowly, abject, superstitious, and passive nation into a people of character, will, faith, and positivity. The reason for the rapid and lightning-like progress of Islam in Medina was this very firm foundation built during the Meccan period by the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and in the light of the Quranic verses.

The verses of the Meccan Surahs are all proportionate to this specific goal.

However, the Medinan period was the era of forming the Islamic government, the era of Jihad against enemies, and the era of building a healthy society based on human values and social justice. Therefore, Medinan Surahs, in many of their verses, deal with the details of legal, moral, economic, penal issues, and other individual and social needs.

If Muslims today wish to renew their ancient greatness, they must implement this exact program and fully traverse these two periods. Until that firm ideological foundation is built, the superstructure issues will lack stability and strength.

In any case, since Surah Al-A'raf is one of the Meccan Surahs, all the characteristics of a Meccan Surah are reflected in it. Thus, we see:

At the beginning, it makes a brief and firm reference to the issue of "Origin and Resurrection."

Then, to revive the human personality, it explains the story of the creation of Adam with great importance.

Afterward, it enumerates one by one the covenants that Allah took from the children of Adam on the path of guidance and righteousness.

Then, to show the failure and frustration of nations that deviate from the path of Monotheism, justice, and piety, and also to show the victory of true believers, it narrates the stories of many past nations and previous Prophets such as "Noah," "Lot," and "Shu'ayb," ending with the detailed history of the Children of Israel and the struggle of "Moses" against Pharaoh.

At the end of the Surah, it returns once more to the issue of Origin and Resurrection, thereby completing the beginning and the end.

The Importance of This Surah

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he said: "Whoever recites Surah Al-A'raf every month will be among those on the Day of Resurrection who have no fear upon them, nor shall they grieve (مِنَ الَّذِينَ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ). And if he recites it every Friday, he will be among those who enter Paradise without reckoning on the Day of Resurrection." He also said: "In this Surah are clear verses (Ayat Muhkamat); do not forget their 'reading,' 'recitation,' and 'standing by them' (acting upon them), for they will testify on the Day of Resurrection before Allah for those who recited them!" [1]

A point that is clearly understood from the above narration is that the narrations regarding the virtue of the Surahs do not mean that merely reciting these Surahs yields all those great results and fruits. Rather, what gives this reading its ultimate value is faith in their content and then acting according to it.

Therefore, in the above narration, we read: "their reading, their recitation, and standing by them." Also, in this same narration, we read that he said: the one who recites this Surah will be an instance of "those who have no fear upon them, nor shall they grieve" on the Day of Resurrection. This is, in fact, a subtle reference to verse 35 of this very Surah, which says: "Then whoever fears Allah and reforms (himself and society), no fear shall be upon them, nor shall they grieve" (فَمَنِ اتَّقى‌ وَ أَصْلَحَ فَلا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ).

As you observe, this status is exclusive to those who possess piety (Taqwa) and step onto the path of reform. Furthermore, the Quran is fundamentally a book of "belief" and "action," and reading and recitation are considered a preliminary to this subject.

"Raghib" in his book "Al-Mufradat," under the root "Tilawah," says that the meaning of "they recite it with its true recitation" (يَتْلُونَهُ حَقَّ تِلاوَتِهِ [2]) is that they follow the verses of the Quran with knowledge and action. That is, "recitation" (Tilawah) has a concept higher than "reading" (Qira'ah) and is accompanied by a type of contemplation, reflection, and action.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 2; and Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 2. [^2]: Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 121.
